| **Occlusion Sensitivity** | $\Delta_c(i,j) = P(y_c \mid x) - P(y_c \mid x_{\text{occ}})$ | Patch size | Intuitive, model-agnostic | Slow (many forward passes) |
| **t-SNE** | Minimize $KL(P \Vert Q)$ over $y \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ | 2D/3D | Cluster visualization | Non-deterministic, no global structure |
| **PCA** | Eigen-decomposition of $\Sigma = X X^T$ | 2D/3D | Fast, deterministic | Only linear structure |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Fully Connected Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1((x1))
    inp2((x2))
    inp3((x3))
    inp4((x4))

    n1["Neuron 1"]
    n2["Neuron 2"]
    n3["Neuron 3"]

    out1((a1))
    out2((a2))
    out3((a3))

    inp1 -- w11 --> n1
    inp1 -- w21 --> n2
    inp1 -- w31 --> n3

    inp2 -- w12 --> n1
    inp2 -- w22 --> n2
    inp2 -- w32 --> n3

    inp3 -- w13 --> n1
    inp3 -- w23 --> n2
    inp3 -- w33 --> n3

    inp4 -- w14 --> n1
    inp4 -- w24 --> n2
    inp4 -- w34 --> n3

    n1 -- f --> out1
    n2 -- f --> out2
    n3 -- f --> out3
```

*Description:* Each input neuron $x_j$ is connected to every output neuron with a unique weight $w_{ij}$. Every output neuron computes a weighted sum, adds a bias, and passes through activation $f$ to produce $a_i$.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: FC Layer in a CNN Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Image 224x224x3] --> B[Conv Block 1]
    B --> C[Conv Block 2]
    C --> D[Conv Block 3]
    D --> E[Conv Block 4]
    E --> F[Conv Block 5]
    F --> G[Flatten Layer]
    G --> H[FC Layer 1 - 4096 units]
    H --> I[ReLU Activation]
    I --> J[Dropout 0.5]
    J --> K[FC Layer 2 - 4096 units]
    K --> L[ReLU Activation]
    L --> M[Dropout 0.5]
    M --> N[FC Layer 3 - 1000 units]
    N --> O[Softmax Activation]
    O --> P[Class Probabilities]

    V1[Weight Visualization<br/>FC1 filters reshaped] -.-> H
    V2[Activation Visualization<br/>Feature maps of Conv5] -.-> F
    V3[t-SNE Projection<br/>Penultimate features] -.-> M
    V4[Saliency Map<br/>Gradient of class score] -.-> A
```

*Description:* A typical CNN classifier with FC head. The diagram shows the forward path (solid arrows) and the four visualization entry points (dashed arrows), illustrating where each visualization technique taps into the network.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Backpropagation Information Flow Through FC Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Loss L] --> A[Compute gradient of L w.r.t. a]
    A --> B[Multiply by f prime z Hadamard]
    B --> C[Delta - local gradient]
    C --> D1[Outer product with x for W grad]
    C --> D2[Direct copy for b grad]
    C --> D3[Multiply W transpose for x grad]
    D1 --> E1[Update W]
    D2 --> E2[Update b]
    D3 --> F[Pass gradient to previous layer]
```

*Description:* Information flow during backpropagation. The single $\delta$ vector is reused three times via matrix operations to produce gradients for $W$, $b$, and the input $x$ — a hallmark of efficient FC backprop.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: t-SNE Visualization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Raw Input Images] --> CNN[Trained CNN Backbone]
    CNN --> F[Penultimate Feature Vectors]
    F --> P[Compute Pairwise Affinities P in high-D]
    P --> Q[Initialize Random low-D Embeddings Y]
    Q --> KL[Minimize KL Divergence P Q via Gradient Descent]
    KL --> O[2D t-SNE Plot]

    O --> C1[Cluster 1 - Class A]
    O --> C2[Cluster 2 - Class B]
    O --> C3[Cluster 3 - Class C]
```

*Description:* End-to-end t-SNE pipeline for visualizing CNN features. Well-separated clusters indicate that the network has learned discriminative representations.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Saliency Map Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    I[Input Image X] --> F[Forward Pass]
    F --> S[Class Score Sc]
    S --> G[Backward Pass - compute gradient w.r.t. X]
    G --> M[Saliency Map equals absolute value of gradient]
    M --> H[Heatmap Overlay on Input]
    H --> V[Visual Interpretation]
```

*Description:* Saliency maps highlight pixels that most influence the predicted class score, providing per-pixel interpretability for any differentiable model.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
**Define a Fully Connected (FC) layer. State the number of parameters in an FC layer with input dimension 4 and output dimension 5, including biases.**

**Model Answer:**

A Fully Connected (FC) layer is a neural network layer in which every neuron of the layer is connected to every neuron of the preceding layer. Each connection has an independent weight, and each output neuron has an independent bias. Mathematically, it implements:

$$z = W x + b, \quad a = f(z)$$

Parameter count with input $m = 4$, output $n = 5$:

$$
\text{Params} = n \cdot (m + 1) = 5 \cdot (4 + 1) = 5 \cdot 5 = 25
$$

So the FC layer contains **25 learnable parameters** (20 weights + 5 biases).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
**List and briefly explain any three techniques used for feature visualization in deep neural networks.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Activation (Feature Map) Visualization:** Forward pass an input through the network and display the output activations of intermediate layers as images. Each feature map shows which spatial regions triggered a particular filter.

2. **Saliency Map:** Compute the gradient of the class score with respect to the input pixels. Pixels with large absolute gradient are the most influential for the prediction. Formula: $S_c = \vert \nabla_x S_c \vert$.

3. **t-SNE Projection:** Apply t-SNE to the penultimate-layer features of many samples to obtain a 2D embedding. Well-separated clusters indicate that the network has learned discriminative representations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of a Fully Connected layer in a deep neural network, with its mathematical formulation. Derive the expressions for forward pass and backward pass gradients. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the t-SNE algorithm for feature visualization. State its objective function and explain how it overcomes the crowding problem of SNE. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Architecture:** A Fully Connected (FC) layer contains $n$ neurons, each connected to all $m$ inputs of the previous layer. Each connection carries a weight $W_{ij}$ and each neuron has a bias $b_i$.

**Forward Pass Derivation:**

The pre-activation for neuron $i$:

$$
z_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} W_{ij} x_j + b_i
$$

Vectorized form:

$$
z = W x + b, \quad a = f(z)
$$

where $f$ is the activation function (e.g., ReLU, sigmoid, softmax).

**Backward Pass Derivation:**

Given upstream gradient $\nabla_a \mathcal{L}$, the chain rule gives:

$$
\delta = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a} \odot f'(z)
$$

Gradient w.r.t. weights (outer product):

$$
\nabla_W \mathcal{L} = \delta \cdot x^{T}
$$

Gradient w.r.t. bias:

$$
\nabla_b \mathcal{L} = \delta
$$

Gradient w.r.t. input (to propagate backward):

$$
\nabla_x \mathcal{L} = W^{T} \delta
$$

**Update Rule:**

$$
W \leftarrow W - \eta \nabla_W \mathcal{L}, \quad b \leftarrow b - \eta \nabla_b \mathcal{L}
$$

**Total Parameter Count:** $n(m+1)$ per FC layer.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Motivation:** t-SNE is a nonlinear dimensionality reduction technique introduced by van der Maaten and Hinton (2008) to visualize high-dimensional features in 2D or 3D while preserving local neighborhood structure.

**Step 1 — High-D Affinities:**
Define the conditional probability that $x_i$ would pick $x_j$ as its neighbor under a Gaussian centered at $x_i$:

$$
p_{j \mid i} = \frac{\exp(-\|x_i - x_j\|^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(-\|x_i - x_k\|^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}
$$

Symmetrize:

$$
p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \mid i} + p_{i \mid j}}{2N}
$$

**Step 2 — Low-D Affinities:**
Use a Student-t distribution with 1 degree of freedom in the low-dimensional space:

$$
q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \|y_i - y_j\|^2)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l} (1 + \|y_k - y_l\|^2)^{-1}}
$$

**Step 3 — Objective Function:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{t\text{-}SNE} = KL(P \Vert Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}
$$

**Step 4 — Optimization:**
Minimize $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to $y_i$ using gradient descent:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_i} = 4 \sum_j (p_{ij} - q_{ij})(y_i - y_j)(1 + \|y_i - y_j\|^2)^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Overcoming the Crowding Problem:**
In SNE, the Gaussian kernel in low-D space decays too fast, so moderately distant points in high-D get crammed together. t-SNE uses the heavy-tailed Student-t distribution in low-D, which gives moderate points disproportionately large $q_{ij}$ values, pulling them apart and creating more visible clusters.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[Alternative Internal Choice]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a CNN classifier. Highlight the role and placement of FC layers, and compute the total parameter count of the FC head for a flattened input of size 25088 with three FC layers of sizes 4096, 4096, and 1000. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the following visualization techniques with their mathematical basis: (i) Saliency Maps (ii) Occlusion Sensitivity (iii) Guided Backpropagation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**CNN Classifier Architecture:**

A standard CNN classifier consists of:
1. **Convolutional Blocks** (Conv → ReLU → Pool) for hierarchical feature extraction.
2. **Flatten Layer** to convert the 3D feature map into a 1D vector.
3. **Fully Connected Head** (1–3 FC layers) for classification.
4. **Softmax Output** for probability distribution over classes.

**Role of FC Layers:**
FC layers at the end of a CNN act as a **multinomial logistic regression (softmax classifier)** on top of the learned features. They combine spatially distributed features into class decisions.

**Parameter Calculation:**

Given flattened input size = 25088:

- **FC1 (25088 → 4096):**

$$
\text{Params}_{FC1} = 25088 \times 4096 + 4096 = 102{,}760{,}448 + 4{,}096 = 102{,}764{,}544
$$

- **FC2 (4096 → 4096):**

$$
\text{Params}_{FC2} = 4096 \times 4096 + 4096 = 16{,}777{,}216 + 4{,}096 = 16{,}781{,}312
$$

- **FC3 (4096 → 1000):**

$$
\text{Params}_{FC3} = 4096 \times 1000 + 1000 = 4{,}096{,}000 + 1{,}000 = 4{,}097{,}000
$$

- **Total FC Parameters:**

$$
\text{Total}_{FC} = 102{,}764{,}544 + 16{,}781{,}312 + 4{,}097{,}000 = 123{,}642{,}856
$$

**Key Observation:** This constitutes over **89%** of VGG-16's total 138M parameters, motivating modern architectures to use Global Average Pooling instead.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**(i) Saliency Maps (Simonyan et al., 2013):**

The saliency map is defined as the gradient of the class score $S_c$ with respect to the input image $x$:

$$
M_{ij}(x) = \left\vert \frac{\partial S_c(x)}{\partial x_{ij}} \right\vert
$$

Each pixel value indicates how much a small change in that pixel would affect the class score. Pixels with large saliency are the most discriminative for the predicted class. Saliency maps are computed via a single backward pass and are model-agnostic.

**(ii) Occlusion Sensitivity (Zeiler & Fergus, 2014):**

A grey square patch of size $k \times k$ is slid over the input image, and the class probability is monitored at each location:

$$
\Delta_c(i,j) = P(y_c \mid x) - P(y_c \mid x_{\text{occ}}(i,j))
$$

Regions producing the largest drop in probability are the most critical for the network's prediction. This method is intuitive, works for any black-box model, but requires many forward passes.

**(iii) Guided Backpropagation (Springenberg et al., 2014):**

A modification of standard backpropagation where the ReLU in the backward pass is applied to both the upstream gradient *and* the downstream gradient:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l-1)}} = \mathbb{1}(z^{(l-1)} > 0) \odot \mathbb{1}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l)}} > 0\right) \odot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l)}}
$$

This produces sharper, more visually interpretable saliency maps by suppressing negative gradients, focusing only on positive contributions to the class score.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the bias term** in parameter count → formula is $n(m+1)$, not $nm$. Examiners specifically check this.
> 2. **Confusing SNE and t-SNE kernels** → SNE uses Gaussian in both spaces; t-SNE uses Gaussian in high-D and **Student-t in low-D**. Mixing them up costs marks.
> 3. **Wrong activation at output** → Softmax (not sigmoid) for multiclass; Sigmoid (not softmax) for binary.
> 4. **Missing the Hadamard product symbol $\odot$** in backprop derivation → examiners want explicit notation.
> 5. **Not stating learning rate $\eta$** in the update rule → always write $W \leftarrow W - \eta \nabla_W \mathcal{L}$.
> 6. **Confusing Saliency Map and Occlusion Sensitivity** → Saliency is gradient-based (one backward pass); Occlusion is perturbation-based (many forward passes).
> 7. **Skipping units/dimensions in parameter calculation** → write dimensions explicitly, e.g., "25088 × 4096 weight matrix plus 4096 bias terms."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:**

**Fully Connected Layers:**
- [ ] Definition: every neuron connected to all neurons in previous layer
- [ ] Forward pass: $z = Wx + b$, $a = f(z)$
- [ ] Parameter count: $n(m+1)$ including biases
- [ ] ReLU default activation for hidden layers
- [ ] Softmax for multiclass output, Sigmoid for binary
- [ ] FC layers placed at end of CNN as classifier head
- [ ] FC layers require fixed-size input (hence flatten layer)

**Backpropagation Through FC Layer:**
- [ ] Local gradient: $\delta = \nabla_a \mathcal{L} \odot f'(z)$
- [ ] Weight gradient: $\nabla_W \mathcal{L} = \delta x^T$ (outer product)
- [ ] Bias gradient: $\nabla_b \mathcal{L} = \delta$
- [ ] Input gradient: $\nabla_x \mathcal{L} = W^T \delta$
- [ ] Update: $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$

**Weight Visualization:**
- [ ] Reshape $W$ rows back to input dimensions
- [ ] For ConvNets, plot first-layer filters directly
- [ ] Shows *what* the network is looking for

**Feature Visualization:**
- [ ] Activation maps: intermediate layer outputs
- [ ] Saliency map: $\vert \nabla_x S_c \vert$
- [ ] Occlusion sensitivity: prediction drop under masked input
- [ ] Guided backpropagation: ReLU on forward + backward gradients
- [ ] Grad-CAM: class activation heatmap from final conv layer

**t-SNE:**
- [ ] Nonlinear dimensionality reduction for visualization
- [ ] High-D: Gaussian kernel → $p_{ij}$
- [ ] Low-D: Student-t kernel → $q_{ij}$
- [ ] Objective: minimize $KL(P \Vert Q)$
- [ ] Heavy-tailed Student-t solves the crowding problem
- [ ] Preserves local structure, distorts global

**Common KTU Viva Questions:**
- [ ] Why FC at end of CNN, not beginning? (Translation equivariance + fixed-size input)
- [ ] Why is FC parameter count so high? ($n \cdot m$ scales multiplicatively)
- [ ] Difference between SNE and t-SNE? (Kernel choice in low-D space)
- [ ] Saliency map vs occlusion sensitivity? (Gradient-based vs perturbation-based)
- [ ] What is the role of the Hadamard product in backprop? (Element-wise activation derivative multiplication)

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of t-SNE

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE)** is a non-linear, unsupervised **dimensionality reduction** technique introduced by **Laurens van der Maaten and Geoffrey Hinton (2008)**. It is specifically designed for the **high-dimensional data visualization** by mapping data points from a high-dimensional space into a **2-dimensional** or **3-dimensional** space while preserving the **local neighborhood structure** of the data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** t-SNE is a critical visualization tool in Deep Learning pipelines. It is used to visualize learned feature embeddings from CNNs, RNNs, and Autoencoders. Unlike PCA (which is linear and global), t-SNE is **non-linear** and **local-structure preserving**.

Formally, t-SNE models pairwise similarities between data points in both the high-dimensional and low-dimensional spaces using **probability distributions**, and minimizes the **Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence** between these two distributions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Flattened World Map"**

Imagine you are an astronomer mapping the **3-dimensional positions of stars** in a galaxy onto a **2-dimensional paper**. You cannot just "flatten" the galaxy — the relative distances and neighborhood relationships will be completely lost. To preserve the *local structure* (i.e., which stars are near each other, which clusters exist), you need a clever projection method.

- **PCA** is like a rigid shadow projection — it preserves only the *overall shape* (global structure) but crushes local clusters together.
- **t-SNE** is like an intelligent cartographer who studies *which stars are neighbors* in the galaxy, and then carefully arranges them on paper so that **neighbors stay neighbors**, and **distant stars stay far apart**.

In short: **t-SNE answers the question — "Which points are neighbors to which other points?"** — and reconstructs that neighbor map in 2D.

## 1.3 Why t-SNE is Used in Deep Learning

- **Visualization of high-dimensional embeddings** (e.g., word2vec, BERT features, CNN feature maps).
- **Cluster discovery** in unlabeled datasets.
- **Model interpretability** — checking whether a neural network has learned separable feature representations.
- Pre-training inspection for downstream classifiers.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** t-SNE is widely used in **biomedical imaging**, **NLP embeddings**, **anomaly detection**, and **generative model** (GAN/VAE) latent space analysis. It is a *visualization* tool, **not** a general-purpose preprocessing step like PCA.

## 1.4 Key Characteristics of t-SNE

| Property | Value/Description |
|---|---|
| **Type** | Non-linear, non-parametric |
| **Preserves** | Local structure (neighborhood) |
| **Uses** | Probability distributions (Gaussian in HD, Student's t in LD) |
| **Loss Function** | Kullback–Leibler Divergence |
| **Output Dim** | Typically 2D or 3D |
| **Perplexity Hyperparameter** | **5 to 50** (commonly 30) |
| **Learning Rate** | Usually **100 to 1000** |
| **Iterations** | Typically **1000** with early exaggeration |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse t-SNE with PCA. t-SNE is **not** used for feature extraction in supervised learning pipelines. It is exclusively a **visualization** technique. Cluster distances in t-SNE plots have **no global meaning** — only local neighborhoods are reliable.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Philosophy of t-SNE

The fundamental idea of t-SNE can be broken into **three logical steps**:

1. **Step 1 — Convert Euclidean distances in HIGH-D into conditional probabilities** representing similarities. A Gaussian distribution is centered on each point, and the probability that point $i$ would pick point $j$ as its neighbor is computed.
2. **Step 2 — Define a similar probability distribution in LOW-D** using a **Student's t-distribution** (with 1 degree of freedom, i.e., a Cauchy distribution). This is critical because the t-distribution has **heavier tails** than the Gaussian.
3. **Step 3 — Minimize the KL divergence** between the high-D and low-D probability distributions using **gradient descent**, so the low-D map mirrors the high-D neighbor relationships.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Logic

### **Step 1: Conditional Probability in High-Dimensional Space**

For a data point $\mathbf{x}_i$ in the high-dimensional space, the conditional probability $p_{j \vert i}$ that $\mathbf{x}_i$ would pick $\mathbf{x}_j$ as its neighbor is defined as:

$$p_{j \vert i} = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\frac{\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_k \rVert^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)}$$

- $p_{j \vert i}$ is **high** when $\mathbf{x}_j$ is close to $\mathbf{x}_i$.
- $\sigma_i$ is the **bandwidth** of the Gaussian, determined by the **perplexity** hyperparameter.
- By convention, $p_{i \vert i} = 0$.

### **Step 2: Symmetrized Joint Probability**

To make the probability symmetric (since $p_{j \vert i} \neq p_{i \vert j}$ in general), a joint probability is defined:

$$p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \vert i} + p_{i \vert j}}{2N}$$

where $N$ is the total number of data points.

### **Step 3: Conditional Probability in Low-Dimensional Space**

In the low-dimensional map, points are denoted $\mathbf{y}_i$. A **Student's t-distribution with 1 degree of freedom (Cauchy distribution)** is used:

$$q_{ij} = \frac{\left(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2\right)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}\left(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_k - \mathbf{y}_l \rVert^2\right)^{-1}}$$

- $q_{ii} = 0$ (a point is not its own neighbor).
- The **heavy tails** of the t-distribution allow **moderately distant points** in high-D to be mapped as **far apart** in low-D — solving the **crowding problem**.

### **Step 4: KL Divergence Loss Function**

$$\mathcal{C} = \mathrm{KL}(P \,\|\, Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \, \log\!\left(\frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}\right)$$

The goal of t-SNE is to find low-D coordinates $\mathbf{y}_i$ that **minimize** this KL divergence via gradient descent.

### **Step 5: Gradient of the KL Divergence**

The gradient of the cost with respect to the low-D point $\mathbf{y}_i$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = 4 \sum_{j} \left(p_{ij} - q_{ij}\right)\left(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j\right)\left(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2\right)^{-1}$$

- The term $(p_{ij} - q_{ij})$ acts as a **force**:
  - If $p_{ij} > q_{ij}$ (high-D neighbors too far in low-D) → **attractive force** pulls them together.
  - If $p_{ij} < q_{ij}$ (non-neighbors too close in low-D) → **repulsive force** pushes them apart.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Student's t-distribution in Low-D?** The heavy tail of the t-distribution ensures that when two dissimilar points (small $p_{ij}$) are far apart in low-D (small $q_{ij}$), the **ratio $p_{ij}/q_{ij}$** does not become extremely small, avoiding the gradient vanishing problem. This is the key innovation of t-SNE over its predecessor, **SNE (Stochastic Neighbor Embedding)**.

## 2.3 The Perplexity Hyperparameter

Perplexity is a user-defined parameter that **smooths** the probability distribution:

$$\mathrm{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}$$

where $H(P_i)$ is the Shannon entropy of the conditional distribution $P_i$:

$$H(P_i) = -\sum_j p_{j \vert i} \, \log_2 p_{j \vert i}$$

- Typical values: **5 to 50**.
- Perplexity loosely corresponds to the **effective number of neighbors** for each point.
- Different perplexity values can produce **drastically different visualizations** of the same data.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $p_{j \vert i} = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2\right)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp\!\left(-\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_k \rVert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2\right)}$ | Conditional probability (high-D) | $\mathbf{x}_i$: high-D point, $\sigma_i$: bandwidth |
| 2 | $p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \vert i} + p_{i \vert j}}{2N}$ | Symmetrized joint probability | $N$: number of data points |
| 3 | $q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_k - \mathbf{y}_l \rVert^2)^{-1}}$ | Joint probability (low-D, t-distribution, dof=1) | $\mathbf{y}_i$: low-D map point |
| 4 | $\mathcal{C} = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log(p_{ij} / q_{ij})$ | KL divergence loss (cost function) | To be minimized via gradient descent |
| 5 | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = 4 \sum_j (p_{ij} - q_{ij})(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1}$ | Gradient for update rule | Used in iterative optimization |
| 6 | $\mathrm{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}$ | Perplexity definition | $H(P_i)$: Shannon entropy |
| 7 | $H(P_i) = -\sum_j p_{j \vert i} \log_2 p_{j \vert i}$ | Shannon entropy of $P_i$ | Bits |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** t-SNE is a critical post-hoc analysis tool. After training a CNN, engineers extract the **penultimate layer features** and pass them through t-SNE to **visualize class separability**. If clusters are well-separated in the t-SNE plot, the network has learned **discriminative embeddings**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Symmetrized Joint Probability

### Starting Point: Asymmetric Conditional Probability

The conditional probability $p_{j \vert i}$ is defined such that nearby points have high probability, and far points have low probability. Using a Gaussian centered at $\mathbf{x}_i$:

$$p_{j \vert i} = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2\right)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp\!\left(-\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_k \rVert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2\right)}$$

**Problem:** $p_{j \vert i} \neq p_{i \vert j}$ in general because the Gaussian kernels have **different bandwidths** $\sigma_i$ and $\sigma_j$ for each point.

**Solution:** Introduce a **symmetric** joint probability that treats all pairwise relations equally:

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{ij} &= \frac{p_{j \vert i} + p_{i \vert j}}{2N} \\
       &= \frac{1}{2N}\!\left(p_{j \vert i} + p_{i \vert j}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Normalization Check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_j p_{ij} &= \frac{1}{2N}\sum_j \left(p_{j \vert i} + p_{i \vert j}\right) \\
              &= \frac{1}{2N}\left(\sum_j p_{j \vert i} + \sum_j p_{i \vert j}\right) \\
              &= \frac{1}{2N}\left(1 + \frac{1}{N}\right) \approx \frac{1}{2N} \quad \text{(for large } N\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

This ensures $p_{ij}$ is a **valid probability distribution** that sums to approximately 1 over all $i, j$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Low-D Probability using Student's t-Distribution

The t-distribution with 1 degree of freedom (Cauchy distribution) is given by:

$$f(z) = \frac{1}{\pi\left(1 + z^2\right)}$$

The similarity in the low-dimensional space is defined such that $q_{ij} \propto f(\lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert)$. Specifically:

$$q_{ij} = \frac{\left(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2\right)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}\left(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_k - \mathbf{y}_l \rVert^2\right)^{-1}}$$

**Why the heavy tail?** Consider two points where $\lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert$ is large. The denominator $(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1}$ decays as $1/z^2$ (polynomial decay), whereas the Gaussian decays as $\exp(-z^2)$ (exponential decay). The polynomial decay means **moderately distant points in high-D** can be **far apart in low-D** without making $q_{ij}$ negligibly small.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Gradient of the KL Divergence

Starting from the cost function:

$$\mathcal{C} = \mathrm{KL}(P \,\|\, Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \, \log p_{ij} - \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \, \log q_{ij}$$

Since the first term does not depend on $\mathbf{y}_i$, we only differentiate the second term:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = -\sum_{j} p_{ij} \, \frac{\partial \log q_{ij}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i}$$

Recall that $q_{ij}$ is defined as:

$$q_{ij} = \frac{w_{ij}}{Z}, \quad \text{where } w_{ij} = (1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1}, \quad Z = \sum_{k \neq l} w_{kl}$$

Taking the logarithm:

$$\log q_{ij} = \log w_{ij} - \log Z$$

So:

$$\frac{\partial \log q_{ij}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = \frac{\partial \log w_{ij}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} - \frac{\partial \log Z}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i}$$

**Compute $\partial \log w_{ij} / \partial \mathbf{y}_i$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log w_{ij} &= -\log\!\left(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2\right) \\
\frac{\partial \log w_{ij}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} &= -\frac{2(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)}{1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $\partial \log Z / \partial \mathbf{y}_i$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \log Z}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} &= \frac{1}{Z} \sum_{k} \frac{\partial w_{ik}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} \\
\frac{\partial w_{ik}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} &= -2(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k \rVert^2)^{-2} \\
\frac{\partial \log Z}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} &= -\frac{2}{Z}\sum_k (\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k \rVert^2)^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Combine using $q_{ik} = w_{ik}/Z$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \log q_{ij}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} &= -\frac{2(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)}{1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2} + \frac{2}{Z}\sum_k (\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k \rVert^2)^{-2} \\
&= -2(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1} + 2\sum_k q_{ik}(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k \rVert^2)^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Substitute back into $\partial \mathcal{C} / \partial \mathbf{y}_i$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} &= -\sum_j p_{ij}\!\left[-2(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1} + 2\sum_k q_{ik}(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k \rVert^2)^{-1}\right] \\
&= 2\sum_j p_{ij}(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1} - 2\sum_j p_{ij}\sum_k q_{ik}(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k \rVert^2)^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

By symmetry of $p_{ij}$ and using $\sum_j p_{ij} \approx \frac{1}{2N}$ (a constant for all $i$), the second term simplifies to:

$$\sum_j p_{ij}\sum_k q_{ik}(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k \rVert^2)^{-1} = \sum_k q_{ik}(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_k \rVert^2)^{-1}$$

So the final gradient is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = 4 \sum_j (p_{ij} - q_{ij})(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)\left(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2\right)^{-1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the constant factor 4:** The factor 4 emerges from the symmetrization (a factor of 2) and a standard scaling used in the original t-SNE paper to simplify the gradient descent update rule. Some textbooks absorb this into the learning rate.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: t-SNE vs. SNE vs. PCA

| Feature | PCA | SNE (vanilla) | **t-SNE** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Linearity** | Linear | Non-linear | Non-linear |
| **HD distribution** | N/A | Gaussian | Gaussian |
| **LD distribution** | N/A | Gaussian | **Student's t (Cauchy)** |
| **Cost function** | Reconstruction error | KL divergence | KL divergence |
| **Crowding problem** | Yes | Yes | **Solved** |
| **Gradient stability** | Closed-form | Unstable | **Stable** |
| **Preserves** | Global structure | Local structure | **Local structure** |
| **Output** | Any dim | Any dim | **2D / 3D** |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:** In modern deep learning, **t-SNE has been superseded** for very large datasets by **UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection)**, which is faster and preserves more global structure. However, t-SNE remains a **KTU syllabus essential** and is widely taught in introductory deep learning courses.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of t-SNE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input High-Dimensional Data X] --> B[Step 1: Compute Pairwise Euclidean Distances]
    B --> C[Step 2: Convert to Conditional Probabilities PjI using Gaussian Kernel]
    C --> D[Step 3: Determine Per-Point Bandwidth SigmaI via Perplexity]
    D --> E[Step 4: Symmetrize to Joint Probability Pij]
    E --> F[Step 5: Initialize Low-D Map Y Randomly]
    F --> G[Step 6: Compute Low-D Similarity Qij using Student t-Distribution dof 1]
    G --> H[Step 7: Compute KL Divergence Loss C]
    H --> I[Step 8: Compute Gradient dC dYi]
    I --> J[Step 9: Update Y using Gradient Descent]
    J --> K{Converged?}
    K -->|No| G
    K -->|Yes| L[Step 10: Output 2D 3D Embedding for Visualization]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Attractive and Repulsive Forces in t-SNE

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HD[High-Dimensional Space]
        X1[Point Xi] ---|short distance| X2[Point Xj]
        X1 ---|long distance| X3[Point Xk]
    end

    HD -->|t-SNE mapping| LD

    subgraph LD[Low-Dimensional Map]
        Y1[Point Yi] ==>|attractive force p ij greater than q ij| Y2[Point Yj]
        Y1 -.->|repulsive force p ij less than q ij| Y3[Point Yk]
    end

    style X1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style X2 fill:#e1f5ff
    style X3 fill:#e1f5ff
    style Y1 fill:#fff4e1
    style Y2 fill:#fff4e1
    style Y3 fill:#fff4e1
```

## 4.3 Perplexity vs. Cluster Size Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Perplexity 5: Focuses on very local neighbors] --> R1[Small tight clusters]
    P2[Perplexity 30: Balanced default] --> R2[Medium sized natural clusters]
    P3[Perplexity 100: Captures broader neighborhood] --> R3[Large merged clusters]
    style P1 fill:#cce5ff
    style P2 fill:#cce5ff
    style P3 fill:#cce5ff
    style R1 fill:#d4edda
    style R2 fill:#d4edda
    style R3 fill:#d4edda
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block diagram illustrates the iterative nature of t-SNE — it is **not** a closed-form solution. The optimization loop (Steps 6–9) is repeated until the KL divergence stabilizes. The force diagram shows the physics-inspired analogy of t-SNE, where pairs of points are connected by springs whose stiffness depends on the mismatch between high-D and low-D probabilities.
