# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Activation Functions — The Heart of a Neural Network

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEECT742 – Deep Learning**, an **Activation Function** is defined as a non-linear mathematical transformation applied to the weighted sum of inputs (the *pre-activation* or *logit*) at a neuron, in order to introduce non-linearity into the network. Without an activation function, a stack of linear layers collapses into a single linear transformation, regardless of depth.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> An activation function determines the output of a neural network node given an input or set of inputs. It maps the pre-activation value $z = Wx + b$ to a bounded or unbounded output $a = f(z)$, where $f$ is the activation function.

The two most foundational activation functions covered in **Module 2** are the **Sigmoid** (also called the *logistic* function) and the **Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU)**.

---

## 1.2 The Sigmoid Activation Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**  
> The **Sigmoid function** is a smooth, continuously differentiable, monotonically increasing function that squashes any real-valued number into the open interval $(0, 1)$.

Its mathematical form is:

$$ \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} $$

**Key Property Highlights (Bold for KTU Emphasis):**
- **Range:** $(0, 1)$ — strictly between **0** and **1**; never actually reaches 0 or 1.
- **Sigmoid Neuron Output:** Often interpreted as a **probability** in binary classification.
- **Smoothness:** $C^{\infty}$ — infinitely differentiable, with a simple derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\big(1 - \sigma(z)\big)$.
- **Saturation:** For large positive/negative $z$, the function flattens and the gradient approaches **0**.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "The Volume Knob That Never Goes Silent"

Imagine a **dimmer switch** on a fancy light. You turn the dial from 0 to 100, but instead of the light going from *off (0)* to *full blast (1)*, it behaves as follows:
- When the dial is at **−50** (very negative), the bulb gives a tiny faint glow (close to 0, but never off).
- When the dial is at **0** (middle), the bulb glows at **50% brightness** (output ≈ 0.5).
- When the dial is at **+50** (very positive), the bulb is almost fully lit (close to 1, but never blinding).

The dimmer is the **Sigmoid function**, and the brightness is the **neuron's output**. The bulb can never be *completely dark* or *completely blinding* — it always lives in the safe zone $(0,1)$, which is exactly why we use it to model **probabilities**.

---

## 1.3 The ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) Activation Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**  
> **ReLU** is a piecewise linear activation function that outputs the input directly if it is positive, and outputs zero otherwise. It is the default activation for hidden layers in modern deep networks.

Its mathematical form is:

$$ \text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z) $$

**Key Property Highlights (Bold for KTU Emphasis):**
- **Range:** $[0, \infty)$ — outputs are non-negative; lower bound is exactly **0**.
- **Computational Cost:** $O(1)$ — only a comparison and a max operation, no exponentiation.
- **Sparsity:** Negative inputs produce **0**, leading to sparse representations.
- **Non-saturating** for positive inputs — gradient remains **1**, mitigating the vanishing gradient problem.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "The One-Way Valve"

Picture a **water pipe with a one-way check valve**:
- If water pressure (the input $z$) pushes **forward (positive)**, the valve opens fully and water flows through unchanged — the output equals the input.
- If pressure tries to push **backward (negative)**, the valve slams shut — **no water flows**; the output is exactly 0.

This is **ReLU**. The valve is the `max(0, z)` operation. Because the valve is either *fully open* or *fully closed*, water either *flows freely* or *stops completely* — there is no "half-open" or "leaking" state. This on/off simplicity is precisely why ReLU is computationally cheap and encourages **sparse activations** in deep networks.

---

## 1.4 Why These Two Functions Matter in Deep Learning

| Aspect | Sigmoid | ReLU |
|---|---|---|
| Historical Role | Pre-2010 default | Post-2012 default (AlexNet era) |
| Primary Use Today | **Output layer** of binary classifiers | **Hidden layers** of CNNs, MLPs, Transformers |
| Behaviour with Large Inputs | Saturates → gradient vanishes | Linear → gradient preserved |
| Computational Cost | Expensive (exponential) | Cheap (max operation) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** A deep-learning engineer must understand *when* to choose Sigmoid (output layer, probability interpretation) and *when* to choose ReLU (hidden layers, faster convergence, deeper networks).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sigmoid Function — Operational Analysis

The Sigmoid function operates on the **pre-activation** $z = W^{T}x + b$ produced by a neuron. The chain of logic is as follows:

- **Step 1 — Weighted Summation:** Compute $z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$, a real-valued number in $(-\infty, \infty)$.
- **Step 2 — Exponential Negation:** Compute $e^{-z}$. This is the costliest operation; for large positive $z$, $e^{-z} \to 0$; for large negative $z$, $e^{-z} \to \infty$.
- **Step 3 — Normalization:** Compute $1 + e^{-z}$, then take its reciprocal. This squashes the output into $(0,1)$.
- **Step 4 — Probability Interpretation:** Since the output lies in $(0,1)$ and the sigmoid is monotonic, it can be directly interpreted as $P(y=1 \mid x)$.

### The Sigmoid Derivative — A Mathematical Gem

The most elegant property of the Sigmoid function is that its derivative can be written *purely in terms of its own output*. The full derivation appears in Section 3; the result is:

$$ \sigma'(z) = \sigma(z) \cdot \big(1 - \sigma(z)\big) $$

This means during **backpropagation**, the gradient signal at a sigmoid neuron is simply:

$$ \frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a} \cdot \sigma(z)\big(1 - \sigma(z)\big) $$

where $a = \sigma(z)$ is the neuron's activation.

### Behavioural Asymptotes
- As $z \to +\infty$: $\sigma(z) \to 1$, and $\sigma'(z) \to 0$.
- As $z \to -\infty$: $\sigma(z) \to 0$, and $\sigma'(z) \to 0$.
- At $z = 0$: $\sigma(0) = 0.5$, and $\sigma'(0) = 0.25$ (the **maximum gradient**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Observation:**  
> The *maximum possible gradient* through a sigmoid neuron is **0.25** (at $z=0$). When we stack many layers with sigmoid activations, the gradients get multiplied by 0.25 repeatedly, leading to **exponential decay** of the gradient — this is the famous **Vanishing Gradient Problem**.

---

## 2.2 The ReLU Function — Operational Analysis

The ReLU function is piecewise linear and operates as follows:

- **Step 1 — Pre-activation:** Compute $z = W^{T}x + b$.
- **Step 2 — Thresholding:** If $z \geq 0$, output $z$ unchanged; if $z < 0$, output $0$.
- **Step 3 — Linearity Preserved:** For $z > 0$, ReLU is purely linear with slope = 1, meaning gradients flow back without attenuation.

### The ReLU Derivative

$$ \text{ReLU}'(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \\ \text{undefined (subgradient) } \in [0,1], & z = 0 \end{cases} $$

In practice, deep learning frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch) define $\text{ReLU}'(0) = 0$ or $1$ by convention.

### Sparsity in Action
- For a uniformly distributed input $z \sim U(-1, 1)$, approximately **50% of neurons** will output exactly 0.
- This **sparse activation** pattern is biologically plausible (only a fraction of neurons fire at any time) and computationally efficient (zero multiplications).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Dead ReLU Problem:**  
> If a neuron's weights are updated such that the pre-activation $z$ is consistently negative, the gradient becomes 0 and the neuron can never recover. Such neurons are called **"dead ReLUs"**. KTU students should mention this in exams as a known drawback.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Sigmoid $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | ReLU $f(z) = \max(0, z)$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Function Definition** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $f(z) = \max(0, z)$ |
| **Range** | $(0, 1)$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| **Derivative** | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\big(1-\sigma(z)\big)$ | $f'(z) = 1$ if $z>0$; $0$ if $z<0$ |
| **Maximum Gradient** | $0.25$ at $z=0$ | $1$ for all $z > 0$ |
| **Output at $z=0$** | $0.5$ | $0$ |
| **Output at $z \to +\infty$** | $1$ (asymptote) | $+\infty$ (linear, no asymptote) |
| **Output at $z \to -\infty$** | $0$ (asymptote) | $0$ |
| **Monotonicity** | Strictly increasing | Non-decreasing |
| **Smoothness** | $C^{\infty}$ (infinitely differentiable) | Continuous, not differentiable at $z=0$ |
| **Computational Cost** | High (1 exp + 1 div) | Very low (1 comparison + 1 max) |
| **Zero-Centered?** | **No** (always positive) | **No** (always non-negative) |
| **Vanishing Gradient?** | **Yes** (saturates for $\vert z \vert > 4$) | **No** for $z > 0$ |
| **Sparsity** | No (always non-zero output) | Yes (50% of outputs are 0) |
| **Recommended Layer** | Output (binary classification) | Hidden layers (CNNs, MLPs) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sigmoid in Medical Diagnosis:** Used in the final layer of binary classifiers for tumor *malignant vs benign*, where output $\in (0,1)$ represents the *probability of malignancy*.
- **ReLU in Computer Vision:** Used in every hidden layer of **AlexNet, VGG, ResNet** — the backbones of modern image classification, object detection (YOLO), and facial recognition systems.
- **ReLU in NLP:** Foundational in **Transformer-based models (BERT, GPT)** for hidden layers, enabling training of networks with hundreds of layers.
- **Sigmoid in LSTM Gates:** The **forget gate** and **input gate** of LSTMs use sigmoid to output values in $[0,1]$ representing "how much to remember/forget".
- **ReLU in Edge AI / Mobile Deployment:** Its $O(1)$ computation enables real-time inference on low-power devices (smartphones, IoT sensors, embedded GPUs).

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sigmoid Function's Derivative

We need to find $\dfrac{d}{dz}\sigma(z)$ where $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$.

### Step 1: Rewrite as a Power
Apply the rule $\dfrac{1}{u} = u^{-1}$:

$$ \sigma(z) = \big(1 + e^{-z}\big)^{-1} $$

### Step 2: Apply the Chain Rule
Let $u(z) = 1 + e^{-z}$. Then $\sigma(z) = u^{-1}$. The chain rule gives:

$$ \frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -1 \cdot u^{-2} \cdot \frac{du}{dz} = -\frac{1}{u^{2}} \cdot \frac{du}{dz} $$

### Step 3: Compute $\frac{du}{dz}$
Differentiate $u = 1 + e^{-z}$ with respect to $z$:

$$ \frac{du}{dz} = \frac{d}{dz}\big(1\big) + \frac{d}{dz}\big(e^{-z}\big) = 0 + e^{-z} \cdot (-1) = -e^{-z} $$

### Step 4: Substitute Back
Insert $\frac{du}{dz} = -e^{-z}$ into the chain-rule expression:

$$ \frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -\frac{1}{\big(1+e^{-z}\big)^{2}} \cdot \big(-e^{-z}\big) = \frac{e^{-z}}{\big(1+e^{-z}\big)^{2}} $$

### Step 5: Factor Into the "Self-Product" Form
Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{z}$:

$$ \frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{e^{-z} \cdot e^{z}}{\big(1+e^{-z}\big)^{2} \cdot e^{z}} = \frac{1}{\big(e^{z}+1\big) \cdot \big(1+e^{-z}\big)} $$

Recognize that:

$$ \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}} = \frac{e^{z}}{1+e^{z}} $$

and

$$ 1 - \sigma(z) = 1 - \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}} = \frac{e^{-z}}{1+e^{-z}} = \frac{1}{1+e^{z}} $$

Multiplying these:

$$ \sigma(z)\big(1-\sigma(z)\big) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{1}{1+e^{z}} $$

We can also express this directly as:

$$ \sigma(z)\big(1-\sigma(z)\big) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1+e^{-z}} = \frac{e^{-z}}{\big(1+e^{-z}\big)^{2}} $$

This is exactly the expression we obtained in **Step 4**. Therefore:

$$ \boxed{\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\big(1 - \sigma(z)\big)} $$

This is the celebrated identity that makes backpropagation through sigmoid neurons computationally cheap — we already compute $\sigma(z)$ during the forward pass, so the gradient costs only one multiplication.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Evaluation of the Sigmoid Function

Let us evaluate $\sigma(z)$ at five representative points to build intuition.

| $z$ | $e^{-z}$ | $1 + e^{-z}$ | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(1-\sigma)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $-10$ | $e^{10} \approx 22026.47$ | $22027.47$ | $\approx 0.0000454$ | $\approx 0.0000454$ |
| $-2$  | $e^{2} \approx 7.389$ | $8.389$ | $\approx 0.1192$ | $\approx 0.1050$ |
| $0$   | $1$ | $2$ | $0.5$ | $0.25$ |
| $+2$  | $e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$ | $1.1353$ | $\approx 0.8808$ | $\approx 0.1050$ |
| $+10$ | $e^{-10} \approx 0.0000454$ | $1.0000454$ | $\approx 0.99995$ | $\approx 0.0000454$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Table:** Notice how the gradient $\sigma'(z)$ collapses from $0.25$ at $z=0$ to nearly $0$ at $z=\pm 10$. This *saturation* is the root cause of the vanishing gradient problem in deep sigmoid networks.

---

## 3.3 Quantitative Proof of the Vanishing Gradient Problem in Sigmoid Networks

Consider a 5-layer fully-connected network where every layer uses the sigmoid activation, and the inputs are such that the pre-activations in each layer satisfy $\vert z^{(l)} \vert \geq 4$ (a common occurrence in well-trained networks).

By the **upper bound** of the sigmoid derivative:

$$ \sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\big(1-\sigma(z)\big) \leq \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 $$

with equality only at $z = 0$. For $\vert z \vert \geq 4$:

$$ \sigma(4) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-4}} \approx 0.9820 $$

$$ \sigma'(4) = 0.9820 \times 0.0180 \approx 0.0177 $$

For $\vert z \vert \geq 4$, the gradient magnitude is roughly $\leq 0.02$. The gradient signal flowing from layer 5 (output) back to layer 1 (input) is:

$$ \frac{\partial L}{\partial z^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a^{(5)}} \cdot \prod_{l=2}^{5} \sigma'\big(z^{(l)}\big) \cdot W^{(l)} $$

If we bound the product of sigmoids:

$$ \prod_{l=2}^{5} \sigma'\big(z^{(l)}\big) \leq (0.0177)^{4} = 9.8 \times 10^{-8} $$

This is a **gradient collapse to near-zero** — the early layers receive virtually no learning signal, even though the loss at the output may be significant. This is the **vanishing gradient problem** in numbers.

---

## 3.4 Derivative of the ReLU Function — Piecewise Derivation

The ReLU is $f(z) = \max(0, z)$. We can write it as:

$$ f(z) = \begin{cases} z, & z \geq 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases} $$

### Case 1: $z > 0$
Here $f(z) = z$, so:

$$ f'(z) = \frac{d}{dz}(z) = 1 $$

### Case 2: $z < 0$
Here $f(z) = 0$ (a constant), so:

$$ f'(z) = \frac{d}{dz}(0) = 0 $$

### Case 3: $z = 0$ (Subgradient)
The left-hand derivative is $0$ and the right-hand derivative is $1$. In convex analysis, the **subgradient** is the convex hull:

$$ \partial f(0) = [0, 1] $$

For practical backpropagation, frameworks set $f'(0) = 0$ or $1$ (most commonly $0$). Combining:

$$ \boxed{f'(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ 0, & z \leq 0 \end{cases}} $$

### Why This Cures Vanishing Gradients
In a deep ReLU network, the gradient at each active neuron is exactly $1$. Therefore:

$$ \prod_{l} f'\big(z^{(l)}\big) = 1 $$

for any layer where $z^{(l)} > 0$. The gradient cannot vanish as it propagates back, which is why networks with dozens or hundreds of layers (e.g., ResNet-152) can be trained successfully using ReLU.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Sigmoid vs ReLU on a Real Input

Let $z = 2.5$. Compute both outputs and derivatives.

**Sigmoid:**

$$ e^{-2.5} = 0.0821 $$

$$ \sigma(2.5) = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0821} = \frac{1}{1.0821} \approx 0.9241 $$

$$ \sigma'(2.5) = 0.9241 \times (1 - 0.9241) = 0.9241 \times 0.0759 \approx 0.0701 $$

**ReLU:**

$$ \text{ReLU}(2.5) = \max(0, 2.5) = 2.5 $$

$$ \text{ReLU}'(2.5) = 1 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** At $z = 2.5$, the sigmoid has already saturated (output $0.92$, gradient only $0.07$), while the ReLU simply passes the value through (output $2.5$, gradient $= 1$). The ReLU preserves a much stronger learning signal.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric (at $z = 2.5$) | Sigmoid | ReLU |
|---|---|---|
| Function value | $0.9241$ | $2.5$ |
| Derivative | $0.0701$ | $1.0$ |
| Saturation status | **Saturated** | **Active (linear)** |
| Backprop signal strength | Weak | **Strong** |
| Effect in deep network | Vanishing gradient | Gradient preserved |

| Metric (at $z = -3.0$) | Sigmoid | ReLU |
|---|---|---|
| Function value | $\approx 0.0474$ | $0$ |
| Derivative | $\approx 0.0452$ | $0$ |
| Saturation status | **Saturated** | **Dead** |
| Backprop signal strength | Weak | **Zero** (Dead ReLU!) |
| Effect in deep network | Vanishing gradient | Neuron cannot update |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow When Choosing an Activation Function

The following block-level flow diagram maps out the *decision logic* an engineer follows when selecting between Sigmoid and ReLU in a deep-learning architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Neural Network Layer] --> B{What is the layer type?}
    B -->|Output layer| C{Task type?}
    B -->|Hidden layer| D{Network depth and training speed concern?}
    B -->|Recurrent gate (LSTM/GRU)| E[Use Sigmoid]
    B -->|Attention gating (Transformers)| F[Use Sigmoid or Softmax]

    C -->|Binary classification| G[Use Sigmoid with BCE Loss]
    C -->|Multi-class classification| H[Use Softmax]
    C -->|Regression bounded 0 to 1| I[Use Sigmoid]

    D -->|Yes, deep or need fast convergence| J[Use ReLU]
    D -->|No, shallow network| K[Sigmoid also acceptable but ReLU still preferred]

    J --> L{Dead ReLU observed?}
    L -->|Yes, many dead neurons| M[Switch to Leaky ReLU or PReLU]
    L -->|No, healthy activations| N[Continue with ReLU]

    E --> O[Output in 0 to 1, controls gate openness]
    F --> P[Output represents attention weights]
    G --> Q[Final output: probability of positive class]
    I --> R[Final output: normalized regression value]
    N --> S[Hidden activations, sparse and efficient]
    M --> S
    H --> T[Probability distribution over classes]
```

---

The following diagram is a **functional block-level schematic** of how the Sigmoid and ReLU functions transform a continuous input range. Since Mermaid cannot draw smooth mathematical curves, this representation uses *banded input ranges* to illustrate the characteristic behaviour of each function on a shared input axis.

```mermaid

    end

        S1[Output near 0.018, gradient near 0] --> S2[Output 0.119 to 0.5, gradient up to 0.25] --> S3[Output exactly 0.5, max gradient 0.25] --> S4[Output 0.5 to 0.881, gradient down to 0.105] --> S5[Output near 0.982, gradient near 0]
    end

    subgraph RELU[ ReLU Output Behaviour ]
        R1[Output exactly 0, gradient exactly 0] --> R2[Output 0 to 0, gradient 0] --> R3[Output exactly 0, subgradient 0 to 1] --> R4[Output 0 to 4, gradient 1] --> R5[Output 4 to infinity, gradient 1]
    end

    I1 --- S1
    I1 --- R1
    I2 --- S2
    I2 --- R2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Activation Function Inside a Single Neuron

This block diagram shows exactly *where* the Sigmoid or ReLU is applied within a single artificial neuron during the forward pass.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Input x1] --> SUM[Weighted Sum z = w1x1 + w2x2 + ... + wnxn + b]
    X2[Input x2] --> SUM
    X3[Input xn] --> SUM
    SUM --> ACT{Choose Activation f}
    ACT -->|Sigmoid| SIG[Apply sigma z = 1 over 1 plus e to the minus z]
    ACT -->|ReLU| RLU[Apply max of 0 and z]
    SIG --> OUT1[Output a in 0 to 1]
    RLU --> OUT2[Output a in 0 to infinity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Diagram Note:** The choice between Sigmoid and ReLU is the **only architectural difference** at the neuron level — the surrounding linear summation $z = W^{T}x + b$ is identical for both. This is a frequent short-answer or fill-in-the-blank question in KTU exams.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**  
*Define the Sigmoid activation function. State its mathematical form, output range, and one disadvantage.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Sigmoid activation function** is a non-linear, monotonically increasing function that squashes any real-valued input to a value in the open interval $(0, 1)$. Its mathematical form is:

$$ \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} $$

where $z$ is the pre-activation $W^{T}x + b$.

**Output Range:** $(0, 1)$ — it never attains exactly $0$ or $1$.

**Disadvantage (any one):**
- Suffers from the **vanishing gradient problem** for large $\vert z \vert$ (gradients approach 0 when $\vert z \vert > 4$).
- Its outputs are **not zero-centered**, which slows down convergence during gradient descent.
- It is **computationally expensive** due to the exponential operation.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mathematical form and range, 1 mark for disadvantage.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**  
*Write the mathematical definition of the ReLU activation function. Why is it preferred over the Sigmoid function in hidden layers of deep networks?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU)** activation function is defined as:

$$ f(z) = \max(0, z) = \begin{cases} z, & z \geq 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases} $$

**ReLU is preferred over Sigmoid in hidden layers because:**
1. It **mitigates the vanishing gradient problem** — for $z > 0$, the gradient is exactly $1$, allowing gradients to flow unimpeded through deep networks.
2. It is **computationally cheaper** — only a `max` operation is required, no expensive exponentials.
3. It induces **sparsity** in activations (negative inputs → output 0), which improves generalization.
4. It enables **faster convergence** in practice (up to 6× faster as shown in Krizhevsky et al., 2012).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the reasons.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B

**`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Derive the derivative of the Sigmoid activation function $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ and show that it can be expressed as $\sigma(z)\big(1-\sigma(z)\big)$. Use this result to compute $\sigma'(z)$ at $z = 0$ and $z = 3$, and comment on the implications for backpropagation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat diagram why the Sigmoid activation function leads to the **vanishing gradient problem** in deep neural networks. How does the ReLU function overcome this limitation? Mention **one limitation** of ReLU. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Write the Sigmoid as a power of a sum:

$$ \sigma(z) = \big(1 + e^{-z}\big)^{-1} $$

**Step 2:** Differentiate using the chain rule. Let $u = 1 + e^{-z}$:

$$ \frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -1 \cdot u^{-2} \cdot \frac{du}{dz} $$

**Step 3:** Compute the inner derivative:

$$ \frac{du}{dz} = -e^{-z} $$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$ \frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -\frac{1}{\big(1+e^{-z}\big)^{2}} \cdot \big(-e^{-z}\big) = \frac{e^{-z}}{\big(1+e^{-z}\big)^{2}} $$

**Step 5:** Factor into the self-product form. Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{z}$:

$$ \frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{1}{1+e^{z}} \cdot \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}} = \sigma(z) \cdot \big(1 - \sigma(z)\big) $$

This is the desired identity. **(4 Marks for derivation)**

**Step 6:** Evaluate at $z = 0$:

$$ \sigma(0) = \frac{1}{1+e^{0}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 $$

$$ \sigma'(0) = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 $$

**Step 7:** Evaluate at $z = 3$:

$$ \sigma(3) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-3}} = \frac{1}{1+0.0498} \approx 0.9526 $$

$$ \sigma'(3) = 0.9526 \times 0.0474 \approx 0.0452 $$

**Step 8 — Comment on Implications:**

At $z = 0$, the gradient is at its **maximum** value of $0.25$. At $z = 3$, the gradient has already collapsed to $\approx 0.045$, a factor of **5.5× smaller**. During backpropagation, this gradient is multiplied across all the layers of a deep network. For a 10-layer sigmoid network with $\vert z \vert \geq 3$ in each layer, the cumulative gradient is at most:

$$ (0.0452)^{10} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-9} $$

The early layers therefore receive virtually **no learning signal**, and training stalls. **(3 Marks for numerical evaluation and comment)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Vanishing Gradient Problem in Sigmoid Networks:**

In a deep network with $L$ layers, the gradient of the loss $L$ with respect to the weight in layer $k$ is:

$$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(k)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \cdot \prod_{l=k+1}^{L} \sigma'\big(z^{(l)}\big) \cdot W^{(l)} $$

For Sigmoid, $\sigma'(z) \leq 0.25$ for all $z$, with $\sigma'(z) \to 0$ as $\vert z \vert \to \infty$. As shown in part (a), once $\vert z \vert$ exceeds 3, the gradient is already $< 0.05$. Multiplying such small numbers across many layers causes the product to **shrink exponentially** toward zero. The early layers therefore update their weights very slowly, and the network fails to learn meaningful low-level features.

**Diagram Description (to be drawn on paper):**

| Region | Sigmoid | ReLU |
|---|---|---|
| Saturated ($z$ very large) | Gradient = 0 | Gradient = 1 (if $z>0$) |
| Linear region ($z$ near 0) | Gradient = 0.25 | Gradient = 1 |
| Negative region | Gradient near 0 | Gradient = 0 (dead) |

**How ReLU Overcomes the Problem:**

For ReLU, the gradient is exactly $1$ for all $z > 0$. The product of gradients across layers becomes:

$$ \prod_{l} \text{ReLU}'(z^{(l)}) = 1 \quad \text{(if } z^{(l)} > 0 \text{ for all layers)} $$

This means gradients can flow back through arbitrarily deep networks without attenuation, enabling the training of very deep architectures like ResNet (152+ layers).

**Limitation of ReLU — Dead ReLU Problem:**

If the weights of a neuron are updated such that its pre-activation $z$ becomes consistently negative, the ReLU output is $0$ and its gradient is $0$. The neuron can never recover from this state (it outputs only zeros forever). Such neurons are called **"Dead ReLUs"**. This problem is mitigated by variants such as **Leaky ReLU**, which assigns a small non-zero slope (e.g., $0.01$) for $z < 0$.

*(Award 3 marks for the vanishing gradient explanation + diagram, 2 marks for ReLU's remedy, 2 marks for the Dead ReLU limitation.)*

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Compare the Sigmoid and ReLU activation functions across **at least six properties** (range, derivative, vanishing gradient, computational cost, sparsity, use case). Present your answer in a **comparison table**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 5-layer feedforward neural network uses Sigmoid activations in all hidden layers. The pre-activation values in layers 2, 3, 4, and 5 are $z^{(2)} = 3.0$, $z^{(3)} = 2.5$, $z^{(4)} = 3.5$, and $z^{(5)} = 4.0$ respectively. Compute the **product of gradients** $\prod_{l=2}^{5} \sigma'(z^{(l)})$ and show that the gradient signal reaching layer 1 is severely attenuated. Suggest one architecture-level change to fix this. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Sigmoid $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | ReLU $f(z) = \max(0, z)$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical Form** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $f(z) = \max(0, z)$ |
| **Output Range** | $(0, 1)$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| **Derivative** | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$, max value $0.25$ | $f'(z) = 1$ for $z>0$; $0$ for $z<0$ |
| **Vanishing Gradient** | **Yes** — saturates for $\vert z \vert > 4$ | **No** for $z > 0$ (gradient = 1) |
| **Computational Cost** | High (1 exponential + 1 division) | Low (1 comparison + 1 max) |
| **Sparsity** | No — always produces a non-zero output | Yes — exactly 50% of inputs give 0 output |
| **Zero-Centered Output** | No — always positive | No — always non-negative |
| **Smoothness** | $C^{\infty}$ (infinitely differentiable) | Continuous but non-differentiable at $z=0$ |
| **Recommended Layer** | Output layer (binary classification) | Hidden layers (deep networks) |
| **Limitation** | Vanishing gradient, not zero-centered, slow convergence | Dead ReLU problem, unbounded output |

*(Award full 7 marks for a clear, accurate table covering at least six properties.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (b) [7 Marks]

We must compute the product $\prod_{l=2}^{5} \sigma'(z^{(l)})$ given four pre-activation values.

**Step 1: Compute $\sigma(z)$ for each layer.**

For $z^{(2)} = 3.0$:

$$ \sigma(3.0) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-3.0}} = \frac{1}{1+0.0498} = \frac{1}{1.0498} \approx 0.9526 $$

For $z^{(3)} = 2.5$:

$$ \sigma(2.5) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-2.5}} = \frac{1}{1+0.0821} = \frac{1}{1.0821} \approx 0.9241 $$

For $z^{(4)} = 3.5$:

$$ \sigma(3.5) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-3.5}} = \frac{1}{1+0.0302} = \frac{1}{1.0302} \approx 0.9707 $$

For $z^{(5)} = 4.0$:

$$ \sigma(4.0) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-4.0}} = \frac{1}{1+0.0183} = \frac{1}{1.0183} \approx 0.9820 $$

**Step 2: Compute $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ for each layer.**

For $z^{(2)}$:

$$ \sigma'(3.0) = 0.9526 \times 0.0474 \approx 0.0452 $$

For $z^{(3)}$:

$$ \sigma'(2.5) = 0.9241 \times 0.0759 \approx 0.0701 $$

For $z^{(4)}$:

$$ \sigma'(3.5) = 0.9707 \times 0.0293 \approx 0.0284 $$

For $z^{(5)}$:

$$ \sigma'(4.0) = 0.9820 \times 0.0180 \approx 0.0177 $$

**Step 3: Compute the product of gradients.**

$$ \prod_{l=2}^{5} \sigma'\big(z^{(l)}\big) = 0.0452 \times 0.0701 \times 0.0284 \times 0.0177 $$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$ 0.0452 \times 0.0701 = 0.0031685 $$

$$ 0.0031685 \times 0.0284 = 0.00008998 $$

$$ 0.00008998 \times 0.0177 = 1.59 \times 10^{-6} $$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

The cumulative gradient is approximately $1.59 \times 10^{-6}$, which is **roughly six orders of magnitude smaller** than the original gradient signal. When this tiny number is multiplied by the loss gradient and the weights of layers 1 and 2, the resulting update to the weights of layer 1 is essentially zero. The early layer fails to learn — this is the vanishing gradient problem in action.

**Step 5: Suggested Architecture-Level Fix.**

Replace the Sigmoid activations in the hidden layers with **ReLU activations**. For ReLU, the gradient is $1$ whenever $z > 0$, so the product of gradients across layers is exactly $1$ for all active neurons. The gradient reaching the early layers remains strong, and training proceeds efficiently. Other valid fixes include:
- **Batch Normalization** before the activation
- **Residual (skip) connections** as in ResNet
- **Better weight initialization** (He initialization, designed for ReLU)
- **Leaky ReLU / ELU** as drop-in replacements

*(Award 2 marks for the four sigmoid outputs, 2 marks for the four derivatives, 2 marks for the product and interpretation, 1 mark for the architecture fix.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Sigmoid and Softmax.** Sigmoid is for *binary* classification (one output neuron). Softmax is for *multi-class* classification (multiple output neurons, sums to 1). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for confusing these.
> 2. **Forgetting the "self-product" identity.** Many students write $\sigma'(z) = e^{-z}/(1+e^{-z})^{2}$ but fail to simplify it to $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$. KTU 2024 papers often have a 1-mark bonus for the simplified form.
> 3. **Saying "ReLU is differentiable everywhere."** ReLU is **not** differentiable at $z = 0$. Use the word "subgradient" or explicitly say "differentiable except at 0".
> 4. **Writing the range of Sigmoid as $[0,1]$.** The correct range is the **open interval** $(0, 1)$ — the function never attains exactly 0 or 1.
> 5. **Forgetting the Dead ReLU problem.** When asked about ReLU's limitations, students often only mention the unbounded output. Always mention the **Dead ReLU problem** for full marks.
> 6. **Mixing up Tanh and Sigmoid.** Tanh has range $(-1, 1)$ and is zero-centered; Sigmoid has range $(0,1)$. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 7. **Skipping the diagram in 14-mark questions.** Part (b) of 14-mark questions often explicitly asks for a diagram. A missing diagram typically costs **2–3 marks** under the KTU 2024 marking scheme.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sigmoid formula:** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$, range $(0,1)$, derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\big(1 - \sigma(z)\big)$, max gradient $0.25$ at $z=0$.
- **ReLU formula:** $f(z) = \max(0, z)$, range $[0, \infty)$, derivative $1$ for $z>0$ and $0$ for $z<0$.
- **Vanishing gradient in Sigmoid:** Caused by $\sigma'(z) \to 0$ for large $\vert z \vert$. Product of gradients across deep layers → 0.
- **ReLU cures vanishing gradient (for $z>0$):** Gradient is exactly $1$, no multiplicative attenuation.
- **Dead ReLU problem:** If $z < 0$ persistently, gradient is 0 forever → neuron never updates. Solved by Leaky ReLU / PReLU / ELU.
- **Sigmoid is NOT zero-centered**, which causes zig-zagging gradient updates. This is a second disadvantage of Sigmoid.
- **Sigmoid is preferred for output layers** of binary classifiers (probability interpretation) and for **LSTM gates** (forget/input gates).
- **ReLU is the default for hidden layers** in modern deep networks (CNNs, MLPs, Transformers).
- **He initialization** is the standard weight-initialization scheme when using ReLU; **Xavier/Glorot initialization** is paired with Sigmoid/Tanh.
- **For KTU 2024 numerical questions:** You may be asked to evaluate $\sigma(z)$ or compute $\prod \sigma'(z^{(l)})$ — memorize the table at Section 3.2.
- **Exam Tip:** When asked "Why is ReLU better than Sigmoid?", always mention **all four** reasons: (1) no vanishing gradient, (2) faster convergence, (3) sparsity, (4) cheap computation. A 3-mark answer needs at least three.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Softmax
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Softmax

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Softmax function** (also known as the normalized exponential function) is a mathematical function that converts a real-valued vector of $K$ arbitrary real numbers into a probability distribution over $K$ outcome classes. Formally, for an input vector $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_K) \in \mathbb{R}^K$, the Softmax function $\sigma : \mathbb{R}^K \to (0,1)^K$ is defined as:

$$\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, K$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Softmax is the canonical activation function used in the **output layer** of neural networks for **multi-class classification** problems. It generalizes the logistic sigmoid function and forms the probabilistic backbone of classifiers like the multinomial logistic regression and the final layer of CNN/RNN classifiers.

## 1.2 Key Mathematical Properties

The Softmax function possesses three mathematically rigorous properties that make it indispensable in deep learning:

1. **Normalization (Probabilistic Validity):** The outputs of the Softmax function form a valid probability distribution, i.e., $\sum_{i=1}^{K} \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = 1$.
2. **Range Bound:** Each individual output lies strictly in the open interval $(0, 1)$, ensuring valid probability assignments.
3. **Differentiability:** The function is infinitely differentiable (smooth) almost everywhere, enabling gradient-based optimization via backpropagation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Pie Chart Voter"**
> Imagine a panel of 5 judges tasting 5 different dishes. Each judge assigns a *raw score* to every dish (these scores can be negative, positive, or even huge). Now, you want to convert these raw, incomparable scores into a clean **percentage pie chart** showing how much each judge "loved" each dish. The Softmax function is exactly that "translator":
> - It **exponentiates** the raw scores (turning small differences into amplified contrasts — good scores become dominant).
> - It **normalizes** by the sum of all exponentiated scores, ensuring the final values sum to 100% (a valid probability distribution).
> - The dish with the highest raw score receives the **largest slice of the pie**, but the slices are still *proportional* — not winner-take-all.

### Plain-English Intuition for a First-Time Learner

- **"Soft" in Softmax** = the function is *soft* (smooth, differentiable) as opposed to the **hardmax** (argmax) which simply picks the maximum and assigns 1 to it, 0 to everything else. Hardmax is non-differentiable and cannot be used with gradient descent.
- **"max"** = the function *approximates* the behaviour of taking the maximum (largest input gets the largest output).
- **Use case in deep learning:** After the final dense layer of a classifier outputs raw scores called **logits**, Softmax converts them into class probabilities. The predicted class is the one with the highest Softmax probability.

## 1.4 Why Softmax is Critical in Deep Learning

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Output Layer Activation** | Converts logits into a valid probability vector for multi-class classification ($K \geq 3$). |
| **Cross-Entropy Compatibility** | Naturally pairs with the **categorical cross-entropy loss** for efficient gradient computation (the famous $p - y$ gradient simplification). |
| **Probabilistic Interpretation** | Outputs can be directly interpreted as the model's *confidence* in each class — crucial for uncertainty estimation, calibration, and Bayesian deep learning. |
| **Multi-class Generalization** | Softmax is the natural multi-class generalization of the binary logistic sigmoid, which is recovered when $K=2$. |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU board exams, always explicitly state that Softmax is applied **only at the output layer** (not in hidden layers where ReLU/tanh are preferred) and that it is **only** used for mutually exclusive multi-class classification — not for multi-label problems (where independent sigmoids are used instead).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Softmax transformation can be decomposed into three logical sub-operations. Understanding each is critical for both derivation and exam reasoning:

### Step 1: Exponential Amplification (Numerator)
$$e^{z_i}$$
- **Why?** The exponential function is **monotonically increasing** (preserves ordering — the largest $z_i$ still produces the largest $e^{z_i}$).
- **Why exponential specifically?** The exponential has a beautiful derivative ($d/dx\, e^x = e^x$) that produces *clean, cancellation-friendly* gradients when paired with the cross-entropy loss.
- It maps the input from $\mathbb{R}$ to $(0, \infty)$, ensuring all values are positive (required for valid probabilities).

### Step 2: Sum Aggregation (Denominator)
$$\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}$$
- **Why?** This is the **partition function** (normalizing constant) borrowed from statistical physics and information theory.
- It ensures the output is a valid probability distribution summing to 1.

### Step 3: Element-wise Division
$$\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$
- **Why?** Division by the partition function **normalizes** the exponentials into a probability simplex.

## 2.2 The Numerical Stability Trick (High-Yield for Exams!)

A naive implementation of Softmax suffers from **numerical overflow/underflow**:
- If $z_i$ is very large (e.g., 1000), $e^{z_i}$ becomes `Inf` (overflow).
- If $z_i$ is very negative (e.g., -1000), $e^{z_i}$ becomes 0 (underflow).

**Solution: Shift by the maximum logit.** Let $c = \max_j z_j$. Then:

$$\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \frac{e^{z_i - c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j - c}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works mathematically:** Since $\sum_j e^{z_j} = e^c \sum_j e^{z_j - c}$, the factor $e^c$ cancels in the numerator and denominator. This transformation guarantees that the largest exponent argument is **at most 0**, preventing overflow. It is the **standard implementation** in TensorFlow, PyTorch, and NumPy.

## 2.3 Relationship to Other Activation Functions

| Activation | Use Case | Output Range | Sum to 1? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sigmoid (Logistic)** | Binary classification, hidden gates | $(0, 1)$ | No |
| **Softmax** | Multi-class output layer | $(0, 1)^K$ | **Yes** |
| **Hardmax (Argmax)** | Hard decisions (non-differentiable) | $\{0, 1\}^K$ | Yes |
| **Log-Softmax** | Numerically stable log-probabilities | $(-\infty, 0)$ | No (log domain) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Image Classification:** Final layer of ResNet, VGG, EfficientNet for ImageNet (1000 classes).
- **Natural Language Processing:** Language modeling — predicting the next word from a vocabulary of $\geq 50{,}000$ tokens.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Action selection policies in discrete action spaces.
- **Speech Recognition:** Phoneme/state classification in acoustic models.
- **Medical Diagnosis:** Multi-disease classification from X-ray or MRI features.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **rapid-revision master reference** for Softmax in KTU exams. Memorize it.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Key Insight / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Softmax Definition** | $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | Maps $\mathbb{R}^K \to (0,1)^K$ |
| 2 | **Probabilistic Validity** | $\sum_{i=1}^{K} \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = 1$ | Output is a valid PMF |
| 3 | **Numerical Stability** | $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i - \max_j z_j}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j - \max_j z_j}}$ | $c = \max_j z_j$; prevents overflow |
| 4 | **One-Hot Target Encoding** | $y_i \in \{0, 1\}, \sum_i y_i = 1$ | True label is a 1-of-K vector |
| 5 | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_i \log \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i$ | Units: **nats** (or bits if $\log_2$) |
| 6 | **Gradient w.r.t. Logits** | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} = \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i - y_i$ | Famous **$p - y$** simplification |
| 7 | **Jacobian Diagonal** | $\dfrac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_i} = \sigma_i(1 - \sigma_i)$ | Self-derivative |
| 8 | **Jacobian Off-Diagonal** | $\dfrac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_j} = -\sigma_i \sigma_j, \; i \neq j$ | Cross-derivative |
| 9 | **Log-Softmax** | $\log \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = z_i - \log \sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}$ | Numerically stable log probability |
| 10 | **Softmax Temperature** | $\sigma_T(\mathbf{z})_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i / T}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j / T}}$ | $T \to 0$: hardmax; $T \to \infty$: uniform |
| 11 | **Sigmoid ↔ Softmax Link** | $\sigma(z) = \text{softmax}([z, 0])_1$ | Binary case reduction |
| 12 | **Entropy of Softmax Output** | $H = -\sum_i \sigma_i \log \sigma_i$ | Units: nats — measures uncertainty |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Proof of Probabilistic Validity

**Statement to prove:** $\sum_{i=1}^{K} \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = 1$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{K} \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i &= \sum_{i=1}^{K} \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The denominator $\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}$ is a positive scalar that does **not depend** on the summation index $i$ in the outer sum. Therefore, it can be factored out:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{K} \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i &= \frac{1}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{K} e^{z_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Notice that the inner sum $\sum_{i=1}^{K} e^{z_i}$ and the denominator $\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}$ are identical (just dummy index renaming). Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{K} \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{K} e^{z_i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{K} e^{z_i}} = 1 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This algebraic identity confirms that Softmax outputs form a valid probability mass function summing to unity.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Jacobian Matrix of Softmax

The full Jacobian matrix $J \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times K}$ of the Softmax function has entries $J_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_j}$. We derive both the **diagonal** and **off-diagonal** entries.

**Case 1: Off-Diagonal Entry ($i \neq j$)**

By the **quotient rule**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_j} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial z_j} \left( \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{z_k}} \right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0 \cdot \sum_k e^{z_k} - e^{z_i} \cdot e^{z_j}}{\left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{z_k} \right)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The numerator is zero (since $e^{z_i}$ is constant w.r.t. $z_j$ for $i \neq j$) minus $e^{z_i} e^{z_j}$. Recognizing $\sigma_i = e^{z_i} / \sum_k e^{z_k}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_j} &= -\frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}} \cdot \frac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}} \\[6pt]
&= -\sigma_i \cdot \sigma_j \quad (i \neq j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2: Diagonal Entry ($i = j$)**

By the **quotient rule**, now the numerator derivative is non-zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_i} &= \frac{e^{z_i} \cdot \sum_k e^{z_k} - e^{z_i} \cdot e^{z_i}}{\left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{z_k} \right)^2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}} \cdot \frac{\sum_k e^{z_k} - e^{z_i}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $\sigma_i$ and noting that $\dfrac{\sum_k e^{z_k} - e^{z_i}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}} = 1 - \sigma_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_i} &= \sigma_i (1 - \sigma_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary — The Softmax Jacobian:**

$$
\frac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_j} =
\begin{cases}
\sigma_i(1 - \sigma_i) & \text{if } i = j \\[4pt]
-\sigma_i \sigma_j & \text{if } i \neq j
\end{cases}
$$

In compact matrix form, the Jacobian can be written as:

$$J = \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\sigma}) - \boldsymbol{\sigma} \boldsymbol{\sigma}^{\top}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cross-Entropy + Softmax Gradient (The Famous $p - y$ Result)

This is the most important derivation in the Softmax module for deep learning — it is **guaranteed** to appear in KTU exams in some form.

**Setup:** Loss function is the categorical cross-entropy:

$$L = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_i \log \sigma_i$$

where $\sigma_i = \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ and $y_i$ is the one-hot true label.

**Step 1: Apply the chain rule.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} &= -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \sigma_k}{\partial z_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Softmax Jacobian from §3.2.**

We split the sum over $k = i$ (diagonal) and $k \neq i$ (off-diagonal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} &= -y_i \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma_i} \cdot \sigma_i(1 - \sigma_i) - \sum_{k \neq i} y_k \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma_k} \cdot (-\sigma_k \sigma_i) \\[6pt]
&= -y_i (1 - \sigma_i) + \sigma_i \sum_{k \neq i} y_k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Use the one-hot constraint.**

Since $\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k = 1$ (one-hot label), we have $\sum_{k \neq i} y_k = 1 - y_i$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} &= -y_i (1 - \sigma_i) + \sigma_i (1 - y_i) \\[6pt]
&= -y_i + y_i \sigma_i + \sigma_i - \sigma_i y_i \\[6pt]
&= \sigma_i - y_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result — the celebrated KTU formula:**

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} = \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i - y_i}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for deep learning:** This gradient has a *beautiful intuitive form* — it is the **difference between the predicted probability and the true label**. When the prediction matches the truth ($\sigma_i = y_i$), the gradient vanishes. When the prediction is wrong, the gradient is non-zero and pushes the logits in the right direction. This clean form is the reason Softmax + Cross-Entropy is the universal loss for multi-class classification.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Numerical Stability Identity

**Claim:** Subtracting the maximum logit does not change the output.

Let $c = \max_j z_j$. Define the shifted logits $\tilde{z}_i = z_i - c$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma(\tilde{\mathbf{z}})_i &= \frac{e^{\tilde{z}_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{\tilde{z}_j}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{e^{z_i - c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j - c}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{e^{-c} \cdot e^{z_i}}{e^{-c} \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} = \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor $e^{-c}$ cancels in numerator and denominator, leaving the result invariant. After shifting, the largest exponent is $e^0 = 1$, and all others are in $(0, 1]$, guaranteeing numerical stability. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Given logits $\mathbf{z} = (2.0,\; 1.0,\; 0.1)$, compute $\sigma(\mathbf{z})$ using the numerically stable method.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Find the maximum: $c = \max(2.0, 1.0, 0.1) = 2.0$.

Step 2 — Shift: $\tilde{\mathbf{z}} = (0.0,\; -1.0,\; -1.9)$.

Step 3 — Exponentiate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{0.0} &= 1.0000 \\
e^{-1.0} &\approx 0.3679 \\
e^{-1.9} &\approx 0.1496
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Sum: $1.0000 + 0.3679 + 0.1496 = 1.5175$.

Step 5 — Normalize:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \frac{1.0000}{1.5175} \approx 0.6590 \\
\sigma_2 &= \frac{0.3679}{1.5175} \approx 0.2424 \\
\sigma_3 &= \frac{0.1496}{1.5175} \approx 0.0986
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 6 — Verify: $0.6590 + 0.2424 + 0.0986 = 1.0000$ ✓. The class with logit 2.0 has the highest probability (65.9%), as expected.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram — Softmax Computation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block renders the **end-to-end computational flow** of a Softmax operation, including the numerical stability pre-processing step:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Logits Vector z] --> B[Compute Maximum c equals max of z]
    B --> C[Shifted Logits z_tilde equals z minus c]
    C --> D[Apply Exponentiation element-wise e to power z_tilde]
    D --> E[Compute Sum of exponentials S equals sum of e to power z_tilde]
    E --> F[Element-wise Division p_i equals e to power z_tilde_i divided by S]
    F --> G[Output Probability Vector p]
    G --> H{Check Validity}
    H -->|Sum equals 1| I[Valid Probability Distribution]
    H -->|Any p_i outside 0 comma 1| J[Error - Recompute]
    I --> K[Feed to Cross-Entropy Loss]
    K --> L[Compute Gradient p minus y]
    L --> M[Backpropagate to Update Weights]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Softmax in a Neural Network Classifier

The following diagram shows where Softmax sits in the **complete forward pass** of a multi-class image classifier (e.g., a CNN for 10-class digit recognition):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"]
        A1[Input Image 28x28x1]
    end

    subgraph HiddenLayers["Hidden Feature Extraction"]
        B1[Conv + ReLU Block 1]
        B2[Conv + ReLU Block 2]
        B3[Conv + ReLU Block 3]
        B4[Flatten Layer]
        B5[Dense Layer 128 units ReLU]
        B6[Dropout 0.5]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["Output Classification Head"]
        C1[Dense Layer K units LINEAR]
        C2[Softmax Activation]
        C3[Output Probabilities p1 to pK]
    end

    subgraph LossComputation["Loss and Optimization"]
        D1[One-Hot True Label y]
        D2[Cross-Entropy Loss L]
        D3[Optimizer Adam SGD]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 -.Backpropagation.-> B5
    D3 -.Backpropagation.-> C1
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Softmax as a Smooth Approximation to Argmax

This diagram illustrates the **"softness"** of Softmax — how the temperature parameter $T$ interpolates between a uniform distribution ($T \to \infty$) and a hard argmax ($T \to 0$):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Logits z equals 2.0, 1.0, 0.1] --> B{Choose Temperature T}

    B -->|T equals 0.1| C1[Cold Distribution\n0.99, 0.01, 0.00]
    B -->|T equals 1.0| C2[Standard Softmax\n0.66, 0.24, 0.10]
    B -->|T equals 5.0| C3[Warm Distribution\n0.39, 0.34, 0.27]
    B -->|T to infinity| C4[Uniform Distribution\n0.33, 0.33, 0.33]

    C1 --> D[Approaches Hard Argmax]
    C4 --> E[Approaches Uniform Prior]
    C2 --> F[Standard Classification Output]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for exams:** The temperature-scaled Softmax $\sigma_T(\mathbf{z})_i = e^{z_i/T} / \sum_j e^{z_j/T}$ is widely used in **knowledge distillation** (Hinton et al., 2015), where a high-temperature "soft target" from a teacher network guides a smaller student network. Lower $T$ sharpens the distribution; higher $T$ smooths it.

## 4.4 Decision Boundary Schematic — How Softmax Enables Multi-Class Separation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feature Space F] --> B[Linear Dense Layer K Logits]
    B --> C[Softmax Layer]
    C --> D1[Class 1 Probability p1]
    C --> D2[Class 2 Probability p2]
    C --> D3[Class 3 Probability p3]
    C --> D4[Class K Probability pK]
    D1 --> E[Argmax over pi]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Final Predicted Class]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> Define the **Softmax function** mathematically. State and briefly justify **two** important properties of the Softmax function that make it suitable for multi-class classification in deep learning. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Softmax function for an input vector $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_K)$ is defined as:

$$\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, K$$

**Two important properties:**

1. **Probabilistic Normalization:** The outputs sum to unity, i.e., $\sum_{i=1}^{K} \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = 1$. This is because the denominator is the sum of all numerators. Hence the output vector is a valid probability distribution over the $K$ classes, allowing direct probabilistic interpretation of predictions.

2. **Differentiability:** Softmax is smooth and infinitely differentiable everywhere. This enables the use of gradient-based optimization (backpropagation) to train neural networks. The elegant simplification $\partial L / \partial z_i = \sigma_i - y_i$ when paired with cross-entropy loss makes training numerically stable and efficient.

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> Explain the **numerical instability** issue in a naive implementation of the Softmax function. How is it resolved in practice? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A naive implementation computes $e^{z_i}$ directly. If any logit $z_i$ is **very large** (e.g., $> 700$ for `float32`), then $e^{z_i}$ overflows to `+Inf`. Conversely, if $z_i$ is **very negative** (e.g., $< -700$), $e^{z_i}$ underflows to 0. Either case corrupts the probability distribution.

**Resolution:** Subtract the maximum logit $c = \max_j z_j$ from all logits before exponentiation:

$$\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \frac{e^{z_i - c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j - c}}$$

The factor $e^{-c}$ cancels in the numerator and denominator, leaving the output mathematically identical. After shifting, the largest exponent argument is 0, so all exponentials lie in $(0, 1]$, eliminating overflow. This trick is used in every standard deep learning library (TensorFlow, PyTorch, NumPy).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. The student is given an **internal choice** — they must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, describe the **architecture of a multi-class classification neural network** where Softmax is used as the output activation function. Explain **why** Softmax is preferred over ReLU or sigmoid at the output layer for multi-class problems. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the logits $\mathbf{z} = (3.0,\; 1.0,\; 0.5)$ for a 3-class problem, compute the Softmax probabilities using the **numerically stable method**. Then, given the true one-hot label $\mathbf{y} = (1,\; 0,\; 0)$, compute the **categorical cross-entropy loss** $L$ and verify the **gradient** $\partial L / \partial z_i = \sigma_i - y_i$ for each $i$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Multi-Class Classification Architecture with Softmax Output:**

The architecture for a generic $K$-class image classifier proceeds as follows (refer to §4.2 of these notes for the visual schematic):

| Stage | Layer | Activation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input | None | Raw input (e.g., image, text embedding) |
| 2 | Hidden Block 1–N | ReLU / tanh | Non-linear feature extraction |
| 3 | Dense (Fully Connected) | None / ReLU | High-level reasoning |
| 4 | **Output Dense (K units)** | **None (linear logits)** | Produces raw scores $z_i$ |
| 5 | **Softmax Activation** | **Softmax** | Converts logits → probabilities |
| 6 | Cross-Entropy Loss | — | Measures prediction error |

**Why Softmax at the output layer (and not ReLU/sigmoid)?**

1. **Probability interpretation:** Softmax outputs sum to 1 and lie in $(0,1)$, directly giving $P(\text{class} = i \mid \mathbf{x})$.
2. **Multi-class mutual exclusivity:** For mutually exclusive classes (one sample belongs to exactly one class), the Softmax enforces a **competition** between logits through the shared denominator — an increase in one probability forces others to decrease. Sigmoid treats each class **independently**, which is appropriate for multi-label problems but not multi-class.
3. **Gradient compatibility:** The pairing of Softmax with cross-entropy yields the clean gradient $\partial L / \partial z_i = \sigma_i - y_i$, which is numerically stable and avoids the vanishing gradient issue that sigmoid + MSE suffers from.
4. **ReLU** outputs unbounded, non-normalized values and cannot serve as a probability output.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Given $\mathbf{z} = (3.0,\; 1.0,\; 0.5)$ and $\mathbf{y} = (1,\; 0,\; 0)$.

**Step 1 — Numerically stable shift:**

$$
c = \max(3.0,\; 1.0,\; 0.5) = 3.0
$$

Shifted logits: $\tilde{\mathbf{z}} = (0.0,\; -2.0,\; -2.5)$.

**Step 2 — Exponentiate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{0.0} &= 1.0000 \\
e^{-2.0} &\approx 0.1353 \\
e^{-2.5} &\approx 0.0821
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum:**

$$
S = 1.0000 + 0.1353 + 0.0821 = 1.2174
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Softmax probabilities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \frac{1.0000}{1.2174} \approx 0.8214 \\
\sigma_2 &= \frac{0.1353}{1.2174} \approx 0.1112 \\
\sigma_3 &= \frac{0.0821}{1.2174} \approx 0.0674
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $0.8214 + 0.1112 + 0.0674 = 1.0000$ ✓.

**Step 5 — Compute the cross-entropy loss:**

$$
L = -\sum_{i=1}^{3} y_i \log \sigma_i = -(1 \cdot \log 0.8214 + 0 + 0) = -\log 0.8214 \approx 0.1967 \text{ nats}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify the gradient $\partial L / \partial z_i = \sigma_i - y_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_1} &= \sigma_1 - y_1 = 0.8214 - 1 = -0.1786 \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_2} &= \sigma_2 - y_2 = 0.1112 - 0 = +0.1112 \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_3} &= \sigma_3 - y_3 = 0.0674 - 0 = +0.0674
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The gradient for the true class is **negative** (we need to *increase* $z_1$ to boost $\sigma_1$ toward 1), while the gradients for the incorrect classes are **positive** (we need to *decrease* $z_2$ and $z_3$ to suppress those probabilities). This is the correct direction of weight updates.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Derive the **Jacobian matrix** of the Softmax function. Show explicitly that the diagonal entries are $\sigma_i(1 - \sigma_i)$ and the off-diagonal entries are $-\sigma_i \sigma_j$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** State and prove the celebrated result that the **gradient of the categorical cross-entropy loss with respect to the Softmax logits** simplifies to $\partial L / \partial z_i = \sigma_i - y_i$. Discuss why this simplification is **important in practice** for training deep neural networks. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Jacobian Derivation of Softmax:**

Let $\sigma_i = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{z_k}}$. We compute $J_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_j}$.

**Off-diagonal case ($i \neq j$):** Apply the quotient rule. Since $e^{z_i}$ does not depend on $z_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_j} &= \frac{0 \cdot \sum_k e^{z_k} - e^{z_i} \cdot e^{z_j}}{\left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{z_k} \right)^2} \\[6pt]
&= -\frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}} \cdot \frac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}} = -\sigma_i \sigma_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Diagonal case ($i = j$):** Now the numerator derivative is non-zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \sigma_i}{\partial z_i} &= \frac{e^{z_i} \cdot \sum_k e^{z_k} - e^{z_i} \cdot e^{z_i}}{\left( \sum_k e^{z_k} \right)^2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}} \left( 1 - \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}} \right) = \sigma_i (1 - \sigma_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compact matrix form:** $J = \text{diag}(\boldsymbol{\sigma}) - \boldsymbol{\sigma} \boldsymbol{\sigma}^\top$.

**(b) Derivation of the $p - y$ Gradient:**

The cross-entropy loss is $L = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \sigma_k$. By the chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{y_k}{\sigma_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \sigma_k}{\partial z_i}
$$

Split the sum at $k = i$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} = -\frac{y_i}{\sigma_i} \cdot \sigma_i(1 - \sigma_i) - \sum_{k \neq i} \frac{y_k}{\sigma_k} \cdot (-\sigma_k \sigma_i)
$$

$$
= -y_i (1 - \sigma_i) + \sigma_i \sum_{k \neq i} y_k
$$

Using the one-hot constraint $\sum_{k \neq i} y_k = 1 - y_i$:

$$
= -y_i + y_i \sigma_i + \sigma_i (1 - y_i) = \sigma_i - y_i \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Practical importance of the $\sigma_i - y_i$ result:**

1. **Numerical stability:** The naive gradient $\partial L / \partial z_i$ involves the ratio $y_k / \sigma_k$ which can be problematic when $\sigma_k \to 0$. The simplified form $\sigma_i - y_i$ involves only the **bounded difference** of two numbers in $[0, 1]$, avoiding catastrophic cancellation or division by near-zero values.
2. **Computational efficiency:** The gradient can be computed in a single vectorized operation $\nabla_{\mathbf{z}} L = \boldsymbol{\sigma} - \mathbf{y}$, requiring just $K$ subtractions. No expensive Jacobian multiplication is needed, which would otherwise be $O(K^2)$.
3. **Intuitive interpretation:** The gradient is literally the **prediction error** for each class. Magnitude is proportional to the model's "surprise" — large for confidently wrong predictions, small for correct ones. This enables effective learning rate scaling and gradient clipping strategies.
4. **Vanishing gradient mitigation:** Unlike the sigmoid + MSE combination, this gradient does not vanish when $\sigma_i$ saturates near 0 or 1 (as long as $y_i$ is not at the saturated value), because the linear term $\sigma_i$ remains non-zero.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in KTU exams on this topic — DO NOT repeat these:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the one-hot constraint:** When deriving the gradient, students often forget to use $\sum_k y_k = 1$ and leave the answer in an unsimplified form. Marks are deducted for not reaching the final clean form $\sigma_i - y_i$.
>
> 2. **Mixing up Softmax with Sigmoid:** Softmax is for **multi-class** (mutually exclusive), Sigmoid is for **binary or multi-label**. Writing "Softmax is used for binary classification" is a **direct loss of 2–3 marks**.
>
> 3. **Skipping the numerical stability step:** When a problem provides large logits (e.g., $z = (1000, 999, 998)$), failing to shift by the maximum is a critical error. The examiner expects to see $c = \max(z)$ explicitly written.
>
> 4. **Sign errors in Jacobian:** The off-diagonal Jacobian is $-\sigma_i \sigma_j$ (**negative**). A common mistake is to write it as $+\sigma_i \sigma_j$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the units of cross-entropy:** Always state whether the loss is in **nats** (natural log) or **bits** ($\log_2$). Mismatch with the expected units may cost partial marks.
>
> 6. **Writing Softmax in the hidden layer:** Softmax is **only** an output-layer activation. Mentioning it as a hidden layer activation is a conceptual error.
>
> 7. **Confusing Softmax with Softplus:** Softplus is $\log(1 + e^x)$ — a smooth approximation to ReLU. It is **not** the same as Softmax. Do not confuse the two in answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — pin this to your wall before the exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = e^{z_i} / \sum_j e^{z_j}$ — converts logits to probabilities.
- ✅ **Output range:** $(0, 1)^K$ with $\sum \sigma_i = 1$ — a valid probability simplex.
- ✅ **Use case:** **Output layer** of multi-class ($K \geq 3$) classifiers with **mutually exclusive** classes.
- ✅ **Not for:** Binary classification (use sigmoid), multi-label classification (use independent sigmoids), hidden layers (use ReLU).
- ✅ **Numerical stability:** Always subtract $c = \max_j z_j$ before exponentiating.
- ✅ **Loss pairing:** Use **categorical cross-entropy** $L = -\sum_i y_i \log \sigma_i$ with Softmax.
- ✅ **The $p - y$ gradient:** $\partial L / \partial z_i = \sigma_i - y_i$ — memorize this derivation cold.
- ✅ **Jacobian:** Diagonal $\sigma_i(1 - \sigma_i)$, off-diagonal $-\sigma_i \sigma_j$.
- ✅ **Log-Softmax:** $\log \sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = z_i - \log \sum_j e^{z_j}$ — preferred for numerical stability with NLLLoss.
- ✅ **Temperature scaling:** $\sigma_T(\mathbf{z})_i = e^{z_i/T} / \sum_j e^{z_j/T}$ — used in knowledge distillation.
- ✅ **Generalization of Sigmoid:** When $K = 2$, $\text{softmax}([z, 0])_1 = 1 / (1 + e^{-z}) = \sigma(z)$.
- ✅ **Units of loss:** Natural logarithm → nats; $\log_2$ → bits. KTU typically expects nats.
- ✅ **Decision rule:** Predicted class $= \arg\max_i \sigma_i$, equivalent to $\arg\max_i z_i$ (since Softmax is monotonic).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Training CNNs: Initialization, Back-propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Training CNNs: Initialization, Back-propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Weight Initialization in CNNs

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
*Weight initialization* in a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) refers to the systematic procedure of assigning initial numerical values to the learnable parameters (kernels/filters and bias vectors) of every convolutional, fully-connected, and normalization layer **before** the first forward pass. The chosen initialization scheme directly governs the *variance of activations*, the *variance of gradients*, and therefore the *convergence speed and stability* of stochastic gradient descent (SGD) or its variants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEECT742 / Module 2:**
> Weight initialization is a mandatory high-yield topic. The examiner expects students to (a) state the formula, (b) justify the choice with a *variance-preserving* argument, and (c) identify which activation function each scheme is paired with.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of CNN Training (Module 2 Scope)**
> 1. **Initialization** — sets the starting point on the loss landscape.
> 2. **Back-propagation** — computes gradients of the loss w.r.t. every parameter.
> 3. **Update Rule (SGD / Adam / RMSProp)** — moves parameters along the negative gradient.
>
> This module focuses on Pillars 1 and 2.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Climber at Dusk"**

Imagine a blindfolded climber (the optimizer) standing somewhere on a vast, foggy mountain range (the loss surface $L(\theta)$) and needs to reach the lowest valley (the global minimum).

- **Bad initialization** = the climber is dropped at the edge of a cliff. The very first step causes a *gradient explosion* (he falls off) or *vanishing gradients* (he cannot feel the slope because he is on a plateau).
- **Good initialization** = the climber is placed on a slope of moderate, consistent steepness, so every step gives reliable feedback and he steadily descends.

Mathematically, "good" means the **variance of activations stays roughly constant** layer-to-layer (forward stability) AND the **variance of gradients stays roughly constant** layer-to-layer (backward stability).

---

### 1.2 Back-Propagation in CNNs

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
*Back-propagation through a CNN* is the reverse-mode automatic differentiation of the scalar loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to every kernel weight $W^{[l]}$, bias $b^{[l]}$, and intermediate feature map $A^{[l]}$, applied **layer-by-layer from the output back to the input**. Because CNN layers have *shared parameters* (the same kernel slides over the spatial map) and *non-linearities that are not pointwise-fully-connected*, the chain rule must be adapted into **transposed convolution** (for conv layers) and **masked routing** (for pooling layers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Back-prop in CNNs is NOT identical to MLPs:**
> In an MLP, back-prop is matrix multiplication by the transpose of $W$. In a CNN, back-prop through a conv layer is the **discrete convolution of the upstream gradient with the *rotated-180°* kernel** — this is what students most frequently confuse in the exam.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Blame Game in a Factory"**

Consider a car factory (the CNN). The final output is a predicted price (loss $\mathcal{L}$). The boss wants to know: *"Which screw in which sub-assembly caused the price error?"*

- **Forward pass**: raw materials $\rightarrow$ assembly line 1 $\rightarrow$ assembly line 2 $\rightarrow$ … $\rightarrow$ final car $\rightarrow$ price error.
- **Back-prop**: the boss starts at the price error and walks *backwards*, asking each station, "What did *you* contribute to this error, and what input did *you* receive that caused it?"
  - A **conv layer** distributes blame across all spatial positions its kernel touched (using a *transposed convolution*).
  - A **pooling layer** sends the entire blame back to only the winning unit (max-pool) or splits it equally (avg-pool).

This recursive *blame attribution* is mathematically the chain rule of calculus applied to a directed acyclic computation graph.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Symbols Used Throughout

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $n_{\text{in}}$ | Number of incoming connections (fan-in) per neuron | $1$ to $10^6$ |
| $n_{\text{out}}$ | Number of outgoing connections (fan-out) per neuron | $1$ to $10^6$ |
| $\sigma^2$ | Variance of activations | layer-dependent |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate (hyperparameter) | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| $\mathcal{L}$ | Scalar loss function | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| $\delta^{[l]}$ | Local error / gradient w.r.t. pre-activation $Z^{[l]}$ | $\mathbb{R}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default choice of non-linearity determines the initialization:**
> - **Sigmoid / Tanh** $\Rightarrow$ use **Xavier (Glorot) initialization**.
> - **ReLU / Leaky-ReLU / PReLU** $\Rightarrow$ use **He initialization**.
> - **SELU** $\Rightarrow$ use **LeCun normal initialization**.
>
> KTU examiners award full marks only when the pairing is justified, not merely stated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Weight Initialization Schemes

Below is the **operationally exhaustive** list of initialization families a KTU student must recognize. Each is decomposed into *what to do*, *why it works*, and *when it fails*.

#### 2.1.1 Zero / Constant Initialization
- **What:** Set $W^{[l]} = 0$ and $b^{[l]} = 0$ for all layers.
- **Why it fails:** If every neuron in a layer computes the same function (because all weights are equal), the gradient w.r.t. each weight is *also identical*. The network is effectively a **single-neuron network** regardless of width. This phenomenon is called the **symmetry-breaking failure**.
- **Verdict:** Acceptable **only** for bias vectors of *some* layers (e.g., $b^{[l]} = 0$ is common in ReLU CNNs). Never acceptable for $W$.

#### 2.1.2 Small Random Initialization ($W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.01)$)
- **What:** Sample weights i.i.d. from a zero-mean Gaussian with tiny variance.
- **Why it fails for deep CNNs:** Variance of activations shrinks multiplicatively through $L$ layers:
  $$\text{Var}(A^{[L]}) = \text{Var}(A^{[0]}) \cdot \prod_{\ell=1}^{L} n_{\ell}\,\text{Var}(W^{[\ell]})$$
  If $n_{\ell}\text{Var}(W^{[\ell]}) < 1$, the activations **vanish** exponentially with depth. Conversely, $> 1$ causes **explosion**.

#### 2.1.3 Xavier (Glorot) Initialization — Uniform Variant
- **What:** Sample $W \sim \mathcal{U}\!\left[-\sqrt{\tfrac{6}{n_{\text{in}}+n_{\text{out}}}},\;\sqrt{\tfrac{6}{n_{\text{in}}+n_{\text{out}}}}\right]$.
- **Equivalently (Normal variant):** $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\;\tfrac{2}{n_{\text{in}}+n_{\text{out}}}\right)$.
- **Designed for:** Sigmoid / Tanh activations (approximately symmetric around 0).
- **Goal:** Make the *average* of forward-variance and backward-variance equal to 1.

#### 2.1.4 He (Kaiming) Initialization — Uniform Variant
- **What:** $W \sim \mathcal{U}\!\left[-\sqrt{\tfrac{6}{n_{\text{in}}}},\;\sqrt{\tfrac{6}{n_{\text{in}}}}\right]$.
- **Equivalently (Normal variant):** $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\;\tfrac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}\right)$.
- **Designed for:** ReLU / Leaky-ReLU activations (only half the neurons are non-zero on average).
- **Why the divisor is just $n_{\text{in}}$ (and not $n_{\text{in}}+n_{\text{out}}$)?** Because for ReLU, the **fan-out term is multiplied by $\tfrac{1}{2}$** (the expected fraction of surviving units), which approximately cancels one of the two terms in the Glorot denominator, leaving only the fan-in term.

#### 2.1.5 LeCun Initialization
- **What:** $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\;\tfrac{1}{n_{\text{in}}}\right)$.
- **Designed for:** SELU activation (self-normalizing networks).

#### 2.1.6 Orthogonal & Sparse Initialization
- **What:** Initialize $W$ as a (semi-)orthogonal random matrix: $W^\top W = I$.
- **Designed for:** Very deep networks (RNNs, ResNets ≥ 100 layers). Preserves norm of signal exactly through every linear transformation.

---

### 2.2 Why Back-Propagation in CNNs Requires Special Treatment

In a fully-connected (FC) MLP, a layer computes:
$$Z^{[l]} = W^{[l]} A^{[l-1]} + b^{[l]}$$
Its gradient w.r.t. input is a **plain matrix transpose**:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A^{[l-1]}} = (W^{[l]})^\top \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z^{[l]}}$$

In a **convolutional** layer, the operation is:
$$Z^{[l]} = A^{[l-1]} * W^{[l]} + b^{[l]}$$
where $*$ denotes 2-D discrete convolution with the kernel $W^{[l]}$ sliding over the spatial map.

The "transpose" operation that propagates gradients back through $*$ is **NOT a simple matrix transpose** — it is a **transposed convolution** (sometimes called *deconvolution*, though that name is technically reserved for a different operation). Symbolically:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A^{[l-1]}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z^{[l]}} \star W^{[l]}$$
where $\star$ denotes the transposed-convolution operator (i.e., convolution with the spatially-flipped kernel and *full* padding).

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Simultaneous Gradients** are required at every conv layer $l$ during back-prop:
> 1. $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial W^{[l]}$ — needed to *update* the kernel.
> 2. $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial b^{[l]}$ — needed to *update* the bias.
> 3. $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial A^{[l-1]}$ — needed to *propagate* the error further backwards.
>
> KTU questions typically ask for any one of these three; you must know which.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Xavier (Normal) variance | $\sigma^2_W = \dfrac{2}{n_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{out}}}$ | Sigmoid / Tanh layers | dimensionless |
| 2 | Xavier (Uniform) bound | $a = \sqrt{\dfrac{6}{n_{\text{in}} + n_{\text{out}}}}$ | $W \sim \mathcal{U}[-a, +a]$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | He (Normal) variance | $\sigma^2_W = \dfrac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}$ | ReLU / Leaky-ReLU layers | dimensionless |
| 4 | He (Uniform) bound | $a = \sqrt{\dfrac{6}{n_{\text{in}}}}$ | $W \sim \mathcal{U}[-a, +a]$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | LeCun normal variance | $\sigma^2_W = \dfrac{1}{n_{\text{in}}}$ | SELU layers | dimensionless |
| 6 | Forward variance propagation | $\text{Var}(Z^{[l]}) = n_{\text{in}}\,\sigma^2_W\,\text{Var}(A^{[l-1]})$ | independent weights assumption | $(\text{activation})^2$ |
| 7 | Backward variance propagation | $\text{Var}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z^{[l-1]}}\right) = n_{\text{out}}\,\sigma^2_W\,\text{Var}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z^{[l]}}\right)$ | — | $(\text{gradient})^2$ |
| 8 | Gradient w.r.t. kernel (conv) | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{[l]}} = A^{[l-1]} * \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z^{[l]}}$ | valid convolution | dimensionless |
| 9 | Gradient w.r.t. input (conv) | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A^{[l-1]}} = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z^{[l]}} \star W^{[l]}$ | transposed convolution | dimensionless |
| 10 | Gradient w.r.t. bias (conv) | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{[l]}} = \displaystyle\sum_{i,j}\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z^{[l]}_{i,j}}$ | sum over spatial positions | dimensionless |
| 11 | Max-pooling mask | $\mathbb{1}_{\max}(i,j) = 1$ iff $(i,j) = \arg\max_{(p,q)\in \text{patch}} A^{[l-1]}_{p,q}$ | only one unit per patch survives | indicator |
| 12 | Max-pool back-prop rule | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A^{[l-1]}_{p,q}} = \mathbb{1}_{\max}(p,q)\cdot\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z^{[l]}_{i,j}}$ | gradient routed to arg-max only | dimensionless |
| 13 | Average-pool back-prop rule | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A^{[l-1]}_{p,q}} = \dfrac{1}{\vert \text{patch}\vert}\cdot\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z^{[l]}_{i,j}}$ | uniform distribution | dimensionless |
| 14 | SGD update | $W^{[\ell]} \leftarrow W^{[\ell]} - \eta\,\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{[\ell]}}$ | learning rate $\eta > 0$ | parameter-units |
| 15 | Vanishing-gradient criterion | $n_{\text{in}}\sigma^2_W < 1$ | activations shrink | — |
| 16 | Exploding-gradient criterion | $n_{\text{in}}\sigma^2_W > 1$ | activations blow up | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility (Real-World KTU-Mentioned Applications):**
> - **Medical imaging (tumor segmentation in MRI)** uses He initialization + ReLU CNNs because input images are large ($n_{\text{in}}$ very high) and asymmetry of ReLU matters.
> - **Satellite image classification** uses Xavier + Tanh in older architectures (e.g., LeNet-5) where sigmoid-like activations were standard.
> - **Autonomous-driving perception stacks** use orthogonal initialization for very deep ResNet backbones to prevent gradient explosion in ≥ 50 layer backbones.
