# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define feature extraction. Why is it essential in deep learning pipelines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Feature extraction is the process of transforming raw, high-dimensional input data into a compact, discriminative, and informative set of numerical descriptors (feature vectors) that preserve task-relevant information.
- **Importance (2 marks):**
  1. Reduces the **curse of dimensionality**, making downstream models computationally tractable.
  2. Removes redundant/irrelevant information, improving **generalization** and reducing overfitting.
  3. Introduces useful **invariances** (translation, rotation, illumination) so the model focuses on discriminative content rather than nuisance variations.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Distinguish between hand-crafted and deep learning-based feature extraction methods with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Hand-crafted | Deep Learning |
|---|---|---|
| Design | Designed by humans using domain knowledge | Automatically learned from data |
| Examples | SIFT, HOG, LBP, Gabor | CNN, Autoencoder, ViT |
| Adaptability | Fixed; cannot adapt to new data | Data-driven; improves with more data |
| Performance | Plateaus on complex tasks | Scales with data and compute |
| Engineering effort | High (feature engineering) | Low (architecture + training) |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Module carries an internal choice. You are expected to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the hierarchical feature extraction process in a **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)**. Discuss how features evolve from low-level to high-level as we go deeper. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the output feature map dimensions and the total number of trainable parameters for the following CNN layer:
- Input image size: $64 \times 64 \times 3$
- Number of filters: $16$, each of size $3 \times 3$
- Stride $S = 1$, Padding $P = 1$ **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hierarchical Feature Extraction in CNN (7 marks):**

A CNN processes input data through alternating convolution, activation, and pooling layers, gradually building a hierarchy of features.

- **Layer 1 — Low-level features (2 marks):**
  The first convolutional layer learns **edge detectors, color gradients, and oriented lines**. Each filter activates strongly in regions where a specific edge pattern is present. The ReLU activation introduces non-linearity, allowing the network to detect these patterns regardless of intensity sign.

- **Layer 2–3 — Mid-level features (2 marks):**
  Subsequent layers combine the edge maps to detect **corners, textures, and motifs** (e.g., T-junctions, repeated patterns). The receptive field grows, allowing the network to "see" larger regions of the input.

- **Layer 4+ — High-level features (2 marks):**
  Deeper layers combine mid-level features to recognize **object parts** (eyes, wheels) and eventually **entire objects** (faces, cars). These features are highly semantic and task-specific.

- **Diagram (1 mark):** (As shown in Section 4.2)

This hierarchical abstraction is what makes CNNs so powerful for visual recognition — the network automatically discovers the best features for the task.

**(b) Computation of Output Dimensions and Parameters (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Output dimension formula.**

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{64 - 3 + 2(1)}{1} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{64 - 3 + 2}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 63 \right\rfloor + 1 = 64
$$

So each filter produces a $64 \times 64$ feature map.

**Step 4 — Final feature map shape.**

$$
64 \times 64 \times 16
$$

**Step 5 — Trainable parameters per filter.**

Each filter spans the full input depth (3 channels), so each filter has $3 \times 3 \times 3 = 27$ weights plus 1 bias = **28 parameters**.

**Step 6 — Total parameters.**

$$
\text{Total Params} = 28 \times 16 = 448
$$

**Final Answer:** Output feature map is $64 \times 64 \times 16$, with **448 trainable parameters**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the **Autoencoder** architecture for unsupervised feature extraction. Derive the reconstruction loss and explain the role of the bottleneck layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a dataset with covariance matrix eigenvalues $[5.0, 3.0, 1.5, 0.5]$, determine the number of principal components $k$ required to retain at least **90%** of the variance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Autoencoder for Feature Extraction (7 marks):**

- **Architecture (3 marks):**
  An autoencoder consists of two parts:
  - **Encoder** $f$: maps input $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ to a hidden code $h \in \mathbb{R}^k$ where $k \ll d$.
  - **Decoder** $g$: maps $h$ back to a reconstruction $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$.

$$
h = f(W_e x + b_e), \quad \hat{x} = g(W_d h + b_d)
$$

- **Loss Function Derivation (2 marks):**
  For a single sample, the L2 reconstruction loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x}) = \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert_2^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{d} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2
$$

For $N$ samples, the average loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \Vert x_j - \hat{x}_j \Vert_2^2
$$

This is minimized via backpropagation.

- **Bottleneck Role (2 marks):**
  The bottleneck layer (latent code $h$) forces the network to learn a **compressed representation** that retains only the most salient features needed to reconstruct the input. If the bottleneck is too narrow, information is lost; if too wide, the network learns the identity function (useless). The bottleneck effectively performs **non-linear PCA**.

**(b) PCA — Choosing $k$ (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Sum of all eigenvalues.**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} \lambda_i = 5.0 + 3.0 + 1.5 + 0.5 = 10.0
$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative variance ratios.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1 PC: } & \quad \frac{5.0}{10.0} = 0.50 = 50\% \\
\text{2 PCs: } & \quad \frac{5.0 + 3.0}{10.0} = \frac{8.0}{10.0} = 0.80 = 80\% \\
\text{3 PCs: } & \quad \frac{5.0 + 3.0 + 1.5}{10.0} = \frac{9.5}{10.0} = 0.95 = 95\% \\
\text{4 PCs: } & \quad 1.00 = 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the smallest $k$ such that the ratio $\geq 90\%$.**

Since $80\% < 90\%$ but $95\% \geq 90\%$, we need $k = 3$.

**Final Answer:** $k = 3$ principal components are required to retain at least 90% of the variance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $+1$** in the output dimension formula is the **most common mistake**, costing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing cross-correlation with convolution** in CNN derivations — remember, CNNs use cross-correlation (no kernel flip), but for exam purposes, both interpretations are usually accepted.
> 3. **Autoencoder diagrams without arrows** lose 1 mark — always show the data flow direction explicitly.
> 4. **PCA**: Always show the **cumulative ratio computation step-by-step**; do not just state the final answer.
> 5. **Padding terms** in formulas: omitting $2P$ is a frequent mark-losing error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Feature Extraction (Module 2)**

### Core Concepts
- [x] Feature extraction = transforming raw data into a compact, discriminative representation.
- [x] **Three paradigms**: hand-crafted (SIFT, HOG), deep (CNN, Autoencoder), transfer learning (pre-trained models).
- [x] **CNN hierarchy**: edges → textures → parts → objects (as depth increases).

### Key Formulas
- [x] **Convolution output**: $O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$
- [x] **Conv layer parameters**: $(F_h \cdot F_w \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot K$
- [x] **PCA reconstruction error**: sum of discarded eigenvalues
- [x] **Autoencoder loss**: $\mathcal{L} = \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert_2^2$
- [x] **Max pooling** vs **average pooling** formulas

### Engineering Knowledge
- [x] **Pooling** provides translation invariance and reduces computation.
- [x] **Transfer learning** is preferred when the new dataset is small.
- [x] **Autoencoders** perform **non-linear PCA** at the bottleneck.
- [x] **Receptive field** grows with depth, enabling larger context.

### Common KTU Pitfalls
- [x] Always include **floor brackets** $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ in dimension formulas.
- [x] Don't forget the **bias term** when counting parameters.
- [x] For **PCA**, show the **cumulative ratio table** explicitly.
- [x] When discussing CNN features, mention both **low-level and high-level** examples.

### Quick Mnemonic
> **"E-T-P-O"** — Edges → Textures → Parts → Objects (CNN feature hierarchy)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fine Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fine Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fine-Tuning** is a transfer learning technique in deep learning wherein the weights of a pre-trained neural network (trained on a large, generic dataset) are further adjusted—typically via continued backpropagation—using a smaller, task-specific dataset to specialize the model for a downstream application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Fine-tuning is the supervised training process that adapts a pre-trained model's parameters $\theta$ to minimize a task-specific loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{task}}(\theta; \mathcal{D}_{\text{new}})$, starting from the weights $\theta_0$ learned on the source domain $\mathcal{D}_{\text{source}}$.

Formally, fine-tuning solves the optimization:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \; \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}}(\theta; \mathcal{D}_{\text{new}}), \quad \text{with initialization } \theta \leftarrow \theta_0$$

where $\theta_0$ are the weights obtained after pre-training on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{source}}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Specialist Doctor:**
> Imagine hiring a **general physician** (pre-trained model) who already knows human anatomy, diagnostics, and pharmacology. To make them a **cardiology specialist** (fine-tuned model), you don't throw away their medical school education — you just give them **focused residency training** in cardiology (task-specific data). Their foundational knowledge is preserved, but their weights are **slightly nudged** toward cardiology expertise.

Similarly, in deep learning:
- The **pre-trained network** = general feature extractor (edges, textures, shapes).
- **Fine-tuning** = gentle adjustment of all (or some) weights using domain-specific data.
- The **learning rate used is typically very small** (e.g., $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$) so that we don't catastrophically overwrite the learned features.

## 1.3 Where Fine-Tuning Fits in Deep Learning

| Stage | Purpose | Dataset Size |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-training** | Learn general representations | Large (millions of samples) |
| **Fine-Tuning** | Adapt to specific task | Small to medium (hundreds–thousands) |
| **Inference** | Apply to new inputs | N/A |

## 1.4 Key Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Source Domain ($\mathcal{D}_S$):** The large dataset used for pre-training (e.g., ImageNet with **1.28M images**, **1000 classes**).
> - **Target Domain ($\mathcal{D}_T$):** The smaller dataset for the new task (e.g., custom medical X-rays).
> - **Source Task ($T_S$):** Original training objective (e.g., ImageNet classification).
> - **Target Task ($T_T$):** New task to solve (e.g., tumor classification).
> - **Backbone:** The pre-trained feature extractor portion of the network (excluding the classifier head).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Fine-Tuning Works — The Theoretical Foundation

The success of fine-tuning rests on three empirical observations:

1. **Hierarchical Feature Reusability:** Early layers of deep CNNs learn generic features (edges, corners, color blobs) that transfer across vision tasks.
2. **Manifold Proximity:** Source and target task optimal weights lie in **neighboring regions** of the loss landscape.
3. **Sample Efficiency:** Strong initialization (from $\theta_0$) drastically reduces the number of gradient steps needed for convergence on $\mathcal{D}_T$.

## 2.2 Fine-Tuning vs. Feature Extraction

> [!NOTE]
> These two are **commonly confused** in KTU exams — know the difference cold.

| Aspect | Feature Extraction | Fine-Tuning |
|---|---|---|
| **Backbone weights** | Frozen ($\nabla\theta_{\text{backbone}} = 0$) | Updated (unfrozen) |
| **Trainable params** | Only classifier head | All (or partial) layers |
| **Compute cost** | Low | Moderate to High |
| **Performance** | Baseline | Usually higher |
| **Risk of overfitting** | Lower | Higher on small datasets |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Fine-Tuning Procedure

**Step 1 — Select Pre-trained Model**
Choose architecture compatible with target domain: ResNet, VGG, BERT, GPT, etc.

**Step 2 — Replace the Classifier Head**
Discard the final $K_S$-class softmax layer; replace with a new head matching $K_T$ target classes.

**Step 3 — Choose Training Strategy**

Three principal strategies exist:

- **Strategy A — Full Fine-Tuning:** Unfreeze all layers and train end-to-end.
- **Strategy B — Partial Fine-Tuning:** Freeze first $L$ layers, fine-tune only the top layers.
- **Strategy C — Discriminative Learning Rates:** Use smaller learning rates for early layers and larger rates for later layers.

**Step 4 — Set Hyperparameters**

- Learning rate $\eta \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-3}]$ (smaller than pre-training rate)
- Weight decay $\lambda \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-2}]$
- Few epochs (typically 3–20)

**Step 5 — Train with Early Stopping**
Monitor validation loss to prevent catastrophic forgetting.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Equations for KTU Board Examinations**

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$ | Standard SGD update during fine-tuning | $\eta \approx 1\text{e-}4$ |
| 2 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{K_T} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Cross-entropy loss for new classifier head | — |
| 3 | $\eta_l = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L-l}$ | Discriminative LR for layer $l$ (total layers $L$) | $\gamma \in [0.9, 0.95]$ |
| 4 | $\theta_l^{t+1} = \theta_l^t - \eta \nabla_{\theta_l}\mathcal{L} + \lambda \theta_l^t$ | Weight decay update (L2 regularization) | $\lambda \approx 5\text{e-}4$ |
| 5 | $\text{Freeze}(l) = \mathbb{1}[\,l \le L_{\text{frozen}}\,]$ | Indicator for frozen layer mask | — |
| 6 | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}} + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{KD}}(p_S, p_T)$ | Knowledge distillation loss (optional) | $\lambda \in [0.5, 2.0]$ |
| 7 | $p(y \vert x) = \text{softmax}(z/T)$ | Soft targets with temperature $T$ | $T \in [3, 20]$ |

> **Note on notation:** The vertical bars inside `\mid` are used here to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Medical Imaging:** Fine-tune ImageNet-pretrained ResNet on limited CT/MRI datasets.
- **NLP:** Fine-tune BERT on sentiment analysis, NER, or QA tasks.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Fine-tune detection backbones on custom road datasets.
- **Industrial Defect Detection:** Adapt pre-trained CNNs to manufacturing quality control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Lower Learning Rate in Fine-Tuning

Let us formalize **why** fine-tuning requires a smaller learning rate than pre-training.

**Starting Point:** The pre-trained model sits at a local minimum $\theta_0$ on the source loss landscape $\mathcal{L}_S(\theta)$. When we switch to the target task with loss $\mathcal{L}_T(\theta)$, we perform a Taylor expansion around $\theta_0$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_T(\theta) \approx \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_0) + \nabla \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_0)^T (\theta - \theta_0) + \frac{1}{2}(\theta - \theta_0)^T H_T (\theta - \theta_0)
$$

where $H_T$ is the Hessian of $\mathcal{L}_T$ at $\theta_0$.

**Step 1 — Gradient Term Dominance (Small $\Delta \theta$):**

A single SGD step computes the update:

$$
\Delta \theta = -\eta \nabla \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_0)
$$

**Step 2 — Bound on Update Magnitude:**

To ensure that the parameters remain within a trust region where the second-order approximation holds, we require:

$$
\Vert \Delta \theta \Vert_2 = \eta \Vert \nabla \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_0) \Vert_2 \le \epsilon
$$

This gives the constraint:

$$
\eta \le \frac{\epsilon}{\Vert \nabla \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_0) \Vert_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Empirical Consequence:**

Since $\theta_0$ is a minimum on $\mathcal{L}_S$ (so $\nabla \mathcal{L}_S(\theta_0) \approx 0$) but **not** on $\mathcal{L}_T$, the gradient $\nabla \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_0)$ can be **large** when $\mathcal{L}_T$ differs significantly from $\mathcal{L}_S$. To stay in the local quadratic basin, we must use $\eta \ll \eta_{\text{pretrain}}$.

This is the rigorous justification for the **rule of thumb**: use $\eta_{\text{finetune}} \approx \eta_{\text{pretrain}} / 10$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Discriminative Learning Rate Schedule

**Setup:** A network has $L$ layer groups indexed $l = 1, 2, \ldots, L$ (from input to output). Layer 1 learns low-level features; layer $L$ learns task-specific features.

**Goal:** Assign learning rate $\eta_l$ to layer $l$ such that early layers are updated gently and later layers more aggressively.

**Standard Discriminative LR Formula:**

$$
\eta_l = \eta_L \cdot \gamma^{L-l}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

- $\eta_L$ is the base learning rate for the **last layer group** (typically the classifier).
- $\gamma \in (0, 1)$ is the decay factor.
- For $l = L$: $\eta_L = \eta_L \cdot \gamma^0 = \eta_L$ (full rate).
- For $l = L-1$: $\eta_{L-1} = \eta_L \cdot \gamma$.
- For $l = 1$: $\eta_1 = \eta_L \cdot \gamma^{L-1}$ (smallest rate).

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Suppose $L = 5$, $\eta_L = 1 \times 10^{-3}$, $\gamma = 0.9$. Compute $\eta_l$ for each layer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_5 &= 1 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.9^{0} = 1.00 \times 10^{-3} \\
\eta_4 &= 1 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.9^{1} = 9.00 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_3 &= 1 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.9^{2} = 8.10 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_2 &= 1 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.9^{3} = 7.29 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_1 &= 1 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.9^{4} = 6.56 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This schedule recognizes that **early-layer features are generic and reusable**, so they need only minor adjustments, while **later-layer features are task-specific** and benefit from more substantial updates.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Strategy

| Scenario | Dataset Size | Similarity to Source | Recommended Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tiny custom dataset | $< 1\text{k}$ samples | High | Feature extraction (freeze backbone) |
| Small medical dataset | $1\text{k}$–$10\text{k}$ samples | Low | Partial FT with discriminative LR |
| Moderate dataset | $10\text{k}$–$100\text{k}$ samples | Moderate | Full fine-tuning with small $\eta$ |
| Large dataset | $> 100\text{k}$ samples | Any | Full fine-tuning or train from scratch |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** The smaller the target dataset and the larger the domain gap, the more layers should be **frozen**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Catastrophic Forgetting Bound

Fine-tuning can cause **catastrophic forgetting** of source knowledge. A common mitigation is Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC), which adds a quadratic penalty:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{EWC}}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}}(\theta) + \sum_i \lambda_i (\theta_i - \theta_{0,i})^2
$$

where $\lambda_i = \frac{F_{ii}$ (diagonal of Fisher information matrix) measures parameter importance.

**Gradient Update with EWC:**

$$
\theta_i^{t+1} = \theta_i^t - \eta \left[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{task}}}{\partial \theta_i} + 2\lambda_i (\theta_i^t - \theta_{0,i}) \right]
$$

**Interpretation:** Parameters with high Fisher information (i.e., critical to source task) are **strongly anchored** to $\theta_0$, while unimportant parameters can move freely.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fine-Tuning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Dataset DS - e.g. ImageNet] --> B[Pre-trained Model θ0]
    B --> C{Choose Strategy}
    C --> D1[Strategy A: Full FT - All layers unfrozen]
    C --> D2[Strategy B: Partial FT - Freeze first L layers]
    C --> D3[Strategy C: Discriminative LR - Layer-wise η]
    D1 --> E[Replace Classifier Head - K_T classes]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Fine-Tune on Target Dataset DT]
    F --> G{Validation Loss}
    G -->|Decreasing| H[Continue Training]
    G -->|Plateau or Rising| I[Early Stop - Save θ*]
    H --> F
    I --> J[Fine-Tuned Model θ*]
```

## 4.2 Layer Freezing Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Layer 1: Frozen η = 0]:::frozen
    L2[Layer 2: Frozen η = 0]:::frozen
    L3[Layer 3: Low LR η = 1e-5]:::lowlr
    L4[Layer 4: Medium LR η = 1e-4]:::medlr
    L5[Layer 5: High LR η = 1e-3]:::highlr
    L6[New Classifier Head]:::newhead
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6

    classDef frozen fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#2874a6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lowlr fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef medlr fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef highlr fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef newhead fill:#e8daef,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Fine-Tuning Strategy Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Dataset size?} --> Q1a{Tiny less than 1k} --> R1[Feature Extraction Only]
    Q1 --> Q1b{Small 1k to 10k} --> Q2{Domain similar to source?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[Partial FT - Freeze first 3 layers]
    Q2 -->|No| R3[Discriminative LR with strong freezing]
    Q1 --> Q1c{Large more than 100k} --> R4[Full Fine-Tuning or Train from Scratch]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define fine-tuning in the context of deep learning. How does it differ from training a neural network from scratch?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Fine-tuning is a transfer learning technique where a neural network pre-trained on a large source dataset $\mathcal{D}_S$ is further trained (with typically a smaller learning rate) on a target dataset $\mathcal{D}_T$ to adapt it to a new related task.

**Key differences from training from scratch (any 2):**

1. **Initialization:** Fine-tuning starts from pre-trained weights $\theta_0$, not random initialization.
2. **Data requirement:** Fine-tuning needs much less target data (often $100\times$ to $1000\times$ less).
3. **Compute cost:** Fine-tuning requires fewer epochs and less GPU time.
4. **Generalization:** Fine-tuning leverages previously learned features, reducing overfitting risk on small datasets.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List any three hyperparameters that require special attention when fine-tuning a pre-trained model. Justify each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Learning rate $\eta$:** Should be **smaller** (typically $10\times$ to $100\times$ smaller) than pre-training rate to avoid catastrophic forgetting of source knowledge.
2. **Number of frozen layers $L_f$:** Determines how much of the backbone is preserved. More layers frozen = less overfitting on small datasets.
3. **Number of epochs:** Should be kept **low** (typically 3–20) because the model is already near a good solution.
4. (Bonus) **Weight decay $\lambda$:** Often increased to prevent overfitting on small target datasets.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly confuse **feature extraction** with **fine-tuning** and lose 2–3 marks. Always explicitly state whether weights are frozen or updated. Also, never use a learning rate equal to pre-training — examiners deduct marks for this.

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of transfer learning. Discuss the different strategies of transfer learning with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the fine-tuning process in detail. Explain why a smaller learning rate is preferred during fine-tuning, with mathematical justification. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Transfer Learning Definition (2 marks):**
Transfer learning is a machine learning paradigm where knowledge gained from training on a **source task** $T_S$ with dataset $\mathcal{D}_S$ is leveraged to improve learning on a **related target task** $T_T$ with dataset $\mathcal{D}_T$, where $\mathcal{D}_T \ll \mathcal{D}_S$ in size.

**Four major strategies (4 marks):**

1. **Pre-training + Feature Extraction:** Use pre-trained CNN as fixed feature extractor; train only a new classifier (SVM, MLP) on extracted features.
2. **Pre-training + Fine-Tuning:** Initialize from pre-trained weights and continue training all layers with small $\eta$.
3. **Frozen Feature Extraction + Partial Fine-Tuning:** Freeze early layers, fine-tune later layers.
4. **Domain Adaptation:** Techniques like CORAL, DANN to align feature distributions.

**Diagram (1 mark):** (Reference diagram in Section 4.1)

**Pipeline as diagram must show:** Source Dataset $\to$ Pre-trained Model $\to$ Strategy Selection $\to$ Target Adaptation.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step-by-step Fine-Tuning Process (3 marks):**

1. Select pre-trained model $f(\cdot; \theta_0)$.
2. Replace final classifier layer to match $K_T$ target classes.
3. Optionally freeze the first $L_f$ layers.
4. Train using SGD with learning rate $\eta_{\text{ft}} \ll \eta_{\text{pretrain}}$.
5. Use validation-based early stopping.
6. Optionally apply discriminative learning rates.

**Mathematical Justification for Smaller LR (4 marks):**

Starting from the local quadratic approximation around $\theta_0$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_T(\theta) \approx \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_0) + g^T \Delta\theta + \frac{1}{2} \Delta\theta^T H \Delta\theta
$$

where $g = \nabla \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_0)$ and $H = \nabla^2 \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_0)$.

The optimal step satisfying the trust-region constraint $\Vert \Delta\theta \Vert_2 \le \epsilon$ leads to:

$$
\eta \le \frac{\epsilon}{\Vert g \Vert_2}
$$

Since the source loss $\mathcal{L}_S$ has $\nabla \mathcal{L}_S(\theta_0) \approx 0$ but $\nabla \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_0)$ can be **large**, we must use a small $\eta$ to remain in the basin of attraction around $\theta_0$ and avoid catastrophic forgetting of source features.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the role of pre-trained models in deep learning. Explain the architecture of a typical pre-trained CNN used for image classification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the fine-tuning workflow. Also describe the technique of discriminative learning rates with a numerical example for a 5-layer network. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Role of Pre-trained Models (3 marks):**

1. Provide strong initialization, reducing training time from days to hours.
2. Enable learning on small datasets where training from scratch would overfit.
3. Serve as general-purpose feature extractors usable across tasks.
4. Democratize deep learning by removing the need for massive compute.

**Typical Pre-trained CNN Architecture (4 marks):**

Example — ResNet-50 backbone:

- **Conv1:** $7 \times 7$ conv, stride 2 $\to$ captures low-level edges.
- **Conv2_x:** 3 residual blocks $\to$ textures and patterns.
- **Conv3_x:** 4 residual blocks $\to$ mid-level parts.
- **Conv4_x:** 6 residual blocks $\to$ object parts.
- **Conv5_x:** 3 residual blocks $\to$ high-level semantic features.
- **AvgPool + FC:** Original 1000-class ImageNet classifier (to be replaced).

**Total parameters:** $\approx 25.6\text{M}$.

**Key Insight:** Early layers are highly transferable; later layers are task-specific.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Fine-Tuning Workflow Diagram (3 marks):** (Reference Section 4.1 diagram)

The workflow consists of: load pre-trained $\to$ freeze/select layers $\to$ replace head $\to$ fine-tune with small $\eta$ $\to$ early stop $\to$ deploy.

**Discriminative Learning Rates — Numerical Example (4 marks):**

For a 5-layer network ($L = 5$), base learning rate $\eta_L = 1 \times 10^{-3}$, decay factor $\gamma = 0.9$:

The formula:

$$
\eta_l = \eta_L \cdot \gamma^{L-l}
$$

Computation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_5 &= 1 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.9^{0} = 1.00 \times 10^{-3} \\
\eta_4 &= 1 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.9^{1} = 9.00 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_3 &= 1 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.9^{2} = 8.10 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_2 &= 1 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.9^{3} = 7.29 \times 10^{-4} \\
\eta_1 &= 1 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.9^{4} = 6.56 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Layer 1 (closest to input) is updated with the **smallest** rate, while layer 5 (closest to output) is updated with the **largest** rate. This preserves generic low-level features while allowing task-specific high-level features to adapt quickly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Fine Tuning**

- ✅ **Fine-tuning** = continued training of a pre-trained model on a new task with a smaller learning rate.
- ✅ Always **replace the final classifier layer** to match the number of target classes $K_T$.
- ✅ Use a learning rate **10× to 100× smaller** than the pre-training rate ($\eta \approx 1\text{e-}4$).
- ✅ **Feature extraction** freezes the backbone; **fine-tuning** updates it.
- ✅ Use **early stopping** to prevent overfitting on small datasets.
- ✅ **Discriminative LR** formula: $\eta_l = \eta_L \cdot \gamma^{L-l}$ with $\gamma \in [0.9, 0.95]$.
- ✅ Smaller target dataset $\Rightarrow$ freeze more layers.
- ✅ Cross-entropy loss is standard: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_i y_i \log \hat{y}_i$.
- ✅ Watch out for **catastrophic forgetting** — mitigate via EWC or low $\eta$.
- ✅ Common pre-trained models: ResNet, VGG, Inception (vision); BERT, GPT (NLP).
- ✅ **ImageNet stats:** 1.28M training images, 1000 classes, 224×224 resolution.
- ✅ KTU examiners expect **diagrams** for any fine-tuning workflow question — always include one.
- ✅ Always **justify the learning rate choice** with the trust-region argument.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequence Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sequence Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Sequence Models?

A **Sequence Model** is a class of deep neural network architectures designed to process, model, and predict data points that occur in a sequential order, where each element is contextually dependent on the previous (and often future) elements. Unlike feedforward networks that assume inputs are independent, sequence models explicitly preserve and propagate temporal/sequential dependencies through a hidden state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Sequence Models are neural networks whose inputs or outputs (or both) are sequences, and which model the dependency between elements of the sequence using recurrent or attention-based connections.*

Mathematically, given an input sequence $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \dots, \mathbf{x}^{(T)}$, a sequence model learns a function:
$$
f_{\theta} : (\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \dots, \mathbf{x}^{(T)}) \longrightarrow (\mathbf{y}^{(1)}, \mathbf{y}^{(2)}, \dots, \mathbf{y}^{(T)})
$$

where $T$ is the sequence length, $\theta$ are the learnable parameters, and the ordering carries semantic meaning.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "Reading a Novel"

Imagine you are reading a murder-mystery novel. To understand **who committed the crime on page 287**, you must remember:
- Who was introduced on **page 3**
- The clue mentioned on **page 56**
- The alibi given on **page 142**

Your brain maintains a **running memory (hidden state)** that gets updated with every page you read. A **Sequence Model works identically** — it reads one token (word/time-step) at a time and updates its internal memory to capture dependencies across the entire sequence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Not Feedforward Networks?**
> - They require **fixed-size inputs** — but sentences/videos/audio have variable lengths.
> - They cannot share features learned at one position with another position.
> - They process inputs **independently**, destroying temporal order.

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Sequence Problems

Sequence Models handle **four canonical problem types** that KTU examiners love to test:

| Problem Type | Input | Output | Example Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **One-to-One** | Single vector | Single vector | Standard classification (image tagging) |
| **One-to-Many** | Single vector | Sequence | Image captioning |
| **Many-to-One** | Sequence | Single vector | Sentiment classification of a review |
| **Many-to-Many (Synced)** | Sequence | Sequence (same length) | Part-of-speech tagging, video frame classification |
| **Many-to-Many (Delayed)** | Sequence | Sequence (different length) | Machine translation, speech-to-text |

## 1.4 The Core Building Blocks We Will Study

In Module 3, KTU expects mastery of the following sequence architectures in increasing order of sophistication:

1. **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)** — the foundational architecture
2. **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** — the training algorithm
3. **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** — solves vanishing gradients
4. **Gated Recurrent Units (GRU)** — a streamlined LSTM variant
5. **Bidirectional RNNs** — leverages future context
6. **Encoder–Decoder / Seq2Seq Models** — for variable-length mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) — Architecture

A standard RNN processes a sequence one time-step at a time. At each time-step $t$, it maintains a **hidden state** $\mathbf{h}^{(t)}$ that acts as a "summary" of all information seen up to that point.

### Core Recurrence Relations

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \tanh\left( \mathbf{W}_{xh}\,\mathbf{x}^{(t)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\,\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{b}_h \right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}^{(t)} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}\,\mathbf{h}^{(t)} + \mathbf{b}_y
$$

Where the parameters are **shared across every time-step** — this is the parameter-sharing principle that makes RNNs translation-invariant over sequence position.

### Role of Each Parameter Matrix

| Symbol | Shape | Function | Initialization Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{W}_{xh}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$ | Maps current input to hidden | Xavier / He |
| $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$ | Propagates previous memory | Orthogonal (preferred) |
| $\mathbf{W}_{hy}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_y \times d_h}$ | Maps hidden to output | Xavier / He |
| $\mathbf{b}_h, \mathbf{b}_y$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}, \mathbb{R}^{d_y}$ | Bias vectors | Zeros |

## 2.2 Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

BPTT unrolls the RNN across all $T$ time-steps into a deep feedforward graph, then applies standard backpropagation. The total loss is the sum of per-time-step losses:

$$
\mathcal{L}^{(t)}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}\!\left(\mathbf{y}^{(t)}, \hat{\mathbf{y}}^{(t)}\right)
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}(\theta)
$$

The gradient flowing back to step $k$ is computed by the **chain rule applied $T-k$ times**:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(t)}} \cdot \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}}
$$

### The Vanishing Gradient Problem — Why Vanilla RNNs Fail

Each multiplicative factor in the chain $\prod \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}}$ contains $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ whose spectral radius $\rho(\mathbf{W}_{hh})$ governs the gradient's decay/explosion:

$$
\left\Vert \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}} \right\Vert \;\leq\; \left(\rho(\mathbf{W}_{hh})\right)^{t-k}
$$

- If $\rho(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) > 1$ → **Exploding gradients** (cured by **gradient clipping** at threshold $\tau$: $\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \frac{\tau \mathbf{g}}{\Vert \mathbf{g} \Vert}$).
- If $\rho(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) < 1$ → **Vanishing gradients** → the network cannot learn long-range dependencies. **This is the killer flaw solved by LSTM/GRU.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vanishing gradient** does NOT mean the gradient becomes zero numerically. It means the contributions from distant time-steps become exponentially smaller than those from nearby steps, so the model effectively learns only short-term patterns.

## 2.3 Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)

Introduced by Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997), the LSTM replaces the simple hidden-state update with a **memory cell** $\mathbf{c}^{(t)}$ regulated by three learned gates.

### LSTM Gate Equations

$$
\mathbf{f}^{(t)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{f}\!\left[\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(t)}\right] + \mathbf{b}_f\right) \quad \text{(Forget gate)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{i}^{(t)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{i}\!\left[\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(t)}\right] + \mathbf{b}_i\right) \quad \text{(Input gate)}
$$

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{c}}^{(t)} = \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{c}\!\left[\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(t)}\right] + \mathbf{b}_c\right) \quad \text{(Candidate memory)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{c}^{(t)} = \mathbf{f}^{(t)} \odot \mathbf{c}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{i}^{(t)} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}^{(t)} \quad \text{(Cell update)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{o}^{(t)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{o}\!\left[\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(t)}\right] + \mathbf{b}_o\right) \quad \text{(Output gate)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \mathbf{o}^{(t)} \odot \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{c}^{(t)}\right)
$$

### Intuition for Each Gate

| Gate | Symbol | Question It Answers | Range |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Forget | $\mathbf{f}^{(t)}$ | "What should I throw away from past memory?" | $[0,1]$ |
| Input | $\mathbf{i}^{(t)}$ | "What new info should I write into memory?" | $[0,1]$ |
| Output | $\mathbf{o}^{(t)}$ | "What should I expose from memory as hidden state?" | $[0,1]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The element-wise multiplication $\odot$ (Hadamard product) is what allows the LSTM to **selectively retain** information. If $\mathbf{f}^{(t)} = \mathbf{1}$ and $\mathbf{i}^{(t)} = \mathbf{0}$, the cell state is preserved perfectly — creating a "gradient highway" that mitigates vanishing gradients.

## 2.4 Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)

The GRU (Cho et al., 2014) simplifies the LSTM by merging the cell and hidden state and using only **two gates**:

$$
\mathbf{z}^{(t)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{z}\!\left[\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(t)}\right]\right) \quad \text{(Update gate)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{r}^{(t)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{r}\!\left[\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(t)}\right]\right) \quad \text{(Reset gate)}
$$

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)} = \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}\!\left[\mathbf{r}^{(t)} \odot \mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(t)}\right]\right) \quad \text{(Candidate)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \left(1 - \mathbf{z}^{(t)}\right) \odot \mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{z}^{(t)} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)}
$$

### LSTM vs GRU — KTU Comparison Table

| Feature | LSTM | GRU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Separate cell state $\mathbf{c}^{(t)}$ | Yes | No (merged with $\mathbf{h}$) |
| Parameter count | $4 \times (d_h^2 + d_h d_x)$ | $3 \times (d_h^2 + d_h d_x)$ |
| Training speed | Slower | ~25% faster |
| Long-sequence performance | Better on very long sequences | Comparable on short/medium |
| When to prefer | Speech recognition, machine translation | Small datasets, fast prototyping |

## 2.5 Bidirectional RNN (Bi-RNN)

For tasks where **both past AND future context matter** (e.g., named entity recognition, POS tagging), a Bi-RNN runs two RNNs in parallel — one forward, one backward — and concatenates their hidden states:

$$
\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)} = \text{RNN}_{\text{fwd}}\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(t)}, \overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t-1)}\right)
$$

$$
\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)} = \text{RNN}_{\text{bwd}}\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(t)}, \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t+1)}\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \left[\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)};\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)}\right]
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Bi-RNNs cannot be used for real-time/causal tasks** like next-word prediction during text generation, because they require the entire future sequence at inference time.

## 2.6 Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq) Architecture

For mapping an input sequence of length $T_x$ to an output sequence of length $T_y$ (where $T_x \neq T_y$), we use a two-stage architecture:

1. **Encoder RNN**: Reads the entire input and compresses it into a fixed-size context vector $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}^{(T_x)}_{\text{enc}}$.
2. **Decoder RNN**: Generates the output sequence one token at a time, conditioned on $\mathbf{c}$.

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(t)}_{\text{enc}} = f_{\text{enc}}\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(t)}, \mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}_{\text{enc}}\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{s}^{(t)}_{\text{dec}} = f_{\text{dec}}\!\left(\mathbf{y}^{(t-1)}, \mathbf{s}^{(t-1)}_{\text{dec}}, \mathbf{c}\right)
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{y}}^{(t)} = \text{softmax}\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o\,\mathbf{s}^{(t)}_{\text{dec}}\right)
$$

### The Information Bottleneck Problem

A single fixed-size context vector $\mathbf{c}$ cannot compress long input sentences — this is known as the **information bottleneck**. The solution is the **attention mechanism** (briefly in Module 3; expanded in Module 4):

$$
\mathbf{c}^{(t)} = \sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \alpha^{(t,i)}\,\mathbf{h}^{(i)}_{\text{enc}}
$$

where $\alpha^{(t,i)}$ are learned attention weights satisfying $\sum_i \alpha^{(t,i)} = 1$.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — questions are guaranteed from it.**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Key Insight |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| RNN hidden update | $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}^{(t)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{b}_h)$ | Same weights reused at every step |
| BPTT total loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$ | Sum of per-step losses |
| Gradient decay bound | $\Vert \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \mathbf{h}^{(k)} \Vert \leq (\rho(\mathbf{W}_{hh}))^{t-k}$ | Controls long-range learning |
| LSTM cell update | $\mathbf{c}^{(t)} = \mathbf{f}^{(t)} \odot \mathbf{c}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{i}^{(t)} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}^{(t)}$ | Additive update → no vanishing |
| GRU hidden update | $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = (1-\mathbf{z}^{(t)}) \odot \mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{z}^{(t)} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)}$ | Linear interpolation |
| Bi-RNN merge | $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = [\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)}; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)}]$ | Concatenation of two passes |
| Gradient clipping | $\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \tau\mathbf{g}/\max(\tau,\Vert\mathbf{g}\Vert)$ | Threshold $\tau$ usually $\in [1,5]$ |
| Attention context | $\mathbf{c}^{(t)} = \sum_i \alpha^{(t,i)}\mathbf{h}^{(i)}_{\text{enc}}$ | Weighted sum, $\sum \alpha = 1$ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speech Recognition (DeepSpeech, Whisper)** — LSTM/Transformer encoders transcribe audio waveforms to text.
- **Machine Translation (Google Translate)** — Originally Seq2Seq with LSTM; now Transformer-based.
- **Financial Forecasting** — Bi-LSTM predicts stock prices using multi-day lookback.
- **Bioinformatics** — LSTM models DNA/protein sequences for gene prediction.
- **Autonomous Driving** — RNN/LSTM models temporal sensor streams (LiDAR, radar, camera).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: BPTT Gradient for Vanilla RNN

### Setup
We unroll a vanilla RNN for $T = 3$ time-steps with loss $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$ and wish to compute $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}$.

### Step 1 — Express the hidden state recursively

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}^{(1)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}^{(0)} + \mathbf{b}_h)
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(2)} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}^{(2)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}_h)
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(3)} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}^{(3)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}^{(2)} + \mathbf{b}_h)
$$

### Step 2 — Output at each time-step

$$
\mathbf{y}^{(t)} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}^{(t)} + \mathbf{b}_y
$$

### Step 3 — Compute gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}_{hy}$ (simple direct path)

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hy}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(t)}}{\partial \mathbf{y}^{(t)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{y}^{(t)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hy}} = \left(\hat{\mathbf{y}}^{(t)} - \mathbf{y}^{(t)}\right) (\mathbf{h}^{(t)})^{\top}
$$

The total gradient for $\mathbf{W}_{hy}$:
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \left(\hat{\mathbf{y}}^{(t)} - \mathbf{y}^{(t)}\right) (\mathbf{h}^{(t)})^{\top}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ (the recursive part)

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(3)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(3)}}{\partial \mathbf{y}^{(3)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{y}^{(3)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(3)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(3)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(t)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(t)}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}
$$

### Step 5 — Expand the chain $\partial \mathbf{h}^{(3)}/\partial \mathbf{h}^{(t)}$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(3)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(t)}} = \prod_{i=t+1}^{3} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(i-1)}} = \prod_{i=t+1}^{3} \text{diag}\!\left(1 - (\mathbf{h}^{(i)})^2\right) \mathbf{W}_{hh}
$$

### Step 6 — Take the spectral norm to bound it

$$
\left\Vert \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(3)}}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(t)}} \right\Vert \;\leq\; \left(\lambda_{\max}\right)^{3-t} \prod_{i=t+1}^{3} \Vert \text{diag}(1-(\mathbf{h}^{(i)})^2) \Vert
$$

where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest singular value of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$. Since each $\tanh$ derivative is bounded in $[0,1]$, the dominant scaling term is $\lambda_{\max}^{3-t}$. This **directly proves the vanishing/exploding gradient theorem**.

### Step 7 — Final interpretation

If $\lambda_{\max} < 1$ and the sequence is long ($3 - t$ is large), the gradient shrinks exponentially → **vanishing gradient**. If $\lambda_{\max} > 1$, it explodes. Hence we **must** use LSTM/GRU or gradient clipping.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — LSTM Forward Pass on a Toy Sequence

### Given
- Sequence: $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = [1, 0]^{\top}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)} = [0, 1]^{\top}, \mathbf{x}^{(3)} = [1, 1]^{\top}$
- Hidden size $d_h = 2$, input size $d_x = 2$
- Initial states: $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} = [0, 0]^{\top}, \mathbf{c}^{(0)} = [0, 0]^{\top}$
- For simplicity, assume all weights = 0.5 matrices, all biases = 0.

$$
\mathbf{W}_f = \mathbf{W}_i = \mathbf{W}_c = \mathbf{W}_o = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 1 — Compute concatenated input at $t=1$

$$
[\mathbf{h}^{(0)}; \mathbf{x}^{(1)}] = [0, 0, 1, 0]^{\top}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute all gates at $t=1$

$$
\mathbf{f}^{(1)} = \sigma\!\left(\begin{bmatrix}0.5 & 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.5\end{bmatrix} [0, 0, 1, 0]^{\top}\right) = \sigma(0.5) \approx [0.622, 0.622]^{\top}
$$

By symmetry, all gates equal $\sigma(0.5) \approx 0.622$ for each of the two hidden units at $t=1$.

### Step 3 — Candidate memory at $t=1$

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{c}}^{(1)} = \tanh(0.5) \approx [0.462, 0.462]^{\top}
$$

### Step 4 — Cell state update

$$
\mathbf{c}^{(1)} = \mathbf{f}^{(1)} \odot \mathbf{c}^{(0)} + \mathbf{i}^{(1)} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}^{(1)} = 0.622 \odot [0,0]^{\top} + 0.622 \odot [0.462, 0.462]^{\top}
$$

$$
\mathbf{c}^{(1)} \approx [0.287, 0.287]^{\top}
$$

### Step 5 — Hidden state at $t=1$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \mathbf{o}^{(1)} \odot \tanh(\mathbf{c}^{(1)}) \approx 0.622 \odot \tanh([0.287, 0.287]^{\top})
$$

$$
\tanh(0.287) \approx 0.280, \quad \mathbf{h}^{(1)} \approx [0.174, 0.174]^{\top}
$$

### Step 6 — Repeat for $t=2, 3$

Following the same procedure, students can verify that the hidden state evolves smoothly without suffering from numerical blow-up — **demonstrating the LSTM's stability** versus vanilla RNN.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In numericals, always compute gates one at a time and round to 3 decimal places. Show the **intermediate $\sigma(\cdot)$** and $\tanh(\cdot)$ activations explicitly.

---

| BPTT | Unrolled backprop | $\mathcal{L} = \sum_t \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$ | Apply (L3) |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** State the vanishing gradient problem in Recurrent Neural Networks. How does the LSTM architecture overcome it?

**Model Answer:**

The vanishing gradient problem occurs during BPTT in vanilla RNNs when the gradient of the loss w.r.t. earlier hidden states decays exponentially because it involves repeated multiplication by the recurrent weight matrix $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$. Formally, the gradient scales as $\rho(\mathbf{W}_{hh})^{T-k}$ for step $k$ in a sequence of length $T$, so when $\rho(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) < 1$, distant time-steps contribute negligibly to learning.

The LSTM overcomes this by introducing a **memory cell** $\mathbf{c}^{(t)}$ updated via **additive** (not multiplicative) interactions:

$$
\mathbf{c}^{(t)} = \mathbf{f}^{(t)} \odot \mathbf{c}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{i}^{(t)} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}^{(t)}
$$

The forget gate $\mathbf{f}^{(t)}$ allows the cell to retain memory across long time spans (when $\mathbf{f}^{(t)} \approx 1$), creating a "gradient highway" where gradients flow back with little decay. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Compare GRU and LSTM with respect to the number of gates, parameter count, and a suitable application for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | LSTM | GRU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Separate cell state | Yes | No (merged with hidden) |
| Parameters | $4(d_h^2 + d_h d_x + d_h)$ | $3(d_h^2 + d_h d_x + d_h)$ |
| Training speed | Slower | ~25% faster |
| Best for | Long sequences (speech, machine translation) | Small datasets, short sequences, fast prototyping |

The GRU is computationally lighter because it merges the cell and hidden states, but LSTM typically wins on tasks requiring very long memory. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Recurrent Neural Network. Derive the forward propagation equations and discuss how parameters are shared across time-steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the working of an LSTM cell. Derive all the gate equations and the cell state update rule. Explain how it addresses the vanishing gradient problem. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Architecture:**
A Recurrent Neural Network processes a sequence $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \dots, \mathbf{x}^{(T)}$ one element at a time. At each step $t$, it maintains a hidden state $\mathbf{h}^{(t)}$ that summarizes all past information. The architecture consists of three weight matrices $\mathbf{W}_{xh}$ (input-to-hidden), $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ (hidden-to-hidden), and $\mathbf{W}_{hy}$ (hidden-to-output).

**Forward Propagation Equations:**

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}^{(t)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{b}_h\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}^{(t)} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}^{(t)} + \mathbf{b}_y
$$

**Parameter Sharing:**
The same matrices $\mathbf{W}_{xh}, \mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{hy}$ are used at **every time-step** $t = 1, \dots, T$. This is crucial because:

1. It allows the network to generalize to sequences of **any length**.
2. It reduces the total parameter count drastically — for a sequence of length $T$, we still only learn **three matrices**, not $3T$.
3. It enables **translation invariance over time** — the model treats the same pattern identically regardless of where it appears in the sequence.

**Unrolled View:** When unrolled across time, the RNN becomes a deep feedforward network of depth $T$ with shared weights. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**LSTM Cell Diagram (textual):** A single LSTM cell receives $\mathbf{x}^{(t)}$ and $\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}$ as inputs, computes three gates (forget, input, output) and a candidate memory vector, then updates the cell state $\mathbf{c}^{(t)}$ and produces $\mathbf{h}^{(t)}$.

**Gate Equations:**

**Forget Gate** — controls what to discard from previous memory:
$$
\mathbf{f}^{(t)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_f [\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}; \mathbf{x}^{(t)}] + \mathbf{b}_f\right)
$$

**Input Gate** — controls what new information to store:
$$
\mathbf{i}^{(t)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_i [\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}; \mathbf{x}^{(t)}] + \mathbf{b}_i\right)
$$

**Candidate Memory** — new information candidates:
$$
\tilde{\mathbf{c}}^{(t)} = \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}_c [\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}; \mathbf{x}^{(t)}] + \mathbf{b}_c\right)
$$

**Cell State Update:**
$$
\mathbf{c}^{(t)} = \mathbf{f}^{(t)} \odot \mathbf{c}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{i}^{(t)} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}^{(t)}
$$

**Output Gate and Hidden State:**
$$
\mathbf{o}^{(t)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o [\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}; \mathbf{x}^{(t)}] + \mathbf{b}_o\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \mathbf{o}^{(t)} \odot \tanh(\mathbf{c}^{(t)})
$$

**How it overcomes vanishing gradient:**
The cell state update is **additive** rather than purely multiplicative. The forget gate $\mathbf{f}^{(t)}$ acts as a learned "carry coefficient." When $\mathbf{f}^{(t)} \approx 1$ and $\mathbf{i}^{(t)} \approx 0$, the cell state propagates unchanged across time-steps:

$$
\mathbf{c}^{(t)} \approx \mathbf{c}^{(t-1)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial \mathbf{c}^{(t)}}{\partial \mathbf{c}^{(t-1)}} \approx 1
$$

This creates a near-unit Jacobian along the memory pathway, so gradients can flow back hundreds of steps without exponential decay. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the Bidirectional RNN architecture. Derive the equations for the forward and backward passes. List two applications and state one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Encoder–Decoder Seq2Seq architecture. Discuss the information bottleneck problem and briefly describe how the attention mechanism addresses it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Bidirectional RNN Architecture:**
A Bi-RNN processes a sequence in **two directions** simultaneously using two separate RNNs:

- **Forward RNN** processes the sequence from $t = 1$ to $T$, producing hidden states $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)}$.
- **Backward RNN** processes the sequence from $t = T$ to $1$, producing hidden states $\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)}$.

**Forward Pass Equations:**
$$
\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)} = f\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{\vec{h}}\,\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{W}_{\vec{x}}\,\mathbf{x}^{(t)} + \mathbf{b}_{\vec{h}}\right)
$$

**Backward Pass Equations:**
$$
\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)} = f\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{\reflectbox{h}}\,\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t+1)} + \mathbf{W}_{\reflectbox{x}}\,\mathbf{x}^{(t)} + \mathbf{b}_{\reflectbox{h}}\right)
$$

**Output Combination (concatenation):**
$$
\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \left[\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)}; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)}\right]
$$

Alternative combinations include summation $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)} + \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)}$ or averaging.

**Applications:**
1. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — determining if a word is a person/location requires both preceding context ("Mr.", "in") and following context ("Inc.", "Street").
2. **Part-of-Speech Tagging** — the word "book" can be a noun or verb depending on surrounding words.
3. **Protein structure prediction** in bioinformatics.

**Limitation:** Bi-RNNs require the **entire input sequence at inference time**, making them unsuitable for real-time/causal tasks such as streaming speech recognition or autoregressive text generation.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Encoder–Decoder Seq2Seq Architecture:**
This architecture maps an input sequence of length $T_x$ to an output sequence of length $T_y$ where $T_x$ and $T_y$ may differ. It has two stages:

**Encoder Stage:**
The encoder is an RNN (or LSTM/GRU) that reads the input sequence token-by-token and compresses all information into a fixed-size context vector:

$$
\mathbf{h}^{(t)}_{\text{enc}} = f_{\text{enc}}\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(t)}, \mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}_{\text{enc}}\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}^{(T_x)}_{\text{enc}}
$$

**Decoder Stage:**
The decoder is another RNN that generates the output sequence one token at a time, conditioned on the context vector $\mathbf{c}$ and previously generated tokens:

$$
\mathbf{s}^{(t)}_{\text{dec}} = f_{\text{dec}}\!\left(\mathbf{y}^{(t-1)}, \mathbf{s}^{(t-1)}_{\text{dec}}, \mathbf{c}\right)
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{y}}^{(t)} = \text{softmax}\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o \mathbf{s}^{(t)}_{\text{dec}}\right)
$$

**Applications:** Machine translation, text summarization, speech recognition.

**Information Bottleneck Problem:**
The entire input sequence is compressed into a **single fixed-size vector** $\mathbf{c}$. For long sentences, this creates a bottleneck — the model cannot retain all relevant information. Performance degrades significantly on sequences longer than ~30 tokens.

**Attention Mechanism Solution:**
Attention replaces the fixed context vector with a **dynamic, time-step-specific context** $\mathbf{c}^{(t)}$ computed as a weighted sum of all encoder hidden states:

$$
\mathbf{c}^{(t)} = \sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \alpha^{(t,i)} \mathbf{h}^{(i)}_{\text{enc}}
$$

The attention weights $\alpha^{(t,i)}$ are learned via a small feedforward network (alignment model) and satisfy $\sum_i \alpha^{(t,i)} = 1$. This allows the decoder to "focus" on different parts of the input at each output step, eliminating the bottleneck and dramatically improving long-sequence performance. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE marks on Sequence Models questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the activation functions.** Writing $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}^{(t)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}$ without the $\tanh(\cdot)$ wrapper — costs **1 mark** every time.
> 2. **Confusing LSTM cell state with hidden state.** The cell state $\mathbf{c}^{(t)}$ and hidden state $\mathbf{h}^{(t)}$ are DIFFERENT vectors. Using them interchangeably will be marked wrong.
> 3. **Omitting the bias terms.** Always include $\mathbf{b}_h, \mathbf{b}_i, \mathbf{b}_f, \mathbf{b}_o, \mathbf{b}_c$ in LSTM equations.
> 4. **Forgetting parameter sharing.** When asked "how is RNN different from a deep network?", the answer must explicitly mention **shared weights across time-steps**.
> 5. **Missing the $[\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}; \mathbf{x}^{(t)}]$ concatenation.** LSTM/GRU gates operate on the concatenation of previous hidden state and current input — not separately.
> 6. **Calling attention "the same as Seq2Seq".** Attention is a *replacement* for the fixed context vector, not the entire architecture.
> 7. **Not explaining WHY LSTM solves vanishing gradients.** Just stating "LSTM uses gates" is insufficient. You must mention the **additive cell update** and **forget-gate gradient highway**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it the night before the exam.**

### Core Concepts to Memorize

- [ ] RNN forward equations: $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}^{(t)} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{b}_h)$; $\mathbf{y}^{(t)} = \mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}^{(t)} + \mathbf{b}_y$.
- [ ] BPTT loss: $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}^{(t)}$.
- [ ] Vanishing gradient bound: $\rho(\mathbf{W}_{hh})^{T-k}$.
- [ ] Gradient clipping: $\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \tau \mathbf{g}/\max(\tau, \Vert \mathbf{g} \Vert)$.
- [ ] LSTM three gates: forget $\mathbf{f}$, input $\mathbf{i}$, output $\mathbf{o}$.
- [ ] LSTM cell update: $\mathbf{c}^{(t)} = \mathbf{f}^{(t)} \odot \mathbf{c}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{i}^{(t)} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}^{(t)}$.
- [ ] LSTM hidden state: $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \mathbf{o}^{(t)} \odot \tanh(\mathbf{c}^{(t)})$.
- [ ] GRU two gates: update $\mathbf{z}$, reset $\mathbf{r}$.
- [ ] GRU hidden update: $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = (1 - \mathbf{z}^{(t)}) \odot \mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{z}^{(t)} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)}$.
- [ ] Bi-RNN merge: $\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = [\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)}; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}^{(t)}]$.
- [ ] Encoder–Decoder context: $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}^{(T_x)}_{\text{enc}}$.
- [ ] Attention context: $\mathbf{c}^{(t)} = \sum_i \alpha^{(t,i)} \mathbf{h}^{(i)}_{\text{enc}}$, with $\sum_i \alpha^{(t,i)} = 1$.

### One-Line Definitions for Rapid Recall

- **RNN:** A neural network that processes sequences by maintaining a recurrent hidden state, sharing weights across time-steps.
- **BPTT:** Backpropagation applied to the unrolled computation graph of an RNN across all $T$ time-steps.
- **Vanishing Gradient:** Exponential decay of gradients during BPTT when $\rho(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) < 1$, preventing long-range learning.
- **Exploding Gradient:** Exponential growth of gradients when $\rho(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) > 1$, controlled by gradient clipping.
- **LSTM:** A gated RNN with separate cell and hidden states, using forget/input/output gates to enable long-term memory.
- **GRU:** A simplified LSTM with update and reset gates; merges cell and hidden state for fewer parameters.
- **Bi-RNN:** Two RNNs processing the sequence in opposite directions, with outputs concatenated.
- **Encoder–Decoder:** A two-stage architecture that compresses input to a context vector and generates output from it.
- **Attention:** A mechanism that replaces the fixed context vector with a dynamic weighted sum over encoder states.
- **Information Bottleneck:** Limitation of standard Seq2Seq where all input info must fit in one fixed-size vector.

### High-Yield Mnemonics

- **LSTM gates F-I-O** → **"Forget, Input, Output"** → **"First In, Out"** (in chronological order of computation).
- **GRU gates Z-R** → **"Zorro Resets"** (update gate Z decides how much to interpolate; reset gate R decides how much past to forget).
- **Bi-RNN = "Two heads better than one"** → forward head sees past, backward head sees future.
- **Vanishing vs Exploding** → **"Vanish = 0, Explode = ∞"** → controlled by $\rho(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) \lessgtr 1$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN): Cell Structure and Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN): Cell Structure and Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN): Cell Structure and Architecture

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a class of artificial neural networks designed to recognize patterns in sequences of data, such as time series, speech, text, or video frames, by maintaining an internal **hidden state (memory)** that captures information from previous time steps through recurrent (feedback) connections.

Unlike a feedforward neural network where activations flow strictly in one direction (input $\rightarrow$ hidden $\rightarrow$ output), an RNN contains a **directed cycle** that allows information from one time step to be fed into the next, making the network **stateful** and inherently suitable for **sequential/temporal data**.

### Key Components of an RNN Cell

- **Input vector** $x_t$ at the current time step $t$.
- **Hidden state vector** $h_t$ which acts as the "memory" of the network.
- **Output vector** $y_t$ at time step $t$.
- **Weight matrices** $W_{xh}$, $W_{hh}$, $W_{hy}$ which are **shared across all time steps**.
- **Bias vectors** $b_h$ and $b_y$.
- **Non-linear activation function** (commonly $\tanh$ or $\text{ReLU}$) applied to the hidden state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Weight Sharing Principle:** The same set of weight matrices is reused at every time step. This drastically reduces the number of parameters and allows the RNN to generalize across variable-length sequences.

## 2. Intuitive Analogy: The Storyteller

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Reading a Story Word-by-Word:**
> Imagine reading a novel one word at a time. To understand the current word, you don't just look at it in isolation — your brain automatically recalls the context of all the words you have read before. Your short-term memory holds this evolving context.
>
> In this analogy:
> - **Each word** = Input $x_t$
> - **Your evolving understanding of the plot** = Hidden state $h_t$
> - **Your prediction of the next word** = Output $y_t$
>
> Just as your memory updates with every new word, the RNN updates its hidden state with every new input. If the story becomes too long, your memory may "fade" (this is the **vanishing gradient problem** we will see later).

---

## 3. Why Feedforward Networks Fail for Sequences

A standard feedforward network expects a **fixed-size input vector**. Sequential data has:

- **Variable length** (sentences have different word counts).
- **Temporal dependencies** (the meaning of a word depends on prior words).
- **Order sensitivity** (permuting the sequence changes meaning).

RNNs solve all three problems through their recurrent cell structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Hidden State Dimensionality Rule:** For an input of dimension $d_x$ and hidden state of dimension $d_h$:
> - $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$
> - $W_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$
> - $W_{hy} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y \times d_h}$
> Total trainable parameters in the recurrent layer = $d_h \times (d_x + d_h + d_y) + d_h + d_y$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Operational Concept Breakdown

The RNN cell performs a **two-stage transformation** at every time step:

### Stage A — Hidden State Update (Memory Refresh)

1. **Receive** the current input $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$ and the previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$.
2. **Compute** a pre-activation by combining both inputs through their respective weight matrices.
3. **Apply non-linearity** (typically $\tanh$) to squash values into the range $(-1, 1)$.
4. **Output** the new hidden state $h_t$, which now encodes the history up to time $t$.

### Stage B — Output Computation

1. **Project** the new hidden state $h_t$ through the output weight matrix $W_{hy}$.
2. **Apply** the output activation (e.g., **softmax** for classification, **linear** for regression).
3. **Return** the predicted output $y_t$.

## 2. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Exam Hall — Memorize These!**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Shape |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Hidden state update | $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$ | Core recurrence, $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ |
| 2 | Output vector (raw) | $\hat{y}_t = W_{hy} h_t + b_y$ | Linear projection of hidden state |
| 3 | Output (softmax) | $y_t = \text{softmax}(\hat{y}_t)$ | Probability distribution over classes |
| 4 | Unrolled sequence | $h_t = f_W(h_{t-1}, x_t)$ | Compact recurrence form |
| 5 | Total cross-entropy loss | $L = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} y_t \log(\hat{y}_t)$ | Loss summed over entire sequence |
| 6 | Mean cross-entropy | $L = -\dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} y_t \log(\hat{y}_t)$ | Normalized loss per time step |
| 7 | BPTT gradient on $W_{hh}$ | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \dfrac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \dfrac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$ | Sum of contributions over all $k \leq t$ |
| 8 | Jacobian term | $\dfrac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{k}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \dfrac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$ | Chain product over intermediate steps |
| 9 | Norm bound on Jacobian | $\left\Vert \dfrac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} \right\Vert \leq \Vert W_{hh}\Vert \cdot \Vert \text{diag}(1-\tanh^2)\Vert$ | Controls vanishing/exploding behavior |
| 10 | Gradient clipping | $g \leftarrow \dfrac{\tau \cdot g}{\Vert g\Vert}$ if $\Vert g\Vert > \tau$ | Used to bound exploding gradients |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations form the foundation for every downstream sequential task — speech recognition (DeepSpeech), machine translation (early seq2seq), time-series forecasting in finance and IoT, music generation (Google Magenta), and biomedical signal analysis (ECG/EEG).

## 3. Real-World Significance

- **Natural Language Processing**: Machine translation, sentiment classification, named entity recognition.
- **Speech Systems**: Acoustic modeling, voice activity detection.
- **Finance**: Stock price prediction, algorithmic trading.
- **Healthcare**: ECG arrhythmia detection, patient monitoring.
- **Video Analytics**: Action recognition, frame-by-frame captioning.

---

## 4. Architectural Patterns of RNNs

KTU examiners frequently ask about the **four canonical input-output configurations**:

| Architecture | Input | Output | Example Use Case |
|--------------|-------|--------|------------------|
| **One-to-One** | Single vector | Single vector | Vanilla classification (equivalent to MLP) |
| **One-to-Many** | Single vector | Sequence | Image captioning (one image $\rightarrow$ word sequence) |
| **Many-to-One** | Sequence | Single vector | Sentiment analysis, video classification |
| **Many-to-Many** | Sequence | Sequence | Machine translation, video frame labeling |

> [!WARNING]
> Many students confuse **many-to-many** with encoder-decoder networks. In a many-to-many model, there are two sub-cases:
> - **Synchronous** ($T_x = T_y$): Named entity tagging, video classification per frame.
> - **Asynchronous** ($T_x \neq T_y$): Machine translation where the encoder reads the full sentence before the decoder starts emitting output.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Directive:** Every algebraic transition below is explicitly written out. **No steps have been skipped.**

## 1. Derivation of the Forward Pass Equations

### Step 1 — Pre-Activation of Hidden Layer

We combine the current input $x_t$ and previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ through a linear transformation:

$$a^{(h)}_t = W_{xh} \, x_t + W_{hh} \, h_{t-1} + b_h$$

**Reasoning:** Both $x_t$ and $h_{t-1}$ contribute linearly to the new state. The bias $b_h$ shifts the activation, improving the network's representational capacity. The two weight matrices $W_{xh}$ and $W_{hh}$ have learned roles — $W_{xh}$ extracts features from current input while $W_{hh}$ propagates historical context.

### Step 2 — Non-Linear Activation

We squash the pre-activation using the hyperbolic tangent function:

$$h_t = \tanh(a^{(h)}_t)$$

**Reasoning:** The $\tanh$ function outputs values in $(-1, +1)$, which keeps the hidden state numerically bounded. Without non-linearity, stacking multiple time steps would reduce to a single large linear transformation — losing representational power.

### Step 3 — Output Projection

We project the hidden state to the output space:

$$\hat{y}_t = W_{hy} \, h_t + b_y$$

**Reasoning:** Each hidden state already encodes the prediction-relevant context. We apply a linear projection to obtain raw scores (logits) for each possible output class.

### Step 4 — Probability Distribution (Optional)

For classification tasks, we convert logits to probabilities:

$$y_t = \text{softmax}(\hat{y}_t) = \frac{\exp(\hat{y}_t)}{\sum_{j} \exp(\hat{y}_{t,j})}$$

**Reasoning:** The softmax ensures outputs sum to 1 and can be interpreted as a categorical probability distribution.

## 2. Derivation of the Loss Function (Cross-Entropy over Time)

For a sequence of length $T$ with true labels $\tilde{y}_t$ and predicted probabilities $y_t$:

### Step 1 — Single Time-Step Loss

The cross-entropy loss for a single time step $t$ is defined as:

$$\mathcal{L}_t = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} \tilde{y}_{t,c} \log(y_{t,c})$$

**Reasoning:** Cross-entropy measures the divergence between the true distribution $\tilde{y}_t$ (one-hot) and predicted distribution $y_t$. The negative log penalizes confident wrong predictions heavily.

### Step 2 — Total Sequence Loss

Summing over all time steps:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \tilde{y}_{t,c} \log(y_{t,c})$$

**Reasoning:** Each time step contributes independently to the total error. Summing them gives the cumulative prediction error over the whole sequence.

### Step 3 — Compact Form (One-Hot Vectors)

If labels are one-hot vectors, only the true class contributes:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log(y_{t, \, \tilde{c}_t})$$

where $\tilde{c}_t$ is the index of the true class at time $t$.

## 3. Derivation of Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

### Step 1 — Gradient w.r.t. Output Layer Weights $W_{hy}$

We differentiate the loss at time $t$ with respect to $W_{hy}$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hy}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial W_{hy}}$$

Computing each factor:

- $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} = y_t - \tilde{y}_t$ (standard softmax derivative)
- $\dfrac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial W_{hy}} = h_t^\top$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hy}} = (y_t - \tilde{y}_t) \, h_t^\top$$

Summing across all time steps:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (y_t - \tilde{y}_t) \, h_t^\top$$

### Step 2 — Gradient w.r.t. Recurrent Weight $W_{hh}$

The hidden state at time $t$ depends on $h_{t-1}$, which itself depends on $h_{t-2}$, and so on. By the **chain rule over time**:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

**Reasoning:** Each past hidden state $h_k$ (for $k \leq t$) contributes to the current loss through a chain of intermediate states $h_{k+1}, h_{k+2}, \ldots, h_t$. We must sum contributions from all such $k$.

The intermediate Jacobian is:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \text{diag}\!\big(1 - \tanh^2(a^{(h)}_i)\big) \, W_{hh}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \Big[ \text{diag}\!\big(1 - \tanh^2(a^{(h)}_i)\big) \, W_{hh} \Big]$$

### Step 3 — Vanishing/Exploding Gradient Analysis

Taking the norm of the Jacobian product:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \right\Vert \leq \big\Vert W_{hh} \big\Vert^{(t-k)} \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \big\Vert \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(a^{(h)}_i)) \big\Vert$$

**Reasoning:**
- If $\Vert W_{hh}\Vert < 1$ (or the product of diagonal terms is small), the gradient **decays exponentially** $\rightarrow$ **vanishing gradient**.
- If $\Vert W_{hh}\Vert > 1$, the gradient **grows exponentially** $\rightarrow$ **exploding gradient**.

For typical activations like $\tanh$, the diagonal terms are bounded by 1, so the dominant factor is $\Vert W_{hh}\Vert^{(t-k)}$. Over long sequences where $t - k$ is large, this leads to severe numerical instability.

### Step 4 — Mitigation Strategies

$$\boxed{\,g \;\leftarrow\; \frac{\tau \cdot g}{\Vert g \Vert} \quad \text{if } \Vert g \Vert > \tau\,}$$

This is **gradient clipping**, used to prevent explosion. To combat vanishing gradients, **gated architectures** (LSTM, GRU) replace the simple $\tanh$ cell with multiplicative gates that preserve gradient flow.

---

## 4. Summary of Parameter Counts

| Parameter | Symbol | Shape | Count |
|-----------|--------|-------|-------|
| Input → Hidden | $W_{xh}$ | $d_h \times d_x$ | $d_h \cdot d_x$ |
| Hidden → Hidden | $W_{hh}$ | $d_h \times d_h$ | $d_h^2$ |
| Hidden → Output | $W_{hy}$ | $d_y \times d_h$ | $d_y \cdot d_h$ |
| Hidden bias | $b_h$ | $d_h \times 1$ | $d_h$ |
| Output bias | $b_y$ | $d_y \times 1$ | $d_y$ |
| **Total** | — | — | $d_h(d_x + d_h + d_y) + d_h + d_y$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 1. Recurrent Cell — Compact (Folded) View

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeX["Input x_t"]
    nodeH1["Hidden h_t_minus_1"]
    nodeCell["RNN CELL\nW_xh, W_hh, b_h"]
    nodeH2["Hidden h_t"]
    nodeY["Output y_t\nW_hy, b_y"]

    nodeX --> nodeCell
    nodeH1 --> nodeCell
    nodeCell --> nodeH2
    nodeH1 --> nodeCell
    nodeCell --> nodeY

    style nodeCell fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C5F8D,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style nodeH2 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#C77A00,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **self-loop** on the RNN cell represents the recurrent connection. The same cell is applied at every time step, but the diagram is shown folded for compactness.

## 2. RNN Architecture — Unrolled Through Time

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA["x_t_minus_1"]
    cellA["RNN CELL t_minus_1"]
    hA["h_t_minus_1"]
    yA["y_t_minus_1"]

    stepB["x_t"]
    cellB["RNN CELL t"]
    hB["h_t"]
    yB["y_t"]

    stepC["x_t_plus_1"]
    cellC["RNN CELL t_plus_1"]
    hC["h_t_plus_1"]
    yC["y_t_plus_1"]

    h0["h_0 (init)"]

    stepA --> cellA
    h0 --> cellA
    cellA --> hA
    cellA --> yA

    hA --> cellB
    stepB --> cellB
    cellB --> hB
    cellB --> yB

    hB --> cellC
    stepC --> cellC
    cellC --> hC
    cellC --> yC

    style cellA fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C5F8D,color:#fff
    style cellB fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C5F8D,color:#fff
    style cellC fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C5F8D,color:#fff
    style hA fill:#F5A623,color:#000
    style hB fill:#F5A623,color:#000
    style hC fill:#F5A623,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Unrolled Diagram:** Each **blue box** is the same RNN cell with **identical weight matrices** (weight sharing). The **orange boxes** are hidden states that act as memory carriers, passed forward in time. This is the form used during **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**.

## 3. Block-Level Functional Architecture (Data Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputLayer["INPUT LAYER\nSequence x_1, x_2, ..., x_T"]
    procUnit["PROCESSING UNIT\nLinear transform + tanh"]
```
