```mermaid
    memory["MEMORY BANK\nHidden states h_0, h_1, ..., h_T"]
    outputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER\nPredictions y_1, y_2, ..., y_T"]
    lossBlock["LOSS COMPUTATION\nCross-entropy"]
    bpttBlock["BPTT ENGINE\nGradient propagation in time"]
    optimBlock["OPTIMIZER\nSGD / Adam / RMSprop"]

    inputLayer --> procUnit
    memory --> procUnit
    procUnit --> memory
    procUnit --> outputLayer
    outputLayer --> lossBlock
    lossBlock --> bpttBlock
    bpttBlock --> optimBlock
    optimBlock --> procUnit

    style procUnit fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style memory fill:#F5A623,color:#000
    style bpttBlock fill:#7B68EE,color:#fff
    style optimBlock fill:#50C878,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level View:** This is the architecture view KTU examiners often expect in 14-mark answers. The **Processing Unit** is the recurrent cell, the **Memory Bank** stores hidden states, the **BPTT Engine** unrolls gradients across time, and the **Optimizer** updates the shared weights using the accumulated gradients.

## 4. Comparison: RNN vs. Feedforward Network (Structural)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FF["FEEDFORWARD NETWORK"]
        ff1["Input x"] --> ff2["Hidden Layer"] --> ff3["Output y"]
    end

    subgraph RN["RECURRENT NETWORK (Unrolled)"]
        rn1a["x_1"] --> rn2a["RNN Cell 1"] --> rn3a["y_1"]
        rn1b["x_2"] --> rn2b["RNN Cell 2"] --> rn3b["y_2"]
        rn1c["x_3"] --> rn2c["RNN Cell 3"] --> rn3c["y_3"]
        rn2a --> rn2b
        rn2b --> rn2c
    end

    style FF fill:#FFE4E1
    style RN fill:#E0F7FA
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a Recurrent Neural Network. Why are RNNs preferred over feedforward networks for sequence data?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a neural network architecture with recurrent (feedback) connections that allow information to persist across time steps, making it suitable for processing sequential data.

**Why preferred over feedforward networks:**
- Feedforward networks require **fixed-size inputs**, whereas RNNs can handle **variable-length sequences**.
- Feedforward networks treat each input **independently**, while RNNs maintain a **hidden state** that captures temporal dependencies.
- RNNs use **weight sharing across time steps**, drastically reducing parameters and improving generalization.
- RNNs naturally model **order-sensitive** data (text, speech, time series).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List and briefly explain the different RNN input-output architectures with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Architecture | Input | Output | Example |
|--------------|-------|--------|---------|
| **One-to-One** | Single vector | Single vector | Image classification (vanilla neural net) |
| **One-to-Many** | Single vector | Sequence | Image captioning |
| **Many-to-One** | Sequence | Single vector | Sentiment analysis |
| **Many-to-Many** (synchronous) | Sequence | Sequence | Named entity tagging |
| **Many-to-Many** (asynchronous) | Sequence | Sequence | Machine translation |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

#### (a) Explain the cell structure and architecture of an RNN with a neat diagram. Derive the forward propagation equations for an RNN cell. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Cell Structure [3 marks]:**

An RNN cell consists of:
- **Input vector** $x_t$ at the current time step.
- **Previous hidden state** $h_{t-1}$ from the previous time step.
- **Weight matrices**: $W_{xh}$ (input-to-hidden), $W_{hh}$ (hidden-to-hidden), $W_{hy}$ (hidden-to-output).
- **Bias vectors**: $b_h$, $b_y$.
- **Non-linear activation**: typically $\tanh$.
- **Outputs**: new hidden state $h_t$ and prediction $y_t$.

The same weights are reused at every time step — this is the **weight sharing principle**.

**2. Forward Propagation Equations [3 marks]:**

Step 1: Hidden state pre-activation
$$a^{(h)}_t = W_{xh} \, x_t + W_{hh} \, h_{t-1} + b_h$$

Step 2: Hidden state with non-linearity
$$h_t = \tanh(a^{(h)}_t)$$

Step 3: Output projection
$$\hat{y}_t = W_{hy} \, h_t + b_y$$

Step 4: Probability output (for classification)
$$y_t = \text{softmax}(\hat{y}_t)$$

**3. Architecture [1 mark]:**

Refer to the unrolled RNN diagram in Section 4. The network is **unrolled through time** for a sequence of length $T$, with each time step sharing the same weights.

---

#### (b) Explain Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) algorithm with mathematical formulation. Discuss the vanishing and exploding gradient problems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. BPTT Concept [1 mark]:**

BPTT is the extension of standard backpropagation to recurrent networks. The network is unrolled across all time steps, and gradients are computed by propagating the error backward through time.

**2. Total Loss [1 mark]:**
$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t$$

**3. Gradient w.r.t. $W_{hy}$ [1 mark]:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (y_t - \tilde{y}_t) \, h_t^\top$$

**4. Gradient w.r.t. $W_{hh}$ [2 marks]:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t} \left( \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} \right) \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

where each factor $\dfrac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(a^{(h)}_i)) \, W_{hh}$.

**5. Vanishing/Exploding Gradient Problem [2 marks]:**

The repeated multiplication of Jacobians over many time steps causes the gradient norm to behave as $\Vert W_{hh}\Vert^{(t-k)}$. If $\Vert W_{hh}\Vert < 1$, gradients vanish; if $\Vert W_{hh}\Vert > 1$, gradients explode. Both prevent effective long-term learning.

**Mitigations**: gradient clipping (for explosion), LSTM/GRU architectures (for vanishing).

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of RNN when unrolled through time. Discuss different RNN architectures (one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, many-to-many) with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Unrolled Architecture [3 marks]:**

Refer to the unrolled RNN diagram. The key idea is that the recurrent cell is replicated $T$ times (once per time step), with the hidden state $h_t$ passed forward as input to the next time step. All replicas share the same parameters $(W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy})$.

**2. Architectural Configurations [4 marks]:**

- **One-to-One**: Standard neural network with one input and one output (e.g., MNIST classification).
- **One-to-Many**: Single input, sequence output (e.g., image captioning — input is an image feature vector, output is a sequence of words).
- **Many-to-One**: Sequence input, single output (e.g., sentiment classification — input is a sentence, output is a positive/negative label).
- **Many-to-Many (Synchronous)**: Sequence in, sequence out with same length (e.g., video frame-level action tagging).
- **Many-to-Many (Asynchronous)**: Encoder-decoder model where input length may differ from output length (e.g., machine translation).

---

#### (b) Derive the gradient equations for BPTT and explain how vanishing gradients affect RNN training. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Forward Recap [1 mark]:**
$$h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h), \quad \hat{y}_t = W_{hy} h_t + b_y$$

**2. Output Layer Gradient [1 mark]:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (y_t - \tilde{y}_t) h_t^\top$$

**3. Recurrent Layer Gradient (BPTT) [3 marks]:**

The hidden state at time $t$ depends on all previous states $h_0, h_1, \ldots, h_{t-1}$. By the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

where:
$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \Big[ \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(a^{(h)}_i)) \, W_{hh} \Big]$$

**4. Vanishing Gradient Impact [2 marks]:**

When $\Vert W_{hh}\Vert < 1$, the gradient norm decays exponentially with $t-k$. Early time steps receive negligible gradient updates, meaning the RNN cannot learn **long-term dependencies**. In practice, RNNs struggle to remember information from more than ~10–20 time steps back.

**Solutions**: Gated cells (LSTM, GRU), orthogonal initialization of $W_{hh}$, and skip connections.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Forgetting weight sharing**: The same $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ are reused at every time step. Drawing "different" weights at each time step loses marks.
> - **Confusing $h_t$ with $y_t$**: $h_t$ is the hidden state (memory); $y_t$ is the output. They are different vectors with different dimensions.
> - **Missing activation in forward pass**: Writing $h_t = W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h$ without $\tanh$ is incomplete — partial credit only.
> - **Vanishing gradient equation**: Students often write $\dfrac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = W_{hh}^{t-k}$ — this is **wrong**; the correct form involves $\text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2)$ factors.
> - **Architecture diagrams**: Drawing only the folded RNN without showing unrolling loses at least 2 marks in a 14-mark answer.
> - **Parameter count**: Not stating dimensions of weight matrices is a frequent deduction.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **RNN core idea**: Process sequences by maintaining a recurrent hidden state $h_t$.
- ✅ **Weight sharing** across time steps is the defining feature of RNNs.
- ✅ **Forward pass equations**: $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$, $\hat{y}_t = W_{hy} h_t + b_y$.
- ✅ **Unrolling**: The recurrent cell is replicated $T$ times for a sequence of length $T$.
- ✅ **Four canonical architectures**: One-to-One, One-to-Many, Many-to-One, Many-to-Many.
- ✅ **BPTT** propagates error gradients backward through time using the chain rule.
- ✅ **Vanishing gradient** arises from repeated Jacobian multiplication; norm behaves as $\Vert W_{hh}\Vert^{(t-k)}$.
- ✅ **Exploding gradient** is mitigated by **gradient clipping**; vanishing by **gated cells** (LSTM/GRU).
- ✅ **Cross-entropy loss** summed over all time steps: $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_t \tilde{y}_t \log(y_t)$.
- ✅ **Softmax output** for classification; **linear output** for regression.
- ✅ **Tanh activation** is standard for hidden states; **sigmoid/softmax** for output (classification).
- ✅ **Limitations**: Cannot effectively learn dependencies beyond ~10–20 time steps (vanilla RNN).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Training RNN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Training RNN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Training a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is the iterative optimization procedure by which the network's parameters $\theta = \{W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}, b_h, b_y\}$ are learned by minimizing a sequence-level loss function $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ over a training corpus of variable-length sequences, using a temporal generalization of backpropagation called **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> Training an RNN refers to the process of computing gradients of the loss with respect to every parameter at **every time step** by unrolling the network across the temporal dimension and applying the chain rule of calculus from the final time step $T$ back to the initial time step $t=1$. This is the backbone of sequence modelling in deep learning.

The training objective is:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}^{(n)}(\theta)$$

where $N$ is the number of training sequences and $\mathcal{L}^{(n)}$ is the per-sequence loss.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Student Reading a Story"

Imagine a student reading a long story aloud and trying to summarize it at the end:

- **Forward pass** = the student reads the story **word-by-word**, holding a mental notepad (the **hidden state** $h_t$) of what they have read so far.
- **Loss computation** = at the end, the teacher checks how wrong the student's summary is.
- **Backward pass (BPTT)** = the teacher traces back from the final summary error, telling the student which earlier words they misunderstood the most. This "blame" must travel all the way back to the **first word**.
- **Vanishing gradient** = a whisper that fades with distance — by the time it reaches the first chapters, the student barely hears any correction.
- **Exploding gradient** = a shout that grows louder with each chapter, blowing out the student's eardrums (numerical overflow).
- **Gradient clipping** = capping the volume of the feedback so the student can still learn.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why training RNNs is uniquely hard:**
> Unlike feedforward networks where gradients flow through a fixed depth, in an RNN the **same weight matrices** ($W_{hh}$, $W_{xh}$) are reused at **every time step**. This creates a *deep computation graph along the time axis*, and repeatedly multiplying the same Jacobian matrix produces the famous **vanishing/exploding gradient problem**.

### 1.3 Standard Training Hyperparameters (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $\eta$ (eta) | Learning rate | **1e-3 to 1e-1** |
| $T$ | Sequence length (truncation window for TBPTT) | **20 – 200** |
| $\tau$ | Truncated BPTT step size | **5 – 50** |
| $\delta$ | Gradient clip threshold | **1.0 – 5.0** |
| $\beta_1, \beta_2$ | Adam optimizer moments | **0.9, 0.999** |
| $\lambda$ | Weight decay (L2 regularizer) | **1e-4 to 1e-5** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Phases of Training an RNN

A complete RNN training cycle (one mini-batch) consists of the following logical phases:

1. **Sequence Unrolling** — replicate the RNN cell $T$ times, one per time step, sharing weights across all replicas.
2. **Forward Pass** — propagate input $x_t$ and previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ to compute $h_t$ and output $y_t$ at each step.
3. **Loss Aggregation** — sum (or average) per-step losses $\mathcal{L}_t$ to obtain sequence loss $\mathcal{L}$.
4. **Backward Pass (BPTT)** — apply chain rule backwards from time $T$ to time $1$, accumulating gradients in the **shared** weight matrices.
5. **Parameter Update** — use an optimizer (SGD, RMSProp, Adam) with the accumulated gradients, optionally clipping them first.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Phase

- **Why unroll?** Because BPTT requires a static acyclic graph; the cyclic recurrence $h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$ must be converted into a deep feedforward graph before backpropagation can be applied.
- **Why sum losses across time?** A sequence-level task (e.g., language modelling) accumulates error at every time step — the loss is *additive over time*.
- **Why accumulate gradients?** Because the same weight matrix appears at every unrolled step, the total gradient w.r.t. $W$ is the **sum** of contributions from each step.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for KTU ESE — every equation below is a high-yield question trigger.**

| # | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh}\,x_t + W_{hh}\,h_{t-1} + b_h)$ | Hidden state update |
| 2 | $y_t = \mathrm{softmax}(W_{hy}\,h_t + b_y)$ | Output prediction |
| 3 | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} y_t^{*\top} \log \hat{y}_t$ | Cross-entropy loss over sequence |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hy}}$ | Output layer gradient (sum over time) |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t} = W_{hy}^{\top}\,(\hat{y}_t - y_t^{*}) + \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_k}{\partial h_t}$ | Total error flowing into $h_t$ from future + present |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}\!\left(1 - h_t^{2}\right)\,\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t}$ | Recursive gradient through time |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathrm{diag}\!\left(1 - h_t^{2}\right)\,\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t}\,h_{t-1}^{\top}$ | BPTT accumulated gradient for $W_{hh}$ |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}\!\left(1 - h_i^{2}\right)\,\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t}$ | Full $k$-step Jacobian product |
| 9 | $\Vert g \Vert \leq \delta \quad \text{where } g' = \dfrac{g \cdot \delta}{\max(\delta, \Vert g \Vert)}$ | Gradient norm clipping rule |
| 10 | $\rho(W_{hh}^{\top}) < 1 \;\Rightarrow\;$ vanishing ; $\rho(W_{hh}^{\top}) > 1 \;\Rightarrow\;$ exploding | Spectral radius condition |

> [!NOTE]
> In equations 6–8, the operator $\prod$ denotes a sequence of matrix multiplications along the time dimension. This product is the **mathematical origin** of vanishing/exploding gradients.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Training RNNs (and their gated successors, LSTM/GRU) is the foundation of:
- **Speech recognition** (Google's acoustic models)
- **Machine translation** (pre-Transformer NMT systems)
- **Time-series forecasting** (energy load, stock prices)
- **Bioinformatics** (protein sequence modelling)
- **Music generation** and **handwriting synthesis**

Understanding BPTT and gradient pathologies is **non-negotiable** for any deep learning engineer, as the same principles transfer to modern sequence models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Pass Through an Unrolled RNN

**Given:** Input sequence $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T)$ and initial hidden state $h_0$.

**Step 1 — Initialize hidden state.** Conventionally, $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$ (the zero vector).

**Step 2 — Compute hidden state at time $t=1$.**

$$h_1 = \tanh(W_{xh}\,x_1 + W_{hh}\,h_0 + b_h)$$

*Logic:* Combine the current input $x_1$ with the (zero) previous state, apply an affine transformation, then squash through the hyperbolic tangent activation to keep values in $(-1, 1)$.

**Step 3 — Compute output at $t=1$.**

$$y_1 = \mathrm{softmax}(W_{hy}\,h_1 + b_y)$$

*Logic:* Project hidden state to vocabulary-sized logits and normalize to a probability distribution.

**Step 4 — Repeat for $t = 2, 3, \ldots, T$.**

$$h_t = \tanh(W_{xh}\,x_t + W_{hh}\,h_{t-1} + b_h), \qquad y_t = \mathrm{softmax}(W_{hy}\,h_t + b_y)$$

*Logic:* At every step, the **same** matrices $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ are reused — this is what defines a recurrent network.

### 3.2 Derivation: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

**Goal:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hh}}$ where $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t$.

**Step 1 — Per-step gradient w.r.t. $W_{hy}$.** This is local to each time step:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hy}} = (\hat{y}_t - y_t^{*})\,h_t^{\top}$$

*Logic:* For a softmax + cross-entropy pair, the gradient of the loss w.r.t. the pre-softmax logits is $(\hat{y}_t - y_t^{*})$; chain-ruling through $W_{hy}\,h_t$ yields the outer product with $h_t$.

**Step 2 — Aggregate over all time steps:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (\hat{y}_t - y_t^{*})\,h_t^{\top}$$

*Logic:* The output weight matrix is *shared* in spirit but contributes independently from each time step; sum the per-step gradients.

**Step 3 — Define a recursive "delta" at the hidden state.** Let $\delta_t \equiv \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_t}$. Then:

$$\delta_t = W_{hy}^{\top}\,(\hat{y}_t - y_t^{*}) \;+\; W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}\!\left(1 - h_{t+1}^{2}\right)\,\delta_{t+1}$$

*Logic:* The error signal at $h_t$ comes from two sources — (i) the **direct** output error at time $t$, and (ii) the **future** error propagated backward from $h_{t+1}$ through $W_{hh}$ and the $\tanh$ derivative.

**Step 4 — Recursive unrolling of $\delta_t$.** Expand recursively:

$$\delta_t = W_{hy}^{\top}\,(\hat{y}_t - y_t^{*}) + \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \left[\prod_{i=t+1}^{k} W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}(1 - h_i^{2})\right] W_{hy}^{\top}\,(\hat{y}_k - y_k^{*})$$

*Logic:* Substitute $\delta_{t+1}$ into the recursive formula and continue expanding. The product term is the Jacobian of the state transition, raised to a power equal to the number of time steps between $t$ and $k$.

**Step 5 — Compute the gradient w.r.t. $W_{hh}$.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathrm{diag}\!\left(1 - h_t^{2}\right)\,\delta_t\,h_{t-1}^{\top}$$

*Logic:* Each occurrence of $W_{hh}$ in the unrolled graph at step $t$ receives gradient $\mathrm{diag}(1 - h_t^{2})\,\delta_t\,h_{t-1}^{\top}$. Summing over $t$ accumulates all contributions.

### 3.3 Derivation: Vanishing/Exploding Gradient Theorem

**Given:** The Jacobian of the hidden transition $J_t = W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}(1 - h_t^{2})$.

**Step 1 — Write the $k$-step gradient.** From equation 8 of the formula sheet:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_k} = \left[\prod_{i=k+1}^{T} J_i\right]\,v$$

where $v = W_{hy}^{\top}\,(\hat{y}_T - y_T^{*})$ is a fixed vector.

**Step 2 — Take the spectral norm.** The spectral norm of a product is bounded by the product of spectral norms:

$$\left\Vert \prod_{i=k+1}^{T} J_i \right\Vert_{2} \;\leq\; \prod_{i=k+1}^{T} \Vert J_i \Vert_{2} \;\leq\; \left(\sup_{i}\,\Vert J_i \Vert_{2}\right)^{T-k}$$

**Step 3 — Assume bounded norms.** For simplicity, suppose $\Vert J_i \Vert_{2} \approx \gamma$ for all $i$. Then:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_k} \right\Vert_{2} \;\leq\; \gamma^{\,T-k}\,\Vert v \Vert_{2}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the bound.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \gamma < 1 &\;\Rightarrow\; \gamma^{\,T-k} \to 0 \text{ as } (T-k) \to \infty &&\text{(vanishing gradient)} \\
\text{If } \gamma > 1 &\;\Rightarrow\; \gamma^{\,T-k} \to \infty \text{ as } (T-k) \to \infty &&\text{(exploding gradient)}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* The same factor $\gamma$ is multiplied once per time step. Geometric growth or decay is therefore exponential in the time-gap $(T-k)$ — this is the **mathematical root** of the long-range dependency problem in vanilla RNNs.

### 3.4 Derivation: Gradient Norm Clipping

**Problem:** When $\Vert g \Vert > \delta$, parameter updates become numerically unstable.

**Solution (Pascanu et al., 2013):**

$$g' = \begin{cases} g & \text{if } \Vert g \Vert \leq \delta \\[4pt] \dfrac{g}{\Vert g \Vert}\,\delta & \text{if } \Vert g \Vert > \delta \end{cases}$$

*Logic:* Scale the gradient vector $g$ so that its norm equals exactly $\delta$, preserving direction but bounding magnitude. This is the **standard solution** to exploding gradients and is implemented in PyTorch via `torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_`.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Training Challenges & Solutions

| Challenge | Mathematical Cause | Standard Solution | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vanishing gradient | $\gamma < 1$ in $\gamma^{T-k}$ | LSTM/GRU gating | Hochreiter 1997 |
| Exploding gradient | $\gamma > 1$ in $\gamma^{T-k}$ | Gradient clipping | Pascanu et al. 2013 |
| Long sequences | BPTT memory $\mathcal{O}(T)$ | Truncated BPTT | Williams & Peng 1990 |
| Slow convergence | Shared weight gradients | Optimizer (Adam, RMSProp) | Kingma & Ba 2015 |
| Exposure bias | Train/test mismatch (teacher forcing) | Scheduled sampling | Bengio et al. 2015 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RNN Unrolling & BPTT Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hidden fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    x1[/x1 INPUT/]:::input
    x2[/x2 INPUT/]:::input
    x3[/x3 INPUT/]:::input
    x4[/x4 INPUT/]:::input

    h1[hidden h1]:::hidden
    h2[hidden h2]:::hidden
    h3[hidden h3]:::hidden
    h4[hidden h4]:::hidden

    y1[output y1]:::output
    y2[output y2]:::output
    y3[output y3]:::output
    y4[output y4]:::output

    L1([loss L1]):::loss
    L2([loss L2]):::loss
    L3([loss L3]):::loss
    L4([loss L4]):::loss

    x1 --> h1
    x2 --> h2
    x3 --> h3
    x4 --> h4

    h1 --> h2
    h2 --> h3
    h3 --> h4

    h1 --> y1
    h2 --> y2
    h3 --> y3
    h4 --> y4

    y1 --> L1
    y2 --> L2
    y3 --> L3
    y4 --> L4
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each column is one time step after unrolling. The **horizontal arrows** between hidden states carry the recurrent information forward. During BPTT, gradients travel in the **reverse direction** — from the losses on the right back through the outputs, hidden states, and shared weight matrices.

### 4.2 BPTT Backward Flow (Gradient Propagation)

```mermaid
flowchart RL
    classDef lossBox fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef deltaBox fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef weightBox fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    L_T([TOTAL LOSS L]):::lossBox
    deltaT[delta at hT]:::deltaBox
    deltaT1[delta at hT-1]:::deltaBox
    deltaT2[delta at hT-2]:::deltaBox
    delta1[delta at h1]:::deltaBox

    Wup[Update W_hh, W_xh, W_hy]:::weightBox

    L_T --> deltaT
    deltaT --> deltaT1
    deltaT1 --> deltaT2
    deltaT2 --> delta1
    delta1 --> Wup

    deltaT -.gradient path.-> deltaT1
    deltaT1 -.gradient path.-> deltaT2
    deltaT2 -.gradient path.-> delta1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Gradients flow **right-to-left** along the time axis. The chain rule multiplies Jacobians at every step — this is exactly where vanishing/exploding gradients originate.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RNN Training Step

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef block fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    A[Input Sequence X]:::block
    B[Initialize h0 equals zero]:::process
    C[Unroll RNN for T steps]:::process
    D[Forward Pass compute ht and yt]:::process
    E[Compute Loss L sum over t]:::process
    F{Gradient Norm check}:::decision
    G[Clip Gradient if exceeds delta]:::process
    H[Backpropagation Through Time]:::process
    I[Accumulate Gradients]:::process
    J[Optimizer Step Adam or SGD]:::process
    K[Updated Parameters]:::block

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F -- norm exceeds threshold --> G
    F -- norm within threshold --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

*Define Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT). Why is it required for training RNNs?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) is the training algorithm for Recurrent Neural Networks. It works by **unrolling** the recurrent network across all $T$ time steps of an input sequence, creating a deep feedforward computation graph, and then applying the standard backpropagation algorithm on this unrolled graph to compute gradients of the loss with respect to every parameter at every time step.

**Why required (1 mark):** Standard backpropagation assumes an **acyclic** graph, but the recurrent connection $h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$ creates a cycle. BPTT resolves this by unrolling the cycle into $T$ layers, allowing the chain rule to compute gradients that account for dependencies across time.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**

*State the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs and mention one standard solution.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Problem (2 marks):** During BPTT, the gradient of the loss at time $T$ w.r.t. a hidden state at time $k < T$ involves a product of Jacobian matrices raised to the power $(T-k)$. If the spectral radius of these Jacobians is less than **1**, this product shrinks exponentially with the time gap $(T-k)$, making gradients of early time steps negligibly small. Consequently, the network cannot learn **long-range dependencies** — parameters influencing distant past inputs receive almost no update signal.

**Solution (1 mark):** Use gated architectures such as **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)** or **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)**, which use additive memory cells and gating mechanisms to preserve gradient flow over long time horizons. *(Alternative accepted: orthogonal initialization of $W_{hh}$, or gradient clipping for the exploding case.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** In Module 3, students must answer **one full 14-mark question**. Two alternatives are provided below; answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Derive the backpropagation through time (BPTT) update rule for the recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$ in a vanilla RNN with $\tanh$ activation. Clearly show the recursive gradient relation at the hidden state and the final aggregated update. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the vanishing and exploding gradient problems in RNNs with mathematical justification. Discuss **two** standard techniques to mitigate each problem. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BPTT Derivation for $W_{hh}$ — 7 marks**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Define the per-step hidden update and loss.

$$h_t = \tanh(W_{xh}\,x_t + W_{hh}\,h_{t-1} + b_h), \qquad \hat{y}_t = \mathrm{softmax}(W_{hy}\,h_t + b_y)$$

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t, \qquad \mathcal{L}_t = -\sum_{c} y_{t,c}^{*}\log \hat{y}_{t,c}$$

*Step 2 (1 mark):* Per-step output-gradient (using softmax + cross-entropy simplification):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hy}} = (\hat{y}_t - y_t^{*})\,h_t^{\top}$$

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Define the error signal $\delta_t \equiv \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_t}$ and write the recursion:

$$\delta_t = W_{hy}^{\top}(\hat{y}_t - y_t^{*}) + W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}(1 - h_{t+1}^{2})\,\delta_{t+1}$$

with base case at $t = T$:

$$\delta_T = W_{hy}^{\top}(\hat{y}_T - y_T^{*})$$

*Step 4 (2 marks):* Apply the chain rule to obtain the gradient contribution from time step $t$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \mathrm{diag}(1 - h_t^{2})\,\delta_t\,h_{t-1}^{\top}$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* Aggregate over all time steps (since $W_{hh}$ is reused):

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathrm{diag}(1 - h_t^{2})\,\delta_t\,h_{t-1}^{\top}}$$

---

**(b) Vanishing/Exploding Gradients & Mitigation — 7 marks**

*Mathematical basis (3 marks):* From the recursive formula, expanding $\delta_k$ for $k < T$ yields:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_k} = \left[\prod_{i=k+1}^{T} W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}(1 - h_i^{2})\right]\,W_{hy}^{\top}(\hat{y}_T - y_T^{*})$$

Let $\gamma = \sup_i \Vert W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}(1 - h_i^{2}) \Vert_2$. Then by submultiplicativity:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_k} \right\Vert_2 \leq \gamma^{\,T-k}\,\Vert W_{hy}^{\top}(\hat{y}_T - y_T^{*}) \Vert_2$$

- If $\gamma < 1$ → $\gamma^{T-k} \to 0$ as $(T-k)$ grows → **vanishing gradient**.
- If $\gamma > 1$ → $\gamma^{T-k} \to \infty$ as $(T-k)$ grows → **exploding gradient**.

*Two mitigation techniques for vanishing gradients (2 marks):*
1. **Gated RNNs (LSTM/GRU):** Replace the simple $\tanh$ cell with a cell that has an **additive** memory update, so gradients flow via additions rather than multiplications — preserving magnitude over long time horizons.
2. **Orthogonal initialization:** Initialize $W_{hh}$ as an orthogonal matrix so its spectral norm is exactly 1, keeping $\gamma \approx 1$ initially.

*Two mitigation techniques for exploding gradients (2 marks):*
1. **Gradient norm clipping:** If $\Vert g \Vert > \delta$, rescale to $g' = \dfrac{g}{\Vert g \Vert}\delta$.
2. **Gradient value clipping:** Clip each component of $g$ to a fixed range $[-\delta, \delta]$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the unrolling of a recurrent neural network across time. Explain why weight sharing across time steps is essential. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Truncated Backpropagation Through Time (TBPTT)**. How does it differ from full BPTT in terms of memory, computational cost, and the length of dependencies it can capture? When is TBPTT preferred in practice? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Unrolling of an RNN — 7 marks**

*Diagram description (3 marks):* A recurrent cell $R$ with parameters $(W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}, b_h, b_y)$ is replicated $T$ times to form an unrolled chain:

$$x_1 \to R \to h_1 \to y_1$$
$$x_2 \to R \to h_2 \to y_2 \;\;\;\; \text{(with } h_1 \to h_2\text{)}$$
$$\vdots$$
$$x_T \to R \to h_T \to y_T$$

Each replica uses the **same** weight matrices but operates on a different time step's input and previous hidden state.

*Why weight sharing is essential (4 marks):*
1. **Parameter efficiency:** Without sharing, the network would require separate weights for every time step — making it impossible to generalize to sequence lengths longer than those seen during training.
2. **Translation equivariance in time:** The relationship between $h_{t-1}$ and $h_t$ should be the *same* regardless of position $t$ — sharing enforces this inductive bias.
3. **Generalization to variable-length sequences:** With shared weights, the model can process sequences of any length $T' \leq T_{\text{train}}$ or $T' > T_{\text{train}}$.
4. **Enables BPTT:** The accumulated gradient $\sum_{t=1}^{T} \partial \mathcal{L}_t / \partial W_{hh}$ is meaningful only because the same $W_{hh}$ appears at every step.

---

**(b) Truncated BPTT (TBPTT) — 7 marks**

*Definition (2 marks):* TBPTT limits the backward pass to only the most recent $\tau$ time steps instead of all $T$ steps. The forward pass still runs for the full sequence (or a longer window), but gradients are computed only within a sliding window of length $\tau$.

*Comparison table (3 marks):*

| Aspect | Full BPTT | Truncated BPTT ($\tau \ll T$) |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | $\mathcal{O}(T)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\tau)$ |
| Compute per step | $\mathcal{O}(T)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\tau)$ |
| Max dependency captured | $T$ steps | $\tau$ steps |
| Bias | Unbiased | Biased gradient estimator |

*Practical preference (2 marks):* TBPTT is preferred when:
- Sequence length $T$ is very large (e.g., $T > 500$, as in raw audio or DNA).
- GPU memory is constrained (a common KTU lab scenario).
- Real-time or online learning is needed (e.g., streaming speech).
- The task primarily depends on recent context (e.g., next-character prediction where only the last 30–50 characters matter).

The trade-off: TBPTT may **fail to capture dependencies longer than $\tau$**, but in many practical applications the bulk of predictive signal lies within a recent window.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks in Training-RNN questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the base case of the BPTT recursion** — at $t = T$, the future gradient term $W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}(1 - h_{T+1}^{2})\,\delta_{T+1}$ does **not exist**; this must be stated explicitly or 1 mark is lost.
> 2. **Conflating $\partial \mathcal{L}_t$ with $\partial \mathcal{L}$** — the gradient w.r.t. $W_{hh}$ is a **sum** over all $t$, not a single time step's gradient. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Stating vanishing gradient as "small numbers" without the spectral-radius argument** — a vague answer loses 2 of the 3 marks allocated.
> 4. **Omitting the softmax–cross-entropy simplification** — many textbooks quote $(\hat{y}_t - y_t^{*})$ as the gradient of the logits; showing the derivation earns a bonus mark.
> 5. **Not distinguishing TBPTT from full BPTT in the bias/variance trade-off** — quoting only "TBPTT is faster" without discussing the **truncation bias** is a half-answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Training RNN (Module 3)**

- ☐ **Training RNN = unroll + forward + loss + BPTT + update** — memorize this 5-step pipeline.
- ☐ **BPTT** applies the chain rule across the time axis after unrolling the network into a deep feedforward graph of depth $T$.
- ☐ **Same weights** ($W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$) appear at every unrolled step — gradients must be **accumulated (summed)** across time.
- ☐ **Recursion on $\delta_t$** is the heart of BPTT: $\delta_t = W_{hy}^{\top}(\hat{y}_t - y_t^{*}) + W_{hh}^{\top}\,\mathrm{diag}(1 - h_{t+1}^{2})\,\delta_{t+1}$.
- ☐ **Vanishing gradients** arise when the spectral radius $\rho < 1$; solved by **LSTM/GRU, orthogonal init**.
- ☐ **Exploding gradients** arise when $\rho > 1$; solved by **gradient norm clipping** at threshold $\delta$.
- ☐ **Truncated BPTT (TBPTT)** trades exact gradients for $\mathcal{O}(\tau)$ memory at the cost of bias.
- ☐ **Teacher forcing** during training feeds ground-truth $y_t^{*}$ as next-step input; mitigates exposure bias via **scheduled sampling**.
- ☐ **Optimizers:** Adam (default), RMSProp, SGD with momentum — all common for RNNs.
- ☐ **Numerical hygiene:** Always clip gradients; use $\tanh$ or ReLU carefully; watch for NaN/Inf in long sequences.
- ☐ **Cross-entropy + softmax** shortcut: gradient w.r.t. logits is simply $\hat{y}_t - y_t^{*}$ — saves derivation time in the exam.
- ☐ **Spectral radius $\rho(A) = \max \vert \lambda_i \vert$** governs the geometric growth/decay of $\gamma^{T-k}$ — must be mentioned in any gradient-pathology answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Back-propagation Through Time, Vanishing and Exploding Gradients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Back-propagation Through Time, Vanishing and Exploding Gradients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 – Back-propagation Through Time, Vanishing and Exploding Gradients

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Back-propagation Through Time (BPTT)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
Back-propagation Through Time (BPTT) is the supervised training algorithm used to update the weights of a **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)**. It is mathematically equivalent to applying the standard back-propagation algorithm to an **unrolled (unfolded in time) computational graph** of the RNN, where each time-step is treated as one layer of a deep feed-forward network. The total gradient with respect to any weight is obtained by summing the gradients contributed at every time-step along the temporal chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology:** Examiners expect the phrase *"BPTT is back-propagation applied to the unrolled computation graph of an RNN"* — use this exact phrasing for full marks in Module 3 short-answer questions.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Chain of Witnesses":**
Imagine a rumour passing through 100 people standing in a line. Person 1 whispers to Person 2, who whispers to Person 3, and so on, until Person 100 finally tells the truth (output). Now, to teach them to be accurate, we must trace the rumour back to its origin to identify *who distorted the message*. **BPTT is exactly this retroactive blame assignment across time**. Each person (time-step) gets a "grade" (gradient) indicating how much they contributed to the final error.

### 1.2 Vanishing Gradient Problem

**Formal Definition:**
The Vanishing Gradient Problem in RNNs refers to the phenomenon where the **partial derivative of the loss with respect to early time-step weights becomes exponentially small** (approaches zero) as the number of time-steps grows. This makes it practically impossible for the network to learn **long-range temporal dependencies** because the error signal cannot flow back to update early weights.

### 1.3 Exploding Gradient Problem

**Formal Definition:**
The Exploding Gradient Problem is the dual counterpart of the vanishing gradient. Here, the gradients **grow exponentially** as they propagate backward through time, causing the weight updates to become numerically unstable (overflow, `NaN`, or oscillating loss). This typically manifests as the training loss becoming `NaN` or `Inf` within a few iterations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Recurrent Weight Regime:**
> - If the recurrent weight matrix spectral radius $\rho(W) < 1$ → gradients **vanish**.
> - If $\rho(W) > 1$ → gradients **explode**.
> - Only when $\rho(W) = 1$ (or close) can gradients sustain their magnitude.

**Real-world Analogy — The Volume Knob:**
Think of BPTT like audio being amplified through 100 speakers in series. If the volume knob on each speaker is set slightly below 1 (e.g., 0.9), the final sound becomes **inaudibly quiet** (vanishing). If the knob is above 1 (e.g., 1.1), the sound becomes a **deafening screech** that blows the speakers (exploding). Standard RNNs face this exact instability.

### 1.4 Key Physical & Mathematical Constants in Deep Learning Context

- **Spectral Radius ($\rho$)**: The largest absolute eigenvalue of the recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$. It dictates gradient flow stability.
- **Effective Learning Rate**: A common rule of thumb is to keep $\vert \text{lr} \cdot \rho \vert \leq 1$ for stability.
- **Memory Time-Scale**: Inversely proportional to $\log(1/\rho)$ — defines how far back the RNN can "remember".

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The BPTT Algorithm — Operational Logic

**Step-by-Step Reasoning:**

1. **Forward Pass Through Time:** The input sequence $(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T)$ is fed one time-step at a time. The hidden state is updated using:
   - $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$
2. **Prediction:** At each (or only the final) time-step, an output $\hat{y}_t$ is produced via $\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy} h_t + b_y)$.
3. **Loss Computation:** Total loss $L$ is the sum (or mean) of per-step losses:
   - $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$
4. **Backward Pass Through Time:** The chain rule is applied **backwards from $t = T$ down to $t = 1$**, computing gradients with respect to every parameter at every time-step.
5. **Gradient Accumulation:** Because the same weight matrix $W_{hh}$ is reused at every time-step, the total gradient is the **sum of per-time-step contributions**.
6. **Parameter Update:** Stochastic Gradient Descent (or Adam, RMSProp, etc.) updates the parameters.

### 2.2 The Critical Why — Why Do Gradients Vanish or Explode?

The root cause lies in the **repeated matrix multiplication** during back-propagation. When we apply the chain rule through $T$ time-steps, the gradient term contains:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

The Jacobian $\dfrac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$ is repeatedly multiplied across $t - k$ time-steps. Its eigenvalues raised to a high power **either decay to zero or blow up to infinity**, depending on whether their magnitude is less than or greater than 1.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Hidden State Update | $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$ | $W_{xh}$: input→hidden, $W_{hh}$: hidden→hidden |
| 2 | Output Prediction | $\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy} h_t + b_y)$ | $W_{hy}$: hidden→output |
| 3 | Total Sequence Loss | $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$ | $L_t$: cross-entropy at step $t$ |
| 4 | BPTT Gradient WRT $W_{hh}$ | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \dfrac{\partial L_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \dfrac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial h_t} \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \dfrac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right) \dfrac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$ | Sum of all time-step contributions |
| 5 | Vanishing Condition | $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1 \implies \rho(W_{hh})^{t-k} \to 0$ | $\rho$: spectral radius |
| 6 | Exploding Condition | $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1 \implies \rho(W_{hh})^{t-k} \to \infty$ | Causes `NaN` |
| 7 | Gradient Clipping | $g \leftarrow \dfrac{g \cdot \theta}{\vert g \vert + \epsilon}$ if $\vert g \vert > \theta$ | Threshold $\theta \in [1, 5]$ typically |
| 8 | LSTM Forget Gate | $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | $\sigma$: sigmoid |
| 9 | LSTM Cell State | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | $\odot$: element-wise product |
| 10 | Gated Gradient Term | $\dfrac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t)$ | Bounded in $[0,1]$ — fixes vanishing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Sheet Notice:** Always remember to wrap every equation using `$$ ... $$` with a blank line before and after. The KTU 2024 board examiners specifically deduct marks for inline equations in long derivations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why BPTT & Gradient Stability Matter |
|--------------------|---------------------------------------|
| **Speech Recognition** | Phonemes depend on long audio context (1–2 seconds ≈ 100 time-steps) |
| **Machine Translation** | Sentence meaning depends on long-distance word dependencies |
| **Stock Price Prediction** | Trends unfold over hundreds of trading days |
| **Music Generation** | Melodic motifs reappear after dozens of bars |
| **Medical ECG/EEG Analysis** | Cardiac arrhythmias must be detected across long temporal windows |
| **Autonomous Driving Trajectory Prediction** | Vehicles must anticipate maneuvers several seconds ahead |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of BPTT Gradient

**Setup:** Consider an RNN with a single hidden layer, sequence length $T$, and loss:
$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$$
where each $L_t = -\sum_i y_{t,i} \log(\hat{y}_{t,i})$ (cross-entropy).

**Forward Equations Recap:**
$$h_t = \tanh(a_t), \quad a_t = W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h$$
$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(o_t), \quad o_t = W_{hy} h_t + b_y$$

**Derivation — Step 1: Gradient with respect to output layer weights $W_{hy}$**

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial o_t} = \hat{y}_t - y_t \equiv \delta_{o,t}$$

This is the standard softmax + cross-entropy derivative. For a multi-step loss:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial o_t} \cdot \frac{\partial o_t}{\partial W_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (\hat{y}_t - y_t) \otimes h_t$$

where $\otimes$ denotes the outer product. **Conversion Logic:** The chain rule expands the composite derivative, and because $o_t = W_{hy} h_t + b_y$, the partial derivative of $o_t$ w.r.t. $W_{hy}$ is the transposed hidden state vector $h_t^T$ acting on the left.

**Derivation — Step 2: Gradient flowing into the hidden state**

Define $\delta_{h,t} = \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial h_t}$ (total gradient arriving at hidden state $h_t$ from both the present output and all future steps).

For $t = T$, only the immediate output contributes:

$$\delta_{h,T} = \frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_T} = W_{hy}^{\top} (\hat{y}_T - y_T)$$

For $1 \leq t < T$, the hidden state $h_t$ influences both $\hat{y}_t$ and $h_{t+1}$:

$$\delta_{h,t} = W_{hy}^{\top} (\hat{y}_t - y_t) + W_{hh}^{\top} \, \delta_{h,t+1} \odot \tanh'(a_{t+1})$$

**Conversion Logic:** The first term is the direct contribution from output at step $t$; the second term is the recursive contribution propagated from the future, scaled by the Jacobian of the hidden transition.

**Derivation — Step 3: Full gradient with respect to $W_{hh}$**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_{h,t} \otimes \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

Since $\dfrac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$ involves all prior hidden states, expanding completely:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \delta_{h,t} \cdot \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

The Jacobian matrix is:

$$\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(a_j)\right) \cdot W_{hh}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The $\tanh$ derivative supplies the diagonal factor, and the recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$ multiplies on the right.

**Derivation — Step 4: Establishing Vanishing/Exploding Condition**

Let $\lambda_{\max}$ denote the largest eigenvalue (in absolute value) of $W_{hh}$. The diagonal factor has entries bounded in $[0, 1]$, so we upper-bound its spectral norm by 1. The spectral norm of the Jacobian product over $n$ time-steps is bounded by:

$$\left\Vert \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right\Vert_2 \leq \vert \lambda_{\max} \vert^{t-k}$$

Taking the long-time limit $t - k \to \infty$:

- If $\vert \lambda_{\max} \vert < 1$ → product $\to 0$ → **Vanishing Gradient**.
- If $\vert \lambda_{\max} \vert > 1$ → product $\to \infty$ → **Exploding Gradient**.

This is the **mathematical proof** of the KTU board's most-asked Module 3 result.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Vanishing Detection

Suppose $W_{hh} = 0.5$ (scalar for simplicity), sequence length $T = 50$, initial gradient magnitude $\delta_{h,T} = 1.0$.

Gradient contribution reaching time-step $k = 1$ from time-step $T$:

$$\text{Gradient} = 0.5^{T-1} = 0.5^{49} \approx 1.78 \times 10^{-15}$$

This value is **smaller than IEEE 754 single-precision machine epsilon** ($1.19 \times 10^{-7}$) — the update becomes numerically zero. **The early time-step effectively stops learning.**

Conversely, if $W_{hh} = 1.5$:

$$\text{Gradient} = 1.5^{49} \approx 1.65 \times 10^{9}$$

This overflows single-precision floating-point range ($3.4 \times 10^{38}$) only at very large $T$, but quickly destabilizes training.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mitigation Strategies Table

| # | Problem | Mitigation Strategy | Mechanism |
|---|---------|---------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Vanishing | **LSTM / GRU** | Gated additive cell-state path keeps gradient near 1 |
| 2 | Vanishing | **Orthogonal Initialization** | $\rho(W_{hh}) = 1$ exactly |
| 3 | Vanishing | **Layer Normalization** | Stabilizes hidden-state scale |
| 4 | Vanishing | **ReLU activations** | Constant derivative 1 (no decay) |
| 5 | Vanishing | **Skip / Residual Connections** | Highway gradient pathway |
| 6 | Exploding | **Gradient Clipping** | Hard threshold on $\Vert g \Vert$ |
| 7 | Exploding | **Truncated BPTT** | Limit backprop window to $\tau$ steps |
| 8 | Exploding | **Weight Regularization** | Penalize large $\|W_{hh}\|$ |
| 9 | Exploding | **Small Init / Identity Init** | Start near stable fixed point |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — RNN Unrolling (BPTT Computational Graph)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputX1["x_1"] --> nodeA1["h_1"]
    inputX2["x_2"] --> nodeA2["h_2"]
    inputX3["x_3"] --> nodeA3["h_3"]
    inputX4["x_4"] --> nodeA4["h_4"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4
    nodeA1 --> outY1["y_1"]
    nodeA2 --> outY2["y_2"]
    nodeA3 --> outY3["y_3"]
    nodeA4 --> outY4["y_4"]
    outY4 --> lossBlock["Loss L"]
    lossBlock -.->|BPTT Reverse| nodeA4
    nodeA4 -.->|BPTT Reverse| nodeA3
    nodeA3 -.->|BPTT Reverse| nodeA2
    nodeA2 -.->|BPTT Reverse| nodeA1
    style lossBlock fill:#ffd1d1,stroke:#cc0000
    style nodeA1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style nodeA2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style nodeA3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style nodeA4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Gradient Flow Stability Across Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["RNN Plain"] --> decisionA{"Spectral Radius rho"}
    decisionA -->|"rho less than 1"| vanishA["Gradient Vanishes"]
    decisionA -->|"rho greater than 1"| explodeA["Gradient Explodes"]
    decisionA -->|"rho equals 1"| stableA["Stable but hard to maintain"]
    startB["LSTM with Forget Gate"] --> gateB["Forget Gate f_t in 0 to 1"]
    gateB --> cellState["Cell State C_t approx C_{t-1}"]
    cellState --> resultB["Gradient Preserved"]
    startC["GRU with Update Gate"] --> gateC["Update Gate z_t"]
    gateC --> resultC["Gradient Smoothed"]
    style vanishA fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style explodeA fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style stableA fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style resultB fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style resultC fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of BPTT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    seqInput["Input Sequence x1 to xT"] --> fwdBlock["Forward Pass Unrolled"]
    fwdBlock --> hiddenLayer["Hidden States h1 h2 ... hT"]
    hiddenLayer --> outPred["Predictions y_hat_1 to y_hat_T"]
    outPred --> lossCalc["Compute Loss L"]
    lossCalc --> bpttCore["BPTT Backward Pass"]
    bpttCore --> gradAccum["Gradient Accumulation across Time"]
    gradAccum --> clipCheck{"Clipping Needed"}
    clipCheck -->|"Yes"| clipOp["Apply Gradient Clipping"]
    clipCheck -->|"No"| updOp["Apply Weight Update"]
    clipOp --> updOp
    updOp --> fwdBlock
    style bpttCore fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style clipCheck fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825
    style updOp fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

What is Back-propagation Through Time (BPTT)? Explain in two sentences how it relates to the unrolled computation graph of an RNN.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
BPTT is the gradient-based learning algorithm used to train Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) by applying the back-propagation algorithm on the RNN **unrolled across all time-steps**. The RNN is "unrolled" by replicating the network $T$ times (once per time-step), creating a deep feed-forward-like computational graph, after which standard chain-rule differentiation is applied from the final loss backward to time-step 1. The same recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$ is shared across all unrolled copies, and its total gradient is the **sum of contributions from every time-step**.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for unrolling concept + 1 mark for shared weight accumulation.)*

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

State the mathematical condition under which the vanishing gradient problem occurs in a simple RNN. What is the role of the spectral radius $\rho$?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The vanishing gradient problem occurs when the **spectral radius of the recurrent weight matrix** $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$. Because the gradient flowing back through $t - k$ time-steps is bounded above by $\rho(W_{hh})^{t-k}$, repeated multiplication causes the gradient to decay **exponentially toward zero**, making early time-step weights impossible to update. The spectral radius $\rho$ is the **largest absolute eigenvalue** of $W_{hh}$ and serves as the multiplier that determines whether gradients vanish, explode, or remain stable.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for condition $\rho < 1$ + 1 mark for exponential decay explanation + 1 mark for spectral radius definition.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the BPTT gradient $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}}$ for an RNN with sequence length $T$, showing the role of the chain rule and the shared recurrent weight. Clearly state the Jacobian term and its significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the vanishing and exploding gradient problems in RNNs using the spectral radius $\rho(W_{hh})$. For $W_{hh} = 0.8$ and $T = 20$, compute the relative gradient magnitude reaching time-step 1. Mention two mitigation techniques. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation of $\partial L / \partial W_{hh}$ — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Forward Pass Setup (1 Mark):**
$$h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$$
$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy} h_t + b_y), \quad L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$$

**Step 2 — Error at Output Layer (1 Mark):**
$$\delta_{o,t} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial o_t} = \hat{y}_t - y_t$$

**Step 3 — Recursive Hidden Gradient (2 Marks):**
$$\delta_{h,t} = W_{hy}^{\top} \delta_{o,t} + W_{hh}^{\top} \, \delta_{h,t+1} \odot \tanh'(a_{t+1})$$

**Step 4 — Full BPTT Gradient via Chain Rule (2 Marks):**
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \delta_{h,t} \cdot \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

The Jacobian term $\dfrac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(a_j)) W_{hh}$ governs the multiplicative scaling per time-step. The same $W_{hh}$ appears at every time-step, so total gradient is a **sum** of all contributions.

**Conversion Logic Notes:**
- The chain rule propagates error backward through each unrolled copy.
- The product term encodes the "memory" — how much gradient survives from step $t$ to step $k$.

**(b) Spectral Radius Analysis & Computation — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Spectral Radius Definition (1 Mark):**
$\rho(W_{hh}) = \max_i \vert \lambda_i \vert$, where $\lambda_i$ are eigenvalues.

**Step 2 — Vanishing / Exploding Conditions (2 Marks):**
- $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1 \implies$ gradient decays exponentially → **Vanishing**.
- $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1 \implies$ gradient grows exponentially → **Exploding**.

**Step 3 — Numerical Computation (3 Marks):**
Given $W_{hh} = 0.8$, $T = 20$, target gradient magnitude reaching $k = 1$:
$$\text{Relative Magnitude} = \rho(W_{hh})^{T-1} = 0.8^{19}$$

Computing step by step:
$$0.8^2 = 0.64$$
$$0.8^4 = 0.4096$$
$$0.8^8 = 0.16777$$
$$0.8^{16} = 0.02815$$
$$0.8^{19} = 0.8^{16} \times 0.8^2 \times 0.8 = 0.02815 \times 0.64 \times 0.8 = 0.01441$$

**Result:** The gradient shrinks to **~1.44 % of its original magnitude** after 19 time-steps — a clear demonstration of vanishing.

**Step 4 — Two Mitigation Techniques (1 Mark):**
1. **LSTM / GRU Architecture** — gated additive cell-state paths keep gradient flow stable.
2. **Gradient Clipping** — caps the norm of exploding gradients to a threshold $\theta$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the unrolled computational graph of an RNN for $T = 4$ time-steps. Explain how BPTT propagates errors backward through this graph. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare vanishing and exploding gradient problems in RNNs in a structured table. Explain how LSTM solves the vanishing gradient problem mathematically, writing the relevant equations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Unrolled RNN & BPTT Backward Pass — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Unrolled Diagram (3 Marks):**

A standard RNN is described by the recurrence $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$. To apply back-propagation, we "unroll" the network:

```
x_1 → [RNN Cell] → h_1 → y_1
                              ↓
x_2 → [RNN Cell] → h_2 → y_2
                              ↓
x_3 → [RNN Cell] → h_3 → y_3
                              ↓
x_4 → [RNN Cell] → h_4 → y_4
```

Each `[RNN Cell]` is the same function with **shared parameters** $W_{xh}$, $W_{hh}$, $W_{hy}$. Horizontal arrows denote the recurrent connection $h_{t-1} \to h_t$ (weight $W_{hh}$).

**Step 2 — Forward Pass Explanation (1 Mark):**
Inputs $x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4$ are processed sequentially; each cell outputs a hidden state $h_t$ and prediction $y_t$. Final loss $L = \sum_{t=1}^{4} L_t$.

**Step 3 — Backward BPTT Pass Explanation (3 Marks):**
- Compute $\delta_{h,4}$ from final loss $L_4$.
- Recursively compute $\delta_{h,t}$ for $t = 3, 2, 1$ using the chain rule across the recurrent edges.
- Sum the per-time-step contributions to obtain the final gradient $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}}$.
- The same error signal flows along **horizontal recurrent arrows in reverse**, hence the name "Through Time".

**(b) Comparison Table & LSTM Math — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Vanishing Gradient | Exploding Gradient |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Gradient Behavior | $\to 0$ exponentially | $\to \infty$ exponentially |
| Spectral Radius | $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$ | $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1$ |
| Learning Impact | Early time-steps stop learning | Loss becomes `NaN`, instability |
| Typical Symptom | Plateau of training loss | Sudden loss spike or overflow |
| Primary Fix | LSTM, GRU, Orthogonal Init | Gradient Clipping, Truncated BPTT |
| Frequency in Practice | More common | Easier to detect & fix |

**Step 2 — LSTM Mathematical Formulation (3 Marks):**

LSTM replaces simple hidden states with a **cell state** $C_t$ that has an additive update path:
$$f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$$
$$i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$$
$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$$
$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$
$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t), \quad o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$$

The key gradient term becomes:
$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t)$$

Because $f_t \in [0, 1]$, the gradient magnitude is bounded between 0 and 1 — preventing exponential decay. The **additive (rather than multiplicative) update** preserves gradient flow across many time-steps.

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 Mark):**
LSTM effectively solves the vanishing gradient problem by creating a "gradient highway" through the cell state, gated by the forget gate $f_t$.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to sum over time-steps** — Many students write only the gradient for a single time-step instead of summing across all $t = 1, \ldots, T$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Confusing $\tanh$ derivative with sigmoid derivative** — The factor $1 - \tanh^2(a_j)$ is specific to the hidden activation. Using $\sigma(1-\sigma)$ loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Not mentioning shared weights** — Examiners explicitly look for the phrase *"same weight matrix shared across time-steps"* in BPTT derivations.
> 4. **Missing the spectral radius** — Vanishing/exploding problems must be tied to $\rho(W_{hh})$, not just "small" or "large" weights.
> 5. **Skipping numerical computation** — Question (b) of Question A demands an explicit numerical answer; showing only the formula $\rho^{T-1}$ without substituting values forfeits the **3-mark computation portion**.
> 6. **Forgetting gradient clipping threshold notation** — Always denote $\theta$ explicitly and write the norm-based clipping equation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BPTT** = Back-propagation applied to the **unrolled** computational graph of an RNN.
- **Unrolling** means replicating the RNN cell $T$ times with **shared weights** $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$.
- **Total gradient** $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} (\cdots) \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \dfrac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} (\cdots)$ — the nested product is the source of gradient instability.
- **Spectral Radius** $\rho(W_{hh}) = \max_i \vert \lambda_i \vert$ governs gradient flow:
  - $\rho < 1$ → **Vanishing**
  - $\rho > 1$ → **Exploding**
  - $\rho = 1$ → Stable (rare in practice)
- **Vanishing gradient** prevents learning long-range dependencies; early time-step weights receive near-zero updates.
- **Exploding gradient** causes numerical overflow, `NaN` loss, and training divergence.
- **LSTM** solves vanishing gradients via the additive cell-state update $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$, with Jacobian $\text{diag}(f_t)$ bounded in $[0,1]$.
- **Gradient Clipping** solves exploding gradients via $g \leftarrow g \cdot \min(1, \theta / \Vert g \Vert)$.
- **Truncated BPTT** limits back-propagation to $\tau$ steps, balancing compute cost and dependency length.
- **Orthogonal Initialization** ensures $\rho(W_{hh}) = 1$ at initialization, improving short-term stability.
- **KTU Board Watch-list:** Always include the spectral radius equation, the BPTT double sum, and a numerical example in long-answer solutions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM): Architecture and Training
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM): Architecture and Training

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** is a specialized variant of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture introduced by *Sepp Hochreiter and Jürgen Schmidhuber (1997)*, specifically engineered to overcome the **Vanishing and Exploding Gradient Problem** inherent in standard RNNs during Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEECT742 - Module 3):**
> *LSTM is a gated recurrent architecture that uses a memory cell regulated by three multiplicative gate units — the **forget gate**, **input gate**, and **output gate** — to selectively retain, write, and read information across long temporal sequences.*

The architecture introduces two state vectors propagating through time:
- **Cell State ($C_t$)**: The long-term memory conveyor belt that carries information across the entire sequence with minimal linear transformations.
- **Hidden State ($h_t$)**: The short-term working output exposed to the next time step and used for predictions.

> [!NOTE]
> The **cell state** is the defining innovation of LSTMs. Unlike standard RNNs where the hidden state is the only memory carrier, LSTMs explicitly separate **long-term memory (cell state)** from **short-term output (hidden state)**, allowing gradient flow to remain stable over hundreds of time steps.

## 1.2 The Problem LSTM Solves — Vanishing Gradients in RNNs

In a vanilla RNN, the hidden state update is:

$$h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t + b_h)$$

During BPTT, the gradient with respect to an early time step $k$ involves repeated multiplication by the same Jacobian matrix $W_{hh}$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$$

If the largest singular value of $W_{hh}$ is **less than 1**, gradients shrink **exponentially** to zero (**vanishing**). If **greater than 1**, they explode. LSTM solves this by replacing the multiplicative recurrence with an **additive update** governed by gated control.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The LSTM as a "Smart Office Whiteboard"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition: Imagine an LSTM cell as a corporate meeting room with a whiteboard.**

| LSTM Component | Whiteboard Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell State $C_t$** | The whiteboard itself | Long-term persistent memory that can be read, written, or erased |
| **Forget Gate $f_t$** | The eraser decision | Decides what old information to discard from the whiteboard |
| **Input Gate $i_t$** | The pen approval | Decides what new information is allowed to be written |
| **Candidate $\tilde{C}_t$** | The drafted notes | New information being proposed to add to the whiteboard |
| **Output Gate $o_t$** | The projector curtain | Decides what portion of the whiteboard is shown to the next meeting |
| **Hidden State $h_t$** | The displayed slide | The filtered, exposed output of this time step |

Just as a well-managed whiteboard retains critical information for future meetings while discarding irrelevant clutter, an LSTM cell **selectively preserves relevant context** across arbitrarily long sequences.

## 1.4 Why LSTM is Critical in Deep Learning Engineering

- **Sequential Modeling**: Speech recognition, machine translation, time-series forecasting.
- **Long-Range Dependencies**: Stock price prediction, music generation, video captioning.
- **Foundation for Modern Architectures**: Inspired GRU, stacked LSTMs, encoder–decoder LSTMs, and attention-augmented seq2seq models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** Standard RNNs can retain dependencies for **~10–20 time steps**, while LSTMs can practically retain them for **hundreds of time steps** under proper initialization and gating.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Gates — Operational Logic

LSTM uses **three sigmoid gates** (output range 0 to 1) that act as **continuous, differentiable masks** over information flow. Each gate takes the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ and current input $x_t$, concatenates them, applies a linear transformation, and passes through a sigmoid activation.

### 2.1.1 The Forget Gate ($f_t$)

**Purpose:** Decides what fraction of the *previous cell state* should be retained.

$$f_t = \sigma\!\left(W_f \cdot \big[h_{t-1},\, x_t\big] + b_f\right)$$

- $f_t = 1$ → Keep everything from $C_{t-1}$.
- $f_t = 0$ → Erase everything from $C_{t-1}$.
- $f_t \in (0,1)$ → Selective forgetting.

**Why it works:** The Hadamard product $f_t \odot C_{t-1}$ allows the network to *learn* the optimal forgetting ratio per dimension.

### 2.1.2 The Input Gate ($i_t$) and Candidate Cell State ($\tilde{C}_t$)

**Purpose:** Decides what *new information* to store in the cell state.

$$i_t = \sigma\!\left(W_i \cdot \big[h_{t-1},\, x_t\big] + b_i\right)$$

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh\!\left(W_C \cdot \big[h_{t-1},\, x_t\big] + b_C\right)$$

- $i_t$ acts as a *gatekeeper* (a sigmoid mask).
- $\tilde{C}_t$ is the *content* (a tanh-bounded candidate, range $-1$ to $+1$).
- Their product $i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ is the **new memory** added to the cell.

### 2.1.3 Cell State Update (The Core Additive Recurrence)

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} \;+\; i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

This is the **vanishing-gradient fix**: instead of multiplying by $W_{hh}$ at every step, the gradient of $C_t$ with respect to $C_{t-1}$ is **purely $f_t$** (a vector of learnable values bounded in $[0,1]$). The gradient highway remains open.

### 2.1.4 The Output Gate ($o_t$)

**Purpose:** Decides what portion of the *cell state* becomes the next hidden state.

$$o_t = \sigma\!\left(W_o \cdot \big[h_{t-1},\, x_t\big] + b_o\right)$$

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

- $\tanh(C_t)$ squashes the cell state to $[-1, 1]$.
- $o_t$ filters which values are exposed as the working output.

## 2.2 Peephole Connections (Optional Variant)

Some LSTM variants allow gates to "peek" at the cell state:

$$f_t = \sigma\!\left(W_f \cdot \big[h_{t-1},\, x_t\big] + b_f\right) \quad \text{(standard)}$$

$$f_t^{\text{peephole}} = \sigma\!\left(W_f \cdot \big[h_{t-1},\, x_t\big] + V_f \odot C_{t-1} + b_f\right)$$

This allows precise timing control (e.g., counting events). KTU students should know peepholes as a **notable architectural extension**, not a default.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\mid` in tables to avoid markdown table-breaking pipes. The following table is the **exam-ready quick reference**.

| Symbol | Meaning | Shape / Dimension | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x_t$ | Input vector at time $t$ | $(n \times 1)$ | $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| $h_{t-1}$ | Previous hidden state | $(m \times 1)$ | $\mathbb{R}^m$ |
| $C_{t-1}$ | Previous cell state | $(m \times 1)$ | $\mathbb{R}^m$ |
| $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$ | Gate weight matrices | $(m \times (m+n))$ | Trainable |
| $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o$ | Gate bias vectors | $(m \times 1)$ | Trainable |
| $f_t$ | Forget gate activation | $(m \times 1)$ | $[0,1]^m$ |
| $i_t$ | Input gate activation | $(m \times 1)$ | $[0,1]^m$ |
| $o_t$ | Output gate activation | $(m \times 1)$ | $[0,1]^m$ |
| $\tilde{C}_t$ | Candidate cell state | $(m \times 1)$ | $[-1,1]^m$ |
| $C_t$ | Updated cell state | $(m \times 1)$ | $\mathbb{R}^m$ |
| $h_t$ | Updated hidden state | $(m \times 1)$ | $[-1,1]^m$ |
| $\sigma(\cdot)$ | Sigmoid function | scalar | $[0,1]$ |
| $\tanh(\cdot)$ | Hyperbolic tangent | scalar | $[-1,1]$ |
| $\odot$ | Hadamard (element-wise) product | $(m \times 1)$ | — |

**Compact LSTM Block (Exam-Revision Form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \sigma\!\left(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f\right) \\
i_t &= \sigma\!\left(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i\right) \\
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh\!\left(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C\right) \\
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \\
o_t &= \sigma\!\left(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o\right) \\
h_t &= o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

LSTMs power real-world systems including:
- **Google Translate** (encoder–decoder LSTM seq2seq models, pre-Transformer era).
- **Apple Siri / Amazon Alexa** (speech recognition acoustic models).
- **Stock Forecasting** (multi-variate time series at hedge funds).
- **Medical ECG/EEG Analysis** (irregular long-sequence physiological signals).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall Mnemonic:** **"FICO"** — **F**orget, **I**nput, **C**ell, **O**utput. Always walk through these four stages in order at every time step.

## 2.5 Training LSTMs — High-Level Pipeline

1. **Forward Pass**: Sequentially compute $f_t, i_t, \tilde{C}_t, C_t, o_t, h_t$ for $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$.
2. **Loss Computation**: Sum of per-time-step losses (e.g., Cross-Entropy) $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$.
3. **Backward Pass (BPTT)**: Unroll the network and apply chain rule across all time steps.
4. **Gradient Clipping**: Clip gradient norm to a threshold (e.g., 5) to prevent explosions.
5. **Parameter Update**: Apply gradient descent (SGD, Adam, RMSProp).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Forward Pass — Concatenation and Linear Projection

Let us define the **concatenated input** at time $t$ as:

$$z_t = \begin{bmatrix} h_{t-1} \\ x_t \end{bmatrix}, \quad z_t \in \mathbb{R}^{(m+n) \times 1}$$

The weight matrices $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$ are row-stacked equivalents of $W_{fh}, W_{fx}$:

$$W_f = \begin{bmatrix} W_{fh} \mid W_{fx} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (m+n)}$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_f z_t &= \begin{bmatrix} W_{fh} \mid W_{fx} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} h_{t-1} \\ x_t \end{bmatrix} \\
&= W_{fh} h_{t-1} + W_{fx} x_t
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves that $W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]$ is mathematically equivalent to two separate affine transforms summed. KTU may ask students to **expand the concatenated form into separated hidden/input weights**.

## 3.2 Full Forward Pass Derivation (One Time Step)

Given the input $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and previous states $h_{t-1}, C_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$:

**Step 1 — Forget Gate Activation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{net}_f &= W_{fh} h_{t-1} + W_{fx} x_t + b_f \\
f_t &= \sigma(\text{net}_f) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\text{net}_f}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Input Gate Activation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{net}_i &= W_{ih} h_{t-1} + W_{ix} x_t + b_i \\
i_t &= \sigma(\text{net}_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Candidate Cell State:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{net}_C &= W_{Ch} h_{t-1} + W_{Cx} x_t + b_C \\
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh(\text{net}_C) = \frac{e^{\text{net}_C} - e^{-\text{net}_C}}{e^{\text{net}_C} + e^{-\text{net}_C}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — New Cell State (Additive Update):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \\
\text{(component-wise)} \quad C_t^{(k)} &= f_t^{(k)} C_{t-1}^{(k)} + i_t^{(k)} \tilde{C}_t^{(k)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Output Gate Activation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{net}_o &= W_{oh} h_{t-1} + W_{ox} x_t + b_o \\
o_t &= \sigma(\text{net}_o)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Hidden State Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_t &= o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) for LSTM

Let $L_t$ be the loss at time $t$ and $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$ the total loss. We derive the gradient of $L$ with respect to $C_{t-1}$ — this is the **key derivative** for understanding vanishing-gradient mitigation.

### 3.3.1 Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Cell State $C_t$

By the chain rule, the gradient flowing into $C_t$ from the loss at $t$ and from future time steps is:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_t} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial C_t} + \frac{\partial L_{t+1}}{\partial C_t}
$$

The first term is the direct local contribution; the second is from $C_{t+1}$ which depends on $C_t$:

$$\frac{\partial C_{t+1}}{\partial C_t} = \text{diag}(f_{t+1})$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_t} &= \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial C_t} + \frac{\partial L_{t+1}}{\partial C_{t+1}} \cdot \frac{\partial C_{t+1}}{\partial C_t} \\
&= \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial C_t} + \left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_{t+1}}\right) \cdot \text{diag}(f_{t+1})
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.2 Recursive Unrolling — The Gradient Highway

Unrolling recursively back to $C_k$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_k} = \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial C_k} + \sum_{j=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_j}{\partial C_j} \prod_{i=k+1}^{j} \text{diag}(f_i)
$$

**Critical Insight:** Unlike vanilla RNN, the product involves only the **forget gate activations $f_i$**, not $W_{hh}^T$. Since $f_i \in [0,1]$, the gradient is bounded but the *learned* forget gates can keep values near 1, allowing the gradient to **flow unimpeded** over long ranges.

### 3.3.3 Gradient w.r.t. Forget Gate Parameters

We need $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_f}$. Applying the chain rule through the forget gate's contribution to $C_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_f} &= \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L}{\partial C_t} \cdot \frac{\partial C_t}{\partial f_t} \cdot \frac{\partial f_t}{\partial \text{net}_f} \cdot \frac{\partial \text{net}_f}{\partial W_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

The four components are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial f_t} &= \text{diag}(C_{t-1}) \\
\frac{\partial f_t}{\partial \text{net}_f} &= \text{diag}\!\left(f_t \odot (1 - f_t)\right) \\
\frac{\partial \text{net}_f}{\partial W_f} &= [h_{t-1}, x_t]^\top
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_f} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_{C,t} \odot C_{t-1} \odot f_t \odot (1 - f_t) \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]^\top
$$

where $\delta_{C,t} = \partial L / \partial C_t$ is the upstream cell-state gradient.

### 3.3.4 Gradient w.r.t. Input Gate, Candidate, and Output Gate (Summary)

By analogous derivations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_i} &= \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_{C,t} \odot \tilde{C}_t \odot i_t \odot (1 - i_t) \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]^\top \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_C} &= \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_{C,t} \odot i_t \odot \big(1 - \tilde{C}_t^2\big) \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]^\top \\
