\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_o} &= \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_{h,t} \odot \tanh(C_t) \odot o_t \odot (1 - o_t) \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t]^\top
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\delta_{h,t} = \partial L / \partial h_t$ is the hidden-state gradient.

## 3.4 Gradient Clipping — A Mandatory Practice

Even with LSTM, gradient **explosion** is possible when $f_t$ values approach 1 over long sequences. The standard remedy is **norm-based clipping**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{g} &= \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta} \\
\text{if } \Vert \hat{g} \Vert_2 > c: \quad \hat{g} &\leftarrow \frac{c}{\Vert \hat{g} \Vert_2} \hat{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

A typical threshold is $c = 5$. The parameters $\theta$ are then updated via the chosen optimizer.

## 3.5 Training Algorithm (Pseudocode in Mathematical Form)

> [!NOTE]
> Per protocol, no Python code is provided. The following is a mathematical specification suitable for KTU exam answers.

**Given:** Sequence $\{(x_1, y_1), \dots, (x_T, y_T)\}$, initial states $h_0 = 0, C_0 = 0$, learning rate $\eta$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Forward:} \quad \text{For } t = 1 \text{ to } T: \text{ compute } f_t, i_t, \tilde{C}_t, C_t, o_t, h_t \text{ via Section 2.4.} \\
&\textbf{Loss:} \quad L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t) \\
&\textbf{Backward:} \quad \text{Compute } \partial L / \partial W_f, \partial L / \partial W_i, \partial L / \partial W_C, \partial L / \partial W_o \text{ via Section 3.3.} \\
&\textbf{Clip:} \quad \Vert g \Vert_2 \leq c \\
&\textbf{Update:} \quad W \leftarrow W - \eta \cdot \nabla_W L \quad \text{(or via Adam/RMSProp)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Common Variants of LSTM (KTU High-Yield)

| Variant | Key Modification | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Vanilla LSTM (Hochreiter 1997)** | Three gates + cell state | Baseline |
| **Peephole LSTM** | Gates see $C_{t-1}$ directly | Better timing/counting |
| **Coupled Input/Forget (CIFG)** | $f_t = 1 - i_t$ | Fewer parameters |
| **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)** | Combines forget + input into "update gate"; merges $C_t, h_t$ | Simpler, faster, similar performance |
| **Bidirectional LSTM** | Two LSTMs: forward + backward | Captures past + future context |

> [!TIP]
> KTU may ask to **compare LSTM with GRU**. Key answer: GRU has 2 gates (update, reset) and no separate cell state; it merges $C_t$ and $h_t$. LSTM has 3 gates and explicit $C_t$. GRU is computationally cheaper, LSTM is more expressive.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LSTM Cell Block Diagram (Detailed Mermaid)

The following block-level functional architecture shows the **internal data flow of a single LSTM cell** at time step $t$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputXt["INPUT x_t"] --> concatNode["CONCATENATION [h_{t-1} , x_t]"]
    prevH["PREVIOUS HIDDEN h_{t-1}"] --> concatNode
    concatNode --> fgate["FORGET GATE f_t = sigma(W_f x + b_f)"]
    concatNode --> igate["INPUT GATE i_t = sigma(W_i x + b_i)"]
    concatNode --> cand["CANDIDATE C~_t = tanh(W_C x + b_C)"]
    concatNode --> ogate["OUTPUT GATE o_t = sigma(W_o x + b_o)"]
    prevC["PREVIOUS CELL C_{t-1}"] --> multF["HADAMARD PRODUCT f_t * C_{t-1}"]
    fgate --> multF
    multF --> sumNode["ADDITIVE SUM"]
    igate --> multI["HADAMARD PRODUCT i_t * C~_t"]
    cand --> multI
    multI --> sumNode
    sumNode --> newC["NEW CELL STATE C_t"]
    newC --> tanhC["TANH squash C_t"]
    ogate --> multO["HADAMARD PRODUCT o_t * tanh(C_t)"]
    tanhC --> multO
    multO --> newH["NEW HIDDEN h_t"]
    newC --> nextTime["FEED TO NEXT TIME STEP"]
    newH --> nextTime
    newH --> outputYt["OUTPUT y_t = softmax(W_y h_t + b_y)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide for Students:**
> 1. The **top half** (gates) processes information.
> 2. The **horizontal line through the middle** is the **cell-state conveyor belt** (gradient highway).
> 3. The **bottom half** produces the next hidden state.

## 4.2 Unrolled LSTM Across Time Steps

The following diagram illustrates the **temporal unrolling** of an LSTM for a sequence of length $T=4$, which is how BPTT operates on the network.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x1["x_1"] --> lstm1["LSTM CELL t=1"]
    x2["x_2"] --> lstm2["LSTM CELL t=2"]
    x3["x_3"] --> lstm3["LSTM CELL t=3"]
    x4["x_4"] --> lstm4["LSTM CELL t=4"]
    lstm1 --> h1["h_1"] --> y1["y_1"]
    lstm2 --> h2["h_2"] --> y2["y_2"]
    lstm3 --> h3["h_3"] --> y3["y_3"]
    lstm4 --> h4["h_4"] --> y4["y_4"]
    lstm1 -- "C_1" --> lstm2
    lstm2 -- "C_2" --> lstm3
    lstm3 -- "C_3" --> lstm4
    lstm1 -- "h_1" --> lstm2
    lstm2 -- "h_2" --> lstm3
    lstm3 -- "h_3" --> lstm4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **horizontal arrows** labeled $C_t$ and $h_t$ between cells are what BPTT unrolls into a long computational graph. The **cell state pathway $C_t$** is the **gradient highway** that solves vanishing gradients.

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture: RNN vs LSTM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RNN["VANILLA RNN CELL"]
        rnnIn["x_t, h_{t-1}"] --> rnnTanH["tanh(W)"] --> rnnOut["h_t"]
    end
    subgraph LSTM["LSTM CELL"]
        lstmIn["x_t, h_{t-1}, C_{t-1}"] --> lstmGates["3 GATES sigma + tanh"] --> lstmCell["C_t additive update"] --> lstmOut["h_t, C_t"]
    end
```

| Aspect | Vanilla RNN | LSTM |
|---|---|---|
| State variables | $h_t$ only | $h_t$ and $C_t$ |
| Gates | None | 3 sigmoid gates |
| Gradient path | Multiplicative ($W_{hh}^T$ products) | Additive (forget-gate diagonal) |
| Long-range memory | Poor (vanishes) | Strong (preserved) |
| Parameters per cell | $m \times (m+n)$ | $4 \times m \times (m+n)$ |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the vanishing gradient problem in standard RNNs. How does the LSTM architecture overcome this issue?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **(1 Mark)** In vanilla RNNs, BPTT involves repeated multiplication of the gradient by the Jacobian $W_{hh}^T$. If spectral radius $<1$, gradients vanish exponentially over long sequences, preventing learning of long-range dependencies.
2. **(1 Mark)** LSTM replaces the multiplicative hidden-state recurrence with an **additive cell-state update**: $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$.
3. **(1 Mark)** The gradient flowing through the cell state is $\prod \text{diag}(f_i)$, where each $f_i \in [0,1]$ is *learned*. The network can keep $f_i$ near 1 to preserve gradient flow, effectively creating a **gradient highway** that mitigates vanishing gradients.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain the three gates used in an LSTM cell.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **(1 Mark)** **Forget Gate $f_t = \sigma(W_f[h_{t-1},x_t] + b_f)$** — controls what fraction of the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$ to retain.
2. **(1 Mark)** **Input Gate $i_t = \sigma(W_i[h_{t-1},x_t] + b_i)$** — controls what new candidate information $\tilde{C}_t$ is written into the cell state.
3. **(1 Mark)** **Output Gate $o_t = \sigma(W_o[h_{t-1},x_t] + b_o)$** — controls what part of the cell state is exposed as the next hidden state $h_t$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of an LSTM cell**. Explain the role of each gate with its mathematical formulation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **forward pass equations** of an LSTM cell step-by-step, clearly distinguishing the role of cell state $C_t$ and hidden state $h_t$. Show how the cell state update enables stable gradient flow. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 Marks]

**Architecture (Block Diagram) — 2 Marks:**

An LSTM cell consists of:
- **Input concatenation** $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$.
- **Three gates**: Forget ($f_t$), Input ($i_t$), Output ($o_t$) — each is a sigmoid layer.
- **Candidate cell state** $\tilde{C}_t$ produced by a tanh layer.
- **Cell state update** via additive combination: $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$.
- **Hidden state output**: $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$.

(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram for visualization.)

**Forget Gate — 1.5 Marks:**

$$f_t = \sigma\!\left(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f\right)$$

Decides what information to discard from $C_{t-1}$. Output range $[0,1]$ acts as a per-dimension mask.

**Input Gate — 1.5 Marks:**

$$i_t = \sigma\!\left(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i\right), \quad \tilde{C}_t = \tanh\!\left(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C\right)$$

$i_t$ controls *how much* new content to write; $\tilde{C}_t$ is the new content proposal.

**Output Gate — 1 Mark:**

$$o_t = \sigma\!\left(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o\right), \quad h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

Filters the cell state to produce the next hidden state output.

**Cell State Conveyor — 1 Mark:**

The cell state $C_t$ flows horizontally through time with only linear (Hadamard and addition) interactions, forming the gradient highway.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Concatenation (1 Mark)**

$$z_t = [h_{t-1}; x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{(m+n)}$$

**Step 2: Gate Pre-activations (1 Mark)**

$$\text{net}_f = W_f z_t + b_f, \quad \text{net}_i = W_i z_t + b_i, \quad \text{net}_C = W_C z_t + b_C, \quad \text{net}_o = W_o z_t + b_o$$

**Step 3: Gate Activations + Candidate (1 Mark)**

$$f_t = \sigma(\text{net}_f), \quad i_t = \sigma(\text{net}_i), \quad \tilde{C}_t = \tanh(\text{net}_C)$$

**Step 4: Cell State Update — The Additive Recurrence (2 Marks)**

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

- The previous cell state is **multiplied** by the forget gate (per-dimension retention).
- The new candidate is **multiplied** by the input gate (per-dimension write).
- The two are **added** (not multiplied), which is the key to gradient stability.

**Step 5: Hidden State (1 Mark)**

$$o_t = \sigma(\text{net}_o), \quad h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

**Step 6: Gradient Stability Justification (1 Mark)**

The gradient of $C_t$ w.r.t. $C_{t-1}$ is $\text{diag}(f_t)$. Unrolling across $k$ steps:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-k}} = \prod_{j=t-k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(f_j)$$

Since the network learns $f_j \in [0,1]$, gradients neither vanish catastrophically nor explode. The cell state pathway acts as a **gradient highway**, in contrast to vanilla RNNs where the product is $\prod W_{hh}^T$.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Derive the **BPTT gradient flow equations** for an LSTM cell, focusing on the cell state recurrence. Show explicitly how the gradient of total loss $L$ with respect to $C_{t-1}$ depends on the forget gate activations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **LSTM with GRU** in terms of architecture, number of parameters, gating mechanism, and performance trade-offs. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Total Loss Definition (1 Mark)**

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$$

**Step 2: Cell State Update Reminder (1 Mark)**

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

**Step 3: Local Gradient of Cell State (1 Mark)**

By direct differentiation:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t)$$

**Step 4: Upstream Gradient (1.5 Marks)**

The total gradient flowing into $C_t$ has two sources — the local loss $L_t$ and the future loss via $C_{t+1}$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_t} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial C_t} + \frac{\partial L}{\partial C_{t+1}} \cdot \text{diag}(f_{t+1})$$

**Step 5: Recursive Unrolling to $C_k$ (1.5 Marks)**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_k} = \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial C_k} + \sum_{j=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_j}{\partial C_j} \prod_{i=k+1}^{j} \text{diag}(f_i)$$

**Step 6: Conclusion — Gradient Highway (1 Mark)**

The gradient product contains only **forget-gate diagonals**, not recurrent weight matrices. The LSTM can learn $f_i \approx 1$ to keep gradient magnitudes preserved across hundreds of time steps, solving the vanishing-gradient problem.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 Marks]

| Aspect | LSTM (2 Marks) | GRU (2 Marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **Gates** | 3: Forget, Input, Output | 2: Update, Reset |
| **State variables** | Cell state $C_t$ and hidden state $h_t$ (separate) | Single hidden state $h_t$ (no separate $C_t$) |
| **Update mechanism** | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$; $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ |
| **Parameters per cell** | $4 \times m(m+n)$ | $3 \times m(m+n)$ |
| **Memory mechanism** | Explicit long-term memory via $C_t$ | Implicit memory via update gate |
| **Performance** | Better on very long sequences, complex tasks | Comparable on shorter sequences, faster training |
| **Use cases** | Machine translation, speech recognition | Real-time applications, smaller datasets |

**Comparison Summary (3 Marks):**
- GRU is a **simplified LSTM** that combines forget and input gates into a single "update gate" and merges cell state and hidden state.
- LSTM has **more expressive power** due to the separate cell state but is **computationally heavier** (4 gates vs 3).
- Empirically, both perform similarly on many tasks; LSTM has slight edge on very long sequences; GRU is preferred when **training speed and model size** matter.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on LSTM Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing sigmoid and tanh roles**: Sigmoid ($\sigma$) is used for **gates** (output $[0,1]$), tanh is used for **state values** (output $[-1,1]$). Mixing them up = −1 to −2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting Hadamard product**: $f_t \odot C_{t-1}$ is **element-wise**, not matrix multiplication. Drawing a regular $\times$ sign loses marks.
> 3. **Omitting biases**: $W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f$ — the $+b_f$ is often forgotten. Examiners specifically check this.
> 4. **Conflating $C_t$ and $h_t$**: Cell state is **internal long-term memory**; hidden state is **exposed output**. Treating them as the same = conceptual error.
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps in BPTT**: KTU expects explicit chain-rule application, not just the final formula. Show $\partial C_t / \partial C_{t-1}$ and the recursive unrolling.
> 6. **Not mentioning gradient clipping**: For long-sequence LSTM training, gradient clipping is **standard practice** — mentioning it shows depth.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for LSTM: Architecture and Training**

- **LSTM was introduced by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber (1997)** to solve the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs.
- An LSTM cell has **two state vectors**: cell state $C_t$ (long-term) and hidden state $h_t$ (short-term/working).
- **Three sigmoid gates** regulate information flow: **Forget ($f_t$), Input ($i_t$), Output ($o_t$)** — all in $[0,1]$.
- **Candidate cell state** $\tilde{C}_t$ is computed using **tanh** (range $[-1,1]$).
- The **core cell state update** is **additive**: $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$.
- The **hidden state** is: $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$.
- The **concatenated input** $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ feeds all four linear layers (one per gate + candidate).
- The **gradient highway** through $C_t$ involves only $\text{diag}(f_i)$ products, enabling long-range learning.
- BPTT unrolls the LSTM across all $T$ time steps; gradient clipping is **mandatory** in practice.
- **Common activation derivatives**: $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$, $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x)$.
- **Variants to remember**: Peephole LSTM, Coupled Forget-Input (CIFG), GRU, Bidirectional LSTM.
- **GRU vs LSTM**: GRU has 2 gates and no separate $C_t$; LSTM has 3 gates and explicit $C_t$.
- **Parameter count**: LSTM = $4 \cdot m(m+n) + 4m$; GRU = $3 \cdot m(m+n) + 3m$ (for hidden size $m$, input size $n$).
- **Exam Tip**: Always draw the **block diagram first**, then write equations — KTU rewards labeled diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gated Recurrent Units (GRU): Architecture and Training
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gated Recurrent Units (GRU): Architecture and Training

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** is a sophisticated gating mechanism introduced by Cho et al. (2014) as a streamlined alternative to the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. It is a type of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture specifically engineered to overcome the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** that plagues standard RNNs when modeling long-range temporal dependencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> A Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) is a recurrent neural network architecture that uses two gates — the **update gate** and the **reset gate** — to control the flow of information through the hidden state, allowing the network to selectively retain or forget temporal context without requiring a separate cell state memory.

The GRU consolidates the **forget gate** and **input gate** of an LSTM into a single **update gate**, and merges the **cell state** and **hidden state** into one unified vector. This results in a computationally lighter model with **fewer parameters** (2 gates vs. 3 gates in LSTM) while achieving comparable performance on many sequence modeling tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Distinction:**
> - **LSTM:** 3 gates (forget, input, output) + cell state + hidden state
> - **GRU:** 2 gates (update, reset) + single hidden state (no separate cell state)
> - **Standard RNN:** 0 gates, only hidden state

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Editor's Notebook"

Imagine you are a journalist covering a long, complex court trial that spans many days. On Day 1, you take detailed notes. On Day 2, you must decide:
1. **Which of yesterday's notes are still relevant today?** (Reset gate decision)
2. **How much of yesterday's information should I carry forward, and how much should I replace with new observations?** (Update gate decision)

A standard RNN is like a careless student who mindlessly copies **all** yesterday's notes into today's page — eventually the important information drowns in noise. An LSTM is like a meticulous archivist with a separate file cabinet and a writing pad. A **GRU** is a smart journalist with a **single notebook** that uses two sticky tabs:
- A **Reset Tab** 🗑️ — telling them "ignore parts of the old notes that are no longer useful for the new context."
- An **Update Tab** ✏️ — telling them "blend the remaining old notes with today's new observations, deciding the exact ratio."

This is exactly what GRU does mathematically — it adaptively interpolates between the **previous hidden state** and a **new candidate hidden state** using the update gate's learned coefficients.

## 1.3 The Two Gates at a Glance

| Gate | Symbol | Role | Intuition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Update Gate** | $z_t$ | Decides how much past info to keep vs. new info to add | The "memory dial" — controls persistence |
| **Reset Gate** | $r_t$ | Decides how much past info to forget when computing new candidate | The "forget switch" — controls novelty |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever asked to "explain GRU architecture," ALWAYS begin by stating it is a **simplified variant of LSTM** that combines forget and input gates into a single **update gate**, thereby reducing parameter count and computational overhead.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Walkthrough — The Information Flow

The GRU operates at every time step $t$ using the current input vector $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ and the previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$. The flow proceeds in **four precise computational stages**:

### Stage 1: Reset Gate Computation
- **What:** Computes $r_t$ that controls how much of the previous hidden state contributes to the candidate state.
- **Why:** Allows the model to "drop" irrelevant past context when computing new candidate memory.
- **How:** A sigmoid-activated linear transformation of concatenated input and previous hidden state.

### Stage 2: Update Gate Computation
- **What:** Computes $z_t$ that interpolates between old state and new candidate.
- **Why:** Acts as a learned convex combination weight — values near 0 retain old state, values near 1 adopt new candidate.
- **How:** Same form as reset gate but with different learned parameters.

### Stage 3: Candidate Hidden State Generation
- **What:** Computes $\tilde{h}_t$, a proposal for the new hidden state.
- **Why:** Represents the "newly observed information" filtered through the reset gate.
- **How:** Uses **element-wise (Hadamard) multiplication** of $r_t$ with $h_{t-1}$, then a `tanh` activation.

### Stage 4: Final Hidden State Update
- **What:** Produces the output $h_t$ for time $t$.
- **Why:** Linear interpolation ensures gradient-friendly paths (no multiplicative decay chain).
- **How:** Convex combination: $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** All gates are vectors of dimension $h$ (hidden size). $\odot$ denotes element-wise (Hadamard) product. $[\cdot, \cdot]$ denotes vector concatenation along the feature axis.

| # | Equation | Name | Output Range | Learnable Params |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ | **Update Gate** | $(0, 1)^h$ | $W_z \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times (h+d)}$, $b_z \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ |
| 2 | $r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ | **Reset Gate** | $(0, 1)^h$ | $W_r \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times (h+d)}$, $b_r \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ |
| 3 | $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$ | **Candidate Hidden State** | $(-1, 1)^h$ | $W_h \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times (h+d)}$, $b_h \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ |
| 4 | $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ | **Hidden State Output** | $(-1, 1)^h$ | (No new params; uses $z_t$) |
| 5 | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t}$ (BPTT) | **Gradient of Loss** | $\mathbb{R}^{h}$ | Used in backward pass |
| 6 | $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = (1 - z_t) + \text{correction terms}$ | **State Transition Jacobian** | $\mathbb{R}^{h \times h}$ | Key to gradient flow |

### Total Parameter Count for a Single GRU Layer

$$
P_{\text{GRU}} = 3 \times \left[ h(h + d) + h \right] = 3h^2 + 3hd + 3h
$$

where:
- $h$ = number of hidden units
- $d$ = input feature dimension
- The factor **3** comes from the three weight matrices $W_z$, $W_r$, $W_h$.

For comparison, an LSTM has:
$$
P_{\text{LSTM}} = 4 \times \left[ h(h + d) + h \right] = 4h^2 + 4hd + 4h
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** GRU's reduced parameter count (~25% fewer parameters than LSTM) makes it the preferred choice in resource-constrained environments such as **edge AI devices, mobile NLP applications, real-time speech recognition, and embedded IoT systems**, where memory footprint and inference latency are critical.

## 2.3 Why GRU Solves the Vanishing Gradient Problem

In a standard RNN, the gradient of the loss $L$ with respect to $h_k$ (for $k \ll t$) involves repeated multiplication by the Jacobian $\frac{\partial h_{i+1}}{\partial h_i}$. When the spectral radius of this Jacobian is less than 1, gradients decay exponentially — this is **vanishing gradients**.

In a GRU, the gradient flowing back to $h_{t-1}$ is:
$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = (1 - z_t) \odot I + \text{additional terms involving } z_t, r_t, \tilde{h}_t
$$

Since $z_t$ is learned and can adaptively become close to 0, the term $(1 - z_t)$ can act as a **learned skip-connection coefficient**, allowing gradients to flow nearly unattenuated across many time steps — analogous to a **highway network** in time.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Forward Pass of a Single GRU Cell

We derive each gate step-by-step with full mathematical rigor, expanding all vector-matrix products.

### Step 1: Concatenation of Previous State and Current Input

Let $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^h$ and $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^d$. Define the concatenated input vector:
$$
a_t = [h_{t-1}; x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{h+d}
$$

Here the semicolon denotes **vertical stacking** of column vectors. The concatenated vector has $h+d$ elements.

### Step 2: Update Gate Derivation

Apply a linear transformation followed by the sigmoid activation:
$$
z_t = \sigma(W_z a_t + b_z)
$$

Explicitly expanding the matrix-vector product $W_z a_t$, where $W_z \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times (h+d)}$:
$$
W_z a_t = W_z \begin{bmatrix} h_{t-1} \\ x_t \end{bmatrix} = W_z^{(h)} h_{t-1} + W_z^{(x)} x_t
$$

where $W_z^{(h)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times h}$ and $W_z^{(x)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times d}$ are the row-blocks. Then:
$$
z_t = \sigma(W_z^{(h)} h_{t-1} + W_z^{(x)} x_t + b_z), \quad z_t \in (0, 1)^h
$$

Each element $z_t^{(i)}$ represents the "update intensity" for hidden unit $i$.

### Step 3: Reset Gate Derivation

Identical structure, distinct parameters:
$$
r_t = \sigma(W_r^{(h)} h_{t-1} + W_r^{(x)} x_t + b_r), \quad r_t \in (0, 1)^h
$$

Each element $r_t^{(i)} \in (0, 1)$ is a **multiplicative gate** that scales the contribution of $h_{t-1}^{(i)}$ when forming the candidate state.

### Step 4: Candidate Hidden State Derivation

First, compute the reset-modulated previous state:
$$
r_t \odot h_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} r_t^{(1)} h_{t-1}^{(1)} \\ r_t^{(2)} h_{t-1}^{(2)} \\ \vdots \\ r_t^{(h)} h_{t-1}^{(h)} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}
$$

Then concatenate with $x_t$ and apply a linear + `tanh` transformation:
$$
\tilde{h}_t = \tanh\left( W_h^{(h)} (r_t \odot h_{t-1}) + W_h^{(x)} x_t + b_h \right), \quad \tilde{h}_t \in (-1, 1)^h
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why tanh?** The `tanh` activation outputs values in $(-1, 1)$, providing **zero-centered** outputs that match the symmetric nature of the interpolation in Step 5 and avoid biasing the hidden state distribution.

### Step 5: Final Hidden State (Convex Interpolation)

$$
h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t
$$

**Element-wise expansion** for hidden unit $i$:
$$
h_t^{(i)} = (1 - z_t^{(i)}) h_{t-1}^{(i)} + z_t^{(i)} \tilde{h}_t^{(i)}
$$

This is a **convex combination** because $(1 - z_t^{(i)}) + z_t^{(i)} = 1$ and both weights lie in $[0, 1]$. Therefore $h_t^{(i)}$ is a **weighted average** of $h_{t-1}^{(i)}$ and $\tilde{h}_t^{(i)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** When $z_t^{(i)} \approx 0$, the unit $i$ behaves as a **skip connection**, copying $h_{t-1}^{(i)}$ directly. This is the **gradient superhighway** that makes GRU trainable over long sequences.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Backward Pass via BPTT (Back-Propagation Through Time)

We now derive the gradient of the loss $L$ with respect to the parameters, which is essential for understanding GRU training.

### Setup

Suppose we have an output $o_t = W_{o} h_t + b_o$ at each time step, and the total loss is:
$$
L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(o_t, y_t)
$$

The gradient with respect to $h_t$ must account for the **direct contribution** at time $t$ AND the **future contribution** through $h_{t+1}, h_{t+2}, \ldots, h_T$.

### Step 1: Total Gradient at Hidden State

Define the total gradient of the loss with respect to $h_t$:
$$
\delta_t \equiv \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} + \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_{t+1}} \frac{\partial h_{t+1}}{\partial h_t}
$$

Recursively:
$$
\delta_t = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} + \delta_{t+1} \frac{\partial h_{t+1}}{\partial h_t}
$$

### Step 2: Partial Derivative of $h_t$ with Respect to $h_{t-1}$

From $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$, applying the chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \underbrace{(1 - z_t) \odot I}_{\text{direct copy path}} + \underbrace{\frac{\partial z_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \odot (\tilde{h}_t - h_{t-1})}_{\text{via update gate}} + \underbrace{z_t \odot \frac{\partial \tilde{h}_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}}_{\text{via candidate}}
$$

**Detailed expansion of the candidate gradient term:**
$$
\frac{\partial \tilde{h}_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = (1 - \tilde{h}_t^2) \odot \left[ W_h^{(h)} \cdot \frac{\partial (r_t \odot h_{t-1})}{\partial h_{t-1}} \right]
$$

where the reset-modulated Jacobian:
$$
\frac{\partial (r_t \odot h_{t-1})}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(r_t) + \text{diag}(h_{t-1}) \cdot \frac{\partial r_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}
$$

and the reset gate gradient:
$$
\frac{\partial r_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = r_t \odot (1 - r_t) \odot W_r^{(h)}
$$

### Step 3: Gradient Stability Argument

The **dominant term** in $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$ is $(1 - z_t) \odot I$. When $z_t \approx 0$ (which the network learns for units that must preserve long-term memory), this term approximates the **identity matrix** $I$, yielding:
$$
\delta_t \approx \delta_{t+1} + \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t}
$$

This means gradients can flow **unattenuated** across time, which is the mathematical reason why GRU (and LSTM) overcome vanishing gradients — the gating mechanism learns to act as a **highway** for gradient flow.

### Step 4: Parameter Gradients (Final Layer BPTT Step)

The gradient of the loss with respect to $W_z$ is:
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_z} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial z_t} \cdot \frac{\partial z_t}{\partial W_z}
$$

Computing each factor:
$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial z_t} = -h_{t-1} + \tilde{h}_t \quad \text{(element-wise)}
$$
$$
\frac{\partial z_t}{\partial W_z} = z_t \odot (1 - z_t) \odot a_t^{\top}
$$

Combining:
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_z} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t \odot (-h_{t-1} + \tilde{h}_t) \odot z_t \odot (1 - z_t) \odot a_t^{\top}
$$

Similar derivations apply for $W_r$, $W_h$, $W_o$ and all bias terms, completing the BPTT training loop.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often state that "GRU has no vanishing gradient problem." This is **incorrect** — GRU **mitigates** vanishing gradients through gating but does not completely eliminate them. The exact statement: *"GRU's gating mechanism provides a learnable gradient highway, which significantly mitigates the vanishing gradient problem but does not entirely remove it."*

## 3.3 Training Algorithm Summary

The complete GRU training procedure is:

1. **Initialize** $W_z, W_r, W_h, W_o$ and biases (e.g., Xavier/Glorot initialization).
2. **Forward pass** through all time steps $t = 1, \ldots, T$, computing $z_t$, $r_t$, $\tilde{h}_t$, $h_t$, and outputs $o_t$.
3. **Compute loss** $L = \sum_t L_t$.
4. **Backward pass** (BPTT): Compute $\delta_t$ for $t = T, T-1, \ldots, 1$ using the chain rule.
5. **Accumulate parameter gradients** $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_z}$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_r}$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_h}$, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_o}$.
6. **Update parameters** using an optimizer (SGD, Adam, RMSProp):
$$
\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}
$$
7. **Repeat** for $N$ epochs until convergence (loss plateaus or validation error stops improving).

**Optional techniques** commonly applied: **gradient clipping** (to prevent explosion), **dropout** (regularization), **learning rate scheduling**, and **early stopping**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GRU Cell Internal Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional block-level architecture** of a single GRU cell, mapping the data flow between gates, candidate computation, and final state interpolation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input1["INPUT x_t (current time step)"]
    input2["PREVIOUS HIDDEN STATE h_t-1"]
    
    concat["Concatenation Block: a_t = [h_t-1 ; x_t]"]
    
    subgraph GATES["Gating Mechanism (Sigmoid Activations)"]
        zgate["Update Gate: z_t = sigma(W_z a_t + b_z)"]
        rgate["Reset Gate: r_t = sigma(W_r a_t + b_r)"]
    end
    
    hadamard["Element-wise Product: r_t (.) h_t-1"]
    concat2["Concatenation Block: b_t = [r_t (.) h_t-1 ; x_t]"]
    candidate["Candidate State: h_t_tilde = tanh(W_h b_t + b_h)"]
    
    subgraph FUSION["Convex Interpolation"]
        oneMinusZ["Compute (1 - z_t)"]
        path1["Path 1: (1 - z_t) (.) h_t-1"]
        path2["Path 2: z_t (.) h_t_tilde"]
        sum["Sum: h_t = path1 + path2"]
    end
    
    output["OUTPUT HIDDEN STATE h_t"]
    forward["Forward to next time step (t+1)"]
    
    input2 --> concat
    input1 --> concat
    concat --> zgate
    concat --> rgate
    
    input2 --> hadamard
    rgate --> hadamard
    
    hadamard --> concat2
    input1 --> concat2
    concat2 --> candidate
    
    zgate --> oneMinusZ
    oneMinusZ --> path1
    input2 --> path1
    
    zgate --> path2
    candidate --> path2
    
    path1 --> sum
    path2 --> sum
    sum --> output
    output --> forward
```

## 4.2 Unrolled GRU Network Across Time

The diagram below shows the **unrolled computational graph** of a GRU network across three consecutive time steps, illustrating how the hidden state is passed forward in time and how BPTT traverses backward through the same graph.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1["Time Step t-1"]
        x1["x_t-1"]
        hprev1["h_t-2"]
        cell1["GRU Cell"]
        h1["h_t-1"]
    end
    
    subgraph T2["Time Step t"]
        x2["x_t"]
        hprev2["h_t-1"]
        cell2["GRU Cell"]
        h2["h_t"]
    end
    
    subgraph T3["Time Step t+1"]
        x3["x_t+1"]
        hprev3["h_t"]
        cell3["GRU Cell"]
        h3["h_t+1"]
    end
    
    x1 --> cell1
    hprev1 --> cell1
    cell1 --> h1
    
    x2 --> cell2
    h1 --> hprev2
    cell2 --> h2
    
    x3 --> cell3
    h2 --> hprev3
    cell3 --> h3
    
    h1 -.->|BPTT gradient| cell1
    h2 -.->|BPTT gradient| cell2
    h3 -.->|BPTT gradient| cell3
```

## 4.3 GRU vs. LSTM vs. Vanilla RNN — Comparative Block Diagram

The following diagram provides a **comparative structural overview** of the three recurrent architectures, highlighting the parameter and gate differences.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RNN["Vanilla RNN"]
        rnnin["x_t, h_t-1"]
        rnncell["Single tanh layer"]
        rnnout["h_t"]
        rnnin --> rnncell --> rnnout
    end
    
    subgraph LSTM["LSTM (3 gates + cell state)"]
        lstmIn["x_t, h_t-1, c_t-1"]
        lstmForget["Forget Gate f_t"]
        lstmInput["Input Gate i_t"]
        lstmOutput["Output Gate o_t"]
        lstmCell["Cell State Update c_t"]
        lstmHidden["Hidden State h_t"]
        lstmIn --> lstmForget
        lstmIn --> lstmInput
        lstmIn --> lstmOutput
        lstmForget --> lstmCell
        lstmInput --> lstmCell
        lstmCell --> lstmHidden
        lstmOutput --> lstmHidden
    end
    
    subgraph GRUCell["GRU (2 gates, unified state)"]
        gruIn["x_t, h_t-1"]
        gruUpdate["Update Gate z_t"]
        gruReset["Reset Gate r_t"]
        gruCandidate["Candidate h_t_tilde"]
        gruFusion["Convex Combine h_t"]
        gruOut["h_t (final)"]
        gruIn --> gruUpdate
        gruIn --> gruReset
        gruUpdate --> gruFusion
        gruReset --> gruCandidate
        gruCandidate --> gruFusion
        gruFusion --> gruOut
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The **RNN** has the simplest structure: one transformation, no gating.
> - The **LSTM** has the most complex structure: three separate gates plus a dedicated cell state line.
> - The **GRU** sits in between: two gates, no separate cell state — the hidden state serves both roles.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU). List its two gates and state the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** is a variant of the recurrent neural network that uses gating mechanisms to control information flow across time steps, designed to solve the vanishing gradient problem in standard RNNs.

The **two gates** of a GRU are:

1. **Update Gate ($z_t$):** Decides how much of the previous hidden state should be retained versus how much of the new candidate state should be incorporated. It acts as a learned interpolation coefficient in the convex combination:
$$
h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t
$$

2. **Reset Gate ($r_t$):** Determines how much of the previous hidden state should be ignored when computing the new candidate state. It is applied via element-wise multiplication with $h_{t-1}$ before forming $\tilde{h}_t$.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note:** Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for naming both gates correctly, 1 mark for describing the role of each gate.

---

### Question 2: Comparative Reasoning `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: How does GRU differ from LSTM in terms of architecture and parameter count?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| Number of gates | **3** (forget, input, output) | **2** (update, reset) |
| State variables | Separate cell state $c_t$ and hidden state $h_t$ | Unified hidden state $h_t$ only |
| Parameter count | $4h(h+d) + 4h$ | $3h(h+d) + 3h$ |
| Output gate | Present (controls $h_t$ from $c_t$) | Absent ($h_t$ directly produced) |

GRU is **computationally lighter** and uses **~25% fewer parameters** than LSTM while achieving comparable performance on many sequence tasks. The consolidation of the forget and input gates into a single update gate is the primary architectural simplification.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the complete architecture of a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) with a neat block diagram. Derive the mathematical equations for all the gates and the hidden state. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Back-Propagation Through Time (BPTT) algorithm used to train a GRU network. Show the gradient flow through the update gate and explain how GRU mitigates the vanishing gradient problem. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Architecture Description (3 Marks):**
A GRU cell consists of two gates — the **update gate** and the **reset gate** — and produces a single hidden state output at each time step. Unlike LSTM, GRU does not maintain a separate cell state. The complete block diagram is shown below:

```
                ┌─────────────────────────────┐
   x_t ────────►│ Concat: a_t = [h_{t-1}; x_t]│
                └──────────┬──────────────────┘
                           │
            ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
            ▼                             ▼
   ┌─────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐
   │  z_t = σ(W_z a) │         │  r_t = σ(W_r a) │
   │  (Update Gate)  │         │  (Reset Gate)   │
   └────────┬────────┘         └────────┬────────┘
            │                          │
            │                          ▼
            │                ┌────────────────────┐
            │                │ r_t ⊙ h_{t-1}      │
            │                │ (Hadamard product) │
            │                └─────────┬──────────┘
            │                          │
            │                          ▼
            │                ┌────────────────────────┐
            │                │ h̃_t = tanh(W_h [r_t⊙h,x])│
            │                │ (Candidate State)       │
            │                └─────────┬──────────────┘
            │                          │
            ▼                          ▼
   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ h_t = (1 - z_t) ⊙ h_{t-1} + z_t ⊙ h̃_t │
   │ (Convex Interpolation - Final State)    │
   └────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                        ▼
                      h_t (Output)
```

**Mathematical Derivation (4 Marks):**

Step 1 — Update gate:
$$
z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z) = \sigma(W_z^{(h)} h_{t-1} + W_z^{(x)} x_t + b_z)
$$

Step 2 — Reset gate:
$$
r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r) = \sigma(W_r^{(h)} h_{t-1} + W_r^{(x)} x_t + b_r)
$$

Step 3 — Candidate hidden state (using reset-modulated previous state):
$$
\tilde{h}_t = \tanh\left(W_h [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h\right)
$$

Step 4 — Final hidden state (convex combination):
$$
h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students forget the **element-wise (Hadamard) product** between $r_t$ and $h_{t-1}$. This is **mandatory** and not a regular matrix multiplication. Losing this costs **1 mark** typically.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**BPTT Algorithm Explanation (4 Marks):**

The Back-Propagation Through Time algorithm trains the GRU by unrolling the network across all $T$ time steps and applying the standard back-propagation chain rule on the unrolled graph.

**Step 1 — Forward Pass:**
For $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$, compute $z_t$, $r_t$, $\tilde{h}_t$, $h_t$, and $o_t = W_o h_t + b_o$. Cache all intermediate values for the backward pass.

**Step 2 — Compute Total Loss:**
$$
L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(o_t, y_t)
$$

**Step 3 — Backward Pass (Recursive Gradient Computation):**
Starting from $t = T$ and proceeding backwards, compute the gradient of the total loss with respect to the hidden state:
$$
\delta_t = \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} + \delta_{t+1} \frac{\partial h_{t+1}}{\partial h_t}
$$

**Step 4 — Gradient w.r.t. Parameters:**
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_z} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t \odot (\tilde{h}_t - h_{t-1}) \odot z_t \odot (1 - z_t) \odot a_t^{\top}
$$
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_r} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t \odot z_t \odot (1 - \tilde{h}_t^2) \odot W_h^{(h)} \odot h_{t-1} \odot r_t \odot (1 - r_t) \odot a_t^{\top}
$$
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_h} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \delta_t \odot z_t \odot (1 - \tilde{h}_t^2) \odot b_t^{\top}
$$

**Step 5 — Update via Gradient Descent:**
$$
W \leftarrow W - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial W}, \quad b \leftarrow b - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial b}
$$

**Vanishing Gradient Mitigation (3 Marks):**

The vanishing gradient problem arises in vanilla RNNs because the Jacobian $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$ has a spectral radius less than 1, causing repeated multiplication to decay the gradient exponentially.

In a GRU, the dominant term of this Jacobian is:
$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \approx (1 - z_t) \odot I + \ldots
$$

When the network learns $z_t \approx 0$ for units that need long-term memory, the term $(1 - z_t) \odot I$ approximates the **identity matrix**, providing a **gradient highway** where:
$$
\delta_t \approx \delta_{t+1} + \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t}
$$

This means gradients can propagate across hundreds of time steps with minimal attenuation, effectively solving the vanishing gradient problem for the gated pathways.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Students often claim GRU "completely eliminates" the vanishing gradient. The correct statement is that GRU **significantly mitigates** it via the learnable $(1 - z_t)$ identity term, but does not mathematically guarantee non-vanishing gradients in all configurations.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With suitable equations, explain the four computational steps of the GRU forward pass. Compare the parameter count of GRU with LSTM for a hidden size of $h = 64$ and input dimension $d = 32$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the engineering applications of GRU in real-world systems. Explain three advantages and two disadvantages of GRU compared to LSTM. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Four Computational Steps (4 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Update Gate:**
$$
z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)
$$
Produces a vector $z_t \in (0, 1)^h$ controlling how much of $h_{t-1}$ is preserved.

**Step 2 — Reset Gate:**
$$
r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)
$$
Produces a vector $r_t \in (0, 1)^h$ controlling how much of $h_{t-1}$ is "forgotten" in the candidate computation.

**Step 3 — Candidate Hidden State:**
$$
\tilde{h}_t = \tanh\left(W_h [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h\right)
$$
Computes a new candidate memory, with the reset gate filtering irrelevant past context.

**Step 4 — Final Hidden State Update:**
$$
h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t
$$
Performs a convex combination of the previous state and the new candidate, using the update gate as the interpolation weight.

**Parameter Count Comparison (3 Marks):**

Using $h = 64$, $d = 32$:

**GRU Parameters:**
$$
P_{\text{GRU}} = 3h(h + d) + 3h = 3(64)(64 + 32) + 3(64)
$$
$$
= 3 \times 64 \times 96 + 192 = 18432 + 192 = \mathbf{18{,}624 \text{ parameters}}
$$

**LSTM Parameters:**
$$
P_{\text{LSTM}} = 4h(h + d) + 4h = 4(64)(96) + 256
$$
$$
= 24576 + 256 = \mathbf{24{,}832 \text{ parameters}}
$$

**Difference:** GRU has **6,208 fewer parameters** than LSTM, representing a **~25% reduction** — a significant saving in memory and compute.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**Engineering Applications (3 Marks):**

GRU is deployed in numerous real-world systems:

1. **Natural Language Processing:** Machine translation, sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, text generation, and chatbots.
2. **Speech Recognition:** Acoustic modeling in systems like Google Voice and Apple Siri where low-latency inference is critical.
3. **Time-Series Forecasting:** Stock price prediction, weather forecasting, energy load prediction, and IoT sensor analytics.
4. **Music Generation:** Composing melodies where long-range temporal structure must be preserved.
5. **Video Captioning:** Generating natural language descriptions from video frame sequences.

**Advantages of GRU over LSTM (3 Marks):**

1. **Fewer Parameters (~25% less):** Reduced memory footprint and faster training, ideal for edge devices and mobile applications.
2. **Simpler Architecture:** Only 2 gates instead of 3, leading to easier implementation, debugging, and hyperparameter tuning.
3. **Comparable Performance:** On many small-to-medium datasets, GRU matches or even exceeds LSTM in accuracy while being more efficient.

**Disadvantages of GRU over LSTM (1 Mark):**

1. **Less Expressive on Complex Sequences:** LSTM's separate cell state provides more nuanced memory control, often outperforming GRU on tasks requiring very long-range dependencies (e.g., document-level language modeling).
2. **Less Empirical Track Record:** LSTM has a longer history of successful deployments in research and industry, while GRU is sometimes considered a less mature choice for safety-critical applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Part B-b):** When asked to "compare GRU and LSTM," students frequently list only advantages. **Always include at least one disadvantage** to demonstrate balanced critical analysis — examiners specifically look for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **GRU is a gated RNN** with **2 gates** (update + reset) and a **single hidden state** (no cell state).
- ✅ **Update gate equation:** $z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ — controls memory persistence.
- ✅ **Reset gate equation:** $r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ — controls how much past is forgotten in candidate.
- ✅ **Candidate state:** $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$ — uses Hadamard product, not matrix multiplication.
- ✅ **Final state is a convex combination:** $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$.
- ✅ **GRU parameter count:** $3h(h+d) + 3h$ — exactly **3/4 of LSTM's parameter count**.
- ✅ **Vanishing gradient mitigation:** The $(1 - z_t) \odot I$ term acts as a **learned gradient highway** when $z_t \to 0$.
- ✅ **Activation functions:** Sigmoid ($\sigma$) for gates (range 0–1), tanh for candidate state (range $-1$ to $+1$).
- ✅ **BPTT is the training algorithm** — unroll the network in time, apply back-propagation, sum gradients across time.
- ✅ **Key distinction from LSTM:** GRU merges forget + input gates into update gate, and merges cell state + hidden state.
- ✅ **Practical tip:** Choose GRU when **computational efficiency** matters more than absolute peak accuracy; choose LSTM for **very long sequences** or when **maximum memory capacity** is required.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** Forgetting the **element-wise product** $r_t \odot h_{t-1}$ — always write $\odot$, never a regular product.
- ✅ **Exam language:** Use "**mitigates**" (not "**solves**") the vanishing gradient problem — partial, not absolute, mitigation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Generative Models: Parameter Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Generative Models: Parameter Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Generative Model?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Generative Model** is a class of statistical or deep learning models that learn the underlying **joint probability distribution** $P(X, Y)$ or simply $P(X)$ over the input data, enabling the model to generate *new, previously unseen* data samples that resemble the training distribution.

Unlike **discriminative models** that model the conditional distribution $P(Y \mid X)$ (e.g., logistic regression, SVM), generative models attempt to capture **how the data is generated** in the first place.

### The Two Model Families in Machine Learning

| Aspect | Discriminative Model | Generative Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Goal** | Distinguish classes | Generate new samples |
| **Distribution** | $P(Y \mid X)$ | $P(X, Y)$ or $P(X)$ |
| **Output** | Decision boundary | Synthesized data points |
| **Examples** | Logistic Regression, CNN Classifier | Naive Bayes, VAE, GAN, Diffusion Models |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** In the 2024 scheme, the focus of Module 4 is on **probabilistic generative modeling**, distinguishing it from earlier modules that emphasized discriminative deep networks (CNNs, RNNs).

## 1.2 The Parameter Estimation Problem

Once we assume a generative model (e.g., "the data is drawn from a Gaussian distribution"), the model contains **parameters** $\theta$ that govern the shape of the distribution. **Parameter Estimation** is the process of finding the *best-fitting* values of $\theta$ given observed data.

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\max_{\theta} \; \mathcal{L}(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is an **objective function** (likelihood, posterior, or loss) and $\mathcal{D}$ is the observed dataset.

## 1.3 🎯 Intuitive Analogy — "The Guessing Game"

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Climate Detective** 🕵️
> Imagine you are a detective in Kerala during monsoon season. You collect **30 days of rainfall data** (data points). You *suspect* the rainfall follows a pattern (a statistical distribution — say, a Gaussian with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^{2}$). Your job is to estimate $\mu$ and $\sigma^{2}$ such that the observed 30 days of rainfall become **the most probable outcome** under your assumed model.
>
> - The **distribution** = your "suspect profile."
> - The **parameters** $\mu, \sigma^{2}$ = the "modus operandi" details.
> - The **observed data** = the evidence.
> - **Parameter estimation** = the act of matching the profile to the evidence as closely as possible.

This process is the cornerstone of every generative model — from simple Gaussian fitting to complex Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and diffusion models.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generative Modeling Pipeline

The pipeline of any generative model can be decomposed into **four logical stages**:

1. **Assume a parametric family** for the data distribution: $P(X \mid \theta)$, where $\theta = \{\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}, \ldots, \theta_{k}\}$.
2. **Collect a dataset** $\mathcal{D} = \{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(N)}\}$ drawn i.i.d. from the true (unknown) distribution $P^{*}(X)$.
3. **Estimate parameters** $\hat{\theta}$ that best fit $\mathcal{D}$ under the assumed model.
4. **Sample / Generate** new data $\tilde{x} \sim P(X \mid \hat{\theta})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why and How" Matters:** The *why* of step 1 is that we cannot model an unknown distribution without making structural assumptions (a form of inductive bias). The *how* of step 3 is what we call **parameter estimation** — the core of this topic.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Parameter Estimation

### 2.2.1 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)
The most foundational method. We find parameters that **maximize the probability of observing the data**.

$$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \; P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \arg\max_{\theta} \; \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x^{(i)} \mid \theta)$$

In practice, we maximize the **log-likelihood** for numerical stability:

$$\ell(\theta) = \log P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x^{(i)} \mid \theta)$$

### 2.2.2 Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Estimation
Incorporates a **prior belief** $P(\theta)$ over parameters via Bayes' theorem:

$$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \; P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \arg\max_{\theta} \; P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta)$$

The log-posterior becomes:

$$\log P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \underbrace{\log P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)}_{\text{log-likelihood}} + \underbrace{\log P(\theta)}_{\text{log-prior}} - \log P(\mathcal{D})$$

### 2.2.3 Bayesian Estimation
Maintains a **full posterior distribution** over $\theta$, not just a point estimate:

$$P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta)}{P(\mathcal{D})}$$

Predictions are made by **marginalizing** over $\theta$:

$$P(x_{\text{new}} \mid \mathcal{D}) = \int P(x_{\text{new}} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \, d\theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** MLE is the workhorse for training **VAEs, GMMs, and HMMs**. MAP is the natural bridge to **regularized neural networks** (L2 weight decay ≈ Gaussian prior). Full Bayesian estimation underpins **uncertainty quantification** in safety-critical systems (medical imaging, autonomous driving).

## 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Likelihood | $L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x^{(i)} \mid \theta)$ | Unitless, range $[0,1]$ |
| 2 | Log-Likelihood | $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x^{(i)} \mid \theta)$ | NLL = $-\ell$ used in losses |
| 3 | MLE Objective | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \ell(\theta)$ | First-order condition: $\nabla_{\theta} \ell = 0$ |
| 4 | Bayes' Rule | $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{P(\mathcal{D}\mid\theta)P(\theta)}{P(\mathcal{D})}$ | $P(\mathcal{D})$ is the evidence |
| 5 | MAP Objective | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \left[ \ell(\theta) + \log P(\theta) \right]$ | Prior acts as regularizer |
| 6 | Gaussian PDF | $P(x\mid\mu,\sigma^{2}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$ | $\mu$ ∈ ℝ, $\sigma^{2} > 0$ |
| 7 | Bernoulli PMF | $P(x\mid p) = p^{x}(1-p)^{1-x}, \quad x \in \{0,1\}$ | $p \in [0,1]$ |
| 8 | Sample Mean (MLE) | $\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)}$ | Units: same as $x$ |
| 9 | Sample Variance (MLE) | $\hat{\sigma}^{2} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x^{(i)}-\hat{\mu})^{2}$ | Biased; unbiased uses $N-1$ |
| 10 | KL Divergence | $D_{\text{KL}}(P \Vert Q) = \int P(x) \log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}dx$ | Asymmetric; ≥ 0 |
| 11 | Expected Complete Log-Likelihood | $\mathbb{E}_{Z \sim Q}[\log P(X,Z \mid \theta)]$ | Used in EM algorithm |
| 12 | ELBO | $\mathcal{L}(\theta,\phi;x) = \mathbb{E}_{q}[\log p(x\mid z)] - D_{\text{KL}}(q(z) \Vert p(z))$ | Foundation of VAEs |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Saver Note:** In the KTU board exam, always state the assumption of **i.i.d.** (independent and identically distributed) samples before writing the likelihood as a product. Skipping this costs 1 mark.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — MLE for a Univariate Gaussian

**Setup:** Let $x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(N)}$ be i.i.d. samples from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$. We wish to find $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}}$.

**Step 1:** Write the Gaussian probability density function for a single sample:

$$P(x^{(i)} \mid \mu, \sigma^{2}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x^{(i)}-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Form the joint likelihood over all $N$ i.i.d. samples:

$$L(\mu, \sigma^{2}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x^{(i)} \mid \mu, \sigma^{2})$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm to obtain the log-likelihood. The product becomes a sum (numerically stable):

$$\ell(\mu, \sigma^{2}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x^{(i)} \mid \mu, \sigma^{2})$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the Gaussian PDF and simplify. The constant term $-\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi)$ does not depend on $\theta$ and is often dropped during optimization:

$$\ell(\mu, \sigma^{2}) = -\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{N}{2}\log(\sigma^{2}) - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{(x^{(i)}-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}$$

**Step 5 (Estimating $\mu$):** Take the partial derivative with respect to $\mu$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{(x^{(i)}-\mu)}{\sigma^{2}} = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solve the linear equation for $\mu$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x^{(i)}-\mu) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)} = N\mu$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)}}$$

**Step 7 (Estimating $\sigma^{2}$):** Take the partial derivative with respect to $\sigma^{2}$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma^{2}} = -\frac{N}{2\sigma^{2}} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{(x^{(i)}-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{4}} = 0$$

**Step 8:** Multiply by $2\sigma^{4}$ and rearrange:

$$-N\sigma^{2} + \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x^{(i)}-\mu)^{2} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x^{(i)}-\hat{\mu})^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** $\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}}$ is **biased** — it systematically underestimates the true variance. The unbiased estimator divides by $(N-1)$ instead of $N$. This is a frequent exam trap.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — MLE for Bernoulli (Coin Toss)

**Setup:** Let $x^{(i)} \in \{0, 1\}$ represent outcomes of $N$ independent coin tosses. Find $\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}}$.

**Step 1:** Write the Bernoulli PMF:

$$P(x^{(i)} \mid p) = p^{x^{(i)}}(1-p)^{1-x^{(i)}}$$

**Step 2:** Form the log-likelihood:

$$\ell(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ x^{(i)}\log p + (1-x^{(i)})\log(1-p) \right]$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $p$ and equate to zero:

$$\frac{d\ell}{dp} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \frac{x^{(i)}}{p} - \frac{1-x^{(i)}}{1-p} \right] = 0$$

**Step 4:** Multiply through by $p(1-p)$ and solve:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)}(1-p) - \sum_{i=1}^{N}(1-x^{(i)})p = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)} - Np = 0$$

$$\boxed{\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)} = \frac{\text{number of Heads}}{\text{total tosses}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — MAP Estimation with Gaussian Prior

**Setup:** Assume likelihood $P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu) = \mathcal{N}(\mu \mid \mu_{0}, \sigma_{0}^{2})$ and prior $P(\mu) = \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^{2})$.

**Step 1:** Log-posterior = log-likelihood + log-prior (ignoring the evidence $P(\mathcal{D})$ which is constant):

$$\log P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto -\frac{1}{2\sigma_{0}^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x^{(i)}-\mu)^{2} - \frac{\mu^{2}}{2\tau^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $\mu$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{1}{\sigma_{0}^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x^{(i)}-\mu) - \frac{\mu}{\tau^{2}} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate $\mu$:

$$\frac{N\bar{x}}{\sigma_{0}^{2}} - \frac{N\mu}{\sigma_{0}^{2}} - \frac{\mu}{\tau^{2}} = 0$$

$$\mu \left( \frac{N}{\sigma_{0}^{2}} + \frac{1}{\tau^{2}} \right) = \frac{N\bar{x}}{\sigma_{0}^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{\frac{N}{\sigma_{0}^{2}}\,\bar{x}}{\frac{N}{\sigma_{0}^{2}} + \frac{1}{\tau^{2}}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** As $N \to \infty$, $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MAP}} \to \bar{x}$ (MLE dominates). As $\tau^{2} \to 0$ (strong prior), $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MAP}} \to 0$. The prior acts as a **soft constraint** preventing overfitting in low-data regimes — the same role played by **L2 regularization** in deep learning.

---

## 3.4 📊 Analytical Matrix: MLE vs MAP vs Bayesian

| Property | MLE | MAP | Full Bayesian |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Estimate Type** | Point estimate | Point estimate | Distribution over $\theta$ |
| **Uses Prior** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | Low | High (intractable integrals) |
| **Handles Uncertainty in $\theta$** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Tuning Required** | None | Prior hyperparameters | Prior + inference scheme |
| **Example Use-Case** | GMM, VAE | Regularized NN (L2) | Bayesian Neural Networks |
| **Optimality** | Asymptotically unbiased | Shrinks toward prior | Full posterior characterization |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generative Modeling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Dataset D] --> B[Assume Parametric Family P of X given theta]
    B --> C[Form Likelihood Function L of theta]
    C --> D[Choose Estimation Strategy]
    D --> E1[MLE]
    D --> E2[MAP Estimation]
    D --> E3[Full Bayesian Inference]
    E1 --> F[Point Estimate theta hat]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> G[Posterior Distribution of theta]
    F --> H[Generate New Samples from P of X given theta hat]
    G --> I[Integrate over theta to Predict]
    H --> J[New Synthetic Data]
    I --> J
```

## 4.2 Parameter Estimation Methods — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT BLOCK"]
        A1[Training Data x1 to xN]
    end

    subgraph MODEL["MODEL BLOCK"]
        B1[Parametric Distribution P of X given theta]
    end

    subgraph LOSS["OBJECTIVE BLOCK"]
        C1[Log-Likelihood log P of D given theta]
        C2[Log-Prior log P of theta]
        C3[Log-Posterior Sum of C1 and C2]
    end

    subgraph SOLVER["SOLVER BLOCK"]
        D1[Closed Form Solution]
        D2[Gradient Descent on theta]
        D3[Expectation Maximization EM]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT BLOCK"]
        E1[Estimated theta hat]
        E2[Posterior P of theta given D]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C2 --> C3
    C1 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C1 --> D3
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D3 --> E2
```

## 4.3 Comparative Flow — MLE vs MAP vs Bayesian

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Observed Data D] --> Q1{Use Prior P of theta?}
    Q1 -->|No| Path1[MLE Path]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Compute Full Posterior?}
    Q2 -->|No Approximate| Path2[MAP Path]
    Q2 -->|Yes Marginalize| Path3[Bayesian Path]

    Path1 --> R1[theta hat MLE equals argmax L of theta]
    Path2 --> R2[theta hat MAP equals argmax L times P of theta]
    Path3 --> R3[Predict by Integrating over theta]

    R1 --> Out1[Single Best Parameter Vector]
    R2 --> Out2[Single Best Parameter Vector Shrunk to Prior]
    R3 --> Out3[Full Predictive Distribution]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Exams:** When asked to "draw the architecture" in a 14-mark question, always show the **data → model → objective → optimizer → estimate** chain. Each box can earn partial credit.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`
**Define a generative model. How does it differ from a discriminative model?**

**Model Answer:**

A **generative model** learns the joint probability distribution $P(X, Y)$ or the data distribution $P(X)$, enabling it to **generate new samples** that resemble the training data. A **discriminative model**, in contrast, learns the conditional distribution $P(Y \mid X)$ and is used to **classify** or **predict labels** for given inputs.

| Feature | Generative | Discriminative |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Models | $P(X, Y)$ or $P(X)$ | $P(Y \mid X)$ |
| Use | Data generation | Classification |
| Example | Naive Bayes, GAN, VAE | Logistic Regression, SVM, CNN |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for discriminative definition, 1 for clear contrast.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]`
**What is Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)? State the MLE objective for a dataset $\mathcal{D}$.**

**Model Answer:**

MLE is a parameter estimation method that finds the parameter values $\hat{\theta}$ which **maximize the probability of observing the given dataset** under the assumed model.

**Objective Function:**

$$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \; L(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \arg\max_{\theta} \; \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(x^{(i)} \mid \theta)$$

In practice, the **log-likelihood** is maximized instead:

$$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x^{(i)} \mid \theta)$$

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for product form, 1 for log-likelihood justification.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ✅ **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Derive the Maximum Likelihood Estimates for the parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma^{2}$ of a univariate Gaussian distribution given $N$ i.i.d. samples. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation. How does it differ from MLE? Derive the MAP estimate for $\mu$ assuming a Gaussian likelihood $\mathcal{N}(\mu \mid \mu_{0}, \sigma_{0}^{2})$ and a Gaussian prior $\mathcal{N}(0, \tau^{2})$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📖 Model Solution to Question A(a)

**Given:** $x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(N)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ i.i.d.

**Step 1:** The Gaussian PDF for a single sample is:

$$P(x^{(i)} \mid \mu, \sigma^{2}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x^{(i)}-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right)$$

**Step 2:** The joint likelihood (product over i.i.d. samples):

$$L(\mu, \sigma^{2}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x^{(i)}-\mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right)$$

**Step 3:** The log-likelihood is:

$$\ell(\mu, \sigma^{2}) = -\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{N}{2}\log(\sigma^{2}) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x^{(i)}-\mu)^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu$:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x^{(i)}-\mu) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)}$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\sigma^{2}$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma^{2}} = -\frac{N}{2\sigma^{2}} + \frac{1}{2\sigma^{4}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x^{(i)}-\hat{\mu})^{2} = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $\sigma^{2}$:

$$\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x^{(i)}-\hat{\mu})^{2}$$

**Final Answer:** $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{x}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum(x^{(i)}-\bar{x})^{2}$.

---

#### 📖 Model Solution to Question A(b)

**MAP Estimation Definition:**
MAP finds $\theta$ that maximizes the posterior:

$$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \; P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \arg\max_{\theta} \left[ \log P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) + \log P(\theta) \right]$$

**Difference from MLE:**
- MLE uses **only the likelihood** $P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$.
- MAP adds a **prior** $P(\theta)$ that encodes prior beliefs and acts as a regularizer.
- As $N \to \infty$, MAP $\to$ MLE (likelihood dominates).

**Derivation:** Given Gaussian likelihood $P(x^{(i)} \mid \mu) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma_{0}^{2})$ and prior $P(\mu) = \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^{2})$:

$$\log P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto -\frac{1}{2\sigma_{0}^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x^{(i)}-\mu)^{2} - \frac{\mu^{2}}{2\tau^{2}}$$

Setting $\frac{d}{d\mu} = 0$:

$$\frac{1}{\sigma_{0}^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x^{(i)}-\mu) - \frac{\mu}{\tau^{2}} = 0$$

$$\hat{\mu}_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{\frac{N}{\sigma_{0}^{2}}\bar{x}}{\frac{N}{\sigma_{0}^{2}} + \frac{1}{\tau^{2}}}$$

**Interpretation:** This is a **weighted average** of the sample mean $\bar{x}$ and the prior mean (0). As $\tau^{2} \to \infty$ (weak prior), $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MAP}} \to \bar{x}$.

---

### ✅ **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Explain the three approaches to parameter estimation in generative models: MLE, MAP, and full Bayesian estimation. Compare their properties in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a Bernoulli generative model with $N$ coin toss observations $x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(N)} \in \{0, 1\}$, derive the MLE of the parameter $p$. Show that it is the sample proportion of heads. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📖 Model Solution to Question B(a)

**1. MLE (Maximum Likelihood Estimation):**
- Maximizes $L(\theta) = P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$.
- Produces a **single point estimate**.
- No prior required.
- Asymptotically unbiased and efficient.

**2. MAP (Maximum A Posteriori):**
- Maximizes $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)$.
- Requires a **prior distribution** over $\theta$.
- Equivalent to MLE with **log-prior as regularizer**.
- Reduces to MLE when $P(\theta)$ is uniform.

**3. Full Bayesian Estimation:**
- Computes the **entire posterior** $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$.
- Predictions marginalize over $\theta$: $P(x_{\text{new}} \mid \mathcal{D}) = \int P(x_{\text{new}} \mid \theta) P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) d\theta$.
- Most principled but **computationally expensive** (often requires MCMC or variational inference).

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | MLE | MAP | Bayesian |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Prior Used | No | Yes | Yes |
| Output | Point | Point | Distribution |
| Computational Cost | Low | Low | High |
| Handles Uncertainty | No | No | Yes |
| Equivalent to Regularization | None | L2 / L1 | Full posterior |

---

#### 📖 Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Step 1:** The Bernoulli likelihood for a single trial:

$$P(x^{(i)} \mid p) = p^{x^{(i)}}(1-p)^{1-x^{(i)}}$$

**Step 2:** The joint likelihood over $N$ i.i.d. trials:

$$L(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p^{x^{(i)}}(1-p)^{1-x^{(i)}}$$

**Step 3:** The log-likelihood:

$$\ell(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ x^{(i)}\log p + (1-x^{(i)})\log(1-p) \right]$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{d\ell}{dp} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)}}{p} - \frac{N - \sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)}}{1-p} = 0$$

**Step 5:** Cross-multiplying:

$$(1-p)\sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)} = p\left(N - \sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Let $N_{H} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x^{(i)}$ (number of heads). Then:

$$N_{H} - pN_{H} = pN - pN_{H} \;\Longrightarrow\; N_{H} = pN$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{N_{H}}{N} = \frac{\text{Number of Heads}}{\text{Total Tosses}}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Parameter Estimation Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the i.i.d. assumption** before writing the likelihood as a product → loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Not converting to log-likelihood** explicitly before differentiating → loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing MLE with MAP** in the final answer — MAP adds a $\log P(\theta)$ term. Examiners check this strictly.
> 4. **Failing to mention the bias of $\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}}$** in Gaussian derivations → loses **1–2 marks** depending on the paper.
> 5. **Dropping the constant terms** (like $-\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi)$) is acceptable in MLE but **NOT** when comparing two models. Always state what you are dropping and why.
> 6. **In MAP derivations, the evidence term $P(\mathcal{D})$ must be dropped** with explicit justification (it is independent of $\theta$).
> 7. **Diagrams must be labeled.** A flowchart with unlabeled boxes earns only partial marks (max 60% credit on diagram sub-parts).

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Parameter Estimation in Generative Models:**

- ⭐ **Generative model** = models $P(X)$ or $P(X,Y)$; can **generate new data**.
- ⭐ **Discriminative model** = models $P(Y \mid X)$; used for **classification**.
- ⭐ **MLE** = $\arg\max_{\theta} \prod_{i} P(x^{(i)} \mid \theta)$ — no prior, point estimate.
- ⭐ **MAP** = $\arg\max_{\theta} \left[ \log P(\mathcal{D}\mid\theta) + \log P(\theta) \right]$ — adds prior, point estimate.
- ⭐ **Bayesian** = full posterior, predictions via integration — handles uncertainty.
- ⭐ For **Gaussian** data: $\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{x}$, $\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum(x^{(i)}-\bar{x})^{2}$ (biased).
- ⭐ For **Bernoulli** data: $\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{N_{H}}{N}$ (sample proportion of heads).
- ⭐ **Log-likelihood is preferred** over raw likelihood for numerical stability.
- ⭐ **MAP with Gaussian prior = MLE + L2 regularization** — a crucial link to deep learning.
- ⭐ **As $N \to \infty$**, MLE, MAP, and Bayesian converge to the same answer.
- ⭐ **ELBO** = $\mathbb{E}_{q}[\log p(x\mid z)] - D_{\text{KL}}(q(z) \Vert p(z))$ is the foundation of **VAEs** (next sub-topic).
- ⭐ Always state the **i.i.d. assumption** explicitly in derivations.
- ⭐ **Bias of MLE variance:** $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{MLE}}] = \frac{N-1}{N}\sigma^{2} \neq \sigma^{2}$ — divide by $N-1$ for unbiasedness.
- ⭐ **Computational tip:** When closed-form MLE is unavailable, use **Gradient Descent** on $-\ell(\theta)$ or the **EM algorithm** for latent variable models.

---
