<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** is a statistical method used to estimate the parameters $\theta$ of an assumed probability distribution by finding the parameter values that **maximize the likelihood function** $L(\theta)$ — i.e., the probability of observing the given data under the assumed model.

Formally, given an observed dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ assumed to be drawn i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) from a distribution $p(x \mid \theta)$, the MLE estimate is:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \, L(\theta) = \arg\max_{\theta} \, p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \arg\max_{\theta} \, \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(x_i \mid \theta)
$$

Where:
- $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}}$ is the **maximum likelihood estimate** of the parameters.
- $L(\theta)$ is the **likelihood function** — a function of parameters $\theta$ for fixed observed data.
- $p(x_i \mid \theta)$ is the **conditional probability** of observing sample $x_i$ given the parameters $\theta$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Probability vs. Likelihood:**
> - **Probability** (forward direction): $P(\text{data} \mid \theta)$ — fixed parameters, vary the data.
> - **Likelihood** (inverse direction): $L(\theta \mid \text{data})$ — fixed data, vary the parameters.
> MLE answers: *"Which parameter value makes the data we observed most probable?"*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Best-Fit Storyteller":**
> Imagine you roll a mysterious coin 100 times and observe **68 heads**. You don't know if the coin is fair.
> - A **probability question** asks: *"If the coin is fair (P=0.5), what's the chance of getting 68 heads?"* → Very low.
> - A **likelihood question** asks: *"For which value of P (the bias) is getting 68 heads the MOST EXPECTED outcome?"* → Intuitively, P ≈ **0.68**.
>
> MLE formalizes this intuition: it picks the parameter value that makes the observed outcome the most *expected* outcome.

In deep learning, this same idea applies: given training data, we want the **network parameters (weights)** that make the observed outputs most probable under the model's predicted distribution.

## 1.3 Why MLE Matters in Deep Learning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> MLE is the **theoretical backbone** of many deep learning loss functions:
> - **Cross-Entropy Loss** (classification) ← MLE under **Bernoulli / Categorical** assumption.
> - **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** (regression) ← MLE under **Gaussian** noise assumption.
> - **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)** explicitly optimize variational lower bounds derived from MLE principles.
> - **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** can be interpreted through MLE-inspired density ratio estimation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of MLE

The MLE procedure follows a strict 5-step pipeline:

1. **Model Assumption:** Choose a parametric distribution family $p(x \mid \theta)$ (e.g., Gaussian, Bernoulli, Poisson).
2. **Construct the Likelihood:** Compute the joint probability of all observed samples assuming independence:
$$
L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(x_i \mid \theta)
$$
3. **Take the Logarithm (Log-Likelihood):** Convert products into sums for numerical stability and easier differentiation:
$$
\ell(\theta) = \log L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(x_i \mid \theta)
$$
4. **Differentiate & Set to Zero:** Compute $\frac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$ and solve for $\theta$.
5. **Validate the Solution:** Confirm the critical point is a **maximum** (second derivative test) and not a saddle point or minimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Logarithm?**
> - Products of many probabilities (each ≤ 1) lead to **underflow** (numbers too small for computers to represent).
> - $\log$ is a **monotonically increasing** function, so $\arg\max L(\theta) = \arg\max \ell(\theta)$.
> - Derivatives of sums are far easier than derivatives of products.

## 2.2 Properties of MLE Estimators

MLE estimators possess several **asymptotic properties** (true as $N \to \infty$):

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Consistency** | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} \to \theta_{\text{true}}$ as $N \to \infty$ |
| **Asymptotic Normality** | $\sqrt{N}(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} - \theta_{\text{true}}) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I^{-1}(\theta))$ |
| **Efficiency** | Achieves the **Cramér-Rao Lower Bound** — lowest possible variance among unbiased estimators |
| **Invariance** | If $\hat{\theta}$ is MLE of $\theta$, then $g(\hat{\theta})$ is MLE of $g(\theta)$ for any function $g$ |

Where $I(\theta)$ is the **Fisher Information Matrix**:
$$
I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \log p(x \mid \theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right] = E\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \log p(x \mid \theta)}{\partial \theta}\right)^2\right]
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Finite-Sample Caveat:** MLE is **not guaranteed to be unbiased** for small datasets. Bias correction (e.g., Bessel's correction for variance) may be required.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Likelihood Function | $L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(x_i \mid \theta)$ | Joint probability of data under parameters $\theta$ |
| Log-Likelihood | $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(x_i \mid \theta)$ | Logarithm of the likelihood |
| MLE Objective | $\hat{\theta} = \arg\max_\theta \, \ell(\theta)$ | Equivalent to $\arg\max L(\theta)$ |
| Stationarity Condition | $\dfrac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$ | First-order necessary condition |
| Fisher Information | $I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\dfrac{\partial^2 \ell}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$ | Measures curvature of log-likelihood |
| Cramér-Rao Bound | $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \dfrac{1}{N \cdot I(\theta)}$ | Lower bound on estimator variance |
| Bernoulli Likelihood | $L(p) = p^{k}(1-p)^{N-k}$ | $k$ successes in $N$ trials |
| Gaussian Likelihood | $L(\mu,\sigma^2) = \prod \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} e^{-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | Normal distribution assumption |
| MLE Mean (Gaussian) | $\hat{\mu} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Sample mean |
| MLE Variance (Gaussian) | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \hat{\mu})^2$ | **Biased** — needs $N-1$ for unbiasedness |
| MLE for Bernoulli | $\hat{p} = \dfrac{k}{N}$ | Sample proportion of successes |
| Connection to KL | $\max_\theta \ell(\theta) \;\Leftrightarrow\; \min_\theta \, D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_\theta\right)$ | MLE minimizes KL divergence |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

In deep learning pipelines, MLE translates directly into **loss functions**:
- **Classification** → minimize negative log-likelihood → **Cross-Entropy Loss**
- **Regression** (Gaussian noise) → minimize negative log-likelihood → **MSE Loss**
- **Probabilistic models** → MLE provides the theoretical justification for maximum likelihood training.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Used Below:** All derivations assume i.i.d. samples $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ with $k$ denoting count of "successes" in Bernoulli case, and $\mu, \sigma^2$ denoting the true mean and variance in Gaussian case.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — MLE for Bernoulli Distribution

**Setup:** A coin has unknown bias $p$ (probability of heads). We observe $N$ tosses with $k$ heads.

**Step 1:** Write the probability of a single observation:
$$
P(x_i \mid p) = p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i}
$$

**Step 2:** Construct the likelihood (joint probability over all samples, assuming independence):
$$
L(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify by counting successes $k = \sum x_i$ and failures $N-k = \sum(1-x_i)$:
$$
L(p) = p^{k}(1-p)^{N-k}
$$

**Step 4:** Take the logarithm to obtain the log-likelihood:
$$
\ell(p) = \log L(p) = k \log p + (N-k)\log(1-p)
$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate with respect to $p$ and set to zero:
$$
\frac{d\ell}{dp} = \frac{k}{p} - \frac{N-k}{1-p} = 0
$$

**Step 6:** Solve the equation algebraically:
$$
\frac{k}{p} = \frac{N-k}{1-p}
$$
$$
k(1-p) = p(N-k)
$$
$$
k - kp = pN - pk
$$
$$
k = pN
$$
$$
\boxed{\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{k}{N}}
$$

**Step 7:** Verify it is a maximum via the second derivative test:
$$
\frac{d^2\ell}{dp^2} = -\frac{k}{p^2} - \frac{N-k}{(1-p)^2} < 0 \quad \forall \, p \in (0,1)
$$

Since the second derivative is strictly negative, the critical point is indeed a **maximum**. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MLE for Gaussian Mean ($\mu$)

**Setup:** Assume data is drawn from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ **known**.

**Step 1:** Single-sample density:
$$
p(x_i \mid \mu) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Likelihood over all $N$ samples:
$$
L(\mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Log-likelihood (constants factor out):
$$
\ell(\mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ -\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right]
$$

**Step 4:** Discard the constant term $-\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2)$:
$$
\ell(\mu) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu)^2 + C
$$

**Step 5:** Maximizing $\ell(\mu)$ is equivalent to minimizing the squared error:
$$
\min_{\mu} \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2
$$

**Step 6:** Differentiate and set to zero:
$$
\frac{d\ell}{d\mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu) = 0
$$

**Step 7:** Solve:
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i - N\mu = 0
$$
$$
\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MLE for Gaussian Variance ($\sigma^2$)

**Setup:** Now assume $\mu$ is **known** (or use $\hat{\mu}$ from above) and estimate $\sigma^2$.

**Step 1:** Log-likelihood as a function of $\sigma^2$:
$$
\ell(\sigma^2) = -\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $\sigma^2$:
$$
\frac{d\ell}{d\sigma^2} = -\frac{N}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2
$$

**Step 3:** Set the derivative to zero:
$$
-\frac{N}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2 = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $2(\sigma^2)^2$:
$$
-N\sigma^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2 = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\sigma^2$:
$$
\boxed{\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biased Estimator Warning:** $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}}$ is **biased** because it uses the MLE of $\mu$ (which itself is estimated from data). The **unbiased** estimator divides by $N-1$ (Bessel's correction):
> $$
> s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2
> $$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — From MLE to Cross-Entropy Loss (Classification)

**Setup:** For a binary classification problem, model predicts $p_\theta(y=1 \mid x)$ for input $x$.

**Step 1:** Likelihood for a single labeled sample $(x_i, y_i)$:
$$
P(y_i \mid x_i, \theta) = p_\theta(y_i \mid x_i) = \hat{p}_i^{y_i}(1-\hat{p}_i)^{1-y_i}
$$
where $\hat{p}_i$ is the model's predicted probability of class 1.

**Step 2:** Negative log-likelihood over the dataset:
$$
-\ell(\theta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize this is the **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) Loss**:
$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)\right]}
$$

**Step 4:** For multi-class ($K$ classes), the **Categorical Cross-Entropy** follows analogously:
$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{\text{CCE}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{i,k} \log \hat{p}_{i,k}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — From MLE to MSE Loss (Regression)

**Setup:** Assume target $y_i = f(x_i) + \epsilon$ with $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ (i.e., Gaussian noise on top of the model's prediction $\hat{y}_i$).

**Step 1:** Conditional likelihood:
$$
p(y_i \mid x_i, \theta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Negative log-likelihood:
$$
-\ell(\theta) = \frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) + \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
$$

**Step 3:** Drop constants (irrelevant to optimization):
$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** MSE loss is **NOT universally optimal** — it is the correct MLE loss **only if** the residual noise is Gaussian. For other noise distributions, a different loss emerges (e.g., **MAE** for Laplacian noise).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary of MLE Solutions

| Distribution | Parameter | MLE Estimate |
|---|---|---|
| Bernoulli($p$) | $p$ | $\hat{p} = k/N$ |
| Gaussian($\mu$, $\sigma^2$ known) | $\mu$ | $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$ |
| Gaussian($\mu$ known, $\sigma^2$ unknown) | $\sigma^2$ | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \mu)^2$ |
| Exponential($\lambda$) | $\lambda$ | $\hat{\lambda} = N / \sum x_i$ |
| Poisson($\lambda$) | $\lambda$ | $\hat{\lambda} = \bar{x}$ |
| Uniform(0, $\theta$) | $\theta$ | $\hat{\theta} = \max(x_i)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The MLE Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Observed Data D"] --> B["Step 1: Choose Parametric Model p(x|theta)"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Build Likelihood L(theta) = Product of p(xi|theta)"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Take Log to Get log-Likelihood l(theta)"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Differentiate dl/d theta = 0"]
    E --> F{"Step 5: Solve Algebraically"}
    F --> G["Step 6: Second Derivative Test"]
    G --> H{"Is it a Maximum?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["OUTPUT: theta_MLE estimate"]
    H -- No --> J["REJECT: Re-evaluate Model or Use Numerical Optimization"]
    I --> K["Use theta_MLE for Prediction or as Input to Deep Learning Pipeline"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — MLE in Deep Learning Context

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["Training Data X, Labels Y"]
    end
    subgraph MODEL["PROBABILISTIC MODEL"]
        B1["Neural Network f_theta"]
        B2["Output Distribution p(y|x, theta)"]
        B1 --> B2
    end
    subgraph MLE["MLE OPTIMIZATION"]
        C1["Construct Likelihood L(theta)"]
        C2["Compute Log-Likelihood l(theta)"]
        C3["Negate to get Loss J(theta) = -l(theta)"]
        C4["Backpropagation and Gradient Descent"]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        D1["Optimized Parameters theta_star"]
        D2["Predictions and Probabilities"]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B2 --> C1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> B1
    D1 --> D2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — MLE to Common Loss Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MLE: Maximize log P(D|theta)"] --> B{"Choose Noise Assumption"}
    B -- "Gaussian Noise" --> C["MSE Loss: Mean Squared Error"]
    B -- "Bernoulli / Categorical" --> D["Cross-Entropy Loss"]
    B -- "Laplacian Noise" --> E["MAE Loss: Mean Absolute Error"]
    B -- "Poisson Counts" --> F["Poisson Negative Log-Likelihood"]
    C --> G["Used for Regression Tasks"]
    D --> H["Used for Classification Tasks"]
    E --> I["Robust Regression"]
    F --> J["Count Data Modeling"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Define Maximum Likelihood Estimation. Explain the role of the log-likelihood function in MLE.**

**Model Answer:**

Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) is a statistical method for estimating the parameters $\theta$ of a probability distribution by maximizing the **likelihood function** $L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(x_i \mid \theta)$ — the probability of observing the given data under assumed parameters.

The **log-likelihood function** $\ell(\theta) = \log L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(x_i \mid \theta)$ plays three key roles:

1. **Numerical Stability:** Converts products of small probabilities (prone to underflow) into sums of manageable numbers.
2. **Mathematical Convenience:** Sums are easier to differentiate than products, simplifying the optimization $\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \theta} = 0$.
3. **Monotonicity Preservation:** Since $\log$ is monotonically increasing, $\arg\max L(\theta) = \arg\max \ell(\theta)$, so optimization is equivalent.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for log-likelihood roles]**

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]**
> **State and explain any three properties of Maximum Likelihood Estimators.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Consistency:** As the sample size $N \to \infty$, the MLE converges to the true parameter value: $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} \to \theta_{\text{true}}$. This means larger datasets give increasingly accurate estimates.

2. **Asymptotic Efficiency:** MLE achieves the **Cramér-Rao Lower Bound** — the minimum possible variance among all unbiased estimators, formally $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}}) \geq \frac{1}{N \cdot I(\theta)}$.

3. **Invariance (Functional Equivariance):** If $\hat{\theta}$ is the MLE of $\theta$, then for any function $g$, the MLE of $g(\theta)$ is $g(\hat{\theta})$. Example: If $\hat{\sigma}^2$ is MLE of variance, then $\hat{\sigma} = \sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2}$ is the MLE of the standard deviation.

**[3 Marks: 1 mark per property]**

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module-Level Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted from Past Paper]**
> **(a)** Derive the Maximum Likelihood Estimate for the parameter $p$ of a **Bernoulli distribution** given $N$ i.i.d. samples with $k$ successes. Show all steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the MLE for both the **mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$** of a Gaussian distribution, treating them as unknown. Comment on whether the variance estimate is biased. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution:

**Step 1:** Single-sample probability mass function (PMF) of Bernoulli:
$$
P(x_i \mid p) = p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i}, \quad x_i \in \{0,1\}
$$

**Step 2:** Likelihood over $N$ i.i.d. samples:
$$
L(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i} = p^{\sum x_i}(1-p)^{N - \sum x_i} = p^{k}(1-p)^{N-k}
$$

**Step 3:** Log-likelihood:
$$
\ell(p) = \log L(p) = k \log p + (N-k)\log(1-p)
$$

**Step 4:** First derivative:
$$
\frac{d\ell}{dp} = \frac{k}{p} - \frac{N-k}{1-p}
$$

**Step 5:** Set to zero and solve:
$$
\frac{k}{p} = \frac{N-k}{1-p} \implies k(1-p) = p(N-k) \implies k = pN
$$
$$
\boxed{\hat{p}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{k}{N}}
$$

**Step 6:** Second derivative verification:
$$
\frac{d^2\ell}{dp^2} = -\frac{k}{p^2} - \frac{N-k}{(1-p)^2} < 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Maximum)}
$$

**[7 Marks: 1 setup + 1 likelihood + 1 log + 1 derivative + 1 solve + 1 verify + 1 final boxed answer]**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution:

**Step 1:** Single-sample Gaussian density:
$$
p(x_i \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Log-likelihood (over $N$ samples):
$$
\ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2
$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $\mu$ and set to zero:
$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu) = 0 \implies \boxed{\hat{\mu}_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}
$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate with respect to $\sigma^2$ and set to zero:
$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma^2} = -\frac{N}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2 = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Solve:
$$
\boxed{\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \hat{\mu})^2}
$$

**Step 6 (Bias Comment):** The estimator $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}}$ is **biased** because $E[\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{MLE}}] = \frac{N-1}{N}\sigma^2 \neq \sigma^2$. The unbiased correction (Bessel's correction) divides by $N-1$:
$$
s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \hat{\mu})^2 \quad \text{where} \quad E[s^2] = \sigma^2
$$

**[7 Marks: 1 density + 1 log-likelihood + 1.5 d/dμ + 1.5 d/dσ² + 1 solve + 1 bias comment]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]**
> **(a)** Starting from the MLE principle, show that minimizing the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** loss is equivalent to performing MLE under a **Gaussian noise assumption**. Derive the MSE loss formally. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Similarly, derive the **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) Loss** from MLE under the assumption of Bernoulli-distributed outputs. Show how this generalizes to multi-class classification. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution:

**Step 1: Setup.** Let the true label be $y_i$ and the model prediction be $\hat{y}_i = f_\theta(x_i)$. Assume:
$$
y_i = \hat{y}_i + \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)
$$

**Step 2: Likelihood.** Conditional probability of observing $y_i$:
$$
p(y_i \mid x_i, \theta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Joint log-likelihood over $N$ i.i.d. samples:**
$$
\ell(\theta) = -\frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
$$

**Step 4: Negative log-likelihood (loss function):**
$$
\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\ell(\theta) = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \frac{N}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2)
$$

**Step 5: Drop constants irrelevant to optimization:**
$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}
$$

**Conclusion:** Minimizing MSE ⇔ Maximizing log-likelihood under Gaussian noise assumption.

**[7 Marks: 1 setup + 1 likelihood + 1 log-likelihood + 1 NLL + 1 simplify + 1 box + 1 conclusion]**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution:

**Step 1: Setup.** For binary classification, model outputs $\hat{p}_i = p_\theta(y_i = 1 \mid x_i)$. The label $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$.

**Step 2: Single-sample likelihood (Bernoulli):**
$$
p(y_i \mid x_i, \theta) = \hat{p}_i^{y_i}(1 - \hat{p}_i)^{1-y_i}
$$

**Step 3: Joint log-likelihood:**
$$
\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1 - \hat{p}_i)\right]
$$

**Step 4: Negate to form the loss (BCE):**
$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1 - \hat{p}_i)\right]}
$$

**Step 5: Generalization to Multi-class (Categorical):** For $K$ classes with one-hot encoded labels $y_{i,k}$ and predicted probabilities $\hat{p}_{i,k}$:
$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{\text{CCE}}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{i,k} \log \hat{p}_{i,k}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Cross-entropy loss is the MLE loss for categorical outputs — the theoretical justification for its use in classification tasks.

**[7 Marks: 1 setup + 1 likelihood + 1 log + 1 negate + 1 box + 1 multi-class + 1 conclusion]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the log-likelihood step** — Many students maximize $L(\theta)$ directly without converting to $\ell(\theta)$, leading to unmanageable derivatives. **(–2 marks typical)**
> 2. **Skipping the second derivative test** — KTU expects confirmation that the critical point is a maximum, not a minimum or saddle. **(–1 to 2 marks)**
> 3. **Confusing MLE variance with unbiased variance** — The MLE divides by $N$; the unbiased estimator divides by $N-1$. Examiners specifically test this distinction. **(–1 mark)**
> 4. **Not stating the i.i.d. assumption** — Without it, the joint likelihood factorization $L = \prod p(x_i \mid \theta)$ is **invalid**. **(–1 mark)**
> 5. **In multi-part questions, presenting only the answer without the derivation chain** — Always show the full algebraic transition. **(–2 to 3 marks)**
> 6. **Confusing likelihood $L(\theta)$ with probability $P$** — Likelihood is a function of $\theta$ for *fixed* data; probability is over data for *fixed* $\theta$. Examiners frequently test this. **(–1 mark)**

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Maximum Likelihood Estimation**

- ✅ **MLE definition:** Find $\theta$ that maximizes $L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(x_i \mid \theta)$ — i.e., makes observed data most probable.
- ✅ **Log-likelihood is the workhorse:** Always convert to $\ell(\theta) = \sum \log p(x_i \mid \theta)$ for numerical stability and easier calculus.
- ✅ **i.i.d. assumption is non-negotiable** — required for the product factorization of the likelihood.
- ✅ **Five-step recipe:** (1) Assume model, (2) Write likelihood, (3) Log it, (4) Differentiate & set to 0, (5) Verify max.
- ✅ **Bernoulli MLE:** $\hat{p} = k/N$ — the sample proportion of successes.
- ✅ **Gaussian MLE (mean):** $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$ — the sample mean.
- ✅ **Gaussian MLE (variance):** $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \hat{\mu})^2$ — **biased**, divide by $N-1$ for unbiasedness.
- ✅ **Cross-Entropy Loss = MLE for Bernoulli / Categorical** distributions.
- ✅ **MSE Loss = MLE under Gaussian noise** assumption — not universally optimal.
- ✅ **Properties:** Consistency, Asymptotic Normality, Efficiency (Cramér-Rao bound), Invariance.
- ✅ **Fisher Information:** $I(\theta) = -E[\partial^2 \ell / \partial \theta^2]$ — measures curvature of the log-likelihood.
- ✅ **Cramér-Rao Bound:** $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{N \cdot I(\theta)}$ — MLE attains this asymptotically.
- ✅ **Connection to KL Divergence:** $\max \ell(\theta) \Leftrightarrow \min D_{\text{KL}}(p_{\text{data}} \Vert p_\theta)$.
- ✅ **In deep learning:** Negative log-likelihood = Loss function; backpropagation computes $\nabla_\theta \ell(\theta)$ to update weights.
- ✅ **MLE is NOT Bayesian** — MLE gives a point estimate; Bayesian methods give a full posterior distribution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GANs: Adversarial Training, Discriminator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** GANs: Adversarial Training, Discriminator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)?

A **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is a deep generative modelling framework introduced by *Goodfellow et al.* in 2014. It consists of **two neural networks** — a **Generator (G)** and a **Discriminator (D)** — that are trained *simultaneously* through an **adversarial (opposing) objective**. The Generator learns the underlying data distribution in order to synthesise new samples, while the Discriminator learns to distinguish "real" samples (drawn from the training set) from "fake" samples (produced by the Generator).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A GAN is a minimax two-player game between two differentiable functions, $G : \mathcal{Z} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$ (Generator, mapping latent noise $z$ to data space) and $D : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow [0,1]$ (Discriminator, mapping a data sample to the probability that it is real). The networks are parametrised by $\theta_G$ and $\theta_D$ respectively, and are updated in alternation using stochastic gradient descent.

### 1.2 What is "Adversarial Training"?

**Adversarial Training** refers to the procedure in which the Generator and the Discriminator are optimised *against* each other. The Discriminator is trained to **maximise** its ability to correctly classify real vs. fake samples, while the Generator is trained to **minimise** the Discriminator's ability to do so. Because the loss of one network becomes the *gain* of the other, the gradients flowing back into $G$ come from a *moving, adversarial target* — namely, the Discriminator itself.

### 1.3 What is the Discriminator?

The **Discriminator $D(x; \theta_D)$** is a binary classifier (typically a deep Convolutional Neural Network) that takes an input $x$ (an image, a spectrogram, or any data vector) and outputs a single scalar probability $\in [0,1]$ representing the likelihood that $x$ was drawn from the *true* data distribution $p_{\text{data}}$ rather than from the Generator's distribution $p_g$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Board Exam:** The Discriminator is *not* a stand-alone classifier — it is an **adaptive loss function** for the Generator. As $D$ improves, the gradient signal it provides to $G$ becomes sharper, pushing $G$ to produce increasingly realistic samples.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Counterfeiter vs. The Detective

Imagine a **counterfeiter (Generator)** trying to forge priceless paintings and a **police detective (Discriminator)** whose sole job is to spot forgeries:

1. At first, the counterfeiter produces crude fakes. The detective easily catches them → $D$ achieves high accuracy.
2. The detective's feedback (gradients) tells the counterfeiter *where* the fakes look wrong.
3. The counterfeiter improves. The detective is forced to learn subtler cues (brush strokes, pigment chemistry, etc.).
4. This cat-and-mouse escalation continues until the counterfeiter's fakes are **indistinguishable from the originals** — at which point the detective can do no better than a 50/50 coin flip.

The mathematical formalisation of this "cat-and-mouse" game is the **minimax value function** described in Section 2.

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU Mistake to Avoid:** Many students write that the Generator and Discriminator "cooperate". They do *not*. Their objectives are strictly **opposed**. The *equilibrium* is cooperative, but the *training signal* is adversarial.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Adversarial (Minimax) Objective Function

The entire GAN framework is governed by a single **value function** $V(D, G)$, which $D$ tries to *maximise* and $G$ tries to *minimise*:

$$
V(D, G) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{x \,\sim\, p_{\text{data}}(x)} \!\big[\,\log D(x)\,\big] \;+\; \mathbb{E}_{z \,\sim\, p_z(z)} \!\big[\,\log\!\big(1 - D(G(z))\big)\,\big]
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Domain / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $p_{\text{data}}(x)$ | True underlying data distribution | Probability density over $\mathcal{X}$ |
| $p_z(z)$ | Prior noise distribution (usually $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ or $U[-1, 1]$) | Probability density over $\mathcal{Z}$ |
| $G(z)$ | Generator mapping: noise $\rightarrow$ fake sample | $\mathcal{Z} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$ |
| $D(x)$ | Discriminator output (probability $x$ is real) | $[0, 1]$ |
| $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ | Expectation operator | Scalar |
| $\theta_G, \theta_D$ | Trainable parameters of $G$ and $D$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n_G}, \mathbb{R}^{n_D}$ |
| $\mathcal{L}_D$ | Discriminator loss (binary cross-entropy) | Scalar |
| $\mathcal{L}_G^{\text{ns}}$ | Non-saturating Generator loss | Scalar |
| $\text{JSD}(\cdot \vert\vert \cdot)$ | Jensen-Shannon Divergence | $[0, \log 2]$ |
| $\nabla_{\theta_D}, \nabla_{\theta_G}$ | Parameter gradients w.r.t. $D$ and $G$ | Vectors |
| $k$ | Number of $D$ updates per $G$ update (often $k=1$) | Integer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative (non-saturating) Generator Loss.** In practice, the term $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ provides *very small gradients* early in training (when $D$ easily rejects fakes). Goodfellow's paper therefore recommends training $G$ to **maximise** $\log D(G(z))$ instead — equivalent at the optimum but with much stronger gradients at the start.

$$
\mathcal{L}_G^{\text{ns}} \;=\; -\,\mathbb{E}_{z \,\sim\, p_z(z)} \!\big[\,\log D\!\big(G(z)\big)\,\big]
$$

## 2.2 Why is the Discriminator a *Critic*, not just a Classifier?

Although $D$ is implemented as a binary classifier with sigmoid output, mathematically it behaves as a **learned distance / divergence estimator**. Concretely:

- An *optimal* discriminator computes the ratio of true to model density (derived in Section 3):

$$
D^*(x) \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) \,+\, p_g(x)}
$$

- Substituting $D^*$ back into $V$ yields a Jensen-Shannon Divergence between $p_{\text{data}}$ and $p_g$:

$$
V(G, D^*) \;=\; -\log 4 \;+\; 2\,\cdot\,\text{JSD}\!\big(p_{\text{data}} \,\big\Vert\, p_g\big)
$$

Thus *minimising* $V$ w.r.t. $G$ is equivalent to *minimising the JSD* between the generated and real distributions.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Training Logic

The training loop alternates gradient updates:

1. **Sample a minibatch** of $m$ noise vectors $\{z^{(1)}, \dots, z^{(m)}\} \sim p_z$ and $m$ real examples $\{x^{(1)}, \dots, x^{(m)}\} \sim p_{\text{data}}$.
2. **Update the Discriminator** (ascend its stochastic gradient):

$$
\theta_D \;\leftarrow\; \theta_D \;+\; \eta \;\nabla_{\theta_D} \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\!\Big[\,\log D(x^{(i)}) + \log\!\big(1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))\big)\Big]
$$

3. **Update the Generator** (descend its stochastic gradient, using the *non-saturating* loss):

$$
\theta_G \;\leftarrow\; \theta_G \;-\; \eta \;\nabla_{\theta_G} \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\log D\!\big(G(z^{(i)})\big)
$$

4. **Repeat** until a stopping criterion is met (fixed epochs, fixed $V$ value, or visual quality).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

GAN-based adversarial training is the foundation of state-of-the-art systems in:

- **Computer Vision:** high-resolution image synthesis (StyleGAN, BigGAN), image-to-image translation (Pix2Pix, CycleGAN), super-resolution (SRGAN), data augmentation.
- **Medical Imaging:** synthesising tumour scans to balance rare-disease datasets.
- **Audio & Speech:** voice cloning (WaveGAN), music generation.
- **NLP:** text generation via GAN-style reinforcement objectives.
- **Anomaly Detection:** the Discriminator's confidence is used as an anomaly score in industrial defect detection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** When asked "what does the Discriminator output?", always answer *"a probability that the input is real (drawn from $p_{\text{data}}$), not a class label."* Examiners consistently test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Discriminator $D^*(x)$

**Goal:** Given a *fixed* Generator $G$, find the Discriminator function $D$ that maximises $V(D, G)$.

**Step 1 — Rewrite the expectations as integrals.** The two expectation terms in $V(D,G)$ can be expressed as integrals over $x \in \mathcal{X}$ by noting that $G(z)$ produces samples whose distribution is $p_g(x)$:

$$
V(D, G) \;=\; \int_{\mathcal{X}} p_{\text{data}}(x)\,\log D(x)\,dx \;+\; \int_{\mathcal{X}} p_g(x)\,\log\!\big(1 - D(x)\big)\,dx
$$

$$
V(D, G) \;=\; \int_{\mathcal{X}} \Big[\,p_{\text{data}}(x)\,\log D(x) \;+\; p_g(x)\,\log\!\big(1 - D(x)\big)\Big]\,dx
$$

**Step 2 — Pointwise optimisation.** Because the integrand is a function of $D(x)$ alone at every point $x$, and $D(x)$ can take any value in $[0,1]$ *independently* at each $x$, the global maximum is found by maximising the integrand pointwise.

For a fixed $x$, define $a = p_{\text{data}}(x)$, $b = p_g(x)$, and $y = D(x) \in [0,1]$. Maximise the scalar function:

$$
f(y) \;=\; a\,\log y \;+\; b\,\log(1 - y)
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and set to zero.**

$$
\frac{df}{dy} \;=\; \frac{a}{y} \;-\; \frac{b}{1 - y} \;=\; 0
$$

$$
\frac{a}{y} \;=\; \frac{b}{1 - y}
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
a\,(1 - y) \;=\; b\,y
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
a \;-\; a\,y \;=\; b\,y
$$

$$
a \;=\; y\,(a + b)
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
y^* \;=\; \frac{a}{a + b}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back** $a = p_{\text{data}}(x)$ and $b = p_g(x)$:

$$
\boxed{\;D^*(x) \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) \,+\, p_g(x)}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Second-derivative check** (confirm maximum):

$$
\frac{d^2 f}{dy^2} \;=\; -\frac{a}{y^2} \;-\; \frac{b}{(1 - y)^2} \;<\; 0 \quad \text{(since } a, b \geq 0 \text{ and } y \in (0,1)\text{)}
$$

Hence $y^*$ is indeed a **maximum** of $f(y)$, confirming $D^*$ globally maximises $V$ pointwise. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Global Value at the Optimum

**Step 1 — Substitute $D^*(x)$ into $V$.** Note $1 - D^*(x) = \dfrac{p_g(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$.

$$
V(G, D^*) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}\!\left[\,\log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}\right] \;+\; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_g}\!\left[\,\log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Add and subtract $\log 2$ inside each expectation** to expose the KL-divergence form. Recall that for any distribution $P$:

$$
\text{KL}(P \,\Vert\, Q) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P}\!\left[\,\log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\right]
$$

After algebra (use $p_{\text{data}} + p_g$ as the mixture $M = \tfrac{1}{2}(p_{\text{data}} + p_g)$):

$$
V(G, D^*) \;=\; -\log 4 \;+\; \text{KL}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\big\Vert\, M\right) \;+\; \text{KL}\!\left(p_g \,\big\Vert\, M\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the Jensen-Shannon Divergence.** By definition:

$$
\text{JSD}(P \,\Vert\, Q) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{KL}\!\left(P \,\big\Vert\, M\right) \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{KL}\!\left(Q \,\big\Vert\, M\right), \quad M = \tfrac{P+Q}{2}
$$

Multiplying through:

$$
V(G, D^*) \;=\; -\log 4 \;+\; 2\,\cdot\,\text{JSD}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\big\Vert\, p_g\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Global minimum analysis.** $\text{JSD} \geq 0$, with equality iff $p_{\text{data}} = p_g$ almost everywhere. Therefore:

$$
\min_G V(G, D^*) \;=\; -\log 4 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad p_g \;=\; p_{\text{data}}
$$

This is the **Nash equilibrium** of the GAN game. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Gradient of the Discriminator Loss

The standard discriminator loss (binary cross-entropy on the two-class problem "real" vs. "fake") is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_D \;=\; -\,\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\!\Big[\,\log D(x^{(i)}) \;+\; \log\!\big(1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))\big)\Big]
$$

The gradient w.r.t. $\theta_D$ for a single sample is:

$$
\nabla_{\theta_D}\,\mathcal{L}_D^{(i)} \;=\; \left[\,\frac{\partial D(x^{(i)})}{\partial \theta_D}\,\frac{1}{D(x^{(i)})}\right] \;-\; \left[\,\frac{\partial D(G(z^{(i)}))}{\partial \theta_D}\,\frac{1}{1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))}\right]
$$

## 3.4 Non-Saturating Generator Loss — Why and How

The "saturating" form $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ has derivative:

$$
\frac{d}{dD}\log(1 - D) \;=\; -\frac{1}{1 - D}
$$

When $D$ is confident (i.e., $D(G(z)) \approx 0$ early in training), this derivative is approximately $-1$ — *constant*. The Generator therefore receives no informative gradient signal about *how* to improve.

In contrast, the non-saturating loss $-\log D(G(z))$ has derivative:

$$
\frac{d}{dD}\!\big[-\log D\big] \;=\; -\frac{1}{D}
$$

When $D(G(z)) \approx 0$, this derivative $\to -\infty$, providing a *strong* push for $G$ to produce samples that $D$ finds more convincing.

## 3.5 Practical Discriminator Training Heuristics (Frequently Asked in ESE)

| Heuristic | Effect |
| :--- | :--- |
| One-sided label smoothing ($y = 0.9$ for real) | Discourages $D$ from becoming over-confident; mild regularisation. |
| Use $k = 1$ or $k = 2$ discriminator updates per generator update | Keeps $D$ slightly ahead of $G$ so it provides useful gradients. |
| Avoid BatchNorm inside $D$ (or use LayerNorm) | Prevents $D$ from leaking information across the batch. |
| Use LeakyReLU in $D$, not ReLU | Allows non-zero gradient when $D$ output is saturated near 0. |
| Spectral Normalisation on $D$ | Bounds Lipschitz constant of $D$, stabilising training. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GAN Adversarial Training Loop — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Latent Noise z ~ p_z] --> B[Generator G with thetaG]
    B --> C[Fake Sample G(z)]
    D[Real Sample x ~ p_data] --> E{Discriminator D with thetaD}
    C --> E
    E --> F[D x: probability real]
    E --> G[D G z: probability real]
    F --> H[Compute Discriminator Loss L_D]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Update thetaD by ascending gradient of L_D]
    C --> J[Non Saturating Generator Loss L_G ns]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    J --> K[Update thetaG by descending gradient of L_G ns]
    I --> L[Repeat until convergence or p_g approx p_data]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Nash Equilibrium: D x = 0 point 5 for all x]
```

## 4.2 Discriminator Internals — Functional Flow Inside $D$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Image x of size HxWxC] --> C1[Conv Block 1: Conv stride 2, LeakyReLU]
    C1 --> C2[Conv Block 2: Conv stride 2, LeakyReLU]
    C2 --> C3[Conv Block 3: Conv stride 2, LeakyReLU]
    C3 --> C4[Conv Block 4: Conv stride 1, LeakyReLU]
    C4 --> F[Flatten to Vector]
    F --> FC1[Fully Connected Layer]
    FC1 --> FC2[Single Logit Neuron]
    FC2 --> SIG[Sigmoid Activation]
    SIG --> OUT[Probability D x in 0 point 1]
```

## 4.3 Equilibrium Analysis — How $D(x)$ Behaves as Training Progresses

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Early Training] -->|D easily separates real and fake| S2[Stage 2: Mid Training]
    S2 -->|G improves, D struggles more| S3[Stage 3: Late Training]
    S3 -->|p_g approx p_data| S4[Stage 4: Nash Equilibrium]
    S1 -.->|D x approx 1 for real, D x approx 0 for fake| N1[Confident Classifier]
    S2 -.->|D x distributions overlap| N2[Soft Classifier]
    S3 -.->|D x distributions nearly coincide| N3[Near Random]
    S4 -.->|D x approx 0 point 5 everywhere| N4[Random Guesser]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram.** The progression $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4$ mirrors the Counterfeiter-vs-Detective analogy. In the **equilibrium stage**, the Discriminator cannot do better than a coin flip on *every* input — the Generator has perfectly mimicked $p_{\text{data}}$. This is mathematically encoded in the *JSD = 0* condition derived in Section 3.2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q: Define the Discriminator in a GAN. What is its role during adversarial training?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Discriminator $D(x; \theta_D)$ is a neural network (typically a deep CNN) that takes a sample $x$ as input and outputs a scalar probability $D(x) \in [0,1]$ indicating the likelihood that $x$ was drawn from the *true* data distribution $p_{\text{data}}$ rather than the Generator's distribution $p_g$. During adversarial training, $D$ is updated to **maximise** its classification accuracy on the two-class problem "real vs. fake", thereby providing an *adaptive, learned loss signal* that the Generator uses to refine its samples. At the Nash equilibrium, $D(x) = 0.5$ for every $x$, meaning $D$ can no longer distinguish real from generated samples.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`

**Q: Write the minimax value function $V(D, G)$ of a GAN and identify which network maximises and which minimises it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The value function is:

$$
V(D, G) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{x \,\sim\, p_{\text{data}}(x)} \!\big[\,\log D(x)\,\big] \;+\; \mathbb{E}_{z \,\sim\, p_z(z)} \!\big[\,\log\!\big(1 - D(G(z))\big)\,\big]
$$

The **Discriminator** $D$ seeks to **maximise** $V$ (correctly classify real as real, fake as fake), while the **Generator** $G$ seeks to **minimise** $V$ (fool $D$ into classifying fake samples as real). Hence the training objective is written compactly as $\min_G \max_D V(D, G)$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the optimal Discriminator function $D^*(x)$ for a fixed Generator $G$ in a GAN. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Explain the concept of *adversarial training* in GANs. Discuss why the non-saturating loss $-\log D(G(z))$ is preferred over the saturating loss $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ in practice. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of $D^*(x)$ — 7 marks:**

Given a fixed Generator $G$, the value function can be rewritten as an integral over data space $x$:

$$
V(D, G) \;=\; \int_{\mathcal{X}} \Big[\,p_{\text{data}}(x)\,\log D(x) \;+\; p_g(x)\,\log\!\big(1 - D(x)\big)\Big]\,dx
$$

Because the integrand depends on $D(x)$ pointwise, we maximise it for each $x$ independently. Setting $a = p_{\text{data}}(x)$, $b = p_g(x)$, $y = D(x)$:

$$
f(y) \;=\; a\,\log y \;+\; b\,\log(1 - y)
$$

Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$
\frac{df}{dy} \;=\; \frac{a}{y} \;-\; \frac{b}{1 - y} \;=\; 0
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
y^* \;=\; \frac{a}{a + b} \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}
$$

Second derivative $-\frac{a}{y^2} - \frac{b}{(1-y)^2} < 0$ confirms this is a maximum. Hence:

$$
D^*(x) \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}
$$

When $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, $D^*(x) = 0.5$ for all $x$. **(Award full 7 marks for complete derivation with second-derivative justification.)**

**(b) Adversarial training & non-saturating loss — 7 marks:**

*Adversarial training* is the alternating optimisation procedure in which $D$ and $G$ are updated in opposition: $D$ maximises $V$ (correctly classifies real vs. fake), while $G$ minimises $V$ (fools $D$). At each iteration:
1. Sample a minibatch of real examples and noise vectors.
2. Update $\theta_D$ by ascending $\nabla_{\theta_D} \log D(x) + \log(1 - D(G(z)))$.
3. Update $\theta_G$ by descending its gradient.

*Saturating loss issue:* With the original objective $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$, the gradient w.r.t. $D$ is $-\frac{1}{1 - D}$, which is $\approx -1$ when $D(G(z)) \approx 0$ (early training). This **flat, near-constant gradient** gives $G$ little information about *which direction* to improve. The Generator can stall for many iterations.

*Non-saturating loss:* Replacing the objective with $-\log D(G(z))$ changes the gradient w.r.t. $D$ to $-\frac{1}{D}$, which $\to -\infty$ as $D(G(z)) \to 0$. This provides a **strong, informative gradient** that pushes $G$ to produce samples that $D$ finds more plausible. The two losses have the *same fixed point* (same equilibrium), but the non-saturating form provides much better optimisation dynamics in practice. **(Award full 7 marks for clear contrast of gradient magnitudes and equilibrium equivalence.)**

---

### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 (Alternative Choice)]`

**(a)** Show that at the optimal Discriminator $D^*$, the GAN value function reduces to:

$$
V(G, D^*) \;=\; -\log 4 \;+\; 2\,\cdot\,\text{JSD}\!\big(p_{\text{data}} \,\big\Vert\, p_g\big)
$$

Explain the significance of the JSD term in the context of adversarial training. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Describe the step-by-step training algorithm of a GAN. State any two practical heuristics used to stabilise the Discriminator's training. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Reduction of $V$ at $D^*$ — 7 marks:**

Substituting $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$ and noting $1 - D^*(x) = \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$ into $V$:

$$
V(G, D^*) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{p_{\text{data}}}\!\left[\,\log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}\right] \;+\; \mathbb{E}_{p_g}\!\left[\,\log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}\right]
$$

Using the identity $\log\!\big(\tfrac{p}{p+q}\big) = \log 2 + \log p - \log(p+q)$, and recognising $\text{KL}$-forms with the mixture $M = \tfrac{1}{2}(p_{\text{data}} + p_g)$:

$$
V(G, D^*) \;=\; -\log 4 \;+\; \text{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \Vert M) \;+\; \text{KL}(p_g \Vert M) \;=\; -\log 4 \;+\; 2\,\text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \Vert p_g)
$$

*Significance:* $\text{JSD} \geq 0$ with equality iff $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$. Therefore $\min_G V(G, D^*) = -\log 4$, achieved only when the Generator has perfectly matched the data distribution. This proves that the GAN objective, when $D$ is optimal, *is* a divergence minimisation problem — adversarial training is mathematically equivalent to **Jensen-Shannon Divergence minimisation** between model and data distributions. **(Award full 7 marks.)**

**(b) Training algorithm & heuristics — 7 marks:**

**Algorithm (per iteration):**
1. Sample minibatch $\{z^{(1)}, \dots, z^{(m)}\} \sim p_z$.
2. Sample minibatch $\{x^{(1)}, \dots, x^{(m)}\} \sim p_{\text{data}}$.
3. Update $\theta_D$ by **ascending** the stochastic gradient:
   $\theta_D \leftarrow \theta_D + \eta\, \nabla_{\theta_D} \tfrac{1}{m}\sum_i [\log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))]$
4. Update $\theta_G$ by **descending** the non-saturating gradient:
   $\theta_G \leftarrow \theta_G - \eta\, \nabla_{\theta_G} \tfrac{1}{m}\sum_i \log D(G(z^{(i)}))$
5. Repeat for $T$ iterations / epochs.

**Two heuristics for stable $D$ training:**

1. **One-sided label smoothing:** Replace the label $y = 1$ for real samples with $y = 0.9$. This prevents $D$ from producing extremely large logits and avoids over-confident extrapolations outside the data manifold.

2. **Spectral Normalisation:** Constrain the Lipschitz constant of $D$ by normalising each weight matrix by its largest singular value. This bounds the gradient magnitude and prevents $D$ from becoming too strong too quickly relative to $G$.

*(Other acceptable heuristics: LeakyReLU activations in $D$, two-step $D$ updates per $G$ update, minibatch discrimination, historical averaging of parameters.)* **(Award full 7 marks.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $D$'s role:** Writing that $D$ "classifies images into categories" loses marks. $D$ is a *real-vs-fake* binary classifier only.
> 2. **Forgetting the second-derivative check** in the $D^*$ derivation — examiners specifically test that you verified it is a *maximum*, not a minimum.
> 3. **Mixing up minimisation direction:** $G$ *minimises* $V$, but with the *non-saturating* loss it *maximises* $\log D(G(z))$. Students frequently write these inconsistently.
> 4. **Omitting the noise distribution $p_z$** when stating the value function — a guaranteed 0.5 mark deduction.
> 5. **Confusing JSD with KL:** JSD is symmetric and bounded; KL is asymmetric and unbounded. Examiners will deduct marks if the wrong divergence is named.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GAN = Generator + Discriminator** trained *adversarially* under $\min_G \max_D V(D,G)$.
- **Discriminator $D(x)$** outputs a probability in $[0,1]$; it is a binary classifier for *real vs. fake*, *not* a multi-class classifier.
- **Value function:** $V = \mathbb{E}_{p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$.
- **Optimal Discriminator:** $D^*(x) = \dfrac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$.
- **Global Value at $D^*$:** $V(G, D^*) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \Vert p_g)$.
- **Nash Equilibrium:** $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$ and $D^*(x) = 0.5$ for all $x$.
- **Non-saturating Generator loss:** $-\log D(G(z))$ provides strong early gradients; preferred over $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$.
- **Discriminator training heuristics:** label smoothing, spectral normalisation, LeakyReLU, $k$ inner updates.
- **Stability is hard:** GAN training can suffer from mode collapse, vanishing gradients, and oscillation — always monitor both losses and sample quality.
- **Practical default:** Adam optimiser, learning rate $2 \times 10^{-4}$, $\beta_1 = 0.5$, batch size 64–128 for image GANs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Generator # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)**, a **Generator** is a differentiable neural network function $G(z; \theta_g)$ parameterized by weights $\theta_g$ that maps a sample $z$ drawn from a prior noise distribution $p_z(z)$ (typically a standard Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ or uniform $\mathcal{U}(-1, 1)$) to a synthetic data sample in the same dimensional space as the real data distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$. The generator's operational objective is to learn the underlying true data distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$ by transforming a simple, easily-sampled latent distribution into a complex one, thereby producing samples that are statistically indistinguishable from real data as judged by an adversarial discriminator network. > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight** > The Generator is a **probabilistic generative model** trained via a two-player **minimax game** against a Discriminator $D$. The Generator never sees real data directly — it learns *purely* through the gradient signal back-propagated from the Discriminator's verdict. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Counterfeiter and the Police Imagine a **counterfeiter** (the Generator) trying to produce perfect fake currency, and a **bank inspector** (the Discriminator) whose job is to catch fakes. | Real-World Role | GAN Component | Function | |---|---|---| | Counterfeiter | **Generator $G$** | Crafts fake notes from raw materials | | Bank Inspector | **Discriminator $D$** | Authenticates real vs. fake notes | | Police feedback | **Backpropagated gradient** | Tells counterfeiter *how* to improve | | Genuine currency | Real dataset $x \sim p_{\text{data}}$ | Ground truth distribution | Initially, the counterfeiter produces obvious fakes. Each time the inspector rejects a note, the counterfeiter learns subtle features (watermarks, paper texture, ink layering) that fooled him. Over thousands of iterations, the counterfeiter becomes so skilled that even the inspector cannot reliably distinguish fakes — at this point, the Generator has effectively **captured the data distribution**. ## 1.3 Key Architectural Components > [!NOTE] **Core Building Blocks of a Generator** > 1. **Latent Vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^d$** — Input noise sampled from prior $p_z(z)$, dimensionality $d$ typically 100, 128, or 512. > 2. **Projection Head** — A fully-connected (dense) layer that projects the flat noise vector into a higher-dimensional feature map suitable for convolution. > 3. **Upsampling Backbone** — Repeated blocks of **transposed convolution** (a.k.a. *deconvolution* or *fractionally-strided convolution*), batch normalization, and ReLU/LeakyReLU activations. > 4. **Output Activation** — $\tanh$ for image data (rescales to $[-1, 1]$) or $\sigma$ for bounded positive outputs. ## 1.4 Critical Performance Metrics The Generator's quality is benchmarked using: - **Inception Score (IS)** — measures both *quality* and *diversity* of generated samples. Higher is better. - **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)** — measures Wasserstein-2 distance between Inception feature activations of real and fake batches. **Lower is better.** - **Mode Coverage** — fraction of real-data modes the generator reproduces (related to *mode collapse*). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Player Minimax Game The Generator $G$ and Discriminator $D$ are engaged in a value function $V(D, G)$ that the original Goodfellow et al. (2014) GAN paper formalized as: $$ \min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)} \left[ \log D(x) \right] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)} \left[ \log(1 - D(G(z))) \right] $$ **Interpretation of each term:** - $\log D(x)$ — Discriminator's confidence that a *real* sample $x$ is real. The discriminator wants to push this toward $1$ (so $\log D(x) \to 0$). - $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ — Discriminator's confidence that a *fake* sample $G(z)$ is fake. The discriminator wants $D(G(z)) \to 0$ (so $\log(1-D(G(z))) \to 0$). - The **Generator** is the *minimizer*: it wants $D(G(z)) \to 1$ so the second term drives toward $-\infty$ (low value for $G$). - The **Discriminator** is the *maximizer*: it wants both terms large (positive). ## 2.2 The Generator's Isolated Objective When we isolate the Generator's perspective (treating $D$ as fixed and known), its loss function is: $$ L_G = \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)} \left[ \log(1 - D(G(z))) \right] $$ However, this saturates early in training (when $D$ easily rejects fakes, the gradient vanishes). Hence the **Non-Saturating Heuristic Loss** is universally adopted in practice: $$ L_G^{\text{NS}} = -\mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)} \left[ \log D(G(z)) \right] $$ This is mathematically equivalent at the optimum but provides **strong gradients** when $D$ is confident the sample is fake. ## 2.3 Global Optimality Theorem For a *fixed* $G$, the optimal discriminator is: $$ D^*_G(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} $$ where $p_g$ is the implicit distribution induced by the generator. Substituting this back, the Generator's minimization becomes equivalent to minimizing the **Jensen–Shannon Divergence (JSD)** between $p_{\text{data}}$ and $p_g$: $$ C(G) = -\log(4) + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}\left(p_{\text{data}} \parallel p_g\right) $$ The global minimum is achieved when $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, yielding $C^* = -\log(4)$ and $D^*(x) = \frac{1}{2}$ everywhere (the discriminator cannot distinguish). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] **High-Yield Equations for KTU Examinations** | # | Equation | Meaning | Symbol Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $G: z \mapsto \tilde{x}$, $z \sim p_z(z)$ | Generator's forward map | $\tilde{x}$ = fake sample | | 2 | $V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_x[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1-D(G(z)))]$ | GAN value function | Two-player objective | | 3 | $D^*_G(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x)+p_g(x)}$ | Optimal discriminator for fixed $G$ | $p_g$ = generator's distribution | | 4 | $L_G = \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Saturating generator loss | Suffers vanishing gradient | | 5 | $L_G^{\text{NS}} = -\mathbb{E}_z[\log D(G(z))]$ | Non-saturating loss (practical) | Used in nearly all modern GANs | | 6 | $C(G) = -\log 4 + 2\,\text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\vert\,vert\, p_g)$ | Generator cost at optimal $D$ | Minimum at $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$ | | 7 | $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I_k)$ | Standard Gaussian latent prior | $k$ = latent dim, often $k=128$ | | 8 | $\text{FID} = \vert \mu_r - \mu_g \vert^2 + \text{Tr}\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$ | Fréchet Inception Distance | $r$ = real, $g$ = generated | | 9 | $\theta_g \leftarrow \theta_g - \eta \nabla_{\theta_g} L_G^{\text{NS}}$ | Generator weight update | $\eta$ = learning rate | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Generators drive industry-grade applications: - **Medical Imaging**: Synthesizing high-resolution MRI slices from low-dose scans. - **Data Augmentation**: Generating adversarial training samples for autonomous driving perception systems. - **Drug Discovery**: Proposing novel molecular structures with desired binding properties. - **Creative AI**: Style transfer, super-resolution, image inpainting, text-to-image synthesis (DALL·E, Stable Diffusion all use a generator-style decoder). - **Cybersecurity**: Generating synthetic attack signatures for intrusion detection system training. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Value Function to Optimal Discriminator **Step 1 — Write the discriminator's objective explicitly.** For a fixed generator $G$, the discriminator maximizes: $$ V(D) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x)\, dx + \int_z p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z)))\, dz $$ **Step 2 — Change of variable.** Substitute $x = G(z)$ in the second integral. Since $G$ induces a distribution $p_g(x)$ over data space, we may rewrite: $$ V(D) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x)\, dx + \int_x p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))\, dx $$ **Step 3 — Pointwise optimization.** The integrand for any $x$ is a function of $D(x)$ alone (the two integrals are over the same space). Define $y = D(x) \in (0, 1)$ and maximize: $$ f(y) = p_{\text{data}}(x) \log y + p_g(x) \log(1 - y) $$ **Step 4 — Take the derivative and set to zero:** $$ \frac{df}{dy} = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{y} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - y} = 0 $$ **Step 5 — Solve for $y$:** $$ \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{y} = \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - y} $$ $$ p_{\text{data}}(x)(1 - y) = p_g(x) y $$ $$ p_{\text{data}}(x) = y\bigl[p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)\bigr] $$ $$ \boxed{D^*_G(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}} $$ **Step 6 — Verify it is a maximum** by confirming $f''(y) < 0$: $$ f''(y) = -\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{y^2} - \frac{p_g(x)}{(1-y)^2} < 0 \quad \checkmark $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Generator Cost in Terms of JSD **Step 1 — Substitute $D^*_G(x)$ back into the value function.** At the optimal discriminator, $V(D^*_G, G)$ becomes: $$ C(G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}} \left[ \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \right] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_g} \left[ \log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \right] $$ **Step 2 — Add and subtract $\log 2$ in each expectation:** $$ C(G) = \mathbb{E}_{p_{\text{data}}} \left[ \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{(p_{\text{data}} + p_g)(x)/2} \right] - \log 2 + \mathbb{E}_{p_g} \left[ \log \frac{p_g(x)}{(p_{\text{data}} + p_g)(x)/2} \right] - \log 2 $$ **Step 3 — Recognize the Kullback–Leibler divergences:** $$ C(G) = \text{KL}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\vert\,vert\, \frac{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}{2}\right) + \text{KL}\!\left(p_g \,\vert\,vert\, \frac{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}{2}\right) - 2 \log 2 $$ **Step 4 — Apply the JSD definition.** By construction, $\text{JSD}(P \vert\,vert Q) = \frac{1}{2}\text{KL}(P \vert\,vert M) + \frac{1}{2}\text{KL}(Q \vert\,vert M)$ where $M = (P+Q)/2$. Therefore: $$ \boxed{C(G) = 2 \cdot \text{JSD}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\vert\,vert\, p_g\right) - \log 4} $$ **Step 5 — Establish the global minimum.** The JSD is non-negative and equals zero iff $p_{\text{data}} = p_g$ a.e. Hence: $$ C^*(G) = -\log 4 \quad \text{at} \quad p_g = p_{\text{data}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalence of Non-Saturating Loss at Optimum **Step 1 — Original saturating loss:** $$ L_G^{(1)} = \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))] $$ **Step 2 — Non-saturating loss:** $$ L_G^{(2)} = -\mathbb{E}_z[\log D(G(z))] $$ **Step 3 — Show they share the same stationary point.** Compute the gradient of $L_G^{(1)}$ with respect to generator parameters $\theta_g$ using the chain rule $\nabla_{\theta_g} = (\partial D / \partial \theta_g) \cdot \nabla_{G}$: $$ \nabla_{\theta_g} L_G^{(1)} = \mathbb{E}_z\left[ \frac{-1}{1 - D(G(z))} \cdot \frac{\partial D}{\partial G} \cdot \frac{\partial G}{\partial \theta_g} \right] $$ **Step 4 — Compute the gradient of $L_G^{(2)}$:** $$ \nabla_{\theta_g} L_G^{(2)} = \mathbb{E}_z\left[ \frac{-1}{D(G(z))} \cdot \frac{\partial D}{\partial G} \cdot \frac{\partial G}{\partial \theta_g} \right] $$ **Step 5 — Compare.** Both gradients vanish at the same critical point $D(G(z)) \to 1$, but as $D(G(z)) \to 0$ (early training), $L_G^{(1)}$'s gradient $\to 1$ (vanishing) while $L_G^{(2)}$'s gradient $\to \infty$ (strong signal). Hence the non-saturating loss provides **better learning dynamics** while preserving the global optimum. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Generator Variants Comparison | Generator Type | Loss Function | Strength | Weakness | |---|---|---|---| | **Vanilla GAN Generator** | $-\log D(G(z))$ | Theoretically grounded (JSD minimization) | Mode collapse, unstable | | **WGAN Generator** | $-\mathbb{E}[D(G(z))]$, $D$ is 1-Lipschitz | Meaningful loss correlated with sample quality | Weight clipping is restrictive | | **WGAN-GP Generator** | $-\mathbb{E}[D(G(z))]$ with gradient penalty | Stable training, no mode collapse | Slower per iteration | | **LSGAN Generator** | $\frac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}[(D(G(z)) - 1)^2]$ | Smooth gradients, high-quality images | Requires careful $\sigma$ tuning | | **Conditional Generator** | $L_G + \lambda \cdot L_{\text{aux}}$ (class label input) | Class-conditional synthesis | Needs labelled data | | **StyleGAN Generator** | Same as WGAN-GP + style mixing | State-of-the-art fidelity | Computationally expensive | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Generator Inside a GAN ```mermaid flowchart TB Z["Latent Vector z ~ N(0,I)"]:::inputNode PROJ["Dense Projection
Linear Layer"]:::procNode RESHAPE["Reshape to Feature Map"]:::procNode DC1["Deconv Block 1
ConvT + BN + ReLU"]:::procNode DC2["Deconv Block 2
ConvT + BN + ReLU"]:::procNode DC3["Deconv Block 3
ConvT + BN + ReLU"]:::procNode DC4["Deconv Block 4
ConvT + BN + ReLU"]:::procNode OUT["Tanh Output
Generated Image x_tilde"]:::outputNode Z --> PROJ --> RESHAPE --> DC1 --> DC2 --> DC3 --> DC4 --> OUT classDef inputNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef procNode fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef outputNode fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#991b1b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 GAN Adversarial Training Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR REAL["Real Data x
p_data"]:::realNode NOISE["Noise z
p_z"]:::noiseNode GEN["Generator G
theta_g"]:::genNode FAKE["Fake x_tilde
G of z"]:::fakeNode DISC["Discriminator D
theta_d"]:::discNode VERDICT["D of x or D of G of z
Probability Real"]:::verdictNode LOSS["Compute L_D and L_G_NS"]:::lossNode UPDATE["Backprop + Update
theta_d and theta_g"]:::updateNode REAL --> VERDICT FAKE --> VERDICT NOISE --> GEN --> FAKE VERDICT --> LOSS --> UPDATE UPDATE -.updates weights.-> GEN UPDATE -.updates weights.-> DISC classDef realNode fill:#15803d,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef noiseNode fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef genNode fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef fakeNode fill:#ea580c,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef discNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef verdictNode fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#854d0e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef lossNode fill:#be185d,stroke:#831843,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef updateNode fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Information Flow — Gradient Backpropagation to Generator ```mermaid flowchart TD FAKE_INPUT["Fake Sample G of z"]:::nodeA D_FORWARD["Discriminator Forward Pass"]:::nodeA D_OUTPUT["D of G of z
Scalar Probability"]:::nodeA LOSS_COMP["Compute L_G_NS
negative log D of G of z"]:::nodeB GRAD_D["Gradient w.r.t. D weights
partial L / partial theta_d"]:::nodeB GRAD_G["Gradient w.r.t. G weights
partial L / partial theta_g"]:::nodeB CHAIN["Apply Chain Rule
through D then through G"]:::nodeC PARAM_UPDATE["Update theta_g
theta_g minus eta times grad"]:::nodeC FAKE_INPUT --> D_FORWARD --> D_OUTPUT --> LOSS_COMP LOSS_COMP --> GRAD_D LOSS_COMP --> GRAD_G --> CHAIN --> PARAM_UPDATE classDef nodeA fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef nodeB fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef nodeC fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#991b1b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] **Architectural Note for KTU Viva** > The Generator uses **transposed convolutions** (not standard convolutions) because the input is a low-dimensional vector and the output is a high-dimensional image — we are *upsampling* spatial resolution. Each transposed conv block typically **doubles** spatial dimensions while halving channel depth. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the role of the Generator in a Generative Adversarial Network. How does it differ from a Discriminator?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Generator $G$ in a GAN is a neural network that maps a random noise vector $z$ sampled from a prior distribution $p_z(z)$ to a synthetic data sample $G(z)$ resembling real data drawn from $p_{\text{data}}(x)$. Its role is to learn the underlying data distribution implicitly so as to produce realistic samples that fool the Discriminator. The **Discriminator** $D$ is a binary classifier distinguishing real samples from fakes, while the **Generator** is a sample producer trained adversarially to maximize $D$'s error. The Generator never sees real data directly — it learns solely through the backpropagated gradient signal from the Discriminator's verdict. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Why is the non-saturating loss $-\mathbb{E}_z[\log D(G(z))]$ preferred over the saturating loss $\mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ for training the Generator?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Early in training, when the Generator produces poor samples, the Discriminator confidently rejects them, making $D(G(z)) \approx 0$. - The **saturating loss**'s gradient $\frac{-1}{1-D(G(z))} \to -1$ (bounded), causing the generator to receive a **vanishingly small learning signal** and stall. - The **non-saturating loss**'s gradient $\frac{-1}{D(G(z))} \to -\infty$, providing a **strong gradient** that drives meaningful parameter updates. Both losses share the same global optimum ($D(G(z)) = 1$), but the non-saturating variant yields **practically better convergence**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the optimal discriminator $D^*_G(x)$ for a fixed generator $G$ in the GAN value function: $$ \min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))] $$ Clearly state all assumptions and the role of the implicit generator distribution $p_g$. (7 marks) **(b)** Using the result from (a), show that the Generator's optimization problem is equivalent to minimizing the Jensen–Shannon Divergence between $p_{\text{data}}$ and $p_g$. What is the global minimum value of the cost function $C(G)$? (7 marks) #### Model Solution **(a) Derivation of $D^*_G$:** For a fixed $G$, the value function is rewritten as an integral over data space using the change of variable $x = G(z)$ and the generator's induced distribution $p_g(x)$: $$ V(D) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x)\, dx + \int_x p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))\, dx $$ Since the integrand is a function of $D(x)$ alone at each $x$, we maximize pointwise. For $y = D(x) \in (0,1)$: $$ f(y) = p_{\text{data}}(x) \log y + p_g(x) \log(1 - y) $$ Setting the derivative $f'(y) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{y} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1-y} = 0$ and solving: $$ D^*_G(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} $$ The second derivative $f''(y) < 0$ confirms this is a maximum. The function $D^*$ is bounded in $(0, 1)$ as expected of a probability output. **(b) JSD equivalence and global minimum:** Substituting $D^*_G$ back into $V$: $$ C(G) = \mathbb{E}_{p_{\text{data}}}\!\left[\log \tfrac{p_{\text{data}}}{p_{\text{data}}+p_g}\right] + \mathbb{E}_{p_g}\!\left[\log \tfrac{p_g}{p_{\text{data}}+p_g}\right] $$ Adding and subtracting $\log 2$: $$ C(G) = \text{KL}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\Big\vert\, \frac{p_{\text{data}}+p_g}{2}\right) + \text{KL}\!\left(p_g \,\Big\vert\, \frac{p_{\text{data}}+p_g}{2}\right) - 2\log 2 $$ By the definition of JSD: $$ C(G) = 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \vert p_g) - \log 4 $$ Since $\text{JSD} \geq 0$ with equality iff $p_{\text{data}} = p_g$: $$ \boxed{C^*_{\min}(G) = -\log 4 \quad \text{at} \quad p_g = p_{\text{data}}} $$ At this equilibrium, $D^*(x) = \frac{1}{2}$ — the discriminator is maximally confused. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Describe the architecture of a typical Generator network used in a Deep Convolutional GAN (DCGAN) for image synthesis. Include details on input dimensionality, layer types, activation functions, and output transformation. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain the phenomenon of **mode collapse** in GANs. Discuss two strategies used to mitigate it during Generator training. (7 marks) #### Model Solution **(a) Typical DCGAN Generator Architecture:** | Stage | Layer Type | Output Shape (for 64×64 RGB) | Activation | |---|---|---|---| | Input | Latent vector $z$ | $128 \times 1 \times 1$ | — | | Projection | Dense (fully connected) | $8192 \times 1 \times 1$ | ReLU | | Reshape | Reshape to tensor | $512 \times 4 \times 4$ | — | | Deconv Block 1 | ConvTranspose 4×4, stride 2 | $256 \times 8 \times 8$ | BatchNorm + ReLU | | Deconv Block 2 | ConvTranspose 4×4, stride 2 | $128 \times 16 \times 16$ | BatchNorm + ReLU | | Deconv Block 3 | ConvTranspose 4×4, stride 2 | $64 \times 32 \times 32$ | BatchNorm + ReLU | | Deconv Block 4 | ConvTranspose 4×4, stride 2 | $3 \times 64 \times 64$ | **Tanh** | **Key design principles (Radford et al., 2015):** 1. **No pooling layers** — use strided transposed convolutions for learnable upsampling. 2. **Batch Normalization** after every deconv layer (except the last) for stable gradients. 3. **ReLU** activations in hidden layers, **Tanh** at the output to bound pixels in $[-1, 1]$ matching the normalized real data. 4. **No fully connected layers** at the output (only the initial projection). **(b) Mode Collapse:** **Definition:** Mode collapse occurs when the Generator maps many different input noise vectors to a *single* (or very few) output sample(s), thereby failing to capture the diversity of $p_{\text{data}}$. The Generator exploits a weakness in the Discriminator — producing one "safe" fake that passes the discriminator's test — and collapses to a low-entropy distribution. **Mitigation Strategies:** 1. **Minibatch Discrimination:** Pass real and fake samples through a minibatch layer that computes pairwise distances within a batch and concatenates them as side information to $D$. The discriminator can detect lack of diversity, and the gradient forces the generator to spread outputs. 2. **Wasserstein Loss with Gradient Penalty (WGAN-GP):** Replace the JSD-based loss with the Wasserstein-1 distance, which is well-defined and provides a meaningful gradient even when $p_g$ and $p_{\text{data}}$ have disjoint support. The Lipschitz constraint is enforced via a gradient penalty term, eliminating the need for weight clipping that caused mode collapse in vanilla WGAN. 3. *(Bonus)* **Unrolled GANs:** Compute discriminator updates for $k$ steps before updating the generator, giving the generator foresight into how $D$ will respond, discouraging short-term exploitation of $D$. --- > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing the saturating and non-saturating losses** — examiners *will* deduct marks if you write the wrong loss for the wrong scenario. Memorize the *reason* (vanishing gradient), not just the formula. > 2. **Forgetting the $-\log 4$ constant** in the JSD derivation. Many students write $C(G) = 2 \cdot \text{JSD}$ without subtracting $\log 4$. This is a 2-mark deduction. > 3. **Not justifying the optimality** of $D^*$ — you must show $f''(y) < 0$ to claim a maximum, not just set the derivative to zero. > 4. **Mixing up Tanh vs. Sigmoid** at the generator output. Tanh is the KTU-recommended output activation for normalized image data; sigmoid is *not* appropriate for centered data. > 5. **Omitting the change-of-variable step** in derivation — going directly from $V(D,G)$ to the pointwise optimization without justifying $\int_z p_z(z) \log(1-D(G(z)))\, dz = \int_x p_g(x) \log(1-D(x))\, dx$ is a common 1-mark loss. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Generator in GANs** - ✅ Generator $G$ maps noise $z \sim p_z(z)$ to fake samples $G(z) \in \mathcal{X}$ matching real data space. - ✅ Trained adversarially against Discriminator $D$ in a **minimax** game. - ✅ Value function: $\min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_x[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$. - ✅ **Optimal discriminator** (for fixed $G$): $D^*_G(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$. - ✅ **Generator's true cost** (at optimal $D$): $C(G) = 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \vert p_g) - \log 4$. - ✅ **Global minimum** of $C(G) = -\log 4$ achieved when $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$. - ✅ **Non-saturating loss** $-\mathbb{E}_z[\log D(G(z))]$ is preferred over saturating $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ due to non-vanishing gradients. - ✅ DCGAN Generator uses **transposed convolutions**, **BatchNorm**, **ReLU** internally, and **Tanh** at output. - ✅ **Mode collapse** = generator produces low-diversity samples; mitigated by minibatch discrimination or WGAN-GP. - ✅ Evaluation metrics: **Inception Score (IS) ↑**, **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) ↓**. - ✅ Latent prior $p_z$ is typically $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ or $\mathcal{U}(-1, 1)$ with $d \in \{100, 128, 512\}$. - ✅ Generator **never observes real data** during training — purely gradient-driven learning.

topics.title: Up-sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Up-sampling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Up-sampling** (also called *upscaling*, *spatial up-sampling*, or *resolution enhancement*) is the process of increasing the spatial resolution of a feature map (2D, 3D, or higher-dimensional tensor) by transforming a tensor of a given size into a tensor of larger spatial dimensions. Formally, given an input tensor $X \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times H \times W}$ where $C$ is the number of channels and $H, W$ are spatial dimensions, up-sampling produces an output tensor $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times H' \times W'}$ where $H' > H$ and $W' > W$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Up-sampling refers to any technique that increases the spatial resolution of feature maps. It is the inverse operation of pooling/down-sampling and is fundamental to the *decoder* path of encoder–decoder architectures such as **Autoencoders**, **U-Net**, **FCN (Fully Convolutional Networks)**, and **GAN generators**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for Module 4, up-sampling is studied as a critical component for tasks such as *semantic segmentation*, *image generation*, *image super-resolution*, and *image-to-image translation*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you have a **small passport-sized photograph (low resolution)** and you want to print it on a **large A4 sheet (high resolution)**. You cannot magically invent new pixel information, but you can:
1. **Stretch and repeat each pixel** (like nearest-neighbor — looks blocky),
2. **Smoothly blend neighboring pixels** (like bilinear — looks blurry but smooth),
3. **Use a magnifying glass with smart algorithms** (like bicubic — sharper),
4. **Train a neural network** to *learn* how to fill in plausible details (like transposed convolution).

This "smart enlargement" process is precisely what up-sampling does in deep learning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Up-sampling is **not** the same as pooling reversal. While pooling *reduces* spatial dimensions using hard-coded rules (max, average), up-sampling *increases* spatial dimensions using either *fixed interpolation rules* or *learned parameters*.

## 1.3 Why Up-sampling is Needed

| Application | Reason for Up-sampling |
|---|---|
| **Semantic Segmentation** | Encoder shrinks the image; decoder must restore original size to produce per-pixel class labels. |
| **Image Generation (GANs)** | Generator starts from a small noise vector (e.g., $1 \times 1$ or $4 \times 4$) and progressively grows to full image size (e.g., $256 \times 256$). |
| **Super-Resolution** | Convert a low-resolution image to a high-resolution image with plausible details. |
| **Autoencoders** | Decoder reconstructs the input from a compressed latent representation. |

## 1.4 Up-sampling Factor
