The **up-sampling factor** (also called *scale factor* or *stride*) is denoted by $r$ (or $s$). For a factor of $r$, each spatial dimension increases by $r$ times:

$$H' = r \cdot H, \quad W' = r \cdot W$$

For example, with $r = 2$, a $16 \times 16$ feature map becomes $32 \times 32$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard practice in modern architectures:** Use $r = 2$ at each layer, applied progressively (e.g., $4 \times 4 \to 8 \times 8 \to 16 \times 16 \to 32 \times 32$), rather than a single large up-sampling step.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Up-sampling Methods

Up-sampling methods are broadly classified into two categories:

**A. Parameter-Free (Fixed / Non-Learned) Methods**
1. Nearest Neighbor Interpolation
2. Bed of Nails
3. Bilinear Interpolation
4. Bicubic Interpolation

**B. Parameterized (Learned) Methods**
5. Transposed Convolution (Deconvolution)
6. Unpooling (with MaxPooling indices)
7. Sub-pixel / Pixel Shuffle Convolution
8. Up-sampling + Convolution Block

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Method

### 2.2.1 Nearest Neighbor Interpolation
Each input pixel is replicated into a $r \times r$ block in the output. The mapping is:

$$Y[i, j] = X\left[\left\lfloor \frac{i}{r} \right\rfloor, \left\lfloor \frac{j}{r} \right\rfloor\right]$$

- **Pros:** Fast, no parameters, deterministic.
- **Cons:** Produces blocky, jagged artifacts; lacks smoothness.

### 2.2.2 Bed of Nails
Each input pixel is placed at one position in the output grid, and the rest are filled with zeros.

$$Y[i, j] = \begin{cases} X\left[\frac{i}{r}, \frac{j}{r}\right] & \text{if } i \bmod r = 0 \text{ and } j \bmod r = 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 2.2.3 Bilinear Interpolation
Computes the output value as a *weighted linear combination* of the **4 nearest input pixels**. Weights are proportional to the distance from the target location.

For a target coordinate $(x, y)$ with neighbors at $(x_1, y_1), (x_1, y_2), (x_2, y_1), (x_2, y_2)$:

$$Y(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \sum_{j=1}^{2} w_{ij} \cdot X(x_i, y_j)$$

where weights depend on fractional distances.

### 2.2.4 Bicubic Interpolation
Similar to bilinear but uses **16 nearest neighbors** (a $4 \times 4$ grid) and **cubic** weighting functions. Produces smoother, sharper results than bilinear but is computationally heavier.

### 2.2.5 Transposed Convolution (Deconvolution / Fractionally-Strided Convolution)
A convolution operation with learnable parameters that *increases* spatial dimensions. Conceptually:
1. Insert $(r-1)$ zeros between each input pixel (zero-stuffing),
2. Apply a standard convolution with stride $1$.

This is the **most common up-sampling in deep learning** because it learns *task-specific* up-sampling patterns.

### 2.2.6 Max-Unpooling
Stores the indices of maximum values during max-pooling in the encoder. During decoding, places the values back at those indices and fills the rest with zeros. Used extensively in **SegNet**.

### 2.2.7 Sub-pixel / Pixel Shuffle Convolution
Generates $r^2$ channels and then rearranges them spatially to produce a single channel with $r$ times larger resolution. Avoids checkerboard artifacts.

$$Y[c, r \cdot h + i, r \cdot w + j] = X[c \cdot r^2 + i \cdot r + j, h, w]$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Memorize this table thoroughly. KTU board exams frequently include 3-mark questions directly asking to list up-sampling methods and their formulas.

| Method | Core Formula | Learnable Parameters | Output Size | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nearest Neighbor | $Y[i,j] = X[\lfloor i/r \rfloor, \lfloor j/r \rfloor]$ | **0** | $rH \times rW$ | **Very Low** |
| Bed of Nails | $Y[i,j] = X[i/r, j/r]$ if $(i \bmod r, j \bmod r) = (0,0)$, else $0$ | **0** | $rH \times rW$ | **Very Low** |
| Bilinear | $Y(x,y) = \sum_{i=0}^{1} \sum_{j=0}^{1} w_{ij} X(x_i, y_j)$ | **0** | $rH \times rW$ | **Low** |
| Bicubic | $Y(x,y) = \sum_{k=-1}^{2} \sum_{l=-1}^{2} W_k(x) W_l(y) X_{k,l}$ | **0** | $rH \times rW$ | **Medium** |
| Transposed Convolution | $Y = W^T \ast X + b$ (with zero insertion) | $k \times k \times C_{in} \times C_{out}$ | $rH \times rW$ | **High** |
| Max-Unpooling | $Y[\text{idx}] = X$, else $0$ | **0** (uses stored indices) | $rH \times rW$ | **Low** |
| Pixel Shuffle | $Y[c, r h + i, r w + j] = X[c r^2 + i r + j, h, w]$ | Standard conv weights | $rH \times rW$ | **Medium** |

Where $r$ = up-sampling factor, $k$ = kernel size, $W$ = weight tensor, $b$ = bias, $C_{in}, C_{out}$ = input/output channels.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| Medical Imaging | Up-sampling MRI/CT scans for fine-grained tumor segmentation (U-Net). |
| Autonomous Driving | Semantic segmentation of road scenes (FCN, DeepLab). |
| Satellite Imaging | Super-resolution of low-resolution satellite imagery. |
| Generative AI | GANs for face generation (StyleGAN uses bilinear + noise injection). |
| Video Streaming | Real-time video up-scaling (e.g., NVIDIA DLSS). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bilinear Interpolation

Bilinear interpolation extends linear interpolation to 2D. Given a target point $(x, y)$ that lies between integer coordinates, we first identify the four nearest input pixels:
- $(x_1, y_1) = (\lfloor x \rfloor, \lfloor y \rfloor)$
- $(x_2, y_1) = (\lfloor x \rfloor + 1, \lfloor y \rfloor)$
- $(x_1, y_2) = (\lfloor x \rfloor, \lfloor y \rfloor + 1)$
- $(x_2, y_2) = (\lfloor x \rfloor + 1, \lfloor y \rfloor + 1)$

Let $Q_{11} = X(x_1, y_1)$, $Q_{12} = X(x_1, y_2)$, $Q_{21} = X(x_2, y_1)$, $Q_{22} = X(x_2, y_2)$. Define fractional offsets:

$$u = x - x_1, \quad v = y - y_1$$

**Step 1: Linear interpolation along x-axis (top edge)**

$$R_1 = (1 - u) \cdot Q_{11} + u \cdot Q_{21}$$

**Logic:** When $u = 0$, we want $R_1 = Q_{11}$; when $u = 1$, we want $R_1 = Q_{21}$. Linear interpolation achieves this.

**Step 2: Linear interpolation along x-axis (bottom edge)**

$$R_2 = (1 - u) \cdot Q_{12} + u \cdot Q_{22}$$

**Logic:** Same logic applied at $y = y_2$.

**Step 3: Linear interpolation along y-axis (final value)**

$$Y(x, y) = (1 - v) \cdot R_1 + v \cdot R_2$$

**Logic:** Combine $R_1$ and $R_2$ along the vertical axis.

**Step 4: Expand and combine into the unified bilinear formula**

$$Y(x, y) = (1 - u)(1 - v) Q_{11} + u(1 - v) Q_{21} + (1 - u)v Q_{12} + u \cdot v \cdot Q_{22}$$

Final compact bilinear formula:

$$Y(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \sum_{j=1}^{2} w_{ij} \cdot Q_{ij}$$

with weights $w_{11} = (1-u)(1-v)$, $w_{12} = (1-u)v$, $w_{21} = u(1-v)$, $w_{22} = uv$. Notice that weights sum to **1** (a partition of unity):

$$w_{11} + w_{12} + w_{21} + w_{22} = (1-u)(1-v) + (1-u)v + u(1-v) + uv = 1$$

This guarantees the output remains in the dynamic range of the input.

## 3.2 Derivation of Transposed Convolution Mechanics

Transposed convolution with stride $s > 1$ performs up-sampling. We derive its mechanics for stride $s = 2$ and a $2 \times 2$ input.

**Given:**
- Input feature map $X \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$
- Kernel $K \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$
- Stride $s = 2$

**Step 1: Zero-Stuffing**
Insert $(s - 1) = 1$ zero between every input pixel (both rows and columns), and pad with $(k - 1) = 2$ zeros on each side. The resulting *dilated input* $X'$ has size:

$$X' \in \mathbb{R}^{(H + (H-1)(s-1) + 2(k-1)) \times (W + (W-1)(s-1) + 2(k-1))}$$

For our case: $X' \in \mathbb{R}^{(2 + 1 + 4) \times (2 + 1 + 4)} = \mathbb{R}^{7 \times 7}$.

**Step 2: Standard Convolution with Stride 1**
Apply a standard convolution on $X'$ with stride 1 and kernel $K$. The output size is:

$$H_{\text{out}} = H' - k + 1 = 7 - 3 + 1 = 5$$

So output $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{5 \times 5}$.

**Step 3: Matrix Formulation**
Transposed convolution can be expressed as a matrix multiplication. The standard convolution $Y = K \ast X$ can be written as $\mathbf{y} = A \mathbf{x}$ where $A$ is the *im2col* matrix. The transposed convolution performs:

$$\mathbf{y}_{\text{up}} = A^{T} \mathbf{x}$$

This is why it is called *transposed* — the gradient operation of a standard convolution is its transpose.

**Step 4: Learnable Weights**
Unlike bilinear interpolation, the kernel values $K_{ij}$ are **learned via backpropagation**, allowing the network to discover optimal up-sampling patterns for the task.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for KTU Exams)

> [!NOTE]
> **This comparison matrix is a favorite KTU 14-mark question.** Memorize the trade-offs.

| Criterion | Nearest | Bilinear | Bicubic | Transposed Conv | Pixel Shuffle |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Learnable** | No | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Quality** | Poor (blocky) | Medium (smooth) | Good | Task-dependent | Good |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Fast | Medium | Slow | Medium |
| **Artifacts** | Blockiness | Blurriness | Ringing | Checkerboard | Minimal |
| **Parameters** | **0** | **0** | **0** | $k^2 C_{in} C_{out}$ | $k^2 C_{in} (r^2 C_{out})$ |
| **Best For** | Real-time preview | General pre-upsampling | Image processing | Learned features | Super-resolution |

## 3.4 Checkerboard Artifact Analysis

**Why does transposed convolution produce checkerboard artifacts?**

When the kernel size $k$ is **not divisible** by the stride $s$, the kernel overlaps asymmetrically in the output. Mathematically, the *effective receptive field* is non-uniform across output positions.

**Solution:**
1. Use kernel size that is a multiple of stride: $k = 2s$, $k = 4s$, etc.
2. Replace transposed convolution with `Upsample + Conv` blocks: $Y = \text{Conv}(\text{BilinearUpsample}(X))$.

This is the *Odena et al. (2016)* recommendation, widely adopted in modern architectures like **StyleGAN2**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Up-sampling Techniques — Taxonomy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UPSAMPLING METHODS] --> B[PARAMETER FREE]
    A --> C[PARAMETERIZED LEARNED]
    B --> B1[NEAREST NEIGHBOR]
    B --> B2[BED OF NAILS]
    B --> B3[BILINEAR]
    B --> B4[BICUBIC]
    C --> C1[TRANSPOSED CONVOLUTION]
    C --> C2[MAX UNPOOLING]
    C --> C3[PIXEL SHUFFLE]
    C --> C4[UPSAMPLE PLUS CONV]
    B1 --> D[ZERO PARAMETERS]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    B4 --> D
    C1 --> E[LEARNABLE WEIGHTS]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> E
```

## 4.2 Transposed Convolution — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT X 2X2] --> B[ZERO STUFFING INSERT S MINUS 1 ZEROS]
    B --> C[ZERO PADDING K MINUS 1 ZEROS]
    C --> D[STANDARD CONVOLUTION STRIDE 1 KERNEL K]
    D --> E[OUTPUT Y 4X4]
    F[LEARNABLE WEIGHTS K] --> D
    G[BACKPROP GRADIENTS] --> F
    F --> G
```

## 4.3 Encoder-Decoder Architecture with Up-sampling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I[INPUT IMAGE H X W] --> E1[CONV PLUS POOL]
    E1 --> E2[CONV PLUS POOL H SLASH 2]
    E2 --> E3[CONV PLUS POOL H SLASH 4]
    E3 --> B[BOTTLENECK H SLASH 8]
    B --> U1[UPSAMPLE 2X]
    U1 --> U2[UPSAMPLE 2X]
    U2 --> U3[UPSAMPLE 2X]
    U3 --> O[OUTPUT RECONSTRUCTION H X W]
    E1 -.SKIP CONNECTION.-> U3
    E2 -.SKIP CONNECTION.-> U2
    E3 -.SKIP CONNECTION.-> U1
```

## 4.4 Pixel Shuffle — Rearrangement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT C CHANNELS H X W] --> B[CONVOLUTION OUTPUTS R SQUARE C CHANNELS]
    B --> C[PERMUTE AND RESHAPE TO C CHANNELS R H X R W]
    C --> D[OUTPUT ENLARGED FEATURE MAP]
```

## 4.5 Comparison: Fixed vs Learned Up-sampling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT FEATURE MAP] --> B{METHOD SELECTION}
    B -->|FIXED| C[INTERPOLATION RULE NEAREST BILINEAR BICUBIC]
    B -->|LEARNED| D[UPSAMPLE THEN CONV OR TRANSPOSED CONV OR PIXEL SHUFFLE]
    C --> E[OUTPUT NO LEARNABLE PARAMETERS]
    D --> F[OUTPUT TASK OPTIMIZED PARAMETERS]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define up-sampling. Why is it essential in encoder–decoder architectures?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Up-sampling is the process of increasing the spatial resolution of a feature map, transforming a tensor $X \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times H \times W}$ into $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times H' \times W'}$ with $H' > H$ and $W' > W$.

It is essential in encoder–decoder architectures because:
1. The **encoder** progressively *reduces* spatial dimensions via convolution and pooling to extract high-level semantic features.
2. The **decoder** must *restore* the original spatial resolution to produce pixel-wise outputs (e.g., segmentation masks, reconstructed images, generated images).
3. Up-sampling bridges the low-resolution bottleneck representation to the high-resolution output space.

Common techniques include nearest-neighbor, bilinear, bicubic interpolation, and learnable methods like transposed convolution and pixel shuffle. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between nearest neighbor interpolation and bilinear interpolation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Nearest Neighbor | Bilinear |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $Y[i,j] = X[\lfloor i/r \rfloor, \lfloor j/r \rfloor]$ | Weighted average of 4 nearest pixels |
| **Pixels Used** | Only 1 nearest pixel | 4 nearest pixels |
| **Smoothness** | Blocky, discontinuous | Smooth, continuous |
| **Computational Cost** | Very low | Low |

**Key Difference:** Nearest-neighbor simply *replicates* a single pixel into an $r \times r$ block, producing blocky artifacts. Bilinear computes a *weighted average* of the four nearest pixels based on fractional distances, producing smooth but slightly blurred outputs. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain in detail the various up-sampling techniques used in deep learning. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.**

#### Part (a) — Parameter-Free Techniques (7 marks)

**1. Nearest Neighbor Interpolation:**
Each input pixel is replicated into an $r \times r$ block in the output. Formula: $Y[i,j] = X[\lfloor i/r \rfloor, \lfloor j/r \rfloor]$.
- **Advantage:** Zero parameters, deterministic, very fast.
- **Disadvantage:** Produces blocky, jagged artifacts; poor visual quality.

**2. Bed of Nails:**
Each input pixel is placed at one position in the output grid; all other positions are filled with zeros.
- **Advantage:** Preserves original values exactly; useful when sparsity is acceptable.
- **Disadvantage:** Sparse output wastes computation in subsequent layers.

**3. Bilinear Interpolation:**
Computes output as a weighted linear combination of the 4 nearest pixels. The formula is:
$$Y(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \sum_{j=1}^{2} w_{ij} \cdot X(x_i, y_j)$$
where weights depend on fractional distances from the target point.
- **Advantage:** Smooth, continuous outputs; well-defined mathematical properties (partition of unity).
- **Disadvantage:** Introduces slight blur; cannot recover high-frequency details.

**4. Bicubic Interpolation:**
Uses 16 nearest neighbors and cubic weighting functions for sharper results.
- **Advantage:** Smoother and sharper than bilinear; widely used in classical image processing.
- **Disadvantage:** Computationally expensive; risk of ringing artifacts near edges.

#### Part (b) — Parameterized (Learned) Techniques (7 marks)

**5. Transposed Convolution:**
Performs convolution with learnable kernel on a zero-stuffed input to increase spatial resolution. Conceptually: zero-insert with stride $s$, then apply standard convolution.
- **Advantage:** Task-optimized; can learn complex up-sampling patterns.
- **Disadvantage:** Prone to *checkerboard artifacts* when kernel size is not divisible by stride.

**6. Max-Unpooling:**
Uses indices stored during encoder max-pooling to place values back at original positions.
- **Advantage:** Preserves spatial information from encoder; used in SegNet.
- **Disadvantage:** Sparse output; depends entirely on stored indices.

**7. Sub-pixel / Pixel Shuffle Convolution:**
Generates $r^2$ channels per output channel, then rearranges (shuffles) them spatially to form an enlarged feature map. Formula: $Y[c, r h + i, r w + j] = X[c r^2 + i r + j, h, w]$.
- **Advantage:** Avoids checkerboard artifacts; efficient; used in super-resolution (ESPCN).
- **Disadvantage:** Requires careful channel rearrangement.

**8. Upsample + Convolution Block:**
Performs fixed up-sampling (e.g., bilinear) followed by a standard convolution. Recommended by Odena et al. to avoid checkerboard artifacts.
- **Advantage:** Combines smoothness of interpolation with learnability of convolution.
- **Disadvantage:** Slightly higher computational cost.

**Comparison Summary Table:**

| Method | Parameters | Quality | Speed | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nearest | 0 | Low | Fastest | Real-time preview |
| Bilinear | 0 | Medium | Fast | Default in most frameworks |
| Bicubic | 0 | Good | Medium | Classical image processing |
| Transposed Conv | $k^2 C^2$ | Task-best | Slow | GANs, decoders |
| Pixel Shuffle | $k^2 r^2 C^2$ | Good | Medium | Super-resolution |

**(Total: 14 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**With neat diagrams, explain transposed convolution as an up-sampling operation. Discuss the checkerboard artifact problem and its solutions.**

#### Part (a) — Transposed Convolution Mechanics (7 marks)

**Definition:** Transposed convolution (also called *deconvolution* or *fractionally-strided convolution*) is a learnable up-sampling operation that performs the *transpose* of a standard convolution's im2col matrix.

**Working Steps:**
1. **Zero-Stuffing:** Insert $(s-1)$ zeros between input pixels (where $s$ is the stride/up-sampling factor).
2. **Padding:** Add $(k-1)$ zeros of padding, where $k$ is the kernel size.
3. **Standard Convolution:** Apply a convolution with stride 1 and the learned kernel.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$Y = \text{Conv}(\text{ZeroStuff}(X)) \text{ with stride } 1$$

In matrix form, if standard convolution is $\mathbf{y} = A\mathbf{x}$, then transposed convolution is $\mathbf{y}_{\text{up}} = A^{T}\mathbf{x}$, hence the name.

**Worked Example (Stride 2, 2×2 Input, 3×3 Kernel):**

Input $X$ (2×2):
$$X = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 1 — Zero-stuff with $s-1 = 1$ zero between pixels:
$$X' = \begin{bmatrix} a & 0 & b & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ c & 0 & d & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 2 — Apply 3×3 convolution with stride 1 to produce output (size depends on padding).

**Diagrammatic representation:**

```
Input 2x2   →   Zero-stuffed 3x3   →   Conv with kernel 3x3   →   Output 4x4
[a b]              [a 0 b]                  [k1 k2 k3]
[c d]              [0 0 0]                  [k4 k5 k6]              [enlarged]
                   [c 0 d]                  [k7 k8 k9]
```

The kernel weights $k_1, \ldots, k_9$ are **learned via backpropagation**, making this a parameterized up-sampling method.

#### Part (b) — Checkerboard Artifacts & Solutions (7 marks)

**The Problem:**
When the kernel size $k$ is **not divisible** by the stride $s$, certain output positions receive contributions from *more kernel elements* than others, producing uneven overlap patterns. This manifests visually as alternating bright/dark patches — the *checkerboard artifact*.

**Mathematical Cause:**
Define the *coverage* of output position $p$ as the number of kernel positions contributing to it. If $k \bmod s \neq 0$, coverage varies cyclically across positions. For $s = 2, k = 3$, coverage alternates between $2$ and $1$ across positions.

**Harmonic Analysis (Odena et al.):**
Transposed convolution can be expressed as the *periodic shuffle* of a standard convolution. The artifact arises because the equivalent standard convolution has *uneven stride overlap*, equivalent to multiplying the feature map by a periodic square-wave mask.

**Solutions:**

1. **Use kernel size divisible by stride:** Choose $k = 2s$, $k = 4s$, etc. For $s = 2$, use $k = 4$ instead of $k = 3$.

2. **Use Upsample + Convolution block:**
   Replace `ConvTranspose(s=2, k=3)` with `Conv(k=3, padding=1)(Upsample(mode='bilinear', scale=2)(X))`.
   This decouples up-sampling (fixed) from feature learning (convolution).

3. **Use Pixel Shuffle:**
   Generate $s^2$ feature maps and rearrange them spatially, avoiding the uneven overlap problem.

4. **Use Sub-pixel Convolution (ESPCN):**
   A specialized form of pixel shuffle optimized for super-resolution tasks.

**Modern Practice:**
StyleGAN2, BigGAN, and most state-of-the-art GANs use `Upsample + Conv` blocks instead of transposed convolution. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing up-sampling with deconvolution:** Up-sampling is a *general concept*; deconvolution is *one specific implementation*. Don't use them as synonyms.
> 2. **Forgetting that parameter-free methods have NO learnable weights:** This is a 1-mark loss in 14-mark answers if not stated explicitly.
> 3. **Omitting the up-sampling factor $r$:** Always state $r$ when describing formula. $Y[i,j] = X[\lfloor i/r \rfloor, \lfloor j/r \rfloor]$ loses a mark if written as $Y[i,j] = X[i/2, j/2]$ without defining $r$.
> 4. **Confusing transposed convolution with reverse convolution:** Transposed convolution is the *gradient* of a convolution, not its inverse. It does *not* invert the convolution operation.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams in long answers:** A neat diagram of transposed convolution with zero-stuffing fetches an easy 2 marks. Always include one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Up-sampling** increases spatial resolution; it is the inverse operation of pooling.
- **Two main categories:** Parameter-free (nearest, bilinear, bicubic) and parameterized (transposed conv, pixel shuffle).
- **Nearest Neighbor:** $Y[i,j] = X[\lfloor i/r \rfloor, \lfloor j/r \rfloor]$ — fastest, blockiest.
- **Bilinear:** Weighted sum of 4 neighbors using fractional distances — smooth, slightly blurry.
- **Bicubic:** Weighted sum of 16 neighbors with cubic weighting — sharper than bilinear.
- **Transposed Convolution:** Learnable up-sampling; performs zero-stuffing then standard convolution; risks checkerboard artifacts.
- **Pixel Shuffle:** Generates $r^2$ channels and rearranges spatially; artifact-free; used in ESPCN.
- **Max-Unpooling:** Uses indices stored during max-pool; preserves spatial info; used in SegNet.
- **Upsample + Conv:** Modern best practice to avoid checkerboard artifacts; used in StyleGAN2.
- **The up-sampling factor $r$** multiplies spatial dimensions: $H' = rH$, $W' = rW$.
- **Checkerboard artifacts** occur when kernel size $k$ is not divisible by stride $s$ in transposed convolution.
- **Solution to checkerboard artifacts:** Use $k = 2s$ or replace transposed convolution with `Upsample + Conv`.
- **Common KTU exam traps:** Confusing up-sampling with deconvolution; omitting the factor $r$; ignoring diagrams.
- **Real-world usage:** GANs, U-Net, SegNet, FCN, ESPCN, StyleGAN2, DeepLab.
- **Weights in bilinear interpolation sum to 1** (partition of unity), preserving dynamic range.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformer Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transformer Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Transformer** is a deep learning architecture introduced by Vaswani et al. (2017) in the seminal paper *"Attention Is All You Need"* that relies entirely on a **self-attention mechanism** to compute parallel, position-independent representations of sequential data, completely replacing recurrence (RNN/LSTM) and convolution (CNN). The Transformer is the foundational backbone of modern Large Language Models (LLMs) such as **BERT**, **GPT**, **T5**, and **LLaMA**.

The architecture is composed of stacked **encoder** and **decoder** blocks, each containing:
- **Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHSA)**
- **Position-wise Feed-Forward Networks (FFN)**
- **Residual Connections** and **Layer Normalization (Post-LN or Pre-LN)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEECT742 - Module 4):** The Transformer model is examined as a successor to RNN/LSTM for sequence modeling, with emphasis on the **self-attention mechanism**, **scaled dot-product attention**, **multi-head attention**, **positional encoding**, and **encoder-decoder architecture**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🎬 Real-World Analogy — "The Smart Conference Room"**
>
> Imagine a conference room with **8 people** seated around a table, each holding a different document. A traditional **RNN** processes one document after another in a strict sequence — like passing papers one at a time down the line. A **CNN** looks at fixed windows of nearby people. The **Transformer**, however, is like giving **every person the ability to look at ALL other documents simultaneously** and decide, with a weighted importance score, *how much* to focus on each other document. This is **self-attention** — every token "attends" to every other token in parallel, in a single operation.

### Why Parallelism Matters (KTU Hot Topic)

| Property | RNN/LSTM | Transformer |
|---|---|---|
| Sequential Processing | ✅ Required | ❌ Fully Parallel |
| Long-Range Dependencies | ❌ Vanishing gradient | ✅ Direct via attention |
| Training Speed | Slow | **Fast (GPU-optimized)** |
| Context Window | Limited by hidden state | **Theoretically infinite** |

## 1.3 Core Terminology Cheat-Block

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Symbols (used throughout KTU questions)**
> - $d_{model}$ — Embedding/Model dimension (typically **512**)
> - $h$ — Number of attention heads (typically **8**)
> - $d_k = d_v = d_{model}/h$ — Per-head dimension (typically **64**)
> - $Q, K, V$ — Query, Key, Value matrices
> - $W^Q, W^K, W^V, W^O$ — Learnable projection matrices
> - **Softmax temperature** scales logits to probabilities
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Scaled Dot-Product Attention (Core Engine)

The **atom** of the Transformer is the *Scaled Dot-Product Attention* function. Given three matrices — **Query ($Q$)**, **Key ($K$)**, and **Value ($V$)** — all derived from the same input sequence, attention is computed as:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

### Operational Step-by-Step Breakdown

1. **Project Input Embeddings** $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$ into $Q, K, V$ using learned weight matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$ (or $d_v$).
2. **Compute Compatibility Scores** $QK^{\top}$ — measures how well each query *matches* each key.
3. **Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$** — prevents the softmax from saturating (entering tiny gradient regions) when $d_k$ is large.
4. **Apply Softmax along the key dimension** — converts raw scores into a probability distribution (rows sum to 1).
5. **Weighted Sum with $V$** — aggregates values weighted by attention probabilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$?** When $d_k$ is large, the dot products $QK^{\top}$ grow in magnitude, pushing softmax into regions with extremely small gradients. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance of the dot products to ~1, preserving stable training.

## 2.2 Multi-Head Attention (MHA)

Instead of performing a single attention function with $d_{model}$-dimensional keys/values/queries, the Transformer projects them $h$ times using **different learned projections**, then concatenates and projects again:

$$
\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) \, W^O
$$

$$
\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, \, KW_i^K, \, VW_i^V)
$$

where the projections are parameter matrices $W_i^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$, $W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$, $W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_v}$, and $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{hd_v \times d_{model}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — "Multiple Perspectives"**
> Each head learns to focus on a *different type of relationship* in parallel. One head may track syntactic dependencies (subject-verb), another may track coreference (pronoun-antecedent), another may capture positional locality. This is **representation subspace multi-axis decomposition**.

## 2.3 Positional Encoding

Since self-attention is **permutation-invariant** (it treats inputs as a *set*, not a *sequence*), the Transformer injects **positional information** into token embeddings:

$$
PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)
$$

$$
PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)
$$

The final input embedding is:

$$
X_{\text{input}} = \text{TokenEmbedding} + \text{PositionalEncoding}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why sinusoidal?** It allows the model to **generalize to sequence lengths longer than those seen during training**, because any offset $k$ can be expressed as a linear function of $PE_{pos}$.

## 2.4 Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)

Applied independently and identically to each position:

$$
\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, xW_1 + b_1) W_2 + b_2
$$

This is a **two-layer MLP with ReLU**, with inner dimension $d_{ff} = 4 d_{model}$ (typically **2048** when $d_{model}=512$).

## 2.5 Layer Normalization & Residual Connections

Each sub-layer (attention or FFN) is wrapped with a residual connection and layer norm:

$$
\text{Output} = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))
$$

Two variants exist:
- **Post-LN** (original): $x + \text{Sublayer}(x) \rightarrow \text{LayerNorm}$ — used in the original paper.
- **Pre-LN** (modern LLMs): $x + \text{Sublayer}(\text{LayerNorm}(x))$ — more stable training.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $d_k = 64$ |
| 2 | $d_k = d_v = d_{model}/h$ | Per-head dimension | $d_{model}=512$, $h=8$ |
| 3 | $\text{MHA} = \text{Concat}(h_1,\dots,h_h)W^O$ | Multi-Head Concatenation | $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{hd_v \times d_{model}}$ |
| 4 | $PE_{(pos,2i)} = \sin(pos/10000^{2i/d_{model}})$ | Sinusoidal Position (even) | $i \in [0, d_{model}/2)$ |
| 5 | $PE_{(pos,2i+1)} = \cos(pos/10000^{2i/d_{model}})$ | Sinusoidal Position (odd) | $i \in [0, d_{model}/2)$ |
| 6 | $\text{FFN}(x) = \text{ReLU}(xW_1+b_1)W_2+b_2$ | Position-wise FFN | $d_{ff}=2048$ |
| 7 | $\text{Out} = \text{LayerNorm}(x+\text{Sublayer}(x))$ | Residual + Layer Norm | — |
| 8 | $\text{Complexity} = O(n^2 \cdot d + n \cdot d^2)$ | Self-Attention Complexity | $n$=seq length |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Machine translation (originally English→German/English), text summarization, question answering, sentiment analysis.
- **Computer Vision (CV):** Vision Transformer (**ViT**), DETR, Swin Transformer.
- **Speech & Audio:** Whisper (ASR), AudioLM.
- **Multimodal AI:** CLIP, Flamingo, GPT-4V — combining vision and language.
- **Biology & Healthcare:** AlphaFold 2/3 (protein structure), drug discovery.
- **Time Series & Finance:** PatchTST, Informer for long-horizon forecasting.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Full Scaled Dot-Product Attention (Numerical Walkthrough)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **frequently-asked numerical question** in KTU Module 4. You are typically given a small sentence and asked to compute attention scores, scaled scores, softmax weights, and the final context vector.

**Given:** Sentence = "The cat sat" with token embeddings (already projected into $Q, K, V$ for $d_k = 2$, $h = 1$):

$$
Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 \\ 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where rows correspond to ["The", "cat", "sat"] and $d_k = 2$.

### Step 1 — Compute $QK^{\top}$ (Raw Compatibility Scores)

$$
QK^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Logic:** Each entry $(i,j)$ measures the dot-product similarity between query $i$ and key $j$. E.g., Row 1 ("The") attends to all keys with scores $[1, 0, 1]$.

### Step 2 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$

$$
\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}} = \frac{1}{1.414} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0 & 0.707 \\ 0 & 0.707 & 0.707 \\ 0.707 & 1.414 & 1.414 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Logic:** Scaling prevents the softmax from saturating.

### Step 3 — Apply Softmax Row-wise

For row 1 (token "The"): scores $[0.707, 0, 0.707]$

$$
e^{0.707} = 2.028, \quad e^{0} = 1.000, \quad e^{0.707} = 2.028
$$
$$
\text{Sum} = 2.028 + 1.000 + 2.028 = 5.056
$$
$$
\text{Softmax}_1 = \left[\frac{2.028}{5.056}, \frac{1.000}{5.056}, \frac{2.028}{5.056}\right] = [0.401, 0.198, 0.401]
$$

For row 2 (token "cat"): scores $[0, 0.707, 0.707]$

$$
e^{0} = 1.000, \quad e^{0.707} = 2.028, \quad e^{0.707} = 2.028
$$
$$
\text{Sum} = 5.056
$$
$$
\text{Softmax}_2 = [0.198, 0.401, 0.401]
$$

For row 3 (token "sat"): scores $[0.707, 1.414, 1.414]$

$$
e^{0.707} = 2.028, \quad e^{1.414} = 4.114, \quad e^{1.414} = 4.114
$$
$$
\text{Sum} = 10.256
$$
$$
\text{Softmax}_3 = [0.198, 0.401, 0.401]
$$

Final attention weight matrix:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.401 & 0.198 & 0.401 \\ 0.198 & 0.401 & 0.401 \\ 0.198 & 0.401 & 0.401 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Weighted Sum with $V$

$$
\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = A \cdot V = \begin{bmatrix} 0.401 & 0.198 & 0.401 \\ 0.198 & 0.401 & 0.401 \\ 0.198 & 0.401 & 0.401 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 0 \\ 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Row 1: $0.401(1) + 0.198(2) + 0.401(3) = 0.401 + 0.396 + 1.203 = 2.000$

Row 2: $0.198(1) + 0.401(2) + 0.401(3) = 0.198 + 0.802 + 1.203 = 2.203$

Row 3: $0.198(1) + 0.401(2) + 0.401(3) = 2.203$

$$
\boxed{\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \begin{bmatrix} 2.000 & 0 \\ 2.203 & 0 \\ 2.203 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

> **Logic:** The output is a new representation of each token that is a contextualized weighted combination of all values, where weights come from the attention scores. Note "cat" and "sat" now have similar representations (2.203) because they both attend heavily to the "sat" value (3), while "The" produces a different representation (2.000).

## 3.2 Derivation: Why $\sqrt{d_k}$ Scaling is Needed

Assume query and key components are **independent random variables** with mean $0$ and variance $1$. The dot product $q \cdot k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$ is a sum of $d_k$ independent products.

Each $q_i k_i$ has mean $0$ and variance $1$ (since $E[q_i]=E[k_i]=0$ and they are independent).

Therefore:

$$
\text{Var}(q \cdot k) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_i k_i) = d_k
$$

So the standard deviation grows as $\sqrt{d_k}$. Without scaling, for large $d_k$, the dot products have large magnitudes, pushing softmax into saturated regions where $\partial \text{softmax}/\partial x$ is nearly zero. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ restores unit variance and stable gradients.

## 3.3 Self-Attention Complexity Derivation

For a sequence of length $n$ and dimension $d$:

1. $QK^{\top}$ requires $n \times d \times n = O(n^2 d)$ multiplications.
2. Softmax over $n \times n$ matrix: $O(n^2)$.
3. Multiply with $V$: $n \times n \times d = O(n^2 d)$ multiplications.

$$
\boxed{\text{Time Complexity} = O(n^2 \cdot d)}
$$

This is the **key bottleneck** of Transformers — **quadratic in sequence length**, which is why methods like **FlashAttention**, **Linear Attention**, and **Sparse Attention** (Longformer, BigBird) are active research areas.

## 3.4 Multi-Head Attention Output Dimension Verification

Given:
- Input $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$, $d_{model} = 512$
- $h = 8$ heads, $d_k = d_v = 512/8 = 64$
- Each head outputs $\in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 64}$
- Concatenation: $\mathbb{R}^{n \times (8 \cdot 64)} = \mathbb{R}^{n \times 512}$
- Final projection $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{512 \times 512}$ preserves dimensionality

$$
\boxed{\text{Output dimension} = n \times d_{model} = n \times 512}
$$

> **Logic:** This dimensional preservation is critical — it allows **residual connections** and **stacking** of multiple layers without reshaping.

## 3.5 Positional Encoding — Relative Offset Property

For any fixed offset $k$, $PE_{pos+k}$ can be expressed as a linear function of $PE_{pos}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PE_{pos+k, 2i} &= \sin\!\left(\frac{pos+k}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right) \\
&= \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{k}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right) + \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{k}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a linear combination of $PE_{pos, 2i} = \sin(\dots)$ and $PE_{pos, 2i+1} = \cos(\dots)$, proving the model can learn to **attend by relative position** $k$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Full Transformer Architecture (Encoder-Decoder Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENC[Encoder Block x N=6]
        E1[Input Embedding + Positional Encoding] --> E2[Multi-Head Self-Attention]
        E2 --> E3[Add and Norm 1]
        E3 --> E4[Feed Forward Network]
        E4 --> E5[Add and Norm 2]
        E5 --> E6[Encoder Output]
    end

    subgraph DEC[Decoder Block x N=6]
        D1[Output Embedding + Positional Encoding - Shifted Right] --> D2[Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention]
        D2 --> D3[Add and Norm 1]
        D3 --> D4[Cross-Attention with Encoder K and V]
        D4 --> D5[Add and Norm 2]
        D5 --> D6[Feed Forward Network]
        D6 --> D7[Add and Norm 3]
        D7 --> D8[Linear + Softmax - Output Probabilities]
    end

    E6 --> D4
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Encoder (left):** Self-attention sees the *entire input* bidirectionally.
> - **Decoder (right):** Uses **masked** self-attention (causal — cannot see future tokens), then **cross-attention** reads from the encoder output.

## 4.2 Scaled Dot-Product Attention — Internal Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input X] --> B[Linear Projection WQ]
    A --> C[Linear Projection WK]
    A --> D[Linear Projection WV]
    B --> Q[Query Matrix Q]
    C --> K[Key Matrix K]
    D --> V[Value Matrix V]
    Q --> S1[MatMul Q times K transpose]
    K --> S1
    S1 --> S2[Scale by 1 over sqrt dk]
    S2 --> S3[Optional Masking]
    S3 --> S4[Softmax over Keys]
    S4 --> S5[MatMul with V]
    V --> S5
    S5 --> OUT[Attention Output]
```

## 4.3 Multi-Head Attention — Parallel Subspace Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Input X] --> P1[Head 1: Q1 K1 V1]
    IN --> P2[Head 2: Q2 K2 V2]
    IN --> P3[Head 3: Q3 K3 V3]
    IN --> P4[Head h: Qh Kh Vh]
    P1 --> A1[Scaled Dot-Product Attention]
    P2 --> A2[Scaled Dot-Product Attention]
    P3 --> A3[Scaled Dot-Product Attention]
    P4 --> A4[Scaled Dot-Product Attention]
    A1 --> C[Concatenate all head outputs]
    A2 --> C
    A3 --> C
    A4 --> C
    C --> W[Linear Projection WO]
    W --> MHSA[Multi-Head Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each head operates in its own $d_k = d_{model}/h$ dimensional subspace, learning distinct relational patterns in parallel.

## 4.4 Token Embedding + Positional Encoding Injection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TOK[Input Tokens: The cat sat] --> EMB[Token Embedding Layer]
    EMB --> EMBED[Token Vectors 512-dim]
    POS[Position Indices 0 1 2] --> PE[Sinusoidal Positional Encoding]
    PE --> PEVEC[Position Vectors 512-dim]
    EMBED --> SUM[Element-wise Addition]
    PEVEC --> SUM
    SUM --> X[Final Input to Transformer X]
```

## 4.5 Transformer Variants — Taxonomy Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T[Transformer Family] --> E[Encoder-only]
    T --> D[Decoder-only]
    T --> ED[Encoder-Decoder]

    E --> B1[BERT]
    E --> B2[RoBERTa]
    E --> B3[DistilBERT]

    D --> G1[GPT 1 2 3 4]
    D --> G2[LLaMA 1 2 3]
    D --> G3[Mistral and Qwen]

    ED --> ED1[Original Transformer]
    ED --> ED2[T5]
    ED --> ED3[BART]
    ED --> ED4[FLAN-T5]
```

> **Use-Case Mapping:**
> - **Encoder-only** → classification, NER, embedding (understanding tasks).
> - **Decoder-only** → text generation, chat, code (GPT family).
> - **Encoder-Decoder** → translation, summarization, seq2seq (T5, BART).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the self-attention mechanism in Transformer models. Why is it called "scaled" dot-product attention? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Self-attention is a mechanism in the Transformer that allows each position in a sequence to **attend to all other positions** in the same sequence, computing a weighted representation based on relevance.

The mathematical formulation is:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
$$

It is called **"scaled"** because the raw dot-product scores $QK^{\top}$ are divided by $\sqrt{d_k}$ (square root of the key dimension) **before** applying the softmax function. This scaling normalizes the variance of the dot products to ~1, preventing them from becoming too large, which would push the softmax into regions with near-zero gradients (saturation), thereby ensuring stable and efficient training.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the role of positional encoding in Transformer models. Mention any one limitation of sinusoidal positional encoding. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Since the self-attention mechanism is **permutation-invariant** (it treats input as an unordered set), it has no inherent sense of token order. **Positional encoding** injects information about the position of each token into the input embedding so the model can distinguish sequences with the same tokens in different orders.

Sinusoidal positional encodings are added to token embeddings:

$$
PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin(pos/10000^{2i/d_{model}}), \quad PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos(pos/10000^{2i/d_{model}})
$$

**Limitation:** Sinusoidal encodings use **fixed, non-learned** frequencies. They may not optimally capture the positional patterns needed for a specific task; **learned positional embeddings** often outperform them on tasks with bounded sequence lengths.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of the Transformer model in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of Multi-Head Attention. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Transformer follows an **encoder-decoder architecture** with $N=6$ identical layers stacked in each.

#### **Encoder Block**
Each of the 6 encoder layers contains two sub-layers:
1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention** — allows each token to attend to all input tokens.
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** — a 2-layer MLP applied independently to each position.

Each sub-layer is wrapped with a **residual connection** and **layer normalization**.

#### **Decoder Block**
Each of the 6 decoder layers contains three sub-layers:
1. **Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention** — prevents attending to future tokens (autoregressive property).
2. **Encoder-Decoder Cross-Attention** — queries come from decoder, keys/values from encoder output.
3. **Position-wise FFN** — same as encoder.

#### **Role of Multi-Head Attention**
Instead of one attention function, MHA projects $Q, K, V$ into $h=8$ subspaces:

$$
\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) W^O
$$

Each head learns to attend to information from **different representation subspaces at different positions**, capturing diverse linguistic phenomena (syntactic, semantic, positional) in parallel.

*(Include the block diagram from Section 4.1 for full marks.)*

---

**(b) Compute the scaled dot-product attention output for the following input. Given tokens "I love AI" with $Q, K, V$ matrices of size $3 \times 2$ (assume $d_k=2$). (7 Marks)**

$$
Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Compute $QK^{\top}$

$$
QK^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Scale by $\sqrt{d_k} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$

$$
S = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 1.414 & 0.707 \\ 0 & 0.707 & 0.707 \\ 0.707 & 0.707 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply row-wise softmax.

Row 1: $e^{0.707}=2.028, e^{1.414}=4.114, e^{0.707}=2.028$; sum$=8.170$
$\Rightarrow [0.248, 0.503, 0.248]$

Row 2: $e^{0}=1.000, e^{0.707}=2.028, e^{0.707}=2.028$; sum$=5.056$
$\Rightarrow [0.198, 0.401, 0.401]$

Row 3: $e^{0.707}=2.028, e^{0.707}=2.028, e^{0}=1.000$; sum$=5.056$
$\Rightarrow [0.401, 0.401, 0.198]$

**Step 4:** Compute $A \cdot V$

$$
A \cdot V = \begin{bmatrix} 0.248 & 0.503 & 0.248 \\ 0.198 & 0.401 & 0.401 \\ 0.401 & 0.401 & 0.198 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Row 1: $[0.248(1)+0.503(2)+0.248(3), \ 0.248(2)+0.503(1)+0.248(3)] = [1.999, 1.751]$

Row 2: $[0.198(1)+0.401(2)+0.401(3), \ 0.198(2)+0.401(1)+0.401(3)] = [2.005, 2.000]$

Row 3: $[0.401(1)+0.401(2)+0.198(3), \ 0.401(2)+0.401(1)+0.198(3)] = [1.999, 2.000]$

$$
\boxed{\text{Attention Output} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.999 & 1.751 \\ 2.005 & 2.000 \\ 1.999 & 2.000 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the role of Positional Encoding in Transformers. Derive the sinusoidal positional encoding formula and discuss its key properties. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

#### **Role of Positional Encoding**
Self-attention is **permutation-equivariant** — it processes inputs as a set, not a sequence. Without explicit positional information, the sentences "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" would yield identical representations. **Positional encoding** adds a position-dependent vector to each token embedding so the model can leverage order.

#### **Derivation of Sinusoidal Encoding**

We need a function $PE(pos, i) \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model}}$ satisfying:
1. Unique encoding for each position.
2. Generalizable to sequences longer than training.
3. Distance between positions should be measurable.

Choose **frequencies** forming a geometric progression from $2\pi$ to $2\pi \cdot 10000$:

$$
\omega_i = \frac{1}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}
$$

The encoding at position $pos$ and dimension $2i$ (even), $2i+1$ (odd):

$$
PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin(pos \cdot \omega_i), \quad PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos(pos \cdot \omega_i)
$$

#### **Key Properties**
- **Bounded range:** All values in $[-1, 1]$.
- **Relative position via linear transformation:** For any offset $k$,
  $PE_{pos+k} = M_k \cdot PE_{pos}$ where $M_k$ is a rotation matrix, allowing the model to learn **relative position** attention.
- **Deterministic & non-learned:** No additional parameters, generalizes to longer sequences.

---

**(b) What is Multi-Head Attention? Explain with a block diagram. How does it differ from single-head attention in terms of computational cost and representational power? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

#### **Definition**
Multi-Head Attention runs $h$ parallel scaled dot-product attention functions, each on a different learned linear projection of $Q, K, V$, then concatenates the outputs:

$$
\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h)W^O
$$

#### **Differences from Single-Head**

| Aspect | Single-Head | Multi-Head |
|---|---|---|
| Subspaces | One ($d_{model}$ dims) | $h$ subspaces ($d_{model}/h$ each) |
| Representational Power | Limited — one "view" | **Richer** — multiple parallel views |
| Parameters | $4 d_{model}^2$ | $4 d_{model}^2$ (similar) |
| Computation per head | $O(n^2 d_{model})$ | $O(n^2 d_{model}/h)$ each, total $O(n^2 d_{model})$ |

The **total parameter count is the same**, but MHA allows the model to **jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces** at different positions, capturing diverse linguistic patterns (e.g., one head for subject-verb, another for long-range coreference).

*(Include the MHA diagram from Section 4.3 for full marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling** in the attention formula — *immediate loss of 1 mark*. The paper **explicitly** states "**Scaled** Dot-Product Attention".
> 2. **Confusing encoder vs decoder masking** — Decoder self-attention uses **causal masking** (upper triangle set to $-\infty$ before softmax) to prevent looking at future tokens.
> 3. **Softmax applied row-wise** — each row must sum to 1; students often apply it column-wise by mistake.
> 4. **Positional encoding is *added*, not concatenated** — common conceptual error.
> 5. **Cross-attention vs self-attention** — In cross-attention, $Q$ comes from the decoder, $K$ and $V$ come from the encoder. This distinction is **frequently tested**.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams** — KTU awards 2–3 marks specifically for "neat block diagram with proper labeling"; an absent diagram = lost marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Transformer Models**

- [x] **Transformer = Self-Attention + FFN + Residual + LayerNorm**, stacked in encoder-decoder blocks.
- [x] **Self-Attention Formula:** $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- [x] **Why $\sqrt{d_k}$?** To normalize dot-product variance to 1 and prevent softmax saturation.
- [x] **Multi-Head Attention = $h$ parallel heads**, each with $d_{model}/h$ dims, concatenated and projected by $W^O$.
- [x] **Positional Encoding** is **additive** (not concatenated) and uses **sinusoidal** functions with geometric frequency progression.
- [x] **Positional Encoding Properties:** bounded in $[-1,1]$, allows **relative position** via linear transformation.
- [x] **FFN** is a **position-wise 2-layer MLP** with ReLU, inner dim $4 \times d_{model}$.
- [x] **Residual + LayerNorm** wraps every sub-layer (Post-LN original, Pre-LN modern).
- [x] **Decoder self-attention is MASKED** (causal); cross-attention receives $K,V$ from encoder.
- [x] **Time Complexity of self-attention = $O(n^2 \cdot d)$** — quadratic bottleneck.
- [x] **Variants:** Encoder-only (BERT), Decoder-only (GPT), Encoder-Decoder (T5, BART).
- [x] **Applications:** NLP, CV (ViT), Speech (Whisper), Biology (AlphaFold), Multimodal (CLIP).
- [x] **Hyperparameters in original paper:** $N=6$, $h=8$, $d_{model}=512$, $d_{ff}=2048$, $d_k=d_v=64$.
- [x] **No recurrence, no convolution** — full parallelism is the defining advantage over RNN/CNN.
- [x] **Permutation invariant** — positional encoding is the *only* way order is preserved.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture, Word Embedding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT742 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Architecture, Word Embedding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) Architecture — Formal Definition

A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** is a class of artificial neural networks designed to process **sequential data** (time series, text, speech, video frames) by maintaining a **hidden state** that acts as a form of memory, propagating information from one time step to the next. Unlike a feedforward network, an RNN applies the **same set of parameters** recurrently across all positions in a sequence, and the output at time step $t$ depends on both the current input $x_t$ and the hidden state $h_{t-1}$ computed from previous time steps.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 emphasizes recurrent architectures (RNN, LSTM, GRU) and representation learning (Word Embeddings) as the foundation of sequence modeling and Natural Language Processing (NLP).

## 1.2 Intuition — The "Reading-with-Short-Term-Memory" Analogy

Imagine you are reading a novel aloud. By the time you reach page 200, you still remember the protagonist's name introduced on page 1. Your brain keeps a **rolling summary** of the story as you read. An RNN does the same thing for sequences:

- The **input** at each step is the new word you are reading.
- The **hidden state** is your mental summary of the story so far.
- The **output** is what you say out loud, or your prediction for the next word.

A standard feedforward network is like a person with **amnesia** — it reads each word in isolation and forgets the previous one. An RNN is like a person with a **notebook** that gets updated after every word.

## 1.3 LSTM and GRU — Taming the Memory Problem

The vanilla RNN suffers from the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem**, which prevents it from learning long-range dependencies. Two specialized gated architectures were developed to overcome this:

| Architecture | Full Name | Gating Mechanism | Year Proposed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LSTM** | Long Short-Term Memory | Input, Forget, Output gates + Cell state | 1997 (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber) |
| **GRU** | Gated Recurrent Unit | Reset, Update gates (merged) | 2014 (Cho et al.) |

## 1.4 Word Embedding — Formal Definition

A **Word Embedding** is a **learned, dense, low-dimensional, real-valued vector** representation of words such that words with similar semantic or syntactic meaning are mapped to nearby points in a continuous vector space. Formally, each word $w$ from a vocabulary $V$ is mapped to a vector $\mathbf{e}_w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, where $d$ (typically 50–300) is much smaller than the vocabulary size $\vert V \vert$ (often $10^5$–$10^6$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction — One-Hot vs. Embedding:** A one-hot vector of dimension $\vert V \vert$ is **sparse, high-dimensional, and orthogonal** (dot product = 0 for all distinct words). A word embedding is **dense, low-dimensional, and captures similarity** (similar words have similar vectors).

## 1.5 Intuition — The "Map of Words" Analogy

Imagine plotting every word in the English language on a giant map. On this map:

- **King** and **Queen** would be close to each other.
- **Paris**, **London**, and **Tokyo** would cluster in a "cities" neighborhood.
- **Walked**, **ran**, and **jumped** would cluster in a "verbs of motion" zone.

This map is a **word embedding space**. Each word has coordinates (a vector), and **distance and direction in this space carry meaning**. The famous result $\vec{King} - \vec{Man} + \vec{Woman} \approx \vec{Queen}$ shows that the geometry of embeddings encodes **semantic relationships**.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical constant / standard metric:** The standard embedding dimension $d$ in modern deep learning pipelines is **$d = 300$** (Word2Vec, GloVe), though $d = 100$, $200$, and $768$ (BERT-base) are also common.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vanilla RNN — Operational Steps

At every time step $t$, the RNN performs the following computation:

1. **Receive** the input vector $\mathbf{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_x}$ and the previous hidden state $\mathbf{h}_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$.
2. **Compute** the new hidden state as a linear combination followed by a non-linearity:

   $$\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh\left(\mathbf{W}_{xh} \mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh} \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h\right)$$

3. **Produce** the output $\mathbf{y}_t$ (optional, depends on the task):

   $$\mathbf{y}_t = \text{softmax}\left(\mathbf{W}_{hy} \mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y\right)$$

4. **Pass** $\mathbf{h}_t$ to the next time step.

**Why these steps?** The recurrent weight $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ allows information from the past to flow into the present. The $\tanh$ squashes the activations to $[-1, 1]$, providing non-linearity.

## 2.2 LSTM — Operational Steps

The LSTM introduces a **cell state** $\mathbf{C}_t$ (the "long-term memory highway") and three gates that regulate information flow:

1. **Forget gate** — decides what to throw away from the cell state:
   $$\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma\left(\mathbf{W}_f \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f\right)$$
2. **Input gate** — decides what new information to store:
   $$\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma\left(\mathbf{W}_i \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i\right)$$
   $$\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh\left(\mathbf{W}_C \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_C\right)$$
3. **Cell state update**:
   $$\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$$
4. **Output gate** — decides what to output from the cell state:
   $$\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma\left(\mathbf{W}_o \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_o\right)$$
   $$\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh\left(\mathbf{C}_t\right)$$

**Why these steps?** The $\odot$ (element-wise multiplication) with the sigmoid-gated values acts as a **soft switch** — values near 0 block information, values near 1 let it pass. The **additive cell state update** is the key to avoiding vanishing gradients.

## 2.3 GRU — Operational Steps

The GRU simplifies LSTM by merging the cell state and hidden state, and using only two gates:

1. **Update gate** — controls how much of the past to keep:
   $$\mathbf{z}_t = \sigma\left(\mathbf{W}_z \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_z\right)$$
2. **Reset gate** — controls how much of the past to forget:
   $$\mathbf{r}_t = \sigma\left(\mathbf{W}_r \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_r\right)$$
3. **Candidate hidden state**:
   $$\tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t = \tanh\left(\mathbf{W}_h \cdot [\mathbf{r}_t \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_h\right)$$
4. **Final hidden state**:
   $$\mathbf{h}_t = (1 - \mathbf{z}_t) \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t$$

**Why these steps?** The linear interpolation $(1 - \mathbf{z}_t) \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t$ creates a **gradient highway** (similar to LSTM's cell state) with fewer parameters.

## 2.4 Word2Vec — Two Architectures (Mikolov et al., 2013)

### 2.4.1 Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW)

- **Goal:** Predict the **target (center) word** from a **context window** of surrounding words.
- **Architecture:** Input (one-hot vectors of context words) → Embedding layer (averaged) → Hidden layer → Output softmax over vocabulary.
- **Use case:** Faster to train, better for frequent words.

### 2.4.2 Skip-gram

- **Goal:** Predict **surrounding context words** given a **single center word**.
- **Architecture:** Input (one-hot of center word) → Embedding layer → Hidden layer → Output softmax (one per context position).
- **Use case:** Better for rare words, produces higher-quality embeddings for small corpora.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Description | Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RNN hidden update** | $\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h)$ | Recurrent state transition | $d_h = 128$ to $512$ |
| **Sigmoid gate** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Gate activation, output $\in [0,1]$ | — |
| **LSTM cell state** | $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ | Long-term memory update | — |
| **GRU hidden state** | $\mathbf{h}_t = (1-\mathbf{z}_t)\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t$ | Convex combination update | — |
| **Softmax** | $\text{softmax}(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | Converts logits to probabilities | — |
| **Embedding lookup** | $\mathbf{e}_w = \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{x}_w^{oh}$ | Where $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times \vert V \vert}$ is embedding matrix | $d = 50$ to $300$ |
| **Negative sampling loss** | $L = -\log \sigma(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{u}_{w_I}) - \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log \sigma(-\mathbf{v}_{w_k}^{\top} \mathbf{u}_{w_I})$ | Efficient Word2Vec objective | $K = 5$ to $20$ |
| **Cosine similarity** | $\cos(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \frac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert_2 \, \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert_2}$ | Measures embedding similarity | $\in [-1, 1]$ |
| **Cross-entropy loss** | $L = -\sum_{i} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Standard classification objective | — |
| **BPTT truncation** | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}}$ | Sum of gradients over truncated window | $\tau = 50$ to $100$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** LSTM/GRU architectures power **machine translation** (Google Translate), **speech recognition** (Siri, Alexa), **time-series forecasting** (stock prices, weather), and **music generation**. Word embeddings are the foundational input representation for nearly every modern NLP system, from sentiment analysis to large language models.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The RNN Gradient Flow (Why Gradients Vanish)

Consider an **unrolled** RNN over $T$ time steps. The loss at time step $t$ depends on the hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$, which in turn depends on $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \mathbf{y}_t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{y}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$$

The chain of partial derivatives $\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}$ for $k < t$ is the critical term. Unrolling it:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}}$$

Since $\mathbf{h}_i = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{i-1} + \cdots)$, the Jacobian is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_i}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{i-1}} = \text{diag}\left(1 - \tanh^2(\cdot)\right) \cdot \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}$$

The magnitude of each term is bounded by $\Vert \mathbf{W}_{hh} \Vert$ (the spectral norm). After $T - k$ multiplications:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \right\Vert \leq \Vert \mathbf{W}_{hh} \Vert^{T-k}$$

**Logical conclusion of derivation:**

- If $\Vert \mathbf{W}_{hh} \Vert < 1$ → the product **vanishes exponentially** → gradients become zero → RNN cannot learn long-range dependencies.
- If $\Vert \mathbf{W}_{hh} \Vert > 1$ → the product **explodes exponentially** → gradients become NaN → training diverges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LSTM's trick:** The additive cell state update $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ makes the gradient path a **sum** rather than a **product**, providing a stable gradient highway.

## 3.2 Derivation: Word2Vec Skip-gram with Negative Sampling (SGNS) Loss

Let $\mathbf{u}_{w_I}$ be the **center (input)** word vector and $\mathbf{v}_{w_O}$ be the **context (output)** word vector. The **inner product** $\mathbf{u}_{w_I}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_O}$ scores how compatible the two words are. SGNS samples $K$ "negative" words $w_k$ from a unigram distribution raised to the $\frac{3}{4}$ power.

The **probability that the word pair $(w_I, w_O)$ appears in the corpus** (positive sample) is modeled with a sigmoid:

$$P(D = 1 \mid w_I, w_O) = \sigma\left(\mathbf{u}_{w_I}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_O}\right) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\mathbf{u}_{w_I}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_O}}}$$

**Step 1 — Write the likelihood for one positive sample and $K$ negative samples:**

$$P(w_O \mid w_I) \approx \sigma(\mathbf{u}_{w_I}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_O}) \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{K} \sigma(-\mathbf{u}_{w_I}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_k})$$

**Step 2 — Take the negative log-likelihood to obtain the loss:**

$$L = -\log \sigma(\mathbf{u}_{w_I}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_O}) - \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log \sigma(-\mathbf{u}_{w_I}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_k})$$

**Step 3 — Express the log-sigmoid compactly using the softplus $\log(1 + e^{x})$:**

$$L = -\log \sigma(\mathbf{u}_{w_I}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_O}) - \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log \sigma(-\mathbf{u}_{w_I}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_k})$$

**Step 4 — Compute the gradient w.r.t. the input vector $\mathbf{u}_{w_I}$ (used in stochastic gradient descent):**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{u}_{w_I}} = \left[\sigma(\mathbf{u}_{w_I}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_O}) - 1\right] \mathbf{v}_{w_O} + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sigma(\mathbf{u}_{w_I}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_k}) \cdot \mathbf{v}_{w_k}$$

**Interpretation of the gradient:** When the model predicts the true context word with low probability (i.e., $\sigma(\mathbf{u}^{\top}\mathbf{v})$ is small), the term $[\sigma(\mathbf{u}^{\top}\mathbf{v}) - 1]$ is large and negative, pushing $\mathbf{u}$ toward $\mathbf{v}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing CBOW vs. Skip-gram

| Analytical Aspect | CBOW | Skip-gram |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | $2c$ context words (one-hot) | 1 center word (one-hot) |
| **Output** | 1 center word (softmax) | $2c$ context words (softmax) |
| **Hidden layer** | Average of context word vectors | Pass-through of center word vector |
| **Loss per sample** | $-\log P(w_O \mid \text{context})$ | $-\sum_{c} \log P(w_c \mid w_I)$ |
| **Training speed** | Faster | Slower ($2c \times$ more updates) |
| **Quality on rare words** | Lower | Higher |
| **Sensitive to corpus size** | Small corpora → poor | Works even on small corpora |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Google uses **Skip-gram** in its initial word2vec release, while Facebook's **fastText** extends Skip-gram with subword information. Both are deployed at internet scale for search ranking, recommendation, and ad targeting.


*Caption:* Each hidden state $H_t$ receives input from the current input $X_t$ and the previous hidden state $H_{t-1}$. The same weight matrix $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ is shared across all time steps.

## 4.2 LSTM Cell — Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    xt[Input Xt] --> concat1[Concat Ht-1 and Xt]
    ht1[Hidden Ht-1] --> concat1
    concat1 --> ft[Forget Gate Sigmoid]
    concat1 --> it[Input Gate Sigmoid]
    concat1 --> ot[Output Gate Sigmoid]
    concat1 --> cand[Candidate Tanh]
    ct1[Cell State Ct-1] --> mul1[Multiply]
    ft --> mul1
    it --> mul2[Multiply]
    cand --> mul2
    mul1 --> add[Add]
    mul2 --> add
    add --> ct[New Cell State Ct]
    ot --> mul3[Multiply]
    ct --> tanhout[Tanh]
    tanhout --> mul3
    mul3 --> ht[New Hidden Ht]
    style ct fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ht fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

*Caption:* LSTM cell showing the three gates (forget, input, output), the candidate cell state, and the additive cell-state update pathway.

## 4.3 Word2Vec — Skip-gram Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    inp[Center Word One-Hot] --> emb[Embedding Matrix E]
    emb --> embvec[Word Vector u]
    embvec --> dotpos[Dot Product with Positive Context vO]
    embvec --> dotneg1[Dot Product with Neg Sample 1]
    embvec --> dotneg2[Dot Product with Neg Sample 2]
    embvec --> dotneg3[Dot Product with Neg Sample 3]
    dotpos --> sigpos[Sigmoid]
    dotneg1 --> signeg1[Sigmoid]
    dotneg2 --> signeg2[Sigmoid]
    dotneg3 --> signeg3[Sigmoid]
    sigpos --> loss[Binary Cross Entropy Loss]
    signeg1 --> loss
    signeg2 --> loss
    signeg3 --> loss
    loss --> sgd[SGD Update on E]
    sgd --> emb
    style emb fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style loss fill:#ea9999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

*Caption:* Skip-gram with Negative Sampling. The center word is embedded, then scored against one positive context word and $K$ negative samples drawn from a noise distribution.

## 4.4 Word2Vec — CBOW Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    c1[Context Word 1] --> proj
    c2[Context Word 2] --> proj
    c3[Context Word 3] --> proj
    c4[Context Word 4] --> proj
    proj[Average of Embeddings] --> hid[Hidden Layer]
    hid --> out[Output Softmax over Vocabulary]
    out --> prob[Predicted Center Word]
    style proj fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style out fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

*Caption:* CBOW aggregates the context word vectors (typically by averaging), projects them through a hidden layer, and outputs a softmax distribution over the vocabulary to predict the center word.
